topics.title: Basic Concepts of Colloids and Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Colloids and Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Colloid?

A **colloid** is a heterogeneous, non-homogeneous two-phase system in which one substance (the **dispersed phase**) is finely distributed as particles, droplets, or bubbles (with at least one dimension between **1 nm and 1000 nm**) within a second continuous substance (the **dispersion medium**). The system is intermediate in character between a true solution and a coarse suspension.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *A colloidal system is a heterogeneous system in which one phase (the dispersed phase) is distributed as particles of colloidal size (1 nm – 1000 nm) in a second phase (the dispersion medium or continuous phase).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Think of a glass of milk. The white cloudy appearance is **not** because the fat molecules have dissolved like sugar in water. Instead, tiny droplets of butterfat are *floating* throughout the water, far too small to settle down (sediment) but big enough to scatter light (which is exactly why you cannot see through milk). The droplets never settle, never dissolve — they just remain suspended. This is the everyday meaning of a colloid.

In the same way, fog is a colloid (tiny water droplets in air), whipped cream is a colloid (air bubbles in cream), and even blood is a colloidal suspension of proteins and cells in plasma.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The particles are **invisible to the naked eye**, **do not settle under gravity** (kinetically stable), and **scatter light** (Tyndall effect). These three observations are the practical fingerprints of a colloid.

### 1.3 What is an Interface?

An **interface** is the boundary region where two immiscible (or partially miscible) phases meet. The most common interfaces in colloid science are:

- **Solid – Liquid (S/L)**
- **Liquid – Gas (L/G)** – this is the **surface** of a liquid (often called *free surface*)
- **Solid – Gas (S/G)**
- **Liquid – Liquid (L/L)**
- **Solid – Solid (S/S)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interface vs Surface:** In strict thermodynamic language, a *surface* refers specifically to a condensed-phase / gas boundary, while an *interface* refers to any boundary between two bulk phases. However, in KTU parlance the terms are used interchangeably, except for "free surface" of a liquid.

### 1.4 Real-World Interfaces – Intuition

Imagine dropping water on a freshly waxed car. The drop **beads up** into a near-spherical shape rather than spreading out. This is the *interface* between water (liquid) and wax (solid) behaving energetically. The molecules at the boundary of the water drop are in a different energetic state from those deep inside the bulk — this is what gives rise to **surface tension** and **interfacial tension**, the two pillars of colloid science.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants / Metrics

- **Colloidal size range:** 1 nm – 1000 nm (1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m)
- **Specific surface area (SSA):** Surface area per unit mass, typically **m²/g** for colloids (very high)
- **Surface tension of water at 20 °C:** **γ = 72.8 mN/m** (or **dyn/cm** in CGS)
- **Surface tension of mercury at 20 °C:** **γ = 485.5 mN/m**
- **Standard conditions:** Reported surface tensions are usually at **25 °C** unless otherwise stated.
- **Boltzmann constant (k_B):** **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **Avogadro's number (N_A):** **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- **Universal gas constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, the **surface tension of water at 25 °C = 71.97 mN/m ≈ 72 mN/m** is the value most commonly supplied or assumed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Colloidal Systems

Colloids are classified by the **physical state of the dispersed phase** and the **dispersion medium**:

| Dispersion Medium | Dispersed Phase | Colloid Type | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gas | Liquid | Liquid aerosol | Fog, mist, cloud |
| Gas | Solid | Solid aerosol | Smoke, dust |
| Liquid | Gas | Foam | Whipped cream, soap foam |
| Liquid | Liquid | Emulsion | Milk, mayonnaise, blood |
| Liquid | Solid | Sol | Paint, ink, colloidal gold |
| Solid | Gas | Solid foam | Pumice, styrofoam |
| Solid | Liquid | Gel | Jelly, cheese, butter |
| Solid | Solid | Solid sol | Ruby glass, alloys |

### 2.2 Classification by Affinity (Lyophilic / Lyophobic)

- **Lyophilic colloids** (solvent-loving): The dispersed phase has a strong affinity for the medium. They are **reversible** (can be re-dispersed after drying) and are **thermodynamically stable**. Example: Gum, starch, proteins in water.
- **Lyophobic colloids** (solvent-hating): No affinity, prepared only by special methods, **irreversible** (once dried, cannot be re-dispersed easily) and are **thermodynamically unstable** but kinetically protected. Example: Gold sol, As₂S₃ sol, Fe(OH)₃ sol.

> [!NOTE]
> When the dispersion medium is **water**, the terms **hydrophilic** and **hydrophobic** are used in place of lyophilic and lyophobic respectively.

### 2.3 Classification by Molecular Structure

- **Multimolecular colloids:** Aggregates of many small atoms/molecules (e.g., S₈ in water, gold sol). Particle size > 1 nm because of aggregation.
- **Macromolecular colloids:** Single large molecules with colloidal dimensions (e.g., proteins, cellulose, polymers).
- **Associated colloids (micelles):** Surfactant molecules aggregate above the **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)** to form micelles.

### 2.4 The Core Distinction: Colloid vs True Solution vs Suspension

| Property | True Solution | Colloid | Suspension |
|---|---|---|---|
| Particle size | < 1 nm | 1 nm – 1000 nm | > 1000 nm |
| Visibility | Invisible | Visible under ultramicroscope | Visible to naked eye |
| Tyndall effect | No | **Yes** | May show |
| Settling | No settling | No settling (kinetically stable) | Settles by gravity |
| Filterability | Passes through filter paper | Passes through filter paper, blocked by ultrafilter | Blocked by filter paper |
| Nature | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | Heterogeneous |
| Diffusion | Fast | Slow | Negligible |

### 2.5 The Three Optical Signatures of a Colloid

1. **Tyndall Effect** – Scattering of light by colloidal particles (used in ultramicroscope).
2. **Brownian Motion** – Random zig-zag motion of colloidal particles due to bombardment by molecules of the medium (proved by Perrin, won Nobel 1926).
3. **Osmotic Pressure** – Colloids show very small osmotic pressure (used in molecular weight determination via π = cRT for dilute colloids).

### 2.6 Surface Tension vs Interfacial Tension

- **Surface tension (γ):** Force per unit length (N/m) acting perpendicular to a line drawn on the liquid surface. It is the **excess free energy per unit area** of the surface (J/m² = N/m).
- **Interfacial tension (γ_ij):** Same idea, but at the boundary between two immiscible liquids (L/L) or a solid/liquid system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units:** Surface tension has dual units — **N/m (force/length)** or **J/m² (energy/area)**. They are dimensionally equivalent.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma \;=\; \dfrac{F}{\ell}$ | Surface tension as force per unit length | N/m |
| 2 | $\gamma \;=\; \dfrac{W_{rev}}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{G_{excess}}{A}$ | Surface tension as reversible work per unit area | J/m² |
| 3 | $\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Laplace pressure across a spherical interface (curved surface) | Pa |
| 4 | $\Delta P \;=\; \gamma \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$ | Young–Laplace equation (general, two principal radii) | Pa |
| 5 | $h \;=\; \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | Capillary rise (Jurin's law), θ = contact angle | m |
| 6 | $\gamma_{SG} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$ | Young's equation for contact angle | N/m |
| 7 | $W_{adh} \;=\; \gamma_{SG} + \gamma_{LG} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Work of adhesion (Dupré) | J/m² |
| 8 | $W_{coh} \;=\; 2\gamma_{LG}$ | Work of cohesion (single liquid) | J/m² |
| 9 | $S_{par} \;=\; \gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LG}$ | Spreading coefficient (S > 0 ⇒ complete wetting) | N/m |
| 10 | $\Delta G_{mix} \;=\; nRT \left( x_A \ln x_A + x_B \ln x_B \right)$ | Free energy of ideal mixing (regular solution) | J |
| 11 | $\pi \;=\; cRT$ | Osmotic pressure of a colloid (van't Hoff form) | Pa |
| 12 | $SSA \;=\; \dfrac{A_{surface}}{m_{sample}}$ | Specific surface area | m²/g |
| 13 | $v_{Stokes} \;=\; \dfrac{2r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g}{9\eta}$ | Stokes' settling velocity (suspension, not colloid) | m/s |
| 14 | $\lambda_{Tyndall} \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{4}}$ | Wavelength dependence of Rayleigh scattering | — |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **never** use the pipe symbol `$\vert x \vert$`. Always write `$\lvert x \rvert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` to keep the table rendering intact.

### 2.8 Why are these formulas "high-yield" for KTU?

- The **Young–Laplace equation** appears in nearly every KTU question paper on interfaces.
- **Young's equation** for contact angle is the bread-and-butter of wetting problems.
- **Stokes' law** often appears as a *distractor* in colloid questions (because colloidal particles are too small to obey Stokes' law, and Brownian motion dominates).
- The **spreading coefficient** decides whether a paint or pesticide will spread on a leaf or bead up.

### 2.9 Engineering Utility of Colloids and Interfaces

- **Pharmaceuticals:** Drug delivery via liposomes, micelles, and emulsions.
- **Cosmetics:** Creams, lotions, shampoos (all colloidal).
- **Paints and coatings:** Pigment particles dispersed in oil/water.
- **Food industry:** Mayonnaise (emulsion), ice-cream (foam + emulsion), whipped toppings.
- **Cement and ceramics:** Sol-gel processing.
- **Detergency:** Micellar solubilization of oil/grease.
- **Enhanced oil recovery:** Interfacial tension reduction in porous rocks.
- **Biomedical implants:** Surface modification to control protein adsorption.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Work Required to Increase a Liquid Surface (γ as Energy/Area)

**Statement:** Show that the surface tension of a liquid (in J/m²) is numerically equal to the reversible work required to create one unit area of new surface.

**Derivation:**

Consider a soap film held on a wire frame, one side of which is movable. The film has **two surfaces** (front and back), each of area $A$.

To extend the film reversibly by a small area $dA$, a force $F$ must be applied through a small displacement $dx$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reversible work done} \; W_{rev} &= F \cdot dx \\[4pt]
\text{Since the film has 2 surfaces,} \quad F &= 2\gamma \ell \\[4pt]
\therefore \; W_{rev} &= 2\gamma \ell \cdot dx \\[4pt]
\text{But total new area created} \quad dA_{total} &= 2 \, \ell \, dx \\[4pt]
\therefore \; \gamma &= \dfrac{W_{rev}}{dA_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This proves that surface tension is **reversible work per unit area**, with units of J/m². The factor of 2 appears only for thin films (two surfaces); for a single free surface, no factor of 2 is needed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – The Young–Laplace Equation (Pressure Inside a Bubble)

**Statement:** Derive the pressure difference across a spherical liquid surface (e.g., a soap bubble or liquid drop) of radius $r$ and surface tension $\gamma$.

**Derivation:**

Consider a spherical drop of radius $r$ with internal pressure $P_{in}$ and external pressure $P_{out}$. Blow the drop up slowly by an infinitesimal amount $dr$. The new surface area increases by $dA$ and the volume by $dV$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 4\pi r^{2} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad dA &= 8\pi r \, dr \\[4pt]
V &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad dV &= 4\pi r^{2} \, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

The work done by the pressure difference to expand the drop is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{PV} &= \Delta P \cdot dV = (P_{in} - P_{out}) \cdot 4\pi r^{2} \, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

The work done against surface tension to create new surface is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\gamma} &= \gamma \cdot dA = \gamma \cdot 8\pi r \, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

For **reversible expansion** (mechanical equilibrium), the work done by the internal pressure equals the work needed to create the new surface:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P \cdot 4\pi r^{2} \, dr &= \gamma \cdot 8\pi r \, dr \\[6pt]
\Delta P \cdot 4\pi r^{2} &= 8\pi r \gamma \\[6pt]
\boxed{\;\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Young–Laplace equation** for a sphere. Note that a **soap bubble** (with two surfaces, inner and outer) has $\Delta P = 4\gamma / r$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Young's Equation for Contact Angle

**Statement:** A liquid drop rests on a flat solid surface with contact angle $\theta$. Derive the relationship between the three interfacial tensions acting at the three-phase contact line.

**Derivation:**

Three interfaces meet at the contact line:
- Solid–Gas (SG) — pulls the drop outward along the solid surface.
- Solid–Liquid (SL) — pulls the drop inward along the solid surface.
- Liquid–Gas (LG) — pulls tangentially along the drop surface.

At mechanical equilibrium (the contact line is stationary), the horizontal components of the three interfacial forces must sum to zero. Resolving along the solid surface:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{SG} &= \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad \cos\theta &= \dfrac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Young's equation**, valid for an *ideal* (smooth, homogeneous, rigid) surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A necessary condition for Young's equation to have a real solution is $\lvert \gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL} \rvert \le \gamma_{LG}$. If this fails, the liquid either completely spreads ($\theta = 0°$) or does not wet at all ($\theta = 180°$).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 – Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

**Statement:** Derive the equilibrium height $h$ to which a liquid rises in a capillary tube of radius $r$ immersed in a pool of the same liquid. The contact angle is $\theta$, the liquid density $\rho$, and surface tension $\gamma$.

**Derivation:**

The liquid rises because the upward force due to surface tension around the contact line balances the downward gravitational weight of the column of liquid.

**Upward force** (vertical component of surface tension along the contact perimeter):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{up} &= 2\pi r \cdot \gamma \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Downward force** (weight of the cylindrical liquid column of height $h$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{down} &= m g = (\rho V) g = \rho \cdot (\pi r^{2} h) \cdot g
\end{aligned}
$$

At equilibrium $F_{up} = F_{down}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta &= \pi r^{2} h \rho g \\[6pt]
\boxed{\;h \;=\; \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

For water in a clean glass capillary, $\theta \approx 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1$ and the maximum possible rise is achieved: $h_{max} = 2\gamma / (\rho g r)$.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 – Stokes' Law (Settling of a Sphere)

**Statement:** Derive the terminal velocity $v$ of a rigid sphere of radius $r$ falling under gravity through a viscous fluid of viscosity $\eta$, when the sphere has density $\rho_p$ and the fluid has density $\rho_m$.

**Derivation:**

At terminal velocity, the gravitational force is balanced by buoyancy plus viscous drag (Stokes' drag for laminar flow, $Re < 0.1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weight} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \rho_{p} \, g \\[4pt]
\text{Buoyancy} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \rho_{m} \, g \\[4pt]
\text{Stokes drag} &= 6\pi \eta r v
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting net weight = viscous drag:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}(\rho_{p} - \rho_{m}) g &= 6\pi \eta r v \\[6pt]
\boxed{\;v_{Stokes} \;=\; \dfrac{2 r^{2} (\rho_{p} - \rho_{m}) g}{9 \eta}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Stokes' law is *not* valid for colloidal particles. Colloids are small enough that **Brownian motion** dominates over gravity, and they never truly settle at equilibrium.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Surface Phenomena

| Phenomenon | Driving Force | Key Equation | Magnitude of Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capillary rise | $\gamma \cos\theta / r$ vs $\rho g h$ | $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta / \rho g r$ | Larger for smaller $r$ |
| Laplace pressure | Curvature of interface | $\Delta P = 2\gamma / r$ | Larger for smaller $r$ (tiny bubbles have huge internal pressure) |
| Wetting | Balance of $\gamma_{SG}, \gamma_{SL}, \gamma_{LG}$ | $\cos\theta = (\gamma_{SG}-\gamma_{SL})/\gamma_{LG}$ | Determines if $\theta < 90°$ (wetting) or $> 90°$ (non-wetting) |
| Sedimentation | Gravity vs viscous drag | $v = 2r^2 \Delta\rho g / 9\eta$ | Negligible for $r < 1\,\mu m$ (colloidal regime) |
| Brownian motion | Thermal energy $k_B T$ | $\langle x^2 \rangle = 2Dt$ | Dominates at $r < 1\,\mu m$ |
| Tyndall scattering | Particle polarizability vs $\lambda^4$ | $I \propto 1/\lambda^4$ | Strong for blue light, weak for red |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram – Architecture of a Colloidal System

The following functional architecture flow shows the two main conceptual pillars of Module 1 — *Colloids* and *Interfaces* — and how their sub-components interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Colloidal System] --> B1[Dispersed Phase 1 to 1000 nm]
    A1 --> C1[Dispersion Medium Continuous Phase]
    B1 --> D1[Lyophilic Solvent Loving]
    B1 --> D2[Lyophobic Solvent Hating]
    C1 --> E1[Liquid Medium]
    C1 --> E2[Gas Medium]
    C1 --> E3[Solid Medium]
    A2[Interface Science] --> B2[Liquid Gas Free Surface]
    A2 --> C2[Liquid Liquid Interfacial Tension]
    A2 --> D3[Solid Liquid Wetting Contact Angle]
    A2 --> E4[Solid Gas Adsorption]
    A2 --> F1[Property Surface Tension gamma]
    A2 --> F2[Property Interfacial Tension gamma ij]
    A2 --> F3[Property Capillary Rise]
    A1 --- G1[Common Observable Tyndall Effect]
    A1 --- G2[Common Observable Brownian Motion]
    A2 --- G1
    A2 --- G2
```

### 4.2 Schematic – Three-Phase Contact Line and Young's Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SG[gamma SG Solid Gas] -->|tangent to solid| TL[Three Phase Contact Line]
    SL[gamma SL Solid Liquid] -->|along solid surface| TL
    LG[gamma LG Liquid Gas] -->|tangent to drop at angle theta| TL
    TL --> EQ[Young Equation gamma SG equals gamma SL plus gamma LG cos theta]
```

### 4.3 Schematic – Hierarchy of Colloidal Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Colloids] --> H2[By Phase Combination]
    H1 --> H3[By Affinity to Medium]
    H1 --> H4[By Molecular Structure]
    H2 --> H21[Sol Liquid plus Solid]
    H2 --> H22[Emulsion Liquid plus Liquid]
    H2 --> H23[Foam Gas plus Liquid]
    H2 --> H24[Aerosol Liquid or Solid plus Gas]
    H2 --> H25[Gel Solid plus Liquid]
    H3 --> H31[Lyophilic Reversible]
    H3 --> H32[Lyophobic Irreversible]
    H4 --> H41[Multimolecular Aggregates]
    H4 --> H42[Macromolecular Single Big Molecule]
    H4 --> H43[Associated Micelles Above CMC]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

### 4.5 Schematic – Energy Balance at a Contact Line (Wetting Regimes)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Liquid Drop on Solid] --> S1{Contact Angle theta}
    S1 -->|theta less than 90 degrees| S2[Partial Wetting]
    S1 -->|theta equals 0 degrees| S3[Complete Wetting Spreading Coefficient S greater than 0]
    S1 -->|theta greater than 90 degrees| S4[Non Wetting Beading]
    S1 -->|theta equals 180 degrees| S5[Complete Non Wetting Lotus Effect]
    S2 --> S21[cos theta positive]
    S3 --> S31[gamma SG greater than gamma SL plus gamma LG]
    S4 --> S41[cos theta negative]
```

> [!TIP]
> In the diagram above, each box is a *functional block* representing a wetting regime, not a physical object. This is the recommended approach when a Mermaid block cannot easily render a literal physical sketch.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]
**Define the term *colloid*. Mention any two distinguishing properties that separate a colloid from a true solution.**

**Model Answer:**

A colloid is a heterogeneous, two-phase system in which one phase (the dispersed phase) exists as particles of size **1 nm to 1000 nm** distributed uniformly throughout a second phase (the dispersion medium).

**Two distinguishing properties:**

1. **Tyndall Effect:** Colloidal particles scatter a beam of light passing through the system, making the path of the beam visible. True solutions do **not** show this effect because their dissolved particles (ions/molecules) are smaller than the wavelength of visible light.

2. **Heterogeneity under Ultramicroscope:** Colloidal particles are too small to be seen in an ordinary microscope but become visible as bright specks when viewed against a Tyndall cone in an ultramicroscope. True solutions appear optically empty.

*(Other acceptable distinguishing properties: Brownian motion, slow diffusion, low osmotic pressure, inability to pass through ultrafilter membranes.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

#### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]
**Distinguish between *surface tension* and *interfacial tension* with a suitable example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Surface Tension | Interfacial Tension |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | The excess free energy per unit area of a liquid surface in contact with its own vapour (or vacuum) | The excess free energy per unit area at the boundary between two immiscible condensed phases |
| Phases involved | Liquid – Gas (or vacuum) | Liquid – Liquid, Solid – Liquid, Solid – Solid |
| Numerical value | For water at 25 °C, $\gamma_{LV} \approx 72$ mN/m | For water–benzene at 25 °C, $\gamma_{WL} \approx 35$ mN/m (lower than water–air) |
| Example | The surface of water in a glass, mercury drop in air | A drop of oil floating on water |

**Key Insight:** A surface is a *special case* of an interface where one of the two phases is a gas. The interfacial tension between two liquids is always **less than** the surface tension of the higher-tension liquid alone (Antonow's rule as a rough guide). **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]
**(a)** *Derive the Young–Laplace equation for the pressure difference across a spherical liquid–gas interface. State clearly the assumptions.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A soap bubble of radius 5 mm is blown from a soap solution of surface tension 25 mN/m. Calculate the excess pressure inside the bubble compared to the atmosphere. If the bubble is allowed to coalesce with another identical bubble to form a single bubble, what will be the new radius and excess pressure?* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Young–Laplace Equation**

*[See the full derivation provided in Section 3.2 above. Key assumptions to be stated:]*

**Assumptions:**
1. The interface is *spherical* and *isotropic*.
2. The surface tension $\gamma$ is *uniform* and *constant* over the entire surface.
3. There are no external fields (electric, magnetic) acting on the surface.
4. The system is at *mechanical equilibrium*.
5. The interface has *negligible thickness* compared to the radius of curvature.

**Result:**

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}
$$

**For a soap bubble** (two surfaces, inner and outer):

$$
\Delta P_{bubble} \;=\; \dfrac{4\gamma}{r}
$$

**[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Radius of soap bubble, $r = 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Surface tension, $\gamma = 25 \text{ mN/m} = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m

**Step 1 — Excess pressure of a single soap bubble:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{single} &= \dfrac{4 \gamma}{r} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 20 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The excess pressure inside a single bubble is **20 Pa**.

---

**Step 2 — When two bubbles of equal volume coalesce:**

The total volume is conserved (assuming isothermal, no gas escape):

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \times \left( \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_1^{3} \right) &= \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_2^{3} \\[4pt]
2 r_1^{3} &= r_2^{3} \\[4pt]
r_2 &= 2^{1/3} \cdot r_1 \\[4pt]
r_2 &= 2^{1/3} \times 5 \text{ mm} = 1.2599 \times 5 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
r_2 &\approx 6.30 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3 — New excess pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{new} &= \dfrac{4 \gamma}{r_2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}}{6.30 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{100}{6.30} \approx 15.87 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers:**
- New radius $r_2 \approx$ **6.30 mm** (or $5 \cdot 2^{1/3}$ mm)
- New excess pressure $\Delta P_{new} \approx$ **15.87 Pa** (lower than before, because larger bubbles have lower Laplace pressure — a key driver of Ostwald ripening) **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative).** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]
**(a)** *Define *contact angle*. Derive Young's equation for the contact angle of a liquid drop resting on a flat solid surface. Discuss the conditions under which complete wetting occurs.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A capillary tube of radius 0.5 mm is dipped into water at 25 °C. The contact angle of water on glass is taken as 0°. Calculate the height of capillary rise. Given: surface tension of water at 25 °C = 72 mN/m, density of water = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Contact Angle and Young's Equation**

**Definition:** The *contact angle* $\theta$ is the angle formed at the three-phase contact line (solid–liquid–gas) between the **tangent to the liquid–gas interface** and the **solid surface**, measured through the liquid.

*[See the derivation in Section 3.3 above. The full Young's equation is:]*

$$
\gamma_{SG} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta
$$

**Condition for Complete Wetting:**

Complete wetting means $\theta = 0°$, i.e., the liquid spreads as a film. This requires:

$$
\cos\theta = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \gamma_{SG} \;\ge\; \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}
$$

Equivalently, the **spreading coefficient** $S_{par} = \gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LG} \ge 0$.

In other words, the solid–gas interfacial tension must be at least as large as the sum of the solid–liquid and liquid–gas tensions. Examples: water on perfectly clean glass, hexane on clean water. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Capillary Rise Numerical**

**Given:**
- Capillary radius, $r = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Contact angle, $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$
- $\gamma = 72 \text{ mN/m} = 72 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Jurin's Law:**

$$
h \;=\; \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}
$$

**Substituting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \dfrac{2 \times (72 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})} \\[4pt]
h &= \dfrac{0.144}{4.905} \\[4pt]
\boxed{h \;\approx\; 0.02936 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 2.94 \text{ cm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The water rises to a height of approximately **2.94 cm** inside the capillary tube. **[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for soap bubbles:** A soap bubble has *two* interfaces, so the Laplace pressure is $4\gamma/r$, not $2\gamma/r$. Many students write $2\gamma/r$ for both drops and bubbles — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 2. **Mixing up $\gamma_{SG}$ and $\gamma_{LG}$ in Young's equation:** Always remember the *liquid* is the phase that has the contact angle measured *through* it. The tangent is taken from the liquid–gas side.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Surface tension is often given in **mN/m**, which must be converted to **N/m** before substituting into the Pa formula. The height $h$ in Jurin's law comes out in **metres** if all other units are SI.
> 4. **Neglecting contact angle in capillary rise:** For water-glass, $\theta = 0°$ is the *default*, but for mercury-glass, $\theta = 140°$ and the liquid will *depress* (negative rise). Always check the sign of $\cos\theta$.
> 5. **Stokes' law is for suspensions, not colloids:** Writing Stokes' settling velocity for a colloid is incorrect — colloids do not settle (Brownian motion dominates).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 (Basic Concepts of Colloids and Interfaces):**

- [ ] **Definition of colloid:** heterogeneous system, dispersed phase (1–1000 nm) + dispersion medium.
- [ ] **Colloidal size range:** 1 nm to 1000 nm.
- [ ] **Lyophilic vs Lyophobic:** solvent-loving (reversible, stable) vs solvent-hating (irreversible, kinetically protected).
- [ ] **Multimolecular / Macromolecular / Associated (micellar) colloids** — three structural sub-classes.
- [ ] **Tyndall effect, Brownian motion, low osmotic pressure** are the three fingerprints of a colloid.
- [ ] **Stokes' law does NOT apply to colloids** (no settling).
- [ ] **Surface tension** is excess free energy per unit area; units are N/m or J/m².
- [ ] **Interfacial tension** is the same idea but between two immiscible condensed phases.
- [ ] **Laplace pressure** for a sphere: $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$ (drop), $4\gamma/r$ (bubble).
- [ ] **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$.
- [ ] **Complete wetting condition:** $\gamma_{SG} \ge \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}$, i.e., $S_{par} \ge 0$.
- [ ] **Jurin's law:** $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta / (\rho g r)$.
- [ ] **Work of adhesion (Dupré):** $W_{adh} = \gamma_{SG} + \gamma_{LG} - \gamma_{SL}$.
- [ ] **Work of cohesion:** $W_{coh} = 2\gamma_{LG}$ (single liquid).
- [ ] **Standard $\gamma_{water}$ at 25 °C** ≈ 72 mN/m — memorize.
- [ ] **Standard $\gamma_{mercury}$ at 25 °C** ≈ 485 mN/m.
- [ ] **Micelles form above CMC** (Critical Micelle Concentration).
- [ ] **Wetting regimes:** $\theta < 90°$ (wetting), $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting), $\theta = 180°$ (super-hydrophobic / Lotus effect).
- [ ] **Adsorption ≠ Absorption** — adsorption is a *surface* phenomenon; absorption is a *bulk* phenomenon.
- [ ] **Spontaneous mixing is governed by** $\Delta G_{mix} = \Delta H_{mix} - T \Delta S_{mix}$ (for solution thermodynamics beyond Module 1).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonics for KTU Viva:**
> - **"L-L-L-S-G"** for the five interfaces: **L**iquid-**L**iquid, **L**iquid-**S**olid, **L**iquid-**G**as, **S**olid-**S**olid, **G**as-**S**olid.
> - **"Tiny drops, BIG pressure"** — small $r$ gives large $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$.
> - **"TBC"** — the three colloid fingerprints: **T**yndall, **B**rownian, **C**oagulation-tendency (with low osmotic pressure).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Interfacial Phenomena
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples of Interfacial Phenomena

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Interfacial Phenomena?

**Interfacial phenomena** are the physical and chemical behaviors that arise at the boundary (interface) between two immiscible phases — such as liquid–vapor, liquid–liquid, liquid–solid, or solid–gas. These phenomena are governed by the imbalance of intermolecular forces (cohesive forces within a phase vs. adhesive forces across the boundary), which produces a net excess **surface free energy** quantified as the **surface tension** $\gamma$ (units: **N·m⁻¹** or **J·m⁻²**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> Interfacial phenomena refer to all observable effects — mechanical, thermodynamic, transport, and optical — that originate specifically at the boundary region between two bulk phases, where the local molecular environment differs sharply from either bulk phase. They are central to colloid science because colloids are, by definition, **dispersion systems stabilized by interfacial forces**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretched Rubber Sheet" Model

Imagine a soap film stretched across a wire frame as a thin rubber sheet under tension. The rubber sheet constantly tries to shrink to the smallest possible area — this *pulling force per unit length* is the macroscopic manifestation of **surface tension**. Similarly, molecules at any interface experience a net inward pull because their neighbors on the bulk side outnumber their neighbors on the other side.

- **Surface molecules** → feel imbalanced forces → try to minimize their exposure → minimize interfacial area
- **Bulk molecules** → experience balanced forces from all sides → energetically content

This microscopic picture is what gives rise to spherical droplets (minimum area for a given volume), capillary rise, and the entire family of interfacial effects.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Units | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|----------------|------------------|
| Surface tension | $\gamma$ | N·m⁻¹ or J·m⁻² | Excess free energy per unit area of interface |
| Interfacial tension | $\gamma_{ij}$ | N·m⁻¹ | Tension at the boundary between phases $i$ and $j$ |
| Contact angle | $\theta$ | degrees (°) | Wetting angle of a liquid droplet on a solid |
| Capillary rise | $h$ | m | Height to which a liquid climbs in a narrow tube |
| Surface excess | $\Gamma$ | mol·m⁻² | Amount of solute adsorbed per unit interfacial area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The term **surface tension** is reserved for the liquid–vapor (or liquid–air) interface, while **interfacial tension** is the more general term used for any pair of immiscible phases. Numerically, surface tension values are always higher than interfacial tension against another condensed phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classical Examples of Interfacial Phenomena

The following are the canonical examples — each is high-yield for KTU exams because they directly map to Module 1 learning outcomes.

### A. Capillarity (Capillary Rise / Depression)

When a narrow capillary tube is dipped into a liquid, the liquid either rises (e.g., water in glass) or falls (e.g., mercury in glass) depending on the balance of adhesive vs. cohesive forces.

**Governing Equation (Jurin's Law):**

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

where $\rho$ is liquid density, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, $r$ is capillary radius, and $\theta$ is the contact angle.

**Why it happens:**
- For water–glass: $\theta < 90°$ → $\cos\theta > 0$ → liquid **rises** (adhesion wins)
- For mercury–glass: $\theta > 90°$ → $\cos\theta < 0$ → liquid **falls** (cohesion wins)

### B. Wetting and Spreading

A drop placed on a solid surface adopts a characteristic **contact angle** $\theta$ given by **Young's Equation**:

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$$

- $\gamma_{sv}$ = solid–vapor interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid–liquid interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{lv}$ = liquid–vapor surface tension

**Why it matters:** Spreading coefficient $S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv})$. If $S > 0$, complete wetting occurs (liquid spreads as a film); if $S < 0$, a finite contact angle forms (partial wetting).

### C. Surfactants and Micelle Formation

**Surfactants** (surface-active agents) are amphiphilic molecules with a hydrophobic tail and hydrophilic head. Above the **critical micelle concentration (CMC)**, they self-assemble into **micelles**, drastically lowering $\gamma_{lv}$.

### D. Emulsions and Foams (Colloidal Dispersions)

- **Emulsions**: Liquid–liquid dispersions (oil-in-water O/W, water-in-oil W/O). Stabilized by emulsifiers. Examples: milk, mayonnaise, cosmetic creams.
- **Foams**: Gas-in-liquid dispersions stabilized by surfactants. Examples: soap lather, firefighting foam, beer head.

### E. Adsorption

The accumulation of solute molecules at an interface, quantified by the **Gibbs adsorption isotherm**:

$$\Gamma = -\frac{1}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{d \ln C}$$

### F. Nucleation and Condensation

The formation of a new phase (e.g., a raindrop from vapor) requires overcoming an energy barrier $\Delta G^*$ proportional to $\gamma^3$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU End Semester Examination for Module 1.**

| # | Equation | Name | Typical Application |
|---|----------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | $h = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise) | Capillarity in tubes, soils, porous media |
| 2 | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$ | Young's Equation | Contact angle measurement |
| 3 | $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | Young–Dupré (Work of Adhesion) | Adhesive strength, wetting |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{R}$ (spherical) | Young–Laplace Equation | Droplet/bubble pressure |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{R_1} + \dfrac{2 \gamma}{R_2}$ (general) | Young–Laplace (Two Curvatures) | Non-spherical interfaces |
| 6 | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{1}{RT} \dfrac{d\gamma}{d \ln C}$ | Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm | Surfactant adsorption |
| 7 | $\Delta G^* = \dfrac{16 \pi \gamma^3 v^2}{3 (kT \ln S)^2}$ | Classical Nucleation Barrier | Cloud/droplet formation |
| 8 | $\Pi = \dfrac{RT}{A}$ (ideal 2D gas) | Surface Pressure | Langmuir monolayer studies |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Interfacial phenomena underpin:
- **Petroleum engineering** — enhanced oil recovery, demulsification of crude
- **Pharmaceuticals** — drug delivery vesicles (liposomes), emulsion formulation
- **Coatings & paints** — uniform film formation requires controlled $\gamma_{sl}$
- **Food technology** — texture and stability of foams (ice cream), emulsions (mayonnaise)
- **Microelectronics** — wetting of photoresist on silicon wafers
- **Biomedical devices** — design of biocompatible implants, contact lens wetting

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise) — Exhaustive Steps

**Physical setup:** A capillary tube of internal radius $r$ is dipped into a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\gamma_{lv}$. The liquid climbs to a height $h$ above the bulk flat surface. The contact angle at the meniscus is $\theta$.

### Step 1 — Force Balance on the Liquid Column

Three vertical forces act on the raised liquid column:

1. **Upward force from surface tension:** Acts along the circular contact line of circumference $2\pi r$. The vertical component is:
$$F_{up} = \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta \cdot (2\pi r)$$

2. **Downward gravitational force:** Acts on the liquid column of volume $V = \pi r^2 h$:
$$F_{down} = \rho g V = \rho g (\pi r^2 h)$$

3. **Atmospheric pressure:** Acts equally on both the bulk liquid surface and the meniscus — cancels out (we ignore it in the differential form).

### Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium, the net vertical force is zero:

$$\gamma_{lv} \cos\theta \cdot (2\pi r) = \rho g (\pi r^2 h)$$

### Step 3 — Algebraic Simplification

Divide both sides by $\pi r$:

$$2 \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta = \rho g r h$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Capillary Rise $h$

$$\boxed{h = \frac{2 \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta}{\rho g r}}$$

This is **Jurin's Law**. Note the inverse proportionality to $r$: thinner capillaries produce taller rises (this is why blotting paper, fine fabrics, and tree xylem work).

### Step 5 — Verification by Dimensional Analysis

$$[h] = \frac{[\text{N·m}^{-1}] \cdot [\text{dimensionless}]}{[\text{kg·m}^{-3}] \cdot [\text{m·s}^{-2}] \cdot [\text{m}]} = \frac{\text{kg·s}^{-2}}{\text{kg·m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-2}} = \text{m} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Young–Laplace Equation (Mechanical Equilibrium Across a Curved Interface)

**Setup:** A spherical gas bubble of radius $R$ inside a liquid. The pressure inside the bubble ($P_{in}$) must exceed the pressure outside ($P_{out}$) to balance the surface tension pulling the interface inward.

### Step 1 — Imagine Blowing the Bubble Up by $dR$

If the bubble radius increases by $dR$, the surface area changes from $A = 4\pi R^2$ to $A + dA = 4\pi (R+dR)^2$.

$$dA = 4\pi [(R+dR)^2 - R^2] = 4\pi (2R\,dR + dR^2) \approx 8\pi R\,dR$$

(neglecting $dR^2$ as second-order infinitesimal)

### Step 2 — Work Done Against Surface Tension

Surface free energy increases by:

$$dW_\gamma = \gamma\, dA = 8\pi \gamma R\,dR$$

### Step 3 — Work Done by Pressure Difference

The volume changes by $dV = 4\pi R^2 dR$. The pressure difference $\Delta P = P_{in} - P_{out}$ does work:

$$dW_P = \Delta P \cdot dV = \Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 dR$$

### Step 4 — Energy Conservation

Equate the two work expressions (reversible expansion):

$$\Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 dR = 8\pi \gamma R\,dR$$

Divide both sides by $4\pi R\,dR$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}}$$

### Step 5 — Generalized Form for Two Principal Radii of Curvature

For a non-spherical interface with principal radii $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \gamma\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)}$$

This is the **Young–Laplace equation** in its general form. It explains why small bubbles have higher internal pressure than large ones — and why smaller bubbles tend to disappear at the expense of larger ones (Ostwald ripening).

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Equation to Use?

| Physical Situation | Key Parameter | Governing Equation | Critical Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid rises in a narrow tube | Rise height $h$ | Jurin's Law | $\cos\theta$ sign determines rise vs. fall |
| Drop on flat surface | Contact angle $\theta$ | Young's Equation | Balance of three interfacial tensions |
| Pressure inside a bubble/drop | $\Delta P$ across interface | Young–Laplace | Smaller $R$ → larger $\Delta P$ |
| Surfactant at interface | Surface excess $\Gamma$ | Gibbs Adsorption | Negative slope of $\gamma$ vs. $\ln C$ |
| New phase nucleating | Energy barrier $\Delta G^*$ | Classical Nucleation Theory | $\gamma^3$ dependence — explains supersaturation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Interfacial Phenomena

The following block diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy of interfacial phenomena covered in Module 1, showing how surface tension acts as the unifying thermodynamic driver.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Interfacial Phenomena"]:::root --> B["Static Effects"]
    A --> C["Dynamic Effects"]
    A --> D["Colloidal Systems"]

    B --> B1["Capillarity - Jurin Law"]
    B --> B2["Wetting - Young Equation"]
    B --> B3["Young-Laplace Pressure"]
    B --> B4["Adsorption - Gibbs Isotherm"]

    C --> C1["Spreading Dynamics"]
    C --> C2["Marangoni Effect"]
    C --> C3["Coalescence of Drops"]

    D --> D1["Emulsions - O/W and W/O"]
    D --> D2["Foams - Gas in Liquid"]
    D --> D3["Aerosols - Solid or Liquid in Gas"]
    D --> D4["Sols - Solid in Liquid"]

    B1 --> E["Common Driver: Surface Tension gamma"]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    classDef root fill:#FFE082,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hidden stroke:transparent,fill:transparent
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Capillary Rise Mechanism

For situations where a literal cross-sectional diagram is preferred over a physical sketch, the following block diagram describes the energy and force flow during capillary rise:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Bulk["Bulk Liquid Reservoir"]
        B0["Flat liquid surface at z = 0"]
    end

    subgraph Cap["Inside Capillary Tube radius r"]
        M["Concave meniscus forms contact angle theta"]
        H["Liquid rises to height h above reservoir"]
    end

    subgraph Forces["Vertical Force Balance"]
        F1["Upward: gamma times cos theta times 2 pi r"]
        F2["Downward: rho g pi r squared h"]
        Eq["Equilibrium yields h = 2 gamma cos theta over rho g r"]
    end

    Bulk --> M
    M --> H
    F1 --> Eq
    F2 --> Eq

    classDef sub fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    class Bulk sub
    class Cap sub
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Young's Equation at a Three-Phase Contact Line

A block representation of the three interfacial tensions acting at the contact line where solid, liquid, and vapor phases meet:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Solid["Solid Substrate - bottom"]
        S["Solid phase"]
    end

    subgraph Liquid["Liquid Droplet"]
        L["Liquid phase"]
    end

    subgraph Vapor["Surrounding Vapor"]
        V["Vapor phase"]
    end

    Point["Triple Contact Point"] --> S
    Point --> L
    Point --> V

    GSV["gamma_sv pulls outward along solid surface"]:::force --> Point
    GSL["gamma_sl pulls inward along solid surface"]:::force --> Point
    GLV["gamma_lv pulls tangent to liquid surface"]:::force --> Point

    Theta["Contact angle theta measured inside liquid"]:::angle --> Point

    classDef force fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef angle fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define surface tension. Mention its SI unit and explain the phenomenon of capillary rise with one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Surface tension is defined as the force per unit length acting perpendicular to any line drawn on the liquid surface, tending to pull the surface inward and minimize its area. Mathematically, $\gamma = F/L$. It is also the excess free energy per unit area of the surface, with SI units of **N·m⁻¹** or equivalently **J·m⁻²**.

**Capillary rise example:** When a clean glass capillary tube is dipped in water, the water rises inside the tube to a height $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta / (\rho g r)$. This happens because adhesive forces between water and glass exceed cohesive forces in water ($\theta < 90°$). A practical example is the rise of sap in plant xylem vessels, which transport water from roots to leaves against gravity. **(3 marks — 1 mark each for definition, formula, and example)**

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between emulsions and foams with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Emulsion | Foam |
|---|---|---|
| Type of dispersion | Liquid in liquid (or vice versa) | Gas in liquid (or solid) |
| Continuous phase | O/W: water; W/O: oil | Liquid (usually aqueous) |
| Stabilizer | Emulsifier (surfactant) | Surfactant + thickener |
| Examples | Milk (O/W), butter (W/O) | Soap lather, shaving cream, beer head |

The key distinction is that emulsions have **two condensed (liquid) phases**, while foams have a **gaseous dispersed phase**. Both are stabilized by surfactants that lower interfacial tension and form a rigid film preventing coalescence. **(3 marks — 1.5 marks each for definition + example + differentiation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO alternatives (A or B). You answer either one. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Derive Jurin's Law for capillary rise. A glass capillary tube of radius 0.5 mm is dipped in water at 25 °C. Given $\gamma_{lv} = 0.072$ N·m⁻¹, $\theta = 0°$, $\rho = 1000$ kg·m⁻³, $g = 9.81$ m·s⁻², calculate the height of water rise.**

**Solution:**

The derivation follows Section 3.1 above. Starting from force balance:

$$2 \pi r \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta = \pi r^2 \rho g h$$

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

Substituting values: $\cos 0° = 1$, $r = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m:

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.144}{4.905}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 0.0294 \text{ m} = 2.94 \text{ cm}}$$

**(7 marks — 4 for derivation, 2 for substitution, 1 for final answer with units)**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Explain Young's equation for contact angle. Discuss the three cases of wetting based on the value of $\theta$.**

**Solution:**

At the three-phase contact line, three interfacial tensions act: $\gamma_{sv}$ (solid–vapor), $\gamma_{sl}$ (solid–liquid), and $\gamma_{lv}$ (liquid–vapor). Mechanical equilibrium along the solid surface gives:

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$$

**Three cases:**

1. **Complete wetting, $\theta = 0°$:** $\cos\theta = 1$, so $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}$. The liquid spreads as a thin film. Example: clean water on perfectly clean glass.
2. **Partial wetting, $0° < \theta < 90°$:** The drop is "flat-ish." Adhesion exceeds cohesion. Example: water on a slightly oily surface.
3. **Non-wetting, $\theta > 90°$:** $\cos\theta < 0$. Cohesion dominates; the drop is nearly spherical. Example: water on a waxed (paraffin) surface, or mercury on glass ($\theta \approx 140°$).

**Engineering relevance:** Wetting determines whether paints adhere, whether waterproofing works, and how lubricants spread in bearings. **(7 marks — 3 for equation, 3 for three cases, 1 for engineering relevance)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**State and derive the Young–Laplace equation for the pressure difference across a curved liquid interface.**

**Solution:**

Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.2 above. The final result is:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R} \quad \text{(sphere)}$$

$$\Delta P = \gamma\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right) \quad \text{(general)}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The pressure is always higher on the **concave side** (inside a drop or bubble) than on the convex side (outside). For a soap bubble with two interfaces (inner and outer surfaces), the result becomes $\Delta P = 4\gamma/R$.

**Numerical check:** For a water droplet of radius $R = 1$ mm with $\gamma = 0.072$ N·m⁻¹:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 0.072}{10^{-3}} = 144 \text{ Pa}$$

This is small compared to atmospheric pressure ($\approx 101325$ Pa), but for fog droplets of $R \sim 10 \mu$m, $\Delta P$ becomes 1440 Pa — significant. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Define surfactants. Explain micelle formation and the significance of CMC.**

**Solution:**

**Surfactants** are surface-active agents — amphiphilic molecules possessing both a hydrophilic (polar) head and a hydrophobic (nonpolar) tail. Common examples include sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, anionic), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, cationic), and Triton X-100 (nonionic).

**Mechanism of action:** Surfactants preferentially migrate to interfaces (e.g., air–water) with the hydrophobic tail pointing outward and hydrophilic head in water. This disrupts the strong hydrogen-bonding network of water at the surface, **reducing $\gamma_{lv}$**.

**Micelle formation:** When the bulk concentration of surfactant reaches a critical value — the **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)** — the molecules spontaneously self-assemble into aggregates called **micelles**. In aqueous solution, micelles typically have tails inward (forming a nonpolar core) and heads outward (toward water).

**Significance of CMC:**
- Below CMC: surfactant exists as monomers; surface tension drops sharply with concentration.
- Above CMC: surface tension plateaus; additional surfactant forms micelles.
- CMC marks the onset of solubilization — hydrophobic molecules (oils, greases) can dissolve inside micelle cores, enabling **detergency**.
- Typical CMC values: SDS in water ≈ 8 mM; CTAB ≈ 1 mM.

**Engineering applications:** Detergents, emulsifiers in food/pharma, solubilizing agents in drug delivery, flotation in mineral processing. **(7 marks — 2 for definition, 3 for micelle formation, 2 for CMC significance)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in Jurin's Law:** Always write $\gamma$ in N·m⁻¹, $r$ in meters, $\rho$ in kg·m⁻³. Mixing mm with kg·m⁻³ without conversion loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Sign error in Young's equation:** The correct form is $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$. Writing it as $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ is wrong by 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing surface tension with surface energy:** They have the same numerical value but different units (N·m⁻¹ vs. J·m⁻²). Examiners want both mentioned.
> 4. **Incomplete derivation of Young–Laplace:** Students often skip the energy-equality step. Always write: $dW_\gamma = dW_P$, expand, then simplify.
> 5. **Wrong example for emulsions:** Mayonnaise is an O/W emulsion; butter is a W/O emulsion. Getting these reversed costs a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Surface tension** $\gamma$ is the master variable — it appears in every governing equation of interfacial science.
- **Jurin's Law**: $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta/(\rho g r)$ — capillary rise depends on tube radius, contact angle, liquid density.
- **Young's Equation**: $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ — three-phase equilibrium at a contact line.
- **Young–Laplace**: $\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$ for a sphere — pressure difference scales inversely with radius.
- **Gibbs Isotherm**: $\Gamma = -\frac{1}{RT}\frac{d\gamma}{d\ln C}$ — links surface excess to the slope of surface tension vs. log concentration.
- **Surfactants** lower $\gamma_{lv}$; above CMC they form micelles and enable solubilization/detergency.
- **Three wetting regimes**: $\theta = 0°$ (complete), $0° < \theta < 90°$ (partial wetting), $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting).
- **Colloidal systems** (emulsions, foams, aerosols, sols) are stabilized by interfacial films formed by surfactants.
- **Capillary action** in plants, oil recovery, and chromatography are direct engineering consequences.
- **For KTU numericals**: always carry units through and present final answer in SI with correct significant figures.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Solid in Colloid and Interfacial Science

In the context of **Colloid and Interfacial Science**, the term "Solid" primarily encompasses two interconnected concepts:
1. **Solid sols** — a class of colloidal systems where a solid is dispersed in a continuous liquid medium.
2. **Solid surfaces and solid–liquid interfaces** — the boundary regions where solid phases interact with adjacent fluid phases, governing phenomena such as wetting, adhesion, and capillary action.

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Solid Sol (Colloidal Definition):** A *solid sol* is a heterogeneous colloidal system in which a finely divided **solid** is dispersed in a **liquid** (or sometimes a gas) continuous phase, with particle sizes typically in the range of **1 nm to 1000 nm**. The dispersed solid particles are not dissolved but remain suspended due to Brownian motion, and they do not settle out under gravity for extended periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid–Liquid Interface (KTU Syllabus Highlight):** A solid–liquid interface is the two-dimensional boundary region of molecular dimensions (typically 0.5 nm to 5 nm thick) that separates a solid phase from an adjacent liquid phase. At this interface, the imbalance of intermolecular forces creates a region of excess free energy called the **interfacial free energy (γ_SL)**, measured in **J/m²** or equivalently **N/m**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — "Dust Floating in a Sunbeam":**
Imagine a dark room with a single shaft of sunlight streaming through a window. You can see tiny dust particles suspended and dancing in the beam. These dust particles (the **solid** phase) are floating in air (the **continuous medium**) without falling. They are small enough that the random collisions of air molecules (Brownian motion) keep them aloft. This is essentially what happens in a solid sol — except the dust is in a liquid, and the particles are even smaller, allowing the liquid molecules to keep them suspended indefinitely.

**For Solid–Liquid Interfaces — "Water on a Waxed Car":**
When you pour water on a freshly waxed car body, the water beads up into near-spherical droplets. This is because the solid (wax) surface "repels" the liquid (water) at the molecular level. The contact angle formed is large (>90°), indicating poor wetting. Conversely, on a clean glass surface, water spreads out into a thin film because of strong solid–liquid interactions. This everyday phenomenon is governed entirely by the physics of the solid–liquid interface.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solid particle size (colloidal range) | $d$ | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$ | m (1 nm – 1000 nm) |
| Surface tension of water at 25°C | $\gamma_{LV}$ | **0.07198** | N/m |
| Surface energy of clean glass | $\gamma_{SV}$ | **~0.300 – 0.500** | J/m² |
| Interfacial tension (water–oil) | $\gamma_{OW}$ | **~0.050** | N/m |
| Contact angle of water on clean glass | $\theta$ | **~0°** (complete wetting) | degrees |
| Contact angle of water on PTFE (Teflon) | $\theta$ | **~108° – 115°** | degrees |
| Thickness of interfacial region | $\delta$ | **0.5 – 5** | nm |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Solid–liquid interfacial phenomena are the foundation of many engineering applications, including **detergency**, **oil recovery**, **adhesion technology**, **coatings**, **paints**, **pharmaceutical formulations**, and **biomedical implants**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Solid-Containing Colloidal Systems

Solid-containing systems in colloid science are classified based on the **physical state of the dispersed phase** and the **continuous medium**:

| System Type | Dispersed Phase | Continuous Medium | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sol** | Solid | Liquid | Gold sol, Sulphur sol, Arsenious sulphide sol |
| **Aerosol** | Solid | Gas | Smoke, dust in air |
| **Gel** | Solid (network) | Liquid | Silica gel, gelatin |
| **Solid sol** | Solid | Solid | Ruby glass (gold dispersed in glass) |
| **Suspension** | Solid (>1 µm) | Liquid | Mud, paint, milk of magnesia |

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction:** A true **sol** has particle sizes in the colloidal range (**1–1000 nm**), whereas a **suspension** has particles larger than 1000 nm that settle under gravity relatively quickly.

---

## 2.2 Properties of Solid Sols

### (a) Optical Properties — Tyndall Effect
When a beam of light passes through a solid sol, the colloidal particles scatter the light, making the path of the beam visible. This is the **Tyndall effect**, which is a hallmark of all colloidal systems. The intensity of scattered light is given by the **Rayleigh scattering equation**:

$$I = I_0 \cdot \frac{8\pi^4 N \alpha^2}{\lambda^4 R^2} (1 + \cos^2\theta)$$

where:
- $I$ = intensity of scattered light
- $I_0$ = intensity of incident light
- $N$ = number of scattering particles per unit volume
- $\alpha$ = polarizability of the particle
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident light
- $R$ = distance from the particle to the observer
- $\theta$ = angle of observation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The $\lambda^{-4}$ dependence means **shorter wavelengths (blue light) are scattered far more strongly** than longer wavelengths (red light) — this is why the sky is blue and sunsets are red.

### (b) Kinetic Properties — Brownian Motion
The **Brownian motion** of colloidal particles was quantitatively described by **Albert Einstein** in 1905. The mean square displacement in time $t$ is:

$$\overline{x^2} = \frac{2 k_B T \, t}{\xi}$$

where $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant (**$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**), $T$ is absolute temperature, and $\xi$ is the friction coefficient. For a spherical particle of radius $r$ in a medium of viscosity $\eta$:

$$\xi = 6\pi \eta r$$

Combining gives the **Einstein–Smoluchowski equation**:

$$\overline{x^2} = \frac{k_B T \, t}{3\pi \eta r}$$

### (c) Electrical Properties — Zeta Potential
Solid particles in liquid media acquire a surface charge through:
1. **Ionization** of surface groups (e.g., –COOH → –COO⁻ + H⁺)
2. **Ion adsorption** from solution
3. **Lattice defects** and isomorphous substitution

This creates an **electrical double layer (EDL)** consisting of a **Stern layer** (fixed) and a **diffuse Gouy–Chapman layer** (mobile). The potential at the **shear plane** (the boundary between the moving fluid and the fluid attached to the particle) is the **zeta potential (ζ)**, typically measured in **mV**.

> [!NOTE]
> A solid sol is **stable** when $|\zeta| > 30$ mV. When $|\zeta| < 5$ mV, coagulation occurs rapidly.

### (d) Sedimentation and Stokes' Law
For a particle settling under gravity, the terminal velocity is given by **Stokes' law**:

$$v = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_s - \rho_l) g}{9 \eta}$$

where $\rho_s$ and $\rho_l$ are the densities of the solid and liquid, respectively. For colloidal particles ($r \sim 10$ nm), $v$ is so small that **thermal (Brownian) motion** dominates and prevents sedimentation.

---

## 2.3 Solid–Liquid Interface — Young's Equation

The most fundamental equation governing the solid–liquid interface is **Young's equation (1805)**, which relates the three interfacial tensions acting on a liquid droplet resting on a solid surface:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

where:
- $\gamma_{SV}$ = solid–vapor interfacial tension (surface energy of solid)
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid–liquid interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid–vapor interfacial tension (surface tension of liquid)
- $\theta$ = **contact angle** (measured through the liquid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** Young's equation is a **force balance at the three-phase contact line** where solid, liquid, and vapor meet. The vector sum of the three interfacial tensions must be zero for mechanical equilibrium.

---

## 2.4 Work of Adhesion and Work of Cohesion

### Young–Dupré Equation
The **work of adhesion** ($W_a$) between a solid and a liquid is the reversible work required to separate a unit area of the solid–liquid interface into separate solid–vapor and liquid–vapor interfaces:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

Combining with Young's equation gives the **Young–Dupré equation**:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

### Work of Cohesion
The **work of cohesion** ($W_c$) of a liquid is the reversible work required to separate a unit area of liquid into two liquid–vapor interfaces:

$$W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$$

### Spreading Coefficient
The **spreading coefficient** ($S$) determines whether one liquid will spread on another (or on a solid):

$$S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$$

- If $S > 0$: **complete spreading** occurs.
- If $S < 0$: **partial wetting** with a finite contact angle.
- If $S = 0$: **critical wetting condition**.

For spreading on a solid:

$$S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}) = W_a - W_c$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | **Young's equation** — contact angle equilibrium | N/m |
| 2 | $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | **Work of adhesion** (solid–liquid) | J/m² |
| 3 | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | **Young–Dupré equation** | J/m² |
| 4 | $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ | **Work of cohesion** of a liquid | J/m² |
| 5 | $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ | **Spreading coefficient** | J/m² |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{2r^2(\rho_s - \rho_l)g}{9\eta}$ | **Stokes' settling velocity** | m/s |
| 7 | $\overline{x^2} = \dfrac{k_B T\,t}{3\pi \eta r}$ | **Einstein–Smoluchowski equation** (Brownian motion) | m² |
| 8 | $I \propto \dfrac{N\alpha^2}{\lambda^4}$ | **Rayleigh scattering** (Tyndall effect) | — |
| 9 | $\xi = 6\pi \eta r$ | **Friction coefficient** (Stokes drag) | N·s/m |
| 10 | $h = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma_{LV} r \sin\theta}{2 \rho_l g}}$ | **Capillary rise** in a tube of radius $r$ (with $\theta$) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always specify whether a contact angle is **measured through the liquid** (this is the universal convention) and always include correct units. The symbol $\theta$ is dimensionless (radians or degrees) but interfacial tensions carry units of N/m or J/m².

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Solid/Solid–Liquid Interface Science |
|---|---|
| **Detergency** | Wetting of fabrics; lowering $\gamma_{SL}$ via surfactants |
| **Enhanced Oil Recovery** | Altering wettability of reservoir rocks |
| **Paints & Coatings** | Adhesion of pigments to substrates; contact angle control |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Tablet dissolution; drug particle wetting |
| **Biomedical Implants** | Surface energy control for cell adhesion |
| **Microfluidics** | Capillary-driven flow in lab-on-chip devices |
| **Electronics** | Soldering, wafer cleaning, thin-film deposition |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation from First Principles

### Step 1: Set Up the Problem
Consider a small liquid droplet resting on a flat, smooth, homogeneous solid surface in a vapor environment. At the **three-phase contact line** (where solid, liquid, and vapor meet), three interfaces meet:
- Solid–Vapor (surface area $A_{SV}$)
- Solid–Liquid (surface area $A_{SL}$)
- Liquid–Vapor (surface area $A_{LV}$)

### Step 2: Write the Total Free Energy
The total Helmholtz free energy of the system (assuming constant temperature and volume) is:

$$G_{total} = \gamma_{SV} A_{SV} + \gamma_{SL} A_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} A_{LV}$$

### Step 3: Apply a Virtual Displacement
Move the contact line by a small amount $dx$ in the direction that **increases** $A_{SL}$ by $dA_{SL}$. From geometric considerations of a spherical-cap droplet on a flat surface:

$$dA_{SV} = -dx \cdot L$$
$$dA_{SL} = +dx \cdot L$$
$$dA_{LV} = +dx \cdot L \cdot \cos\theta$$

where $L$ is the length of the contact line (per unit length, $L=1$).

### Step 4: Impose the Equilibrium Condition
At equilibrium, the total free energy is minimized, i.e., $dG_{total} = 0$:

$$dG_{total} = \gamma_{SV} \, dA_{SV} + \gamma_{SL} \, dA_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \, dA_{LV} = 0$$

Substituting:

$$\gamma_{SV}(-dx) + \gamma_{SL}(+dx) + \gamma_{LV}(+dx \cos\theta) = 0$$

### Step 5: Simplify
Dividing through by $dx$ (which is non-zero):

$$-\gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}$$

This is **Young's equation**, the cornerstone of wetting science. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation

**Starting point** — Young's equation:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange to isolate $\gamma_{SL}$:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

**Step 2:** The work of adhesion is defined as:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $\gamma_{SL}$:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$

This is the **Young–Dupré equation**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A water droplet placed on a clean glass slide forms a contact angle of $\theta = 15°$. The surface tension of water is $\gamma_{LV} = 0.072$ N/m. The solid–vapor surface energy is $\gamma_{SV} = 0.310$ J/m². Calculate: (a) the solid–liquid interfacial tension, (b) the work of adhesion, and (c) the work of cohesion. Comment on the wetting behavior.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $\theta = 15° = 15 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.2618$ rad
- $\gamma_{LV} = 0.072$ N/m
- $\gamma_{SV} = 0.310$ J/m²

**Part (a): Solid–Liquid Interfacial Tension**

Apply Young's equation:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

$$\gamma_{SL} = 0.310 - 0.072 \times \cos(15°)$$

$$\gamma_{SL} = 0.310 - 0.072 \times 0.9659$$

$$\gamma_{SL} = 0.310 - 0.06955$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{SL} = 0.2404 \text{ J/m}^2}$$

**Part (b): Work of Adhesion**

Apply the Young–Dupré equation:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

$$W_a = 0.072 \times (1 + 0.9659)$$

$$W_a = 0.072 \times 1.9659$$

$$\boxed{W_a = 0.1415 \text{ J/m}^2}$$

**Part (c): Work of Cohesion**

$$W_c = 2\gamma_{LV} = 2 \times 0.072$$

$$\boxed{W_c = 0.144 \text{ J/m}^2}$$

**Comment on Wetting:**

Since $W_a = 0.1415$ J/m² is very close to $W_c = 0.144$ J/m², the **adhesion between water and glass is nearly equal to the cohesion within water itself**. This is why water spreads readily on clean glass (small contact angle, $\theta = 15°$, indicating strong wetting).

The **spreading coefficient** is:

$$S = W_a - W_c = 0.1415 - 0.144 = -0.0025 \text{ J/m}^2$$

Since $S$ is **slightly negative**, the wetting is almost complete but not quite total spreading — consistent with the small but non-zero contact angle observed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Wetting Regimes and Contact Angle

| Wetting Regime | Contact Angle $\theta$ | $\cos\theta$ | $W_a$ vs $W_c$ | Spreading Coeff. $S$ | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Complete wetting** | $\theta = 0°$ | $+1$ | $W_a > W_c$ | $S > 0$ | Water on clean glass (ideal) |
| **Strong wetting** | $0° < \theta < 90°$ | $0 < \cos\theta < 1$ | $W_a$ approaching $W_c$ | $S$ slightly $< 0$ | Water on glass ($\theta \sim 15°$) |
| **Neutral wetting** | $\theta = 90°$ | $0$ | $W_a = \dfrac{W_c}{2}$ | $S = -W_c/2$ | Water on some polymer surfaces |
| **Weak wetting** | $90° < \theta < 180°$ | $-1 < \cos\theta < 0$ | $W_a < W_c$ | $S$ strongly $< 0$ | Water on paraffin ($\theta \sim 110°$) |
| **Complete non-wetting** | $\theta = 180°$ | $-1$ | $W_a = 0$ | $S = -2\gamma_{LV}$ | Theoretical only (Cassie–Baxter on superhydrophobic) |

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Capillary Rise in a Tube (with Contact Angle)

Consider a liquid of density $\rho_l$ rising in a capillary tube of inner radius $r$ with contact angle $\theta$ against the tube wall.

**Step 1:** The vertical component of surface tension force pulling the liquid up at the meniscus:

$$F_{up} = 2\pi r \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

**Step 2:** The gravitational force pulling the liquid column down (of height $h$):

$$F_{down} = \pi r^2 h \rho_l g$$

**Step 3:** At equilibrium, $F_{up} = F_{down}$:

$$2\pi r \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta = \pi r^2 h \rho_l g$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $h$:

$$h = \frac{2\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{\rho_l g r}$$

$$\boxed{h = \frac{2\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{\rho_l g r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $\theta = 0$ (complete wetting, e.g., water in clean glass), this reduces to the **classic Jurin's law**: $h = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}}{\rho_l g r}$. When $\theta > 90°$, $\cos\theta < 0$ and the liquid **depresses** (e.g., mercury in glass).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Three-Phase Contact Line and Young's Equation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Flat Solid Substrate] --> B[Three-Phase Contact Line]
    B --> C[Solid-Vapor Interface gamma SV]
    B --> D[Solid-Liquid Interface gamma SL]
    B --> E[Liquid-Vapor Interface gamma LV]
    C --> F[Force along solid surface away from droplet]
    D --> G[Force along solid surface toward droplet interior]
    E --> H[Tangential force at contact angle theta]
    F --> I[Mechanical Equilibrium at Contact Line]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Young's Equation: gamma SV = gamma SL + gamma LV cos theta]
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic: Classification of Solid-Containing Colloidal Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Colloidal Systems with Solid Phase] --> Sol[Sols: Solid in Liquid]
    Root --> Aer[Aerosols: Solid in Gas]
    Root --> Gel[Gels: Solid Network in Liquid]
    Root --> SSol[Solid Sols: Solid in Solid]
    Root --> Susp[Suspensions: Solid > 1 micrometer in Liquid]

    Sol --> S1[Gold Sol - Au nanoparticles in water]
    Sol --> S2[Sulphur Sol - S particles in water]
    Sol --> S3[Arsenious Sulphide Sol - As2S3 in water]

    Aer --> A1[Smoke - Carbon in air]
    Aer --> A2[Dust storms - Silicates in air]

    Gel --> G1[Silica gel - SiO2 network in water]
    Gel --> G2[Gelatin - protein network in water]

    SSol --> SS1[Ruby glass - Gold in glass matrix]
    SSol --> SS2[Colored gemstones - metal oxides in mineral]

    Susp --> Su1[Mud - clay in water]
    Susp --> Su2[Paint - pigment in oil/water]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Forces at the Three-Phase Contact Line

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Three-Phase Contact Zone
        direction LR
        S[Solid Surface] --- L[Liquid Droplet]
        S --- V[Ambient Vapor Phase]
        L --- V
    end

    S -->|gamma SV pulls outward| CL[Contact Line]
    L -->|gamma SL pulls inward| CL
    V -->|gamma LV at angle theta| CL

    CL --> Result[Net Force Must be Zero at Equilibrium]
    Result --> YE[Young's Equation Holds]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Solid-Liquid Interface Science

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Solid-Liquid Interface System] --> B[Surface Energy of Solid gamma SV]
    A --> C[Interfacial Tension gamma SL]
    A --> D[Liquid Surface Tension gamma LV]

    B --> E[Measured via Contact Angle Method]
    B --> F[Measured via Wilhelmy Plate Method]

    C --> G[Calculated from Young Equation]
    C --> H[Calculated from Young-Dupre Equation]

    D --> I[Measured via Capillary Rise]
    D --> J[Measured via Du Nouy Ring]

    E --> K[Engineering Application Output]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L[Detergency Design]
    K --> M[Coating Adhesion]
    K --> N[Oil Recovery]
    K --> O[Pharmaceutical Wetting]
```

---

## 4.5 Schematic: Wetting Regimes Based on Contact Angle

```mermaid
graph TD
    W[Contact Angle Theta at Solid-Liquid-Vapor Junction] --> W1[Theta equals 0 degrees]
    W --> W2[Theta between 0 and 90 degrees]
    W --> W3[Theta equals 90 degrees]
    W --> W4[Theta between 90 and 180 degrees]
    W --> W5[Theta approaches 180 degrees]

    W1 --> R1[Complete Wetting - Liquid Spreads Fully]
    W2 --> R2[Strong Wetting - Hydrophilic Behavior]
    W3 --> R3[Neutral Wetting - Boundary Case]
    W4 --> R4[Weak Wetting - Hydrophobic Behavior]
    W5 --> R5[Superhydrophobic - Cassie-Baxter State]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]**
**Define a solid sol. Give two examples and state two characteristic properties that distinguish it from a true solution.**

**Model Answer:**
A **solid sol** is a colloidal system in which a finely divided solid is dispersed in a liquid continuous phase, with particle sizes in the range of **1 nm to 1000 nm**. The particles do not dissolve but remain suspended due to Brownian motion.

*Examples:*
1. Gold sol (gold nanoparticles dispersed in water).
2. Arsenious sulphide sol (As₂S₃ particles in water).

*Distinguishing properties:*
1. **Tyndall effect** — scatters light (true solutions do not).
2. **Heterogeneous nature** — particles are visible under ultramicroscope (true solutions are homogeneous at the molecular level).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]**
**State Young's equation for a liquid drop on a solid surface. What is the physical significance of the contact angle?**

**Model Answer:**
**Young's equation** relates the three interfacial tensions acting at the three-phase contact line:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

where $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, and $\gamma_{LV}$ are the solid–vapor, solid–liquid, and liquid–vapor interfacial tensions, and $\theta$ is the contact angle measured through the liquid.

**Physical significance of contact angle:**
- $\theta < 90°$: liquid **wets** the solid (hydrophilic behavior).
- $\theta > 90°$: liquid **does not wet** the solid (hydrophobic behavior).
- $\theta = 0°$: **complete spreading**.
- It quantifies the balance between **adhesive forces** (solid–liquid) and **cohesive forces** (liquid–liquid).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **Young–Dupré equation** starting from Young's equation and the thermodynamic definition of work of adhesion. Discuss the physical significance of the work of adhesion and the work of cohesion in determining wetting behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sessile drop of formamide ($\gamma_{LV} = 0.058$ N/m) is placed on a solid surface, and the contact angle measured is $\theta = 50°$. Calculate:
  - (i) the work of adhesion,
  - (ii) the work of cohesion,
  - (iii) the spreading coefficient, and
  - (iv) comment on the wetting behavior. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Derivation:**

The **work of adhesion** ($W_a$) is defined as the reversible work required to separate a unit area of the solid–liquid interface into a solid–vapor interface and a liquid–vapor interface. From the Dupré definition:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

From Young's equation:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

Substituting into the Dupré expression:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta)$$

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$

This is the **Young–Dupré equation**.

**Physical significance:**

- The **work of adhesion** represents the strength of the **solid–liquid interaction** at the molecular level. A higher $W_a$ means the liquid "sticks" to the solid more strongly.
- The **work of cohesion** of a liquid is $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$, representing the strength of the **liquid–liquid interaction**.
- If $W_a > W_c$: the liquid prefers to be in contact with the solid, leading to **wetting** (small $\theta$).
- If $W_a < W_c$: the liquid prefers to stay in contact with itself, leading to **non-wetting** (large $\theta$).
- The **spreading coefficient** $S = W_a - W_c$ determines the wetting regime.

**(5 marks for derivation, 2 marks for discussion.)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $\gamma_{LV} = 0.058$ N/m, $\theta = 50°$

$\cos(50°) = 0.6428$

**(i) Work of adhesion:**

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) = 0.058 \times (1 + 0.6428)$$

$$W_a = 0.058 \times 1.6428 = 0.0953 \text{ J/m}^2$$

**(ii) Work of cohesion:**

$$W_c = 2\gamma_{LV} = 2 \times 0.058 = 0.116 \text{ J/m}^2$$

**(iii) Spreading coefficient:**
