It is given by: $$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{rRT}$$ **Physical significance of each term:** - $P$ — equilibrium vapor pressure over the curved surface (Pa) - $P_0$ — saturation vapor pressure over a flat surface at the same temperature (Pa) - $\gamma$ — liquid–vapor interfacial tension (N m⁻¹) - $V_m$ — molar volume of the liquid (m³ mol⁻¹) - $r$ — radius of curvature of the liquid surface (m) - $R$ — universal gas constant (**8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**) - $T$ — absolute temperature (K) **Significance:** It quantitatively predicts that small droplets evaporate more easily (since $P > P_0$ for $r > 0$) and capillary condensation occurs in narrow pores (since $P < P_0$ for concave menisci). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: How does the Kelvin Equation explain the phenomenon of capillary condensation? Why does water condense in narrow pores even when the relative humidity is below 100%?** **Model Answer:** Capillary condensation is the phenomenon where a condensable vapor liquefies inside a porous medium at pressures *below* the saturation pressure $P_0$ of the bulk liquid. The Kelvin Equation provides the thermodynamic explanation: For a concave meniscus inside a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$ with contact angle $\theta$, the equation takes the form: $$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{2\gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_p RT}$$ The negative sign arises because the meniscus is *concave* (effective radius is negative). This makes $\ln(P/P_0) < 0$, hence $P < P_0$. Physically, the molecules at a concave surface are more tightly bound (they have more cohesive neighbors) than at a flat surface, so they condense more readily. For complete wetting ($\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$), the equation simplifies to: $$P/P_0 = \exp\left(-\frac{2\gamma V_m}{r_p RT}\right)$$ Therefore, in **narrower pores** (smaller $r_p$), condensation occurs at progressively *lower* relative humidities. For example, in a 2 nm pore of silica at 298 K, water condenses at roughly 30–40% relative humidity — far below the 100% required for flat surfaces. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and follow an **internal choice pattern**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each part-question below is structured as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** to match actual ESE paper weightage. ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Derive the Kelvin Equation starting from the condition of thermodynamic equilibrium between a spherical liquid droplet and its vapor. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** At **300 K**, the saturation vapor pressure of water is **3535 Pa**. Calculate the equilibrium vapor pressure above a water droplet of radius **5 nm** and above a concave meniscus of radius **5 nm** inside a glass capillary (assume complete wetting, $\theta = 0$). Use $\gamma = 0.0718$ N m⁻¹ and $V_m = 18.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ mol⁻¹. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part A(a) **Step 1 — State equilibrium condition:** At equilibrium between the droplet (liquid) and vapor: $$\mu_l(T, P_l) = \mu_v(T, P)$$ **Step 2 — Ideal gas expression for vapor:** $$\mu_v(T, P) = \mu_v^0(T) + RT \ln(P/P_0)$$ **Step 3 — Liquid under elevated pressure (Young–Laplace):** The internal pressure in the droplet is: $$P_l = P + \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$ For an incompressible liquid: $$\mu_l(T, P_l) = \mu_l^*(T) + V_m (P_l - P_0) = \mu_l^*(T) + V_m \cdot \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$ **Step 4 — Flat surface reference:** At a flat interface, $\mu_l^*(T) = \mu_v^0(T)$. **Step 5 — Equate and simplify:** $$\mu_v^0(T) + RT \ln(P/P_0) = \mu_v^0(T) + \frac{2\gamma V_m}{r}$$ $$RT \ln(P/P_0) = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{r}$$ $$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{rRT}}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Vapor behaves as an ideal gas. 2. Liquid is incompressible (constant $V_m$). 3. Surface tension $\gamma$ is independent of curvature. 4. System is at uniform temperature $T$. 5. The droplet is spherical and the meniscus has uniform curvature. --- #### Model Solution — Part A(b) **Step 1 — Compute the Kelvin constant:** $$k = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{RT} = \frac{2 \times 0.0718 \times 18.0 \times 10^{-6}}{8.314 \times 300}$$ Numerator: $2 \times 0.0718 \times 18.0 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5848 \times 10^{-6}$ J m mol⁻¹ Denominator: $8.314 \times 300 = 2494.2$ J mol⁻¹ $$k = \frac{2.5848 \times 10^{-6}}{2494.2} = 1.0363 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Case 1: Convex droplet (r = +5 nm)** $$r = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$ $$\ln(P/P_0) = \frac{1.0363 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.20726$$ $$P/P_0 = e^{0.20726} = 1.2303$$ $$P_{\text{droplet}} = 1.2303 \times 3535 = 4349 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Case 2: Concave meniscus (r = −5 nm, $\cos\theta = 1$)** $$\ln(P/P_0) = -\frac{1.0363 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = -0.20726$$ $$P/P_0 = e^{-0.20726} = 0.8128$$ $$P_{\text{meniscus}} = 0.8128 \times 3535 = 2873 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 4 — Physical interpretation:** - The **5 nm droplet** has a vapor pressure of **4349 Pa**, about **23% higher** than flat water. This small droplet tends to evaporate. - The **concave meniscus** in a glass capillary has a vapor pressure of **2873 Pa**, about **19% lower**. Vapor will spontaneously condense into the capillary below the saturation pressure. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** Discuss the significance of the Kelvin Equation in the context of **capillary condensation in mesoporous materials**. How is this equation used in the **BJH (Barrett–Joyner–Halenda) method** for pore-size distribution analysis? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mesoporous silica sample is analyzed by nitrogen sorption at **77 K**. The desorption branch shows a sharp drop in adsorbed volume at a relative pressure $P/P_0 = 0.78$. Using the Kelvin Equation, estimate the pore radius. Given for N₂ at 77 K: $\gamma = 8.85 \times 10^{-3}$ N m⁻¹, $V_m = 34.7 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ mol⁻¹, contact angle = 0° (complete wetting). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part B(a) **Capillary Condensation in Mesoporous Materials:** Mesoporous materials (pore widths 2–50 nm, per IUPAC classification) exhibit hysteresis loops in their nitrogen sorption isotherms at 77 K. The *lower* branch (desorption) corresponds to evaporation from pores, and the *upper* branch (adsorption) corresponds to condensation into pores. The Kelvin Equation is the *theoretical bridge* between the observed relative pressure $P/P_0$ and the pore radius. In the **BJH method**, the desorption branch of the isotherm is divided into discrete pressure steps. For each step corresponding to relative pressure $P_i/P_0$: 1. The **Kelvin radius** $r_k$ is computed from: $$r_k = -\frac{2\gamma V_m}{RT \ln(P_i/P_0)}$$ The negative sign reflects the concave meniscus geometry. 2. The **actual pore radius** is then: $$r_p = r_k + t$$ where $t$ is the **statistical thickness of the pre-adsorbed layer** (typically obtained from a reference isotherm, e.g., the Harkins–Jura or Halsey–Faas correction). 3. The **volume of liquid** evaporated in that pressure step is converted (via the density of liquid nitrogen) into a pore volume, and from the cumulative data, a pore-size distribution is constructed. **Significance:** - Provides the *only practical route* to determine mesopore size distributions from gas sorption data. - Underpins quality control in **catalyst manufacturing** (FCC catalysts, hydrocracking catalysts), **pharmaceutical excipients**, and **MEMS fabrication**. - Limitations: BJH underestimates pore sizes below ~3 nm because of the breakdown of macroscopic thermodynamics (Kelvin equation assumes bulk-like $\gamma$ and density). --- #### Model Solution — Part B(b) **Step 1 — Write the Kelvin Equation for capillary evaporation (desorption):** $$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{2\gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_k RT}$$ With $\cos\theta = 1$: $$r_k = -\frac{2\gamma V_m}{RT \ln(P/P_0)}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:** Given: - $P/P_0 = 0.78$ - $\gamma = 8.85 \times 10^{-3}$ N m⁻¹ - $V_m = 34.7 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ mol⁻¹ - $T = 77$ K - $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ **Step 3 — Compute the denominator's $RT$ term:** $$RT = 8.314 \times 77 = 640.178 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $\ln(P/P_0)$:** $$\ln(0.78) = -0.24846$$ **Step 5 — Compute the numerator $2\gamma V_m$:** $$2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 34.7 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 17.70 \times 10^{-3} \times 34.7 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 614.19 \times 10^{-9} = 6.1419 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J m mol}^{-1}$$ **Step 6 — Compute Kelvin radius:** $$r_k = -\frac{6.1419 \times 10^{-7}}{640.178 \times (-0.24846)}$$ $$= -\frac{6.1419 \times 10^{-7}}{-159.06}$$ $$= 3.861 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{r_k \approx 3.86 \text{ nm}}$$ **Step 7 — Physical interpretation:** - The Kelvin radius is approximately **3.86 nm**. - The actual pore radius would be slightly larger (typically by 0.5–1.0 nm) due to the pre-adsorbed nitrogen layer. - The estimated pore diameter is therefore in the **upper mesopore range** (~8–9 nm), consistent with many MCM-41 or SBA-type silicas. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign Errors on Concave Surfaces:** Forgetting that the meniscus in a capillary has a *negative* effective radius. This single error flips the entire sign of $\ln(P/P_0)$ and yields a nonsensical "above-saturation" answer. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** per occurrence. > 2. **Unit Mismatches:** Mixing radius in cm with $R$ in SI — always convert to **meters** before computing. A common mistake is to leave $r$ in nm without converting ($1$ nm $= 10^{-9}$ m). > 3. **Omitting the Molar Volume of the Liquid Phase (not the gas):** Some students mistakenly use the molar volume of the vapor (from the ideal gas law). The Kelvin Equation requires the *condensed phase* molar volume $V_m$. > 4. **Skipping Assumptions:** The derivation part (7 marks) explicitly demands the *list of assumptions* (ideal vapor, incompressible liquid, curvature-independent $\gamma$). Omitting this list costs **2 marks** even if the algebra is correct. > 5. **Confusing the Kelvin Equation with the Young–Laplace Equation:** They are related but distinct. The Kelvin Equation is a *thermodynamic* relation (chemical potential), while the Young–Laplace equation is a *mechanical* one (pressure jump). Examiners test this distinction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Print this checklist and review 24 hours before the exam.** - [ ] The Kelvin Equation is derived from the **equality of chemical potentials** between a curved liquid surface and its vapor, combined with the **Young–Laplace pressure jump**. - [ ] Standard form: $\ln(P/P_0) = 2\gamma V_m / (rRT)$ — memorize as is. - [ ] **Convex surface (droplet):** $P > P_0$ → enhanced evaporation, supersaturation required for nucleation. - [ ] **Concave surface (meniscus in capillary):** $P < P_0$ → spontaneous condensation below saturation. - [ ] **Flat surface:** $P = P_0$ — the reference state. - [ ] The Kelvin radius $r_k = 2\gamma V_m / [RT \ln(P/P_0)]$ defines the *critical radius* for nucleation. - [ ] Capillary condensation in cylindrical pores uses the modified form: $\ln(P/P_0) = -2\gamma V_m \cos\theta / (r_p RT)$. - [ ] **BJH method** uses the Kelvin equation to extract pore-size distributions from N₂ sorption at **77 K**. - [ ] The Kelvin constant $k = 2\gamma V_m / (RT)$ has units of **meters**; always check dimensional consistency. - [ ] Key assumptions: ideal vapor, incompressible liquid, curvature-independent $\gamma$, spherical geometry. - [ ] For very small drops ($r < 1$ nm), corrections like the **Tolman length** and the **van der Waals** term are needed. - [ ] The Kelvin Equation explains the **hysteresis loop** in sorption isotherms: adsorption (ink-bottle pore filling) and desorption (open meniscus evaporation) occur at different $P/P_0$. - [ ] Industrial relevance: **catalyst design, pharmaceutical stability, inkjet printing, cloud physics, MEMS devices**. - [ ] Common numerical data for KTU: water at 298 K with $\gamma = 0.072$ N m⁻¹, $V_m = 18 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ mol⁻¹. - [ ] Always convert: 1 nm = $10^{-9}$ m; 1 Å = $10^{-10}$ m; $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹. - [ ] In BJH calculations, the **actual pore radius** = Kelvin radius + thickness of pre-adsorbed layer $t$. - [ ] **Sign convention in KTU valuation:** Concave = negative radius; always state this explicitly in derivations.

topics.title: Measurement of Surface and Interfacial Tension
Maximum Bubble Pressure Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maximum Bubble Pressure Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Maximum Bubble Pressure Method (MBPM)** is a dynamic tensiometric technique used to determine the **surface tension** (γ) or **interfacial tension** of liquids by measuring the maximum pressure required to detach a bubble formed at the tip of a capillary tube immersed in the test liquid. The method operates on the principle of the **Young-Laplace equation**, where the maximum pressure corresponds to the point at which the bubble assumes a hemispherical geometry with a radius equal to the inner radius of the capillary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The MBPM is explicitly listed under Module 3 of PECHT863 as a key experimental method for the determination of surface and interfacial tension of liquids. Students are expected to derive the working equation, explain the apparatus, and discuss its applicability for dynamic measurements.

The fundamental governing relationship is given by:

$$P_{max} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

where $P_{max}$ is the maximum excess pressure inside the bubble, $\gamma$ is the surface tension of the liquid (in N/m), and $r$ is the inner radius of the capillary tube (in meters).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine blowing a soap bubble at the end of a drinking straw dipped into soapy water. As you gently blow:
- Initially, a tiny bubble forms — you need only a little pressure.
- As the bubble grows, you must blow **harder and harder**.
- At a critical moment, the bubble has a perfect **hemisphere shape** with radius equal to the straw's opening — this is when the pressure inside is at its **maximum**.
- Beyond this point, the bubble suddenly becomes "easier" to blow — it grows larger with less pressure until it pinches off and floats away.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of the liquid's surface like a stretched elastic sheet. Curving it tightly (small radius) requires more force than curving it gently (large radius). The tightest possible curvature for a bubble on a capillary is when it becomes a hemisphere of the capillary's radius — this is the "maximum pressure" condition that the MBPM exploits.

This transient peak in pressure is what the Maximum Bubble Pressure Method captures and converts into a quantitative measure of surface tension.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
- **Surface tension of pure water at 25°C:** $\gamma_{H_2O} = 71.97$ mN/m (commonly rounded to **72 mN/m**)
- **Surface tension of pure water at 20°C:** $\gamma_{H_2O} = 72.75$ mN/m
- **Typical capillary radii used:** $r = 0.1$ mm to $1.0$ mm
- **Bubble formation frequencies:** $f = 0.1$ Hz to $20$ Hz (corresponding to surface ages from ~10 ms to ~10 s)
- **Standard reference temperature:** $T = 25°C$ (298.15 K)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Young-Laplace Equation

The Maximum Bubble Pressure Method is rooted in the **Young-Laplace equation**, which describes the pressure difference across a curved liquid interface:

$$\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

where:
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference across the interface (Pa)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m)
- $R_1, R_2$ = principal radii of curvature (m)

For a **spherical bubble** ($R_1 = R_2 = R$), this simplifies to:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

## 2.2 Bubble Formation Stages at a Capillary Tip

When gas is slowly forced through a capillary tube of radius $r$ immersed in a liquid, the bubble evolution proceeds through distinct geometric stages:

1. **Initial stage (small bubble):** The bubble grows outward from the capillary. The radius of curvature $R$ is much larger than $r$. Pressure is low.
2. **Intermediate stage:** As the bubble expands, $R$ decreases. Pressure rises according to $\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$.
3. **Critical/hemispherical stage:** The bubble assumes a perfect hemisphere with radius $R = r$. This is the **maximum pressure point**.
4. **Post-critical stage:** The bubble continues to grow, but now $R$ starts increasing again. Pressure decreases.
5. **Detachment:** The bubble pinches off and rises through the liquid. A new cycle begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Maximum?** The pressure is inversely proportional to the radius of curvature. The minimum possible radius of curvature for a bubble at the capillary tip is the capillary radius itself (hemispherical geometry). This minimum radius yields the maximum pressure.

## 2.3 Working Equation Derivation

Setting $R = r$ at the hemispherical maximum pressure condition:

$$\Delta P_{max} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

For measurement against atmospheric pressure using a manometer:

$$P_{max} = P_{measured} - P_{hydrostatic}$$

The hydrostatic correction accounts for the depth of immersion of the capillary:

$$P_{hydrostatic} = \rho g h$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of liquid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration ($9.81$ m/s²)
- $h$ = depth of capillary immersion below the liquid surface (m)

Therefore, the **complete working equation** is:

$$\gamma = \frac{r \cdot (P_{max} - \rho g h)}{2}$$

## 2.4 Interfacial Tension (Liquid-Liquid Systems)

When the bubble is formed in one liquid surrounded by another (immiscible) liquid, the equation becomes:

$$P_{max} = \frac{2(\gamma_1 - \gamma_2)}{r}$$

where $\gamma_1$ is the surface tension of the inner liquid (where the bubble forms) and $\gamma_2$ is the interfacial contribution. For the specific case of air bubble in liquid 1 surrounded by liquid 2:

$$\gamma_{12} = \frac{r \cdot P_{max}}{2}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{max} = 2\gamma / r$ | Maximum bubble pressure (basic form) | Pa |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \gamma(1/R_1 + 1/R_2)$ | Young-Laplace equation (general) | Pa |
| 3 | $\Delta P = 2\gamma / R$ | Young-Laplace for spherical interface | Pa |
| 4 | $\gamma = r(P_{max} - \rho g h)/2$ | Working equation (with hydrostatic correction) | N/m |
| 5 | $P_{hydrostatic} = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure of liquid column | Pa |
| 6 | $\gamma_{12} = r \cdot P_{max} / 2$ | Interfacial tension (liquid-liquid) | N/m |
| 7 | $f = 1/t_{surface}$ | Bubble frequency → surface age relationship | Hz |
| 8 | $\gamma_{rel} = \gamma_{sample}/\gamma_{water}$ | Relative surface tension (dimensionless) | — |
| 9 | $\pi = \gamma_0 - \gamma$ | Surface pressure (Gibbs definition) | N/m |
| 10 | $\Gamma = -(\partial \gamma / \partial \mu)/RT$ | Gibbs adsorption isotherm | mol/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Saver Note (KTU):** Always remember to include the **hydrostatic correction** $\rho g h$ in the working equation. Examiners frequently award 1–2 marks specifically for this term. Omitting it is one of the most common mistakes in derivations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Maximum Bubble Pressure Method is extensively used in:

- **Surfactant characterization:** Measuring dynamic surface tension of detergent formulations at short timescales (milliseconds to seconds).
- **Coating and printing industries:** Predicting ink and coating wetting behavior at industrially relevant surface ages.
- **Pharmaceutical formulations:** Quality control of emulsions, foams, and dispersions.
- **Petroleum industry:** Interfacial tension measurements between crude oil and brine for enhanced oil recovery (EOR) studies.
- **Biomedical applications:** Lung surfactant studies (pulmonary surface tension).
- **Food science:** Characterizing foaming properties of proteins and emulsifiers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Working Equation

### Step 1: Starting Point — Young-Laplace Equation

We begin with the general Young-Laplace equation for the pressure difference across a curved liquid interface:

$$\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

**Textual explanation:** This equation states that the pressure difference $\Delta P$ across a curved interface is directly proportional to the surface tension $\gamma$ and inversely proportional to the two principal radii of curvature $R_1$ and $R_2$.

### Step 2: Application to a Spherical Bubble

For a perfectly spherical bubble, both principal radii of curvature are equal ($R_1 = R_2 = R$). Substituting:

$$\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{R} \right) = \gamma \cdot \frac{2}{R} = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

**Textual explanation:** When the bubble is a sphere of radius $R$, the curvature is the same in all directions, simplifying the expression to $2\gamma/R$.

### Step 3: Bubble Geometry at the Capillary Tip

Consider a capillary tube of inner radius $r$ immersed in a liquid. As gas flows through the capillary, a bubble forms at its tip. The bubble geometry changes progressively:

- **Stage A:** The bubble has a large radius of curvature $R \gg r$ (early growth, low pressure)
- **Stage B:** The bubble grows, $R$ decreases, and pressure rises
- **Stage C (Critical):** The bubble becomes a hemisphere of radius equal to the capillary radius ($R = r$)
- **Stage D:** The bubble continues growing, $R$ increases, pressure falls
- **Stage E:** The bubble detaches

**Textual explanation:** The minimum value of $R$ attainable for a bubble growing at the capillary tip is the capillary radius itself, because the bubble cannot have a smaller radius of curvature than the opening from which it emerges.

### Step 4: Maximum Pressure Condition

Setting $R = r$ (the hemispherical condition):

$$\Delta P_{max} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

**Textual explanation:** This is the fundamental working equation. The maximum pressure occurs precisely when the bubble geometry matches the capillary dimensions.

### Step 5: Pressure Measured by the Instrument

In practice, the instrument measures the total gas pressure $P_{gas}$ inside the bubble, which equals atmospheric pressure plus the capillary pressure plus the hydrostatic pressure of the liquid column above the bubble:

$$P_{gas} = P_{atm} + \Delta P_{max} + \rho g h$$

Rearranging:

$$\Delta P_{max} = P_{gas} - P_{atm} - \rho g h$$

**Textual explanation:** The bubble must overcome the atmospheric pressure pushing down on the liquid surface, the capillary pressure of the curved interface, and the weight of the liquid column above the bubble tip.

### Step 6: Expressing Surface Tension

Equating the two expressions for $\Delta P_{max}$:

$$\frac{2\gamma}{r} = P_{gas} - P_{atm} - \rho g h$$

Solving for $\gamma$:

$$\boxed{\gamma = \frac{r}{2} \left( P_{gas} - P_{atm} - \rho g h \right)}$$

**Textual explanation:** This is the **final working equation** used in the laboratory to calculate surface tension from experimentally measured quantities: capillary radius $r$, gas pressure $P_{gas}$, atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$, and immersion depth $h$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Calibration Against a Reference Liquid

### Step 7: Why Calibration is Needed

The capillary radius $r$ is often not measured directly with high precision. Instead, the instrument is **calibrated** using a liquid of known surface tension (typically water at 25°C, $\gamma_{water} = 71.97$ mN/m).

### Step 8: Calibration Equation

From the working equation, the capillary radius is:

$$r = \frac{2\gamma_{water}}{P_{max,water} - \rho_{water} g h}$$

### Step 9: Application to Unknown Sample

Once $r$ is determined, the surface tension of an unknown sample is calculated using:

$$\gamma_{sample} = \frac{r}{2} \left( P_{max,sample} - \rho_{sample} g h \right)$$

**Textual explanation:** This two-step calibration process eliminates systematic errors associated with imprecise capillary dimensions and ensures measurement accuracy.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pressure vs. Bubble Radius

| Bubble Stage | Geometry | Radius of Curvature | Pressure $\Delta P$ |
|--------------|----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Initial (small bubble) | Spheroid, $R \gg r$ | Very large | Very low |
| Growing | Spheroid, $R$ decreasing | Large, decreasing | Increasing |
| **Critical (hemisphere)** | **Perfect hemisphere, $R = r$** | **Minimum = $r$** | **$P_{max} = 2\gamma/r$** |
| Post-critical | Spheroid, $R$ increasing | Increasing | Decreasing |
| Detachment | Necking | Variable | Variable |
| Free bubble in liquid | Sphere | Larger than $r$ | Lower than $P_{max}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The pressure-radius relationship is **not monotonic**. It rises, reaches a maximum at $R = r$, then falls. This is the key signature of the MBPM and distinguishes it from other tensiometric methods like the Du Noüy ring or Wilhelmy plate.

## 3.4 Numerical Example

**Problem:** A capillary of inner radius $r = 0.5$ mm is immersed to a depth $h = 1.0$ cm in water at 25°C. The maximum bubble pressure recorded is $P_{max} = 290$ Pa (gauge pressure). Calculate the surface tension of water.

**Given:**
- $r = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $h = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m
- $\rho_{water} = 997$ kg/m³ (at 25°C)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $P_{max} = 290$ Pa

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate hydrostatic pressure:

$$\rho g h = 997 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\rho g h = 97.81 \text{ Pa}$$

Step 2: Net capillary pressure:

$$\Delta P_{capillary} = P_{max} - \rho g h = 290 - 97.81 = 192.19 \text{ Pa}$$

Step 3: Calculate surface tension:

$$\gamma = \frac{r \cdot \Delta P_{capillary}}{2} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 192.19}{2}$$

$$\gamma = \frac{0.096095}{2} = 0.04805 \text{ N/m} \approx 48.05 \text{ mN/m}$$

**Analysis:** The calculated value (48.05 mN/m) is significantly lower than the literature value for pure water (71.97 mN/m). This discrepancy indicates either (a) a contaminated surface, (b) a surfactant-contaminated sample, or (c) an experimental error in capillary radius or pressure measurement. This example illustrates the importance of pure liquids for validation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apparatus Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional architecture and flow of the Maximum Bubble Pressure Tensiometer:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Supply Cylinder or Pump] --> B[Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[Flow Controller / Needle Valve]
    C --> D[Pressure Transducer Sensor]
    D --> E[Capillary Tube of Known Radius r]
    E --> F[Liquid Sample in Thermostated Cell]
    F --> G[Bubble Formation and Detachment]
    G --> H[Signal Amplifier and Filter]
    H --> I[Data Acquisition System]
    I --> J[Display and Recording Unit]
    F --> K[Temperature Controller]
    K --> F
    D --> L[Maximum Pressure Detector]
    L --> M[Surface Tension Calculator]
    M --> N[Output Gamma Value in N per m]
```

**Textual explanation:** Gas from a cylinder or pump is regulated to a controlled flow rate by a needle valve. The gas passes through a pressure transducer before reaching the capillary tip immersed in the liquid sample. As bubbles form and detach cyclically, the pressure transducer records the time-varying pressure profile, and the maximum value is detected. The data acquisition system processes this signal to compute surface tension using the working equation.

## 4.2 Bubble Formation Cycle Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Bubble Nucleation] --> S2[Stage 2: Bubble Growth]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Hemispherical Geometry]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Bubble Elongation]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Necking and Detachment]
    S5 --> S1
```

**Textual explanation:** This cyclical process repeats continuously. The surface tension measurement corresponds to the maximum pressure reached at Stage 3, which corresponds to a characteristic "surface age" determined by the bubble formation frequency.

## 4.3 Pressure vs. Time Profile Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Pressure at t=0 Atmospheric] --> P1[Rising Pressure Phase]
    P1 --> P2[Maximum Pressure Pmax at Hemisphere]
    P2 --> P3[Falling Pressure Phase]
    P3 --> P4[Detachment and Pressure Reset]
    P4 --> P5[Next Cycle Begins]
    P5 --> P1
```

**Textual explanation:** The pressure-vs-time profile in MBPM shows a characteristic peak-and-valley pattern. The peak (Stage 2) is the maximum pressure point used for surface tension calculation. The valley (Stage 4) corresponds to detachment. The frequency of these cycles determines the "surface age" at which the measurement is taken.

## 4.4 Comparison Block: MBPM vs. Other Tensiometric Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Static Methods
        A1[Wilhelmy Plate Method]
        A2[Du Nouy Ring Method]
        A3[Sessile Drop Method]
    end
    subgraph Dynamic Methods
        B1[Maximum Bubble Pressure Method]
        B2[Oscillating Jet Method]
        B3[Spinning Drop Method]
    end
    A1 --> C1[Equilibrium Surface Tension]
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    B1 --> C2[Dynamic Surface Tension]
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C2
```

**Textual explanation:** The MBPM belongs to the family of dynamic tensiometric methods, distinguishing it from equilibrium methods like Wilhelmy plate and Du Noüy ring. Its primary advantage is the ability to probe surface tension at very short timescales (milliseconds).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q:** Define the Maximum Bubble Pressure Method for surface tension measurement. What is the fundamental equation governing this method?

**Model Answer:**

The Maximum Bubble Pressure Method (MBPM) is a dynamic tensiometric technique in which a bubble of gas is formed at the tip of a capillary tube immersed in a test liquid. The maximum pressure required to form the bubble is measured, and from this pressure, the surface tension of the liquid is calculated using the **Young-Laplace equation**.

The fundamental governing equation is:

$$P_{max} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

where $P_{max}$ is the maximum pressure inside the bubble, $\gamma$ is the surface tension of the liquid, and $r$ is the inner radius of the capillary tube.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q:** Why is the Maximum Bubble Pressure Method classified as a "dynamic" tensiometric method? What timescale range does it typically cover?

**Model Answer:**

The MBPM is classified as a **dynamic method** because the surface tension is measured on a freshly formed interface whose age is continuously changing. By varying the bubble formation frequency, one can measure surface tension at different surface ages.

The method covers surface ages ranging from approximately **1 millisecond (10⁻³ s) to 10 seconds**, depending on the bubble frequency. This makes it particularly valuable for studying:

- Fast adsorption kinetics of surfactants
- Industrial processes involving rapid interface formation (spraying, coating, printing)
- Pulmonary surfactant dynamics

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q:** 
**(a)** Derive the working equation for the Maximum Bubble Pressure Method, clearly explaining the significance of each term. Include the hydrostatic pressure correction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the experimental setup of a Maximum Bubble Pressure Tensiometer with a labeled diagram. Discuss any two advantages and one limitation of this method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation of the Working Equation:**

**Step 1:** Begin with the Young-Laplace equation for a spherical interface:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

**Step 2:** Consider bubble growth at a capillary of radius $r$. The radius of curvature $R$ starts very large, decreases as the bubble grows, reaches a minimum of $r$ (when the bubble is hemispherical), and then increases again.

**Step 3:** Maximum pressure occurs at the hemispherical condition $R = r$:

$$P_{max} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

**Step 4:** The measured pressure includes atmospheric pressure and hydrostatic head:

$$P_{measured} = P_{atm} + P_{max} + \rho g h$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging for surface tension:

$$\gamma = \frac{r \cdot (P_{measured} - P_{atm} - \rho g h)}{2}$$

**Significance of each term:**
- $r$ = capillary radius (geometric parameter)
- $P_{measured}$ = experimentally measured gas pressure
- $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure (background)
- $\rho g h$ = hydrostatic correction due to immersion depth
- $\gamma$ = surface tension (the unknown being determined)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Experimental Setup (as per the block diagram in Section 4):**

The MBPM tensiometer consists of:
1. **Gas supply** (nitrogen cylinder or syringe pump)
2. **Pressure regulator** for controlled gas flow
3. **Capillary tube** of precisely known inner radius $r$
4. **Sample cell** containing the test liquid, maintained at constant temperature
5. **Pressure transducer** to record pressure fluctuations
6. **Data acquisition system** to capture and process the pressure-vs-time signal
7. **Display unit** for real-time surface tension readings

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Short timescale capability:** Can measure surface tension at surface ages as low as 1 ms, enabling kinetic studies of surfactant adsorption.
2. **Simple apparatus:** Does not require complex optical components or precise dimensional measurements of the liquid sample.

**One Limitation:**
1. Requires precise knowledge of the capillary radius; small errors in $r$ propagate directly into errors in $\gamma$ (sensitivity: $\delta\gamma/\gamma = \delta r/r$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q:**
**(a)** Explain with a pressure-vs-time diagram how the maximum pressure is identified in the MBPM. Discuss the physical reason for the existence of a maximum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A capillary of inner radius $r = 0.25$ mm is immersed $1.5$ cm deep in a liquid of density $\rho = 850$ kg/m³. The maximum gauge pressure recorded is $380$ Pa. If the literature value of the surface tension is $35$ mN/m, calculate the percentage error in the measurement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Pressure-vs-Time Profile Explanation:**

The pressure inside a growing bubble at a capillary tip varies with time as follows:

- **Phase 1 (Rising):** Initially, the bubble radius is much larger than the capillary radius. As the bubble grows, the radius of curvature $R$ decreases, and by Young-Laplace ($\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$), the pressure increases.

- **Phase 2 (Peak):** When $R = r$ (hemispherical geometry), $\Delta P$ reaches its maximum value $P_{max} = 2\gamma/r$.

- **Phase 3 (Falling):** Beyond the hemisphere, as the bubble elongates, $R$ starts to increase again, and pressure decreases.

- **Phase 4 (Reset):** The bubble detaches, pressure drops sharply to baseline, and the next cycle begins.

**Physical Reason for Maximum:**

The maximum exists because of the **inverse relationship** between pressure and radius of curvature. The smallest possible radius of curvature for a bubble attached to a capillary of radius $r$ is $r$ itself (the hemispherical geometry). Beyond this geometry, the bubble cannot contract further and instead expands, increasing $R$ and decreasing pressure.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $r = 0.25 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $h = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$ m
- $\rho = 850$ kg/m³
- $P_{max} = 380$ Pa (gauge)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Literature value: $\gamma_{lit} = 35$ mN/m

**Step 1: Calculate hydrostatic pressure:**

$$\rho g h = 850 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\rho g h = 125.07 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Calculate measured surface tension:**

$$\gamma_{measured} = \frac{r \cdot (P_{max} - \rho g h)}{2}$$

$$\gamma_{measured} = \frac{0.25 \times 10^{-3} \times (380 - 125.07)}{2}$$

$$\gamma_{measured} = \frac{0.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 254.93}{2}$$

$$\gamma_{measured} = \frac{0.06373}{2} = 0.03187 \text{ N/m} = 31.87 \text{ mN/m}$$

**Step 3: Calculate percentage error:**

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \gamma_{lit} - \gamma_{measured} \vert}{\gamma_{lit}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 35 - 31.87 \vert}{35} \times 100 = \frac{3.13}{35} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Error} = 8.94\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the hydrostatic correction** ($\rho g h$): Many students compute $\gamma = rP_{max}/2$ directly, which overestimates $\gamma$. This is the single most common error. **Marks lost: 1–2 per instance.**
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Capillary radius must be in **meters** (not mm or cm) when pressure is in Pa. Failing to convert units leads to answers off by factors of $10^3$ to $10^5$.
> 3. **Confusing gauge vs. absolute pressure:** If the problem says "gauge pressure," do NOT subtract atmospheric pressure. If it says "absolute pressure," subtract $P_{atm}$ first.
> 4. **Identifying the maximum:** Some students confuse the maximum pressure point with detachment pressure. They are distinct — maximum is at the hemisphere, detachment occurs later at lower pressure.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Principle:** Maximum pressure occurs when bubble radius equals capillary radius (hemispherical geometry)
- ✅ **Core equation:** $P_{max} = 2\gamma / r$
- ✅ **Working equation:** $\gamma = r(P_{measured} - P_{atm} - \rho g h) / 2$
- ✅ **Foundation:** Young-Laplace equation $\Delta P = \gamma(1/R_1 + 1/R_2)$
- ✅ **For a sphere:** $\Delta P = 2\gamma / R$
- ✅ **Dynamic method:** Surface tension is measured at a specific surface age determined by bubble frequency
- ✅ **Surface age range:** ~1 ms to ~10 s
- ✅ **Calibration:** Typically performed with pure water at 25°C ($\gamma = 71.97$ mN/m)
- ✅ **Hydrostatic correction:** Always include $\rho g h$ when capillary is immersed below the liquid surface
- ✅ **Units check:** $r$ in meters, $P$ in Pascals, $\gamma$ in N/m (or mN/m after multiplying by 1000)
- ✅ **Apparatus components:** Gas source → regulator → capillary → pressure transducer → data acquisition
- ✅ **Interfacial tension:** For liquid-liquid systems, $\gamma_{12} = rP_{max}/2$
- ✅ **Advantages:** Short timescale, simple setup, suitable for kinetic studies
- ✅ **Limitations:** Requires precise capillary radius, single-point measurement per cycle
- ✅ **KTU-relevant applications:** Surfactant characterization, EOR studies, pharmaceutical formulations, coating/printing industries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:** When answering MBPM questions, always:
> 1. State the principle first (hemispherical bubble = maximum pressure)
> 2. Write the Young-Laplace equation
> 3. Apply the hemispherical condition ($R = r$)
> 4. Include the hydrostatic correction
> 5. Discuss dynamic aspect and timescale relevance
> 
> Following this 5-point structure guarantees full marks for derivation-type questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Contact Angle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Contact Angle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **contact angle (θ)** is formally defined as the quantitative measure of the angle formed at the **three-phase contact line** (also called the wetting perimeter or triple line) where the **solid**, **liquid**, and **vapour** phases intersect. It is geometrically constructed by measuring the angle between the **tangent plane to the liquid–vapour interface** and the **tangent plane to the solid surface** at the point of contact, measured through the liquid phase.

Mathematically, the equilibrium contact angle is governed by the **Young Equation** (1805):

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta_Y$$

where each surface/interfacial tension term (with units of **mN·m⁻¹** or **J·m⁻²**) represents:
- $\gamma_{SV}$ → solid–vapour interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{SL}$ → solid–liquid interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{LV}$ → liquid–vapour (surface) tension
- $\theta_Y$ → Young's equilibrium contact angle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The contact angle is the **primary macroscopic descriptor of wettability** of a solid surface by a liquid. It is the single most quoted experimental quantity in surface chemistry, pharmaceutical formulation, coating technology, and adhesion science.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you place a single water droplet gently onto two different kitchen surfaces:

- **On a clean glass slide:** the droplet **spreads out** like a tiny pancake. It "likes" the surface. The contact angle is **small (θ < 30°)** — this is a *wetting* or *hydrophilic* surface.
- **On a Teflon-coated non-stick pan:** the droplet **beads up** like a perfectly round marble. It "dislikes" the surface. The contact angle is **large (θ > 90°)** — this is a *non-wetting* or *hydrophobic* surface.

The **contact angle is essentially a visual "happiness meter"** of the liquid sitting on the solid. A low angle means the liquid is energetically happy spreading out; a high angle means it prefers to minimise contact and ball up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world relevance in KTU curriculum:** Contact angle measurement is the experimental gateway to determining the **work of adhesion**, **surface free energy of solids**, and **spreading coefficient** — all of which are core topics of Module 3 and frequently asked in **ESE Part B questions (14 marks)**.

## 1.3 Standard Classification of Wetting by Contact Angle

| Wetting Regime | Contact Angle Range | Practical Example | Adhesion State |
|---|---|---|---|
| Complete Wetting | $\theta = 0°$ | Water on perfectly clean glass | Strong |
| High Wetting | $0° < \theta < 30°$ | Water on clean metal | Strong |
| Moderate Wetting | $30° \leq \theta < 90°$ | Water on clean steel | Moderate |
| Non-Wetting | $90° \leq \theta < 150°$ | Water on paraffin wax | Weak |
| Superhydrophobic | $\theta \geq 150°$ | Water on lotus leaf | Negligible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold meaning:** The value $\theta = 90°$ is the conventional dividing line between "wetting" and "non-wetting" behaviour. This is an important textbook convention tested in **Part A 3-mark questions**.

---

## 1.4 Three-Phase Contact Line — Geometric Description

At the **triple junction**, three interfacial forces act tangentially to their respective interfaces, pulling the contact line into mechanical equilibrium. Visualise the droplet as a truncated sphere resting on a flat substrate:

- $\gamma_{LV}$ pulls the liquid surface *along the liquid–vapour interface*
- $\gamma_{SL}$ pulls *along the solid–liquid interface*
- $\gamma_{SV}$ pulls *along the solid–vapour interface*

The vector sum of these tensions at the contact line is what defines the equilibrium contact angle $\theta_Y$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Young's Equation Logic

The derivation of Young's equation begins from the principle of **minimisation of total interfacial free energy** of a sessile droplet system at constant temperature, pressure, and number of moles.

### Step-by-step logical breakdown:

1. **Identify the three interfaces present:** solid–vapour, solid–liquid, liquid–vapour. Each contributes an interfacial free energy proportional to its area.
2. **Recognise the geometric coupling:** If the contact angle increases by $d\theta$, the solid–liquid area changes by one sign and the liquid–vapour area changes oppositely (the droplet base shrinks, but the cap height grows).
3. **Apply the thermodynamic equilibrium condition:** At constant $T$, $P$, and $\mu_i$, the differential of the Helmholtz free energy $dF = 0$ for a virtual displacement of the contact line.
4. **Resolve horizontal force balance at the triple line:** The horizontal component of $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ must balance the difference $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$.
5. **Recover Young's equation:** This force resolution directly yields the textbook result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common student error is to "derive" Young's equation by simply balancing three forces as if they were vectors in 2D. The proper foundation is the **energy minimisation argument**. Examiners often award 2 of 7 marks purely for correctly stating the energy basis.

## 2.2 The Young–Dupré Equation — Work of Adhesion

When a liquid spreads on a solid, the work required to **separate** the liquid from the solid (i.e., to create a solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interface while destroying the solid–liquid interface) is called the **work of adhesion**, $W_a$:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV} + \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

Substituting Young's equation into the work of adhesion expression gives the celebrated **Young–Dupré equation**:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

This equation is **the single most important relationship** connecting a directly measurable quantity ($\theta$ and $\gamma_{LV}$) to the energetics of adhesion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Notice that the larger the contact angle, the smaller the work of adhesion. At $\theta = 0°$ (complete wetting), $W_a = 2\gamma_{LV}$ (the **Dupré cohesion limit** — this is the work of cohesion of the liquid with itself). At $\theta = 180°$ (theoretical non-adhesion), $W_a = 0$.

## 2.3 Spreading Coefficient

The **spreading coefficient** $S$ determines whether a liquid will spontaneously spread as a thin film on a solid:

$$S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$$

where $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ is the work of cohesion of the liquid. The condition $S \geq 0$ gives **complete wetting** ($\theta = 0°$); $S < 0$ gives **partial wetting** ($0° < \theta < 180°$).

## 2.4 Contact Angle Hysteresis (CAH)

In practice, the measured contact angle is **not unique** for a given solid–liquid pair. The droplet exhibits:

- **Advancing contact angle ($\theta_A$):** the angle measured as the liquid front advances over a dry surface (largest stable value).
- **Receding contact angle ($\theta_R$):** the angle measured as the liquid front retracts from a wet surface (smallest stable value).

The **contact angle hysteresis** is defined as:

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_A - \theta_R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hysteresis origin:** Surface roughness, chemical heterogeneity, molecular reorientation, surface deformation, and pinning of the triple line by microscopic defects. CAH is a **direct indicator of surface homogeneity** — low CAH → smooth, uniform surface; high CAH → rough or contaminated surface.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Young Equation** | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | $\gamma$ in mN·m⁻¹, $\theta$ in degrees | Equilibrium force balance at triple line |
| 2 | **Young–Dupré** | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | $W_a$ in mJ·m⁻² | Work of liquid–solid adhesion |
| 3 | **Work of Adhesion** | $W_a = \gamma_{LV} + \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Same as above | Direct definition |
| 4 | **Work of Cohesion** | $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ | mJ·m⁻² | Work to separate liquid from itself |
| 5 | **Spreading Coefficient** | $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ | mJ·m⁻² | Spreading tendency of liquid |
| 6 | **Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)** | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | $h$ in m, $\rho$ in kg·m⁻³, $r$ in m | Capillary height in tube |
| 7 | **Wilhelmy Plate Force** | $F = \gamma_{LV} L \cos\theta$ | $F$ in N, $L$ in m | Force on immersed plate |
| 8 | **Fowkes Surface Energy** | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d}$ | mJ·m⁻² | Dispersive component contribution |
| 9 | **Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble** | $\gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta) = 2\left[\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right]$ | mJ·m⁻² | Polar + dispersive SFE calculation |
| 10 | **Wenzel Roughness** | $\cos\theta_W = r\cos\theta_Y$ | $r \geq 1$ dimensionless | Contact angle on rough surface |
| 11 | **Cassie–Baxter Composite** | $\cos\theta_{CB} = f_S(\cos\theta_Y + 1) - 1$ | $f_S$ solid area fraction | Contact angle on composite surface |
| 12 | **Critical Surface Tension (Zisman)** | $\cos\theta = 1 - \dfrac{\gamma_c}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | dimensionless | Empirical wettability index |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Field | Use of Contact Angle Data |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceutical Industry** | Tablet coating uniformity, drug dissolution rate |
| **Paints & Coatings** | Spreading on substrates, defect-free film formation |
| **Microfluidics / Lab-on-chip** | Droplet manipulation, capillary valve design |
| **Oil Recovery (EOR)** | Wettability alteration of reservoir rocks |
| **Biomedical Implants** | Protein adsorption, cell adhesion on titanium |
| **Textile Finishing** | Water-repellent fabric design |
| **Adhesives** | Joint strength prediction |
| **Self-Cleaning Surfaces** | Lotus-effect superhydrophobicity design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Almost any industry that involves a liquid touching a solid needs contact angle data. The KTU Module 3 outcome on this topic maps directly to **industry-ready surface characterisation skills**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Young's Equation from Energy Minimisation

### Setup

Consider a sessile liquid droplet of fixed volume $V$ placed on a smooth, flat, homogeneous, rigid, isotropic solid substrate under saturated vapour pressure, in thermal equilibrium. The total interfacial free energy of the system $F$ is:

$$F = \gamma_{SL}\,A_{SL} + \gamma_{SV}\,A_{SV} + \gamma_{LV}\,A_{LV}$$

where $A_{SL}$, $A_{SV}$, and $A_{LV}$ are the areas of the solid–liquid, solid–vapour, and liquid–vapour interfaces respectively.

### Geometric Constraint

Because the solid substrate is flat and the droplet volume is fixed, the area $A_{SL}$ (the wetted footprint on the solid) is directly linked to the contact angle $\theta$ and the liquid–vapour interfacial area $A_{LV}$. The solid surface area is partitioned as:

$$A_{total} = A_{SL} + A_{SV}$$

So the total free energy can be rewritten as:

$$F = \gamma_{SL}\,A_{SL} + \gamma_{SV}(A_{total} - A_{SL}) + \gamma_{LV}\,A_{LV}$$

$$F = \gamma_{SV}\,A_{total} + (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV})\,A_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\,A_{LV}$$

Since $A_{total}$ is constant for a given substrate, minimising $F$ reduces to minimising:

$$\Phi = (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV})\,A_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\,A_{LV}$$

### Virtual Displacement Argument

Allow the contact line to advance by an infinitesimal amount $dL$ at constant droplet volume. The change in solid–liquid area is:

$$dA_{SL} = + w\,dL$$

where $w$ is the width of the advancing front (for a circular drop, $w = 2\pi R$ where $R$ is the base radius). The corresponding change in liquid–vapour area is:

$$dA_{LV} = - w\cos\theta\,dL$$

The negative sign arises because as the drop spreads, the cap height decreases and the curved liquid surface area shrinks.

### Equilibrium Condition

Setting the differential $d\Phi = 0$:

$$(\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV})\,dA_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\,dA_{LV} = 0$$

$$(\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV})\,(w\,dL) + \gamma_{LV}\,(-w\cos\theta\,dL) = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $w\,dL$ (both are non-zero for a real displacement):

$$(\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV}) - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta_Y}$$

This is **Young's equation**, derived rigorously from interfacial thermodynamics.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Young–Dupré Equation

Starting from the **thermodynamic definition of work of adhesion** for a solid–liquid interface being separated into solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

Substitute Young's equation $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$:

$$W_a = (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}) + \gamma_{LV}$$

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta + \gamma_{LV}$$

$$\boxed{W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sense-check:** For water ($\gamma_{LV} = 72.8$ mN·m⁻¹ at 25 °C) on a clean glass surface ($\theta \approx 0°$), $W_a \approx 72.8 \times (1 + 1) = 145.6$ mJ·m⁻². This is a strong adhesive interaction, consistent with complete wetting.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise and Contact Angle)

A liquid rises inside a thin capillary tube of inner radius $r$ to a height $h$ above the external flat liquid reservoir. The upward force is provided by the surface tension acting along the contact perimeter $2\pi r$:

$$F_{up} = 2\pi r \,\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

The downward force is the weight of the lifted liquid column of cross-section $\pi r^2$ and height $h$:

$$F_{down} = \pi r^2 h \rho g$$

At hydrostatic equilibrium $F_{up} = F_{down}$:

$$2\pi r \,\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = \pi r^2 h \rho g$$

Cancel $\pi r$ from both sides:

$$2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = r h \rho g$$

Solving explicitly for $h$:

$$\boxed{h = \frac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{\rho g r}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Jurin's law is the foundation of the **capillary rise method** of contact angle measurement, applicable for $\theta < 90°$. For $\theta > 90°$, the liquid is depressed (negative $h$), and the same formula applies with a negative value.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Wilhelmy Plate Force Expression

A thin plate of width $w$ and thickness $\delta$ is suspended from a microbalance and dipped into a liquid. The contact perimeter is $L = 2(w + \delta)$. The vertical component of surface tension acting on the wetted perimeter pulls the plate downward (for wetting liquids):

$$F = \gamma_{LV}\,L\cos\theta$$

The microbalance records this force, and with known $L$ and $\gamma_{LV}$, the contact angle can be computed:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{F}{\gamma_{LV}\,L}$$

The Wilhelmy method gives the **average of advancing and receding angles** when the immersion depth is cycled, allowing direct measurement of contact angle hysteresis.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble (OWRK) Surface Energy Components

The total surface free energy of a phase can be decomposed into **dispersive (Lifshitz–van der Waals)** and **polar (acid–base)** components:

$$\gamma = \gamma^d + \gamma^p$$

By **Fowkes' geometric mean assumption**, the solid–liquid interfacial tension becomes:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\left[\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right]$$

Substituting into the Young–Dupré equation and using $\gamma_{LV} = \gamma_L$:

$$\gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left[\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two probe liquids are required:** The OWRK equation has two unknowns ($\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$). One equation is insufficient. By performing contact angle measurements with **two liquids of known $\gamma_L^d$ and $\gamma_L^p$** (e.g., water and diiodomethane), one obtains a 2×2 linear system in the square roots, solvable for $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$.

### Worked Example (typical KTU 14-mark style)

**Given:** Water contact angle on polymer film = $70°$; Diiodomethane contact angle = $35°$. For water: $\gamma_L^d = 21.8$ mN·m⁻¹, $\gamma_L^p = 51.0$ mN·m⁻¹, total $\gamma_L = 72.8$ mN·m⁻¹. For diiodomethane: $\gamma_L^d = 50.8$ mN·m⁻¹, $\gamma_L^p \approx 0$, total $\gamma_L = 50.8$ mN·m⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Equation for water:**

$$72.8(1 + \cos 70°) = 2\left[\sqrt{21.8\,\gamma_S^d} + \sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_S^p}\right]$$

$\cos 70° \approx 0.342$; so $72.8 \times 1.342 = 97.69$ mN·m⁻¹:

$$48.85 = \sqrt{21.8\,\gamma_S^d} + \sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_S^p}$$

**Step 2 — Equation for diiodomethane:**

$$50.8(1 + \cos 35°) = 2\sqrt{50.8\,\gamma_S^d}$$

$\cos 35° \approx 0.819$; $50.8 \times 1.819 = 92.41$:

$$46.20 = 2\sqrt{50.8\,\gamma_S^d} \Rightarrow \sqrt{50.8\,\gamma_S^d} = 23.10$$

$$\gamma_S^d = \frac{(23.10)^2}{50.8} = \frac{533.61}{50.8} \approx 10.50 \text{ mN·m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\gamma_S^p$:**

$$\sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_S^p} = 48.85 - \sqrt{21.8 \times 10.50} = 48.85 - \sqrt{228.9} = 48.85 - 15.13 = 33.72$$

$$\gamma_S^p = \frac{(33.72)^2}{51.0} = \frac{1137.04}{51.0} \approx 22.30 \text{ mN·m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Total surface free energy:**

$$\gamma_S = \gamma_S^d + \gamma_S^p = 10.50 + 22.30 = 32.80 \text{ mN·m}^{-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's sanity check:** The polymer SFE of ~33 mN·m⁻¹ is consistent with a typical mid-range polymer (e.g., PMMA, PET) — physically reasonable. If you obtain a value > 200 mN·m⁻¹, you have almost certainly made a sign error.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Measurement Method

| Method | Best For | Sample Form | Measures | Liquid Volume |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sessile Drop (goniometry)** | Flat, smooth substrates | Solid flat plate | Static $\theta$ | 1–10 µL |
| **Captive Bubble** | Hydrophobic / rough surfaces | Solid flat plate (inverted) | Static $\theta$ | 1–5 mL (bulk) |
| **Wilhelmy Plate** | Uniform coatings, fibres | Plate or fibre | Advancing/Receding | Bulk reservoir |
| **Capillary Rise (Jurin's)** | Powders, packed beds | Powder in tube | Effective $\theta$ | Bulk reservoir |
| **Washburn (wicking)** | Powders, porous media | Powder column | Effective $\cos\theta$ | Bulk reservoir |
| **Tilted Plate** | Hysteresis on flat surfaces | Solid flat plate | $\theta_A$, $\theta_R$ | 5–50 µL |
| **Drop-on-Fibre** | Single fibres, textiles | Single fibre | Static $\theta$ | Sub-µL |
