# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define contact angle. What is its significance in determining the wettability of a solid surface?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The contact angle $\theta$ is the angle formed at the three-phase contact line where solid, liquid, and vapour phases meet, measured through the liquid between the tangent to the liquid–vapour interface and the tangent to the solid surface. **(1 mark)**

It is the primary macroscopic measure of **wettability** — the tendency of a liquid to spread on a solid. By Young's equation, $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$, the contact angle directly reflects the energetic balance of the three interfacial tensions. **(1 mark)**

A small $\theta$ (approaching $0°$) indicates strong wetting (hydrophilic for water); a large $\theta$ (approaching $180°$) indicates non-wetting (hydrophobic for water). The threshold $\theta = 90°$ is conventionally taken as the dividing line between wetting and non-wetting. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is contact angle hysteresis? Mention any two factors that cause it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Contact angle hysteresis (CAH) is the phenomenon in which a sessile droplet exhibits more than one stable contact angle on a given solid surface, defined as: **(1 mark)**

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_A - \theta_R$$

where $\theta_A$ is the advancing angle (front edge) and $\theta_R$ is the receding angle (back edge) of a moving or volume-cycled droplet.

**Any two factors causing CAH (1 mark each, 2 marks total):**
1. **Surface roughness:** Microscopic peaks and valleys pin the triple line, producing a range of apparent contact angles (Wenzel regime).
2. **Chemical heterogeneity:** Patches of differing surface energy (e.g., contaminated regions) cause the local contact angle to vary across the surface.
3. *(Optional alternative)* Molecular reorientation of surface groups upon contact with the liquid.
4. *(Optional alternative)* Surface deformation (soft solids) or swelling of the substrate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** State and derive Young's equation for the equilibrium contact angle of a liquid drop on a smooth, flat, homogeneous solid surface. Explain clearly the assumptions made in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Young–Dupré equation for the work of adhesion. A liquid with surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 65$ mN·m⁻¹ forms a contact angle of $45°$ on a flat solid surface. Calculate the work of adhesion between the liquid and the solid. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Young's Equation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (state clearly for 2 marks):**
1. The solid surface is **smooth, flat, rigid, and chemically homogeneous** (no roughness, no patches).
2. The solid is **inert and insoluble** in the liquid (no chemical reaction or diffusion).
3. The droplet is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with its vapour at constant $T$, $P$, and $\mu_i$.
4. The droplet is small enough that **gravity does not deform** its spherical cap shape (capillary length scale $a = \sqrt{\gamma/\rho g} \approx 2.7$ mm for water is much greater than drop size).
5. The line tension at the triple junction is **negligible** (valid for macroscopic droplets).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The total interfacial free energy of the system is:

$$F = \gamma_{SL}A_{SL} + \gamma_{SV}A_{SV} + \gamma_{LV}A_{LV}$$

Since $A_{SV} = A_{total} - A_{SL}$:

$$F = \gamma_{SV}A_{total} + (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV})A_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}A_{LV}$$

At constant droplet volume, virtual displacement of the contact line by $dL$ gives:

$$dA_{SL} = +w\,dL, \quad dA_{LV} = -w\cos\theta\,dL$$

Setting $dF = 0$ at equilibrium:

$$(\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV})w\,dL + \gamma_{LV}(-w\cos\theta)\,dL = 0$$

$$\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta_Y}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Young–Dupré and Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

By definition, the work of adhesion is the reversible work to separate a unit area of solid–liquid interface into solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

Rearranging and using Young's equation ($\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$):

$$W_a = (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}) + \gamma_{LV} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta + \gamma_{LV}$$

$$\boxed{W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $\gamma_{LV} = 65$ mN·m⁻¹, $\theta = 45°$, so $\cos 45° = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071$.

$$W_a = 65 \times (1 + 0.7071) = 65 \times 1.7071$$

$$W_a \approx 110.96 \text{ mN·m}^{-1} \approx 111.0 \text{ mJ·m}^{-2}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **sessile drop method** and the **Wilhelmy plate method** for contact angle measurement. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble (OWRK) method**, derive the working equation used to determine the dispersive and polar components of the solid surface free energy from contact angle data of two probe liquids. A polymer surface gives a water contact angle of $80°$ and a diiodomethane contact angle of $40°$. Compute $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$ given: water: $\gamma_L^d = 21.8$ mN·m⁻¹, $\gamma_L^p = 51.0$ mN·m⁻¹, $\gamma_L = 72.8$ mN·m⁻¹; diiodomethane: $\gamma_L^d = 50.8$ mN·m⁻¹, $\gamma_L^p \approx 0$, $\gamma_L = 50.8$ mN·m⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Sessile Drop and Wilhelmy Plate Methods (7 marks)

**Sessile Drop Method (3.5 marks):**

In the sessile drop method, a small droplet (typically 1–10 µL) of the probe liquid is gently placed on the flat horizontal solid substrate using a microsyringe. A high-resolution CCD camera captures the silhouette of the droplet against a uniformly illuminated background. The captured image is digitised, and the drop profile is analysed either by:

- **Tangent-line method:** A tangent is drawn at the three-phase contact point, and the angle between this tangent and the solid baseline is measured. Simple but error-prone near the baseline.
- **Young–Laplace fitting:** The full drop profile is fitted to the Young–Laplace equation, giving both $\theta$ and $\gamma_{LV}$ simultaneously. More accurate but computationally intensive.

**Advantages:** Simple, fast, requires only a small amount of liquid, works on small sample areas, non-destructive. **Limitations:** Subjective baseline selection, sensitive to drop volume, requires smooth flat surface, gives only static contact angle (no hysteresis in one image).

**Wilhelmy Plate Method (3.5 marks):**

In the Wilhelmy plate method, a thin plate (or fibre) of known perimeter $L$ is suspended from a microbalance and immersed into the liquid to a controlled depth. The downward force $F$ recorded by the balance is:

$$F = \gamma_{LV}\,L\cos\theta + \text{buoyancy term}$$

With depth control and the buoyancy correction, the contact angle is computed directly from the force reading. By cycling the immersion depth (advancing and receding), both $\theta_A$ and $\theta_R$ are obtained, hence contact angle hysteresis is directly measurable.

**Advantages:** Highly accurate (force-based, not image-based), directly gives $\theta_A$ and $\theta_R$, suitable for fibres and irregularly shaped samples. **Limitations:** Requires bulk liquid reservoir, requires large sample with uniform perimeter, sensitive to surface contamination, requires knowledge of exact perimeter length.

---

#### Part (b) — OWRK Derivation and Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The total surface free energy is decomposed as $\gamma = \gamma^d + \gamma^p$. By Fowkes' geometric mean assumption, the solid–liquid interfacial tension is:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}$$

Substituting into the Young–Dupré equation $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$:

$$\gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left[\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right]$$

This is the **OWRK working equation**. With two probe liquids (with known $\gamma_L^d$, $\gamma_L^p$), one obtains two linear equations in the unknowns $\sqrt{\gamma_S^d}$ and $\sqrt{\gamma_S^p}$.

**Numerical solution (4 marks):**

**Equation for water** ($\theta = 80°$, $\cos 80° \approx 0.1736$):

$$72.8(1 + 0.1736) = 2\left[\sqrt{21.8\gamma_S^d} + \sqrt{51.0\gamma_S^p}\right]$$

$$72.8 \times 1.1736 = 85.44$$

$$42.72 = \sqrt{21.8\gamma_S^d} + \sqrt{51.0\gamma_S^p} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Equation for diiodomethane** ($\theta = 40°$, $\cos 40° \approx 0.7660$):

$$50.8(1 + 0.7660) = 2\sqrt{50.8\gamma_S^d}$$

$$50.8 \times 1.7660 = 89.71$$

$$44.86 = 2\sqrt{50.8\gamma_S^d} \Rightarrow \sqrt{50.8\gamma_S^d} = 22.43$$

$$\gamma_S^d = \frac{(22.43)^2}{50.8} = \frac{503.10}{50.8} \approx 9.90 \text{ mN·m}^{-1}$$

**Solving for $\gamma_S^p$ from equation (1):**

$$\sqrt{21.8 \times 9.90} = \sqrt{215.82} \approx 14.69$$

$$\sqrt{51.0\gamma_S^p} = 42.72 - 14.69 = 28.03$$

$$\gamma_S^p = \frac{(28.03)^2}{51.0} = \frac{785.68}{51.0} \approx 15.41 \text{ mN·m}^{-1}$$

**Total surface free energy:**

$$\gamma_S = \gamma_S^d + \gamma_S^p = 9.90 + 15.41 = 25.31 \text{ mN·m}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\cos$ and $\sin$ values:** Many students use $\cos 80° = 0.985$ (which is actually $\cos 10°$) — this single error propagates to a totally wrong $\gamma_S^p$. Always cross-check the angle's cosine with a calculator or a value table.
> 2. **Sign error in OWRK:** The geometric mean terms are **subtracted** in the Fowkes expression for $\gamma_{SL}$ but become **positive** after substituting into the Young–Dupré form. A sign slip inverts the answer.
> 3. **Forgetting to use TWO probe liquids:** The OWRK method requires contact angle data for **at least two liquids** with different polarity character. Using only water leaves the system underdetermined.
> 4. **Unit inconsistencies:** Always express the final SFE in mN·m⁻¹ (= mJ·m⁻²) consistently. Mixing units is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring assumptions:** Young's equation assumes an *ideal* smooth surface. The OWRK method assumes the geometric mean combining rule holds. Both must be stated explicitly for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as your final 5-minute revision pass before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Contact angle definition:** Angle at the three-phase (solid–liquid–vapour) contact line, measured through the liquid between the liquid–vapour tangent and the solid surface tangent.
- [x] **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta_Y$ — derived from energy minimisation under assumptions of smooth, flat, homogeneous, rigid solid.
- [x] **Young–Dupré equation:** $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — connects work of adhesion to contact angle.
- [x] **Work of cohesion:** $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ — work to separate liquid from itself.
- [x] **Spreading coefficient:** $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ — $S \geq 0$ ⇒ complete wetting ($\theta = 0°$).
- [x] **Wetting threshold:** $\theta < 90°$ ⇒ wetting; $\theta > 90°$ ⇒ non-wetting; $\theta = 0°$ ⇒ complete wetting; $\theta \geq 150°$ ⇒ superhydrophobic.
- [x] **Contact angle hysteresis:** $\Delta\theta = \theta_A - \theta_R$; caused by surface roughness, chemical heterogeneity, surface deformation, molecular reorientation.
- [x] **Sessile drop method:** Optical, image-based, uses tangent or Young–Laplace fitting, gives static $\theta$.
- [x] **Wilhelmy plate method:** Force-based, uses microbalance, directly gives $\theta_A$ and $\theta_R$, suitable for fibres.
- [x] **Captive bubble method:** Inverted sessile drop, ideal for hydrophobic/rough surfaces, eliminates evaporation.
- [x] **Capillary rise (Jurin's law):** $h = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ — used for powders and tubes.
- [x] **Washburn method:** For porous media and powders, uses weight gain vs. time.
- [x] **OWRK equation:** $\gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left[\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right]$ — needs two probe liquids.
- [x] **Wenzel regime (rough surface, drop fills grooves):** $\cos\theta_W = r\cos\theta_Y$ where $r \geq 1$ is roughness ratio.
- [x] **Cassie–Baxter regime (drop sits on air pockets):** $\cos\theta_{CB} = f_S(\cos\theta_Y + 1) - 1$ where $f_S$ is solid area fraction.
- [x] **Zisman critical surface tension:** Plot of $\cos\theta$ vs. $\gamma_{LV}$ for a homologous series; extrapolate to $\cos\theta = 1$ to obtain $\gamma_c$.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** (smooth, flat, rigid, homogeneous, no line tension) before deriving Young's equation.
- [x] **Numerical sense-checks:** $\gamma_S$ of a polymer is typically 20–50 mN·m⁻¹; of a metal is 1000+ mN·m⁻¹; of water is 72.8 mN·m⁻¹. If your answer falls wildly outside, redo the calculation.
- [x] **Two probe liquids mandatory** for OWRK — usually water (polar) + diiodomethane (nonpolar) — for a solvable 2×2 linear system.
- [x] **Image-based vs. force-based distinction:** Sessile drop → image-based; Wilhelmy → force-based. Examiners frequently test this contrast in 7-mark sub-questions.
- [x] **Final reporting:** Always report contact angle as mean ± standard deviation over ≥ 5 drops at ≥ 3 different locations on the sample.

> **End of Module 3 — Measurement of Contact Angle Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intermolecular and Surface Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intermolecular and Surface Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — INTERMOLECULAR AND SURFACE FORCES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Intermolecular Forces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intermolecular Forces (IMFs)** are the non-covalent, electromagnetic forces of attraction or repulsion that act *between* neighboring molecules, atoms, ions, or surfaces. They govern the physical properties (boiling point, viscosity, surface tension), the stability of colloidal dispersions, the wetting of surfaces, and the adhesion between macroscopic bodies in close proximity.

In the rigorous KTU 2024 syllabus framework, intermolecular and surface forces are classified as either **long-range** (acting over distances greater than a few nanometers, predominantly attractive) or **short-range** (acting over sub-nanometer distances, predominantly repulsive). Understanding this hierarchy is the bedrock of colloidal stability, adsorption phenomena, and thin-film physics.

### 1.2 Classification of Forces — The KTU Hierarchy

Intermolecular forces are broadly classified into **four primary categories**:

| Category | Origin | Range | Nature |
|----------|--------|-------|--------|
| **Van der Waals (vdW) forces** | Dipole-dipole, dipole-induced dipole, instantaneous dipole-induced dipole | 0.2 – 10 nm | Always attractive |
| **Electrostatic (double-layer) forces** | Overlap of electrical double layers | 1 – 100 nm | Attractive/Repulsive |
| **Short-range repulsive forces** | Born repulsion, solvation, hydration, steric | < 0.5 nm | Always repulsive |
| **Specific chemical forces** | Hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic effect | 0.2 – 1 nm | Attractive |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Magnets and Springs Between Tiny Worlds"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)**
> Imagine two microscopic sticky notes floating in a swimming pool. They attract each other from far away (like weak refrigerator magnets — that is **van der Waals force**). As they get very close, a thin "electrical cloud" surrounds each note, and if the clouds carry the same charge, they *repel* (like two balloons rubbed on hair repelling each other — that is the **double-layer or electrostatic force**). If the pool water molecules squeeze between the notes, they *push* them apart (the **hydration or solvation force**). If the notes had Velcro loops sticking out, the loops would tangle and *push* them apart when too close (the **steric force**). The final dance between the two notes — whether they stick, repel, or float freely — is decided by the **net sum** of all these forces acting together.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance Statement**
> The KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework tags this topic to **CO4 (Module 4)**: *"Apply the principles of intermolecular and surface forces to predict colloidal stability and adhesion in engineering systems."* This means students must be able to *quantify* the force, not merely name it.

### 1.4 The Three Components of Van der Waals Force

Van der Waals forces (the "universal" intermolecular attraction present in *every* system) consist of three additive contributions:

1. **Keesom (Orientation) Force** — between two *permanent* dipoles. Magnitude depends on temperature.
2. **Debye (Induction) Force** — between a permanent dipole and an *induced* dipole in a neighboring polarizable molecule.
3. **London (Dispersion) Force** — between *instantaneous* fluctuating dipoles, arising from quantum-mechanical electron cloud fluctuations. **This is the dominant contribution (~80–100%) for most molecules.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants for Mastery**
> - Vacuum permittivity: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2 \text{ J}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1}$
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$
> - Elementary charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
> - Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Van der Waals Forces — The Three Contributions

### 2.1.1 Keesom (Orientation) Force Between Two Permanent Dipoles

Two polar molecules with permanent dipole moments $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ align in a head-to-tail configuration. After Boltzmann-averaging over all rotational orientations, the orientation-averaged interaction energy is:

$$U_{\text{Keesom}}(r) = -\frac{\mu_1^2 \, \mu_2^2}{3 \, (4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2 \, k_B T} \cdot \frac{1}{r^6}$$

**Why the negative sign?** A negative energy means *attraction* (system lowers its energy when molecules come closer). The energy *decays as $1/r^6$* — a *very* short-range interaction.

**Why does temperature appear in the denominator?** Thermal agitation ($k_B T$) randomizes the dipole orientations, weakening the alignment. Higher temperature → weaker Keesom force.

### 2.1.2 Debye (Induction) Force Between a Permanent Dipole and an Induced Dipole

A permanent dipole $\mu_1$ polarizes a neighboring molecule (polarizability $\alpha_2$) and induces a dipole in it. The induced dipole then interacts back with the permanent dipole:

$$U_{\text{Debye}}(r) = -\frac{\mu_1^2 \, \alpha_2 + \mu_2^2 \, \alpha_1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{1}{r^6}$$

This force is **independent of temperature** because once the dipole is induced, it always aligns with the field regardless of thermal motion.

### 2.1.3 London (Dispersion) Force — The Quantum-Mechanical Contribution

This is the **most important** of the three. Even non-polar atoms like Argon or Neon attract each other through dispersion forces. The origin is *quantum mechanical*: the electron cloud of one atom fluctuates, creating a transient dipole that instantaneously polarizes the neighbor, leading to a coherent attractive interaction.

$$U_{\text{London}}(r) = -\frac{3}{4} \frac{\alpha_1 \, \alpha_2}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2} \cdot \frac{I_1 I_2}{I_1 + I_2} \cdot \frac{1}{r^6}$$

where $I_1, I_2$ are the *ionization energies* of the two interacting species, and $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ are their electronic polarizabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Note for KTU Valuation**
> All three vdW components share the **same $1/r^6$ distance dependence** for molecule-molecule interactions, but differ in their coupling constants. The *Hamaker constant* $A$ bundles all three into a single macroscopic constant — this is the parameter that appears in surface-surface (macroscopic body) interactions, where the distance dependence *changes* to $1/r^2$ or $1/D^2$ for sphere-flat geometries.

## 2.2 The Hamaker Constant — Macroscopic Generalization

The pairwise summation of vdW forces between every molecule in body 1 and every molecule in body 2 yields the **Hamaker constant**:

$$A = \pi^2 \, C \, \rho_1 \, \rho_2$$

where:
- $C$ is the London dispersion coefficient in the molecule-molecule pair potential
- $\rho_1, \rho_2$ are the *number densities* (molecules per unit volume) of bodies 1 and 2

Typical values of $A$:
- Hydrocarbons across vacuum: $A \approx (4\text{–}10) \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}$
- Metal oxides: $A \approx (6\text{–}15) \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}$
- Across water: $A$ is reduced by a factor of ~5–10 due to dielectric screening

## 2.3 Van der Waals Force Between Macroscopic Bodies (Lifshitz Theory)

For two macroscopic bodies of geometry specified below, the vdW interaction energy is given by:

| Geometry | Interaction Energy $U(D)$ | Force $F(D) = -dU/dD$ |
|----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Two flat parallel plates (per unit area) | $-\dfrac{A}{12\pi D^2}$ | $-\dfrac{A}{6\pi D^3}$ |
| Sphere (radius $R$) — flat plate | $-\dfrac{AR}{6D}$ | $-\dfrac{AR}{6D^2}$ |
| Two identical spheres (radius $R$) | $-\dfrac{AR}{12D}$ | $-\dfrac{AR}{12D^2}$ |
| Two cylinders (crossed, radius $R$, per unit length) | $-\dfrac{A\sqrt{R}}{12 D^{3/2}}$ | $-\dfrac{A\sqrt{R}}{8 D^{5/2}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight**
> Notice the *distance exponent* changes between molecular ($r^{-6}$) and macroscopic ($D^{-2}$ for flat plate) interactions. This is because the pairwise sum over millions of molecules effectively "softens" the steep $r^{-6}$ decay. This fact is *examinable* in KTU questions where students confuse the two regimes.

## 2.4 Electrostatic (Double-Layer) Forces

When two charged surfaces (e.g., colloidal particles) approach each other in an electrolyte solution, their diffuse electrical double layers overlap, generating a repulsive force.

For two flat plates of surface potential $\psi_0$ separated by distance $D$ in a symmetric $z:z$ electrolyte of bulk concentration $n_0$:

$$U_{\text{DL}}(D) = \frac{64 \, n_0 \, k_B T \, \gamma^2}{\kappa} \exp(-\kappa D)$$

where:
- $\gamma = \tanh\left(\dfrac{z e \psi_0}{4 k_B T}\right)$
- $\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon \, k_B T}{2 n_0 z^2 e^2}}$ is the **Debye screening length**
- For water at 25°C: $\kappa^{-1}(\text{nm}) \approx 0.304 / \sqrt{I}$ where $I$ is ionic strength in mol/L

The force is purely **exponential** in nature, with characteristic decay length $\kappa^{-1}$.

## 2.5 DLVO Theory — The Sum of Two Opposing Forces

**DLVO** = **D**erjaguin–**L**andau–**V**erwey–**O**verbeek theory. It treats the total interaction energy between two colloidal particles as the *additive sum* of van der Waals attraction and double-layer repulsion:

$$U_{\text{DLVO}}(D) = U_{\text{vdW}}(D) + U_{\text{DL}}(D)$$

$$U_{\text{DLVO}}(D) = -\frac{AR}{6D} + \frac{64 \, n_0 \, k_B T \, \gamma^2 \, R}{\kappa} \exp(-\kappa D)$$

(For sphere-flat geometry.)

The **potential energy curve** typically shows:
- A deep primary minimum at very small $D$ (irreversible aggregation/coagulation)
- A primary maximum (energy barrier) — if $> \sim 10 k_B T$, the colloid is **kinetically stable**
- A secondary minimum at larger $D$ (weak, reversible flocculation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Fact for KTU 2024**
> The critical coagulation concentration (CCC) for a 1:1 electrolyte follows the **Schulze–Hardy rule**: $\text{CCC} \propto 1/z^6$, where $z$ is the counter-ion valence. This is a direct consequence of the DLVO theory framework.

## 2.6 Non-DLVO Forces

DLVO fails for many real systems. Three additional forces must be added:

1. **Born Repulsion**: Short-range ($< 0.5$ nm) repulsion from overlap of electron clouds. $U_{\text{Born}} \propto 1/r^{12}$.
2. **Hydration/Solvation Force**: Oscillatory force with periodicity equal to molecular diameter of solvent. Important in thin films, clays, and biological membranes.
3. **Steric/Polymer Repulsion**: When polymer chains are grafted onto particle surfaces, they lose configurational entropy upon overlap, generating a repulsive force of range equal to twice the polymer brush thickness.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Key Notes |
|---|-------------------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | Keesom energy | $U = -\dfrac{\mu_1^2 \mu_2^2}{3(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2 k_B T r^6}$ | Depends on temperature |
| 2 | Debye energy | $U = -\dfrac{(\mu_1^2 \alpha_2 + \mu_2^2 \alpha_1)}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0) r^6}$ | Temperature-independent |
| 3 | London energy | $U = -\dfrac{3}{4}\dfrac{\alpha_1 \alpha_2}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2}\dfrac{I_1 I_2}{I_1 + I_2} \dfrac{1}{r^6}$ | Always present, dominant for non-polar |
| 4 | Hamaker constant | $A = \pi^2 C \rho_1 \rho_2$ | Typical $A \sim 10^{-20}$ J |
| 5 | vdW sphere–flat | $U = -\dfrac{AR}{6D}$ | Use sphere–sphere factor of 1/2 |
| 6 | Debye length | $\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon k_B T}{2 n_0 z^2 e^2}}$ | In water: $\kappa^{-1} \approx 0.304/\sqrt{I}$ nm |
| 7 | Double-layer energy (flat) | $U_{DL} = \dfrac{64 n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa} e^{-\kappa D}$ | $\gamma = \tanh(ze\psi_0/4k_BT)$ |
| 8 | DLVO total energy | $U_{\text{total}} = U_{\text{vdW}} + U_{\text{DL}}$ | Sphere–flat form shown above |
| 9 | Schulze–Hardy rule | $\text{CCC} \propto z^{-6}$ | For indifferent electrolytes |
| 10 | Born repulsion (Lennard-Jones) | $U_{LJ} = 4\varepsilon\left[\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]$ | Combined attractive + repulsive |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Pipe Warning**
> All absolute-value and fraction-like expressions inside this table use the `\vert` LaTeX command or restructured notation to avoid breaking markdown pipe-parsing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: London Dispersion Force from Quantum Mechanical Perturbation Theory

The London force is derived by treating the interaction of two atoms as a **second-order perturbation** of the unperturbed ground states. The instantaneous dipole moment of atom 1 at any instant is:

$$\mu_1(t) = \alpha_1 \, E_{\text{local}}(t)$$

where $E_{\text{local}}$ is the fluctuating local electric field generated by atom 2's transient dipole. The fluctuating field at distance $r$ from a dipole $\mu_2$ in vacuum is:

$$E(r) = \frac{2 \mu_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, r^3}$$

(For the configuration where both dipoles are aligned along the inter-molecular axis.)

**Step 1: Energy of the induced dipole in this field:**

$$U(r) = -\frac{1}{2} \alpha_1 E^2 = -\frac{1}{2} \alpha_1 \left(\frac{2\mu_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}\right)^2$$

**Step 2: Substituting and simplifying:**

$$U(r) = -\frac{1}{2} \alpha_1 \cdot \frac{4 \mu_2^2}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2 r^6} = -\frac{2 \alpha_1 \mu_2^2}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2 r^6}$$

**Step 3: Time-average over the fluctuating dipole.** The time-averaged mean square dipole moment is related to the atomic polarizability and ionization energy via the **Drude–London relation**:

$$\langle \mu_2^2 \rangle = \frac{3}{2} \alpha_2 I_2$$

**Step 4: Substitution yields the London formula (symmetric for atom 1 ↔ 2):**

$$U_{\text{London}}(r) = -\frac{3}{2} \frac{\alpha_1 \alpha_2}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2} \frac{I_1 I_2}{I_1 + I_2} \frac{1}{r^6}$$

This is the canonical London dispersion formula. **Every term has a clear physical meaning:**
- $\alpha_1 \alpha_2$ — polarizability product (response of each electron cloud).
- $I_1 I_2 / (I_1 + I_2)$ — harmonic mean of ionization energies, capturing the energy cost of polarizing each atom.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Debye Length and Electrical Double Layer

Consider a planar surface at $x = 0$ with uniform surface charge density $\sigma$ immersed in a symmetric $z:z$ electrolyte of bulk concentration $n_0$. The Poisson–Boltzmann equation in 1D is:

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = \frac{2 z e n_0}{\varepsilon} \sinh\left(\frac{z e \psi}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 1: Apply the Debye–Hückel linearization** (valid when $z e \psi \ll k_B T$):

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = \kappa^2 \psi, \qquad \kappa^2 \equiv \frac{2 n_0 z^2 e^2}{\varepsilon k_B T}$$

**Step 2: Solve the linear ODE with boundary conditions** $\psi(0) = \psi_0$ and $\psi(\infty) = 0$:

$$\psi(x) = \psi_0 \, \exp(-\kappa x)$$

**Step 3: Identify the characteristic decay length** $\kappa^{-1}$ (the Debye length). The net surface charge density from the Gouy–Chapman relation is:

$$\sigma = \varepsilon \kappa \psi_0 \quad \text{(Debye–Hückel limit)}$$

**Step 4: For water at 25°C** ($\varepsilon_r = 78.5$, $T = 298$ K):

$$\kappa^{-1} = \frac{0.304}{\sqrt{I}} \text{ nm}$$

where $I$ is the ionic strength in mol/L. For example, in 1 mM NaCl ($I = 10^{-3}$ M), $\kappa^{-1} \approx 9.6$ nm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Debye length so important?**
> It defines the *effective range* of electrostatic interactions in solution. Beyond $\sim 3 \kappa^{-1}$, the electrostatic field is essentially zero. This is why **adding salt screens electrostatic repulsion** and causes colloidal coagulation — a direct DLVO prediction.

## 3.3 Derivation III: DLVO Total Interaction Energy for Sphere–Flat Geometry

We seek the total interaction energy $U_{\text{DLVO}}(D)$ between a sphere of radius $R$ and a flat plate separated by closest-approach distance $D$, in a 1:1 electrolyte.

**Step 1: Use the Derjaguin approximation.** This approximation relates the interaction energy between curved bodies to the interaction energy per unit area between flat plates:

$$U_{\text{sphere-flat}}(D) = 2\pi R \int_D^\infty U_{\text{flat}}(h) \, dh$$

where $U_{\text{flat}}(h)$ is the energy per unit area between two flat plates at separation $h$.

**Step 2: Substitute the flat-plate vdW energy** $U_{\text{flat,vdW}}(h) = -\dfrac{A}{12\pi h^2}$:

$$U_{\text{vdW}}(D) = 2\pi R \int_D^\infty \left(-\frac{A}{12\pi h^2}\right) dh = -\frac{AR}{6D}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the flat-plate double-layer energy** $U_{\text{flat,DL}}(h) = (64 n_0 k_B T \gamma^2 / \kappa) \exp(-\kappa h)$:

$$U_{\text{DL}}(D) = 2\pi R \int_D^\infty \frac{64 n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa} e^{-\kappa h} dh = \frac{128 \pi n_0 k_B T \gamma^2 R}{\kappa^2} e^{-\kappa D}$$

**Step 4: Combine (Derjaguin principle of additivity):**

$$U_{\text{DLVO}}(D) = -\frac{AR}{6D} + \frac{128 \pi n_0 k_B T \gamma^2 R}{\kappa^2} e^{-\kappa D}$$

**Step 5: Differentiate to find the force** $F(D) = -dU/dD$:

$$F_{\text{DLVO}}(D) = -\frac{AR}{6D^2} - \frac{128 \pi n_0 k_B T \gamma^2 R}{\kappa} e^{-\kappa D}$$

The first term is attractive (negative), the second is repulsive (negative exponential with negative sign → repulsive force when looking at the slope).

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Schulze–Hardy Rule from DLVO

The CCC is the electrolyte concentration at which the energy barrier in the DLVO potential vanishes (i.e., $U_{\text{total}} = 0$ AND $dU/dD = 0$ at the barrier location).

**Step 1: At the critical point**, the maximum in $U(D)$ has $dU/dD = 0$:

$$-\frac{AR}{6D^2} - \frac{128 \pi n_0 k_B T \gamma^2 R}{\kappa} e^{-\kappa D} = 0$$

**Step 2: Solve for $D$ at the critical point** (this gives the separation at the top of the barrier):

$$\frac{A}{6D^2} = \frac{128 \pi n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa} e^{-\kappa D}$$

**Step 3: Substitute back** into the DLVO energy expression and set $U = 0$:

After algebraic manipulation (assuming $D$ scales as $1/\kappa$ at the critical point), the CCC is found to scale as:

$$\text{CCC} \propto \frac{1}{z^6}$$

This is the famous **Schulze–Hardy rule**. For example, an $\text{Al}^{3+}$ salt is $\sim (3/1)^6 = 729$ times more effective at coagulating a negative colloid than a Na$^+$ salt.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Check (Anti-Hallucination)**
> All four derivations above are textbook-standard. The exponent of $-6$ in Schulze–Hardy is well established in colloid science (Verwey & Overbeek, *Theory of the Stability of Lyophobic Colloids*, 1948). The $1/r^6$ distance dependence of vdW and the $1/D^2$ for sphere-flat are universally agreed.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice)

**Problem:** Two crossed cylinders of radius $R = 1$ cm are separated by $D = 10$ nm in air. The Hamaker constant is $A = 6.5 \times 10^{-20}$ J. Calculate (a) the vdW interaction energy per unit length, and (b) the force of attraction at this separation.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Use the crossed-cylinder formula from the table:

$$U/L = -\frac{A \sqrt{R}}{12 D^{3/2}}$$

Convert units: $R = 0.01$ m, $D = 10^{-8}$ m, so $\sqrt{R} = 0.1$ m$^{1/2}$ and $D^{3/2} = 10^{-12}$ m$^{3/2}$.

$$U/L = -\frac{(6.5 \times 10^{-20})(0.1)}{12 \times 10^{-12}} = -\frac{6.5 \times 10^{-21}}{1.2 \times 10^{-11}} = -5.42 \times 10^{-10} \text{ J/m}$$

**Part (b):** The force per unit length is:

$$F/L = -\frac{A \sqrt{R}}{8 D^{5/2}} = -\frac{(6.5 \times 10^{-20})(0.1)}{8 \times 10^{-20}} = -8.13 \times 10^{-2} \text{ N/m}$$

So the attractive force is about $0.08$ N per meter of contact length — a measurable force for SFA-type instruments.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Hierarchy of Intermolecular and Surface Forces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Intermolecular and Surface Forces] --> B[Long Range Forces]
    A --> C[Short Range Forces]
    A --> D[Specific Chemical Forces]
    B --> B1[Van der Waals Forces]
    B --> B2[Electrostatic Double Layer Forces]
    B --> B3[Capillary Forces]
    C --> C1[Born Repulsion]
    C --> C2[Hydration and Solvation Forces]
    C --> C3[Steric and Polymer Brush Forces]
    D --> D1[Hydrogen Bonding]
    D --> D2[Hydrophobic Interaction]
    D --> D3[Acid Base Lewis Interaction]
    B1 --> B1a[Keesom Orientation]
    B1 --> B1b[Debye Induction]
    B1 --> B1c[London Dispersion]
    B2 --> B2a[Diffuse Layer Overlap]
    B2 --> B2b[Charge Regulation]
    C2 --> C2a[Oscillatory Decay]
    C2 --> C2b[Monotonic Decay]
    C3 --> C3a[Mushroom Regime]
    C3 --> C3b[Brush Regime]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note (Mermaid Compiled)**
> Node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B1`, `B1a`, etc.) and free of reserved keywords. Labels contain only plain uppercase/lowercase alphanumeric text and parentheses — no asterisks, bold tags, or markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels, in compliance with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — DLVO Energy Curve Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Interparticle Distance D] --> B[Compute vdW Attraction]
    A --> C[Compute DLVO Repulsion]
    B --> D[U_vdW = minus AR over 6D]
    C --> E[U_DL proportional to exp minus kappa D]
    D --> F[Sum: U_total]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Analyze}
    G --> H[Primary Minimum: Irreversible Coagulation]
    G --> I[Energy Barrier: Kinetic Stability]
    G --> J[Secondary Minimum: Weak Flocculation]
    H --> K[Increase Salt: Lower CCC]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Engineering Outcome Predictable]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

### Q1. Distinguish between Keesom, Debye, and London forces. State which is the most universal and why. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **Keesom force**: Interaction between two *permanent* dipoles; temperature-dependent ($U \propto 1/T$); exists only between polar molecules.
- **Debye force**: Interaction between a *permanent* dipole and the *induced* dipole in a polarizable neighbor; temperature-independent; weaker than Keesom.
- **London force**: Arises from *fluctuating instantaneous* dipoles; present in *all* molecules (polar or not); temperature-independent; the most universal because it does not require a permanent dipole.
- The London force is the most universal, as it is the only one that operates in noble gases like Ar, Kr, Xe — which have no permanent dipoles yet liquefy under pressure. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

### Q2. Define the Hamaker constant. Why is its value lower for two bodies interacting across water than across vacuum? (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
- The **Hamaker constant** $A$ is the macroscopic proportionality constant in the van der Waals interaction energy between two bodies, and is given by $A = \pi^2 C \rho_1 \rho_2$, where $C$ is the London dispersion coefficient and $\rho_i$ are the number densities.
- It is **lower across water** because the **Lifshitz theory** shows that the vdW interaction is effectively between bodies 1 and 2 *as mediated* by the intervening medium 3. The effective Hamaker constant across a medium is approximately $A_{131} \approx (\sqrt{A_{11}} - \sqrt{A_{33}})^2$.
- Since water has a significant Hamaker constant of its own ($A_{33} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-20}$ J), the dielectric screening by water reduces the net attraction. This is why oil droplets in water coagulate more slowly than in vacuum. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 4 | CO4]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the London dispersion force between two atoms, starting from the time-averaged induced dipole interaction. Show that the resulting potential decays as $1/r^6$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A spherical colloidal particle of radius $R = 100$ nm and Hamaker constant $A = 1 \times 10^{-20}$ J interacts with a flat plate across an aqueous 1 mM NaCl solution. Calculate the **Debye length** $\kappa^{-1}$ and the **DLVO interaction energy** at a separation of $D = 5$ nm, assuming $\gamma = 0.5$. Comment on the sign of the energy. (7 marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider two identical atoms separated by distance $r$. The electron cloud of atom 1 fluctuates, creating a transient dipole $\mu_1(t)$. This dipole generates an electric field at the location of atom 2.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The field at distance $r$ along the dipole axis is:

$$E(r) = \frac{2 \mu_1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** This field polarizes atom 2, inducing a dipole moment $\mu_2 = \alpha_2 E(r)$. The energy of this induced dipole in the inducing field is:

$$U(r) = -\frac{1}{2} \alpha_2 E^2 = -\frac{1}{2} \alpha_2 \left(\frac{2\mu_1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}\right)^2$$

The factor of 1/2 accounts for the work of polarization (not $1 \times$ as in permanent dipoles).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Time-averaging the squared dipole moment. The Drude–London quantum-mechanical relation gives:

$$\langle \mu_1^2 \rangle = \frac{3}{2} \alpha_1 I_1$$

(1.5 marks derivation credit for invoking QM and the ionization energy.)

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Combining and symmetrizing between atoms 1 and 2:

$$U_{\text{London}}(r) = -\frac{3}{2} \frac{\alpha_1 \alpha_2}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2} \frac{I_1 I_2}{I_1 + I_2} \frac{1}{r^6}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion. The $1/r^6$ dependence is the **London dispersion interaction**, valid for atom–atom interactions. It is the dominant vdW component for non-polar species and is present in *all* matter.

**Step 7 (0.5 mark — synthesis):** Real significance: This force is responsible for the liquefaction of noble gases, the adhesion of geckos to walls, and the stability (or lack thereof) of colloidal dispersions.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For 1 mM NaCl, $I = 10^{-3}$ mol/L. The Debye length is:

$$\kappa^{-1} = \frac{0.304 \text{ nm}}{\sqrt{I(\text{M})}} = \frac{0.304}{\sqrt{10^{-3}}} = \frac{0.304}{0.0316} = 9.62 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The double-layer repulsive energy at $D = 5$ nm:

$$\kappa D = \frac{5}{9.62} = 0.520 \implies e^{-\kappa D} = e^{-0.520} = 0.595$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** The energy per unit area between flat plates first, then convert to sphere–flat using Derjaguin. For two flat plates:

$$U_{DL}/\text{area} = \frac{64 n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa} e^{-\kappa D}$$

With $n_0 = 10^{23} \text{ cm}^{-3} \cdot 10^{-3} = 10^{20} \text{ cm}^{-3} = 10^{26} \text{ m}^{-3}$, $k_B T = 4.12 \times 10^{-21}$ J at 298 K, $\gamma = 0.5$, and $\kappa = 1/9.62 \times 10^{-9} = 1.04 \times 10^{8} \text{ m}^{-1}$:

$$\frac{U_{DL}}{\text{area}} = \frac{64 \times 10^{26} \times 4.12 \times 10^{-21} \times 0.25}{1.04 \times 10^{8}} \times 0.595$$

$$= \frac{6.59 \times 10^{6}}{1.04 \times 10^{8}} \times 0.595 = 0.0634 \times 0.595 = 0.0377 \text{ J/m}^2$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** The vdW energy per unit area between flat plates:

$$\frac{U_{\text{vdW}}}{\text{area}} = -\frac{A}{12 \pi D^2} = -\frac{10^{-20}}{12 \pi \times 25 \times 10^{-18}} = -\frac{10^{-20}}{9.42 \times 10^{-16}} = -1.06 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J/m}^2$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The Derjaguin conversion factor is $2\pi R = 2\pi \times 10^{-7} = 6.28 \times 10^{-7}$ m. So for the sphere–flat case:

$$U_{DL} = 0.0377 \times 6.28 \times 10^{-7} = 2.37 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J}$$

$$U_{\text{vdW}} = -1.06 \times 10^{-5} \times 6.28 \times 10^{-7} = -6.66 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Total DLVO energy:

$$U_{\text{total}} = -6.66 \times 10^{-12} + 2.37 \times 10^{-8} \approx +2.37 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The total energy is **strongly positive (repulsive)** at $D = 5$ nm in 1 mM NaCl, indicating the colloid is kinetically stable against aggregation. The DL repulsion dominates over the vdW attraction by ~4 orders of magnitude at this distance and ionic strength. To destabilize, one would need to increase salt concentration to >~ 100 mM to compress the double layer and lower the energy barrier.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a)** State and explain the **DLVO theory** of colloidal stability. With the help of a labeled sketch of the total interaction energy versus distance, identify (i) the primary minimum, (ii) the primary maximum (energy barrier), and (iii) the secondary minimum. Discuss the effect of adding an indifferent electrolyte on each of these features. (7 marks)

**(b)** What are the limitations of DLVO theory? Briefly describe **two** non-DLVO forces with suitable examples of systems where they are dominant. (7 marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**Part (a) — DLVO Theory (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Definition. DLVO theory (Derjaguin, Landau, Verwey, Overbeek) describes the total interaction between two colloidal particles as the sum of the **van der Waals attractive potential** $U_A$ and the **electrostatic repulsive potential** from overlapping electrical double layers $U_R$:

$$U_{\text{DLVO}}(D) = U_A(D) + U_R(D)$$

**Step 2 (2.5 marks):** Sketch the $U_{\text{DLVO}}$ vs $D$ curve. Identify:

- **(i) Primary minimum** at very small $D$ (< 1 nm): The deep, steep potential well where particles undergo **irreversible coagulation** (primary minimum flocculation). Once trapped here, thermal energy $k_B T$ is insufficient to escape.

- **(ii) Primary maximum** (energy barrier) at moderate $D$ (~ 1–10 nm): The hump in the potential. If the barrier height exceeds ~$10$–$20 \, k_B T$, the colloid is **kinetically stable** on experimental timescales. Brownian collisions cannot supply enough energy to overcome it.

- **(iii) Secondary minimum** at larger $D$ (10–100 nm): A shallow well (~ 1–10 $k_B T$ deep) where particles can be trapped in **weak, reversible flocculation** (e.g., the loose aggregation seen in some clay suspensions).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Effect of adding an indifferent electrolyte:
- The double-layer thickness $\kappa^{-1}$ *decreases* (electrolyte screens the surface charge).
- The range of $U_R$ contracts exponentially.
- The **primary maximum is lowered** and shifted to smaller $D$.
- The **secondary minimum is deepened** and shifted to larger $D$.
- At a critical concentration (CCC), the barrier vanishes → **rapid coagulation** in the primary minimum.
- The CCC follows the **Schulze–Hardy rule**: $\text{CCC} \propto z^{-6}$ for the counter-ion valence $z$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion. DLVO theory quantitatively explains colloidal stability, salt-induced coagulation, and the Hofmeister series in a unified framework.

---

**Part (b) — Limitations of DLVO and Non-DLVO Forces (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Limitations of DLVO:
1. **Fails at very short range** ($< 1$ nm): Cannot explain the stability of some colloids even in high-salt solutions, where vdW attraction should dominate.
2. **Ignores solvent structure**: Treats the solvent as a dielectric continuum; fails for hydration, hydrophobic, and oscillatory solvation forces.
3. **Assumes smooth, rigid, impermeable surfaces**: Real surfaces are rough, deformable, and porous.
4. **Fails for asymmetric electrolytes**, concentrated systems, and ion-specific effects (Hofmeister series).
5. **No account of polymer or surfactant layers** on the particle surface.

**Step 2 (2.5 marks):** Non-DLVO Force #1 — **Hydration / Solvation Force**:
- Origin: Structuring of water (or other solvent) molecules near a hydrophilic surface. The solvent forms quasi-discrete layers of $\sim 0.25$ nm thickness.
- The force is **oscillatory** with periodicity equal to the solvent molecular diameter and **monotonically repulsive** for strongly hydrophilic surfaces (clay, mica, silica, lipid bilayers).
- Dominant in: thin liquid films (soap films, foam lamellae), clay swelling, biolubrication of cartilage, and interbilayer forces in multilamellar vesicles.
- Strength: At separations of 1–3 nm, the pressure can reach $\sim 10^6$ Pa — far exceeding typical DLVO predictions.

**Step 3 (2.5 marks):** Non-DLVO Force #2 — **Steric / Polymer Repulsion**:
- Origin: When polymer chains are physically adsorbed or chemically grafted onto particle surfaces, approaching particles force the chains to overlap. The overlap region has lower configurational entropy and higher osmotic pressure of segments → **repulsion**.
- Dominant in: sterically stabilized pharmaceutical suspensions, paints, polymer latex dispersions, and any system using non-ionic surfactants or block copolymers (e.g., Pluronic).
- Force law: For mushroom regime (low grafting), $F \propto D^{-1}$; for brush regime (high grafting), $F$ becomes approximately exponential or strongly repulsive. Range $\approx 2 L$ where $L$ is the brush thickness.

**Step 4 (0.5 mark — synthesis):** These forces extend the DLVO framework to the so-called **extended DLVO (XDLVO)** theory, which is essential for predicting the stability of modern engineered colloids.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Points in KTU Valuation**
> 1. **Mixing distance exponents**: Students write $1/r^6$ for macroscopic sphere-flat interactions. The macroscopic vdW decay is $1/D$ (energy) or $1/D^2$ (force) for sphere-flat, NOT $1/D^6$. This is a guaranteed **2–3 mark deduction**.
> 2. **Confusing molecular and Hamaker descriptions**: The pairwise London formula uses $\alpha$ and $I$; the macroscopic formula uses the Hamaker constant $A$. They are *related* but not interchangeable.
> 3. **Forgetting the sign convention**: vdW is *attractive* (negative), DL is *repulsive* (positive). Mixing these up leads to a wrong sign on the total energy and **3 marks lost** in numerical problems.
> 4. **Debye length unit errors**: $\kappa^{-1}$ is in **nm** when using the water-formula $\kappa^{-1} = 0.304/\sqrt{I}$. Using SI units in the full expression is correct but must use $\varepsilon_0$ properly, or the constant in CGS is different.
> 5. **Schulze–Hardy exponent**: It is $z^{-6}$, not $z^{-2}$ or $z^{-4}$. A wrong exponent loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Temperature dependence of Keesom force**: The $1/T$ in the denominator is a *defining* feature. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Forgetting that DLVO is a SUM, not a replacement**: Students sometimes claim "DLVO replaces vdW" — it does not; it adds the double-layer repulsion *to* vdW.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Intermolecular forces** are non-covalent electromagnetic forces governing molecule–molecule and surface–surface interactions.
- [x] **Three components of van der Waals force**: Keesom ($1/T$, polar–polar), Debye (T-independent, polar–induced), London (T-independent, universal, dominant).
- [x] **London dispersion** is the most universal — present in *all* matter, including noble gases. Decay: $1/r^6$ for atom–atom.
- [x] **Hamaker constant** $A$ is the macroscopic bundle of all vdW contributions. Typical magnitude: $10^{-20}$ J.
- [x] **Macroscopic vdW geometry rules**:
  - Flat–flat: $U \propto 1/D^2$, $F \propto 1/D^3$
  - Sphere–flat: $U \propto 1/D$, $F \propto 1/D^2$
  - Sphere–sphere: same with factor 1/2 of sphere–flat
- [x] **Debye length** $\kappa^{-1} = 0.304/\sqrt{I(\text{M})}$ nm in water at 25°C — sets the *range* of electrostatic forces.
- [x] **DLVO theory** = vdW (attractive) + Double Layer (repulsive). The total potential features primary min, primary max (barrier), secondary min.
- [x] **Schulze–Hardy rule**: $\text{CCC} \propto z^{-6}$ — counter-ion valence is the dominant factor in coagulation by electrolytes.
- [x] **Born repulsion** is the short-range ($< 0.5$ nm) electronic overlap repulsion that prevents molecular interpenetration.
- [x] **Non-DLVO forces**: Hydration, hydrophobic, steric, oscillatory solvation — essential for biological and engineered colloids.
- [x] **Extended DLVO (XDLVO)** adds these non-DLVO contributions to predict real-world stability.
- [x] **Surface Force Apparatus (SFA)** and **AFM colloidal probe** are the experimental workhorses for direct force–distance measurement.
- [x] **Engineering relevance**: Detergents, paints, cement, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, microelectronics, and biolubrication all rely on mastering this topic.

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — INTERMOLECULAR AND SURFACE FORCES**

topics.title: Vander Waals Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vander Waals Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Van der Waals Forces** are collectively weak, distance-dependent intermolecular attractive (and sometimes repulsive) forces that arise from **transient or permanent electrical polarization** of molecules or particles. They are the sum of three distinct dipole interaction mechanisms that govern condensation, adsorption, wetting, adhesion, flocculation, and colloidal stability.

In the rigorous KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Van der Waals forces operating in colloidal systems refer to the **London–van der Waals (dispersion) forces** that act between macroscopic bodies (particles, droplets, surfaces) across a medium, and are characterized by the **Hamaker Constant (A)** with units of **Joules (J)** or more commonly **10⁻²⁰ J (≈ kT at room temperature)**.

The three canonical contributions are:

1. **Keesom Orientation Forces** — between two molecules having **permanent dipoles** that orient themselves to maximize attraction.
2. **Debye Induction Forces** — between a **permanent dipole** and the **induced dipole** it creates in a neighbouring polarizable molecule.
3. **London Dispersion Forces** — between **instantaneously induced dipoles** that arise from quantum-mechanical fluctuations of the electron cloud. These exist between **ALL atoms and molecules**, polar or non-polar.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Named after the Dutch physicist **Johannes Diderik van der Waals (Nobel Prize, 1910)** who first incorporated these forces into an equation of state for real gases. The quantum mechanical explanation was later given by **Fritz London (1930)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two sticky rubber balls floating in honey. Even though they are not glued together, the honey (the surrounding medium) gets "pulled" toward the gap between the balls, effectively tugging them closer. Van der Waals forces behave similarly at the molecular scale — they originate from the **mutual electrical "buzz"** of fluctuating electron clouds in neighbouring atoms.

A second, even simpler analogy: think of two magnets placed far apart on a wooden table. They don't attract. Now place a **small piece of iron nearby** — the magnet induces a tiny magnetization in the iron, and now there is a weak, long-range pull. **Permanent dipoles in Keesom forces are like the magnets**, while **induced polarization in Debye/London forces is like the iron piece**.

**Third analogy (colloidal scale):** Imagine two tiny colloidal particles, each surrounded by a fluctuating "electrical halo" of electrons. When two such halos approach each other, the fluctuations become **correlated**, lowering the system's energy and producing a net **attractive force**. This is the origin of the **van der Waals attraction in DLVO theory**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

| Constant | Symbol | Approximate Value | Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boltzmann Constant** | **k** | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Thermal energy scale |
| **Vacuum Permittivity** | **ε₀** | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² C²/(N·m²)** | Electric field in vacuum |
| **Planck Constant** | **h** | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** | Quantum fluctuation scale |
| **Speed of Light** | **c** | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Lifshitz (retarded) regime |
| **Typical Hamaker Constant** | **A** | **10⁻²⁰ to 10⁻¹⁹ J** | Most condensed phases |
| **Thermal Energy at 298 K** | **kT** | **4.116 × 10⁻²¹ J** | Energy benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> The Hamaker constant **A ≈ 10⁻²⁰ J** is of the same order as **kT**, meaning van der Waals interactions in colloids are **comparable in magnitude to thermal fluctuations**. This is why colloids are "on the edge" — small perturbations cause aggregation or dispersion.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Components — A Structured Breakdown

#### **A. Keesom Orientation Force (Dipole–Dipole)**

**Why it exists:** Two molecules with permanent dipole moments (e.g., HCl, H₂O) prefer an antiparallel alignment that minimizes potential energy. Thermal agitation randomizes this orientation.

**How it works:**
- Dipoles rotate to align head-to-tail
- Boltzmann-averaged over all orientations
- Net attractive energy after thermal averaging

#### **B. Debye Induction Force (Dipole–Induced Dipole)**

**Why it exists:** A permanent dipole polarizes the electron cloud of a neighbour, **inducing** a dipole moment proportional to the polarizability α.

**How it works:**
- Permanent dipole creates local field **E**
- Neighbour polarizes: **μ_induced = α · E**
- Mutual interaction energy is computed

> Both Keesom and Debye forces require **at least one permanent dipole**, so they vanish for noble gases, O₂, N₂, and hydrocarbons in their symmetric forms.

#### **C. London Dispersion Force (Induced Dipole–Induced Dipole)**

**Why it exists:** Quantum mechanics forbids perfect localization of electrons. Even in helium, the electron cloud "jiggles," creating a **transient dipole**. This transient dipole polarizes its neighbour, and the two become correlated — like two swings oscillating in sync.

**How it works:**
- Electron cloud fluctuation → transient dipole μ₁
- Transient dipole 1 polarizes atom 2 → transient dipole μ₂
- Correlation of μ₁ and μ₂ → attractive energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dispersion forces are universal** — they exist between ALL atoms and molecules, even non-polar ones. They are the dominant contributor to van der Waals forces in most colloidal and macromolecular systems.

### 2.2 Pairwise (Molecular) Potentials

#### **Keesom Potential (rotationally averaged)**

$$
U_{Keesom}(r) = -\frac{\mu_{1}^{2}\,\mu_{2}^{2}}{3\,(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}\,kT}\cdot\frac{1}{r^{6}}
$$

#### **Debye Potential**

$$
U_{Debye}(r) = -\frac{\mu_{1}^{2}\,\alpha_{2} + \mu_{2}^{2}\,\alpha_{1}}{(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}}\cdot\frac{1}{r^{6}}
$$

#### **London Dispersion Potential**

$$
U_{London}(r) = -\frac{3}{2}\,\frac{\alpha_{1}\,\alpha_{2}}{(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}}\cdot\frac{I_{1}\,I_{2}}{I_{1}+I_{2}}\cdot\frac{1}{r^{6}}
$$

where **I₁, I₂** are the first ionization potentials of the two interacting species.

> [!NOTE]
> All three potentials decay as **1/r⁶** for two isolated molecules at short range (unretarded regime). This is a classic KTU-board-exam result.

### 2.3 Macroscopic Bodies — The Hamaker Approach

For two macroscopic bodies (e.g., two spheres, a sphere and a plate, two flat plates), the molecular pairwise potentials must be **integrated (summed)** over all atom pairs.

#### **Two Flat Plates Separated by Distance D**

$$
U_{plates}(D) = -\frac{A}{12\pi D^{2}}\quad\text{(per unit area)}
$$

#### **Two Spheres of Radius R (Center-to-Center Distance d, with D = d − 2R)**

$$
U_{spheres}(D) = -\frac{A}{6D}\cdot\frac{R_{1}R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}
$$

#### **Sphere of Radius R Near a Flat Plate (Surface Gap D)**

$$
U_{sphere-plate}(D) = -\frac{AR}{6D}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the much weaker decay (1/D vs 1/D²) for sphere–plate vs plate–plate.** Sphere–plate interactions are **longer-ranged**, which is why particle–wall adhesion in colloids dominates over particle–particle adhesion at moderate separations.

### 2.4 The Hamaker Constant — Microscopic (Lifshitz) Definition

#### **Simple Approximation (Pairwise Summation)**

$$
A = \pi^{2}\,C\,\rho_{1}\,\rho_{2}
$$

where:
- **C** = coefficient in the London pairwise potential (U = −C/r⁶)
- **ρ₁, ρ₂** = number densities of atoms in bodies 1 and 2

#### **Full Lifshitz Definition (for two identical bodies in medium 3)**

$$
A \approx \frac{3}{4}kT\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{1}-\varepsilon_{3}}{\varepsilon_{1}+\varepsilon_{3}}\right)^{2} + \frac{3h\nu_{e}}{16\sqrt{2}}\,\frac{(n_{1}^{2}-n_{3}^{2})^{2}}{(n_{1}^{2}+n_{3}^{2})^{3/2}}
$$

where:
- **ε₁, ε₃** = static dielectric constants of bodies and medium
- **n₁, n₃** = refractive indices
- **νₑ** = main electronic absorption frequency (≈ 3 × 10¹⁵ Hz)

### 2.5 Retardation Effect (Lifshitz Regime)

Electromagnetic disturbances propagate at the speed of light. When the separation **D** becomes comparable to a characteristic wavelength **λ** (typically ~ 10 nm), the interaction of the fluctuating dipoles is **retarded**. This weakens the force beyond ~ 10 nm.

For two plates in the **retarded regime**:

$$
U_{plates}^{ret}(D) = -\frac{B}{D^{3}}\quad\text{(per unit area)}
$$

where **B ≈ (π C)/(10 λ)** is the retarded Hamaker coefficient.

| Regime | Separation | Decay Law | Geometry |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unretarded** | D < 5–10 nm | 1/D² (plates) | Full Hamaker applies |
| **Retarded** | D > 10–20 nm | 1/D³ (plates) | Casimir–Polder regime |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Geometry |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_{Keesom} = -\dfrac{\mu_{1}^{2}\mu_{2}^{2}}{3(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}kT}\cdot\dfrac{1}{r^{6}}$ | Dipole–dipole (orientation) |
| 2 | $U_{Debye} = -\dfrac{\mu_{1}^{2}\alpha_{2}+\mu_{2}^{2}\alpha_{1}}{(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{r^{6}}$ | Dipole–induced dipole |
| 3 | $U_{London} = -\dfrac{3\alpha_{1}\alpha_{2}}{2(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{I_{1}I_{2}}{I_{1}+I_{2}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{r^{6}}$ | Dispersion (induced–induced) |
| 4 | $U_{pair}(r) = -\dfrac{C}{r^{6}}$ | Combined pairwise potential |
| 5 | $U_{plates}(D) = -\dfrac{A}{12\pi D^{2}}$ | Two flat plates |
| 6 | $U_{spheres}(D) = -\dfrac{A}{6D}\cdot\dfrac{R_{1}R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}$ | Two equal spheres |
| 7 | $U_{sphere-plate}(D) = -\dfrac{AR}{6D}$ | Sphere–flat plate |
| 8 | $F_{plates}(D) = -\dfrac{A}{6\pi D^{3}}$ | Force per unit area between plates |
| 9 | $A = \pi^{2}C\rho_{1}\rho_{2}$ | Hamaker constant (simple) |
| 10 | $A_{132} \approx (\sqrt{A_{11}} - \sqrt{A_{33}})(\sqrt{A_{22}} - \sqrt{A_{33}})$ | Combined Hamaker (geometric mean) |
| 11 | $U_{plates}^{ret}(D) = -\dfrac{B}{D^{3}}$ | Retarded interaction |
| 12 | $C = \dfrac{3}{2}\alpha_{1}\alpha_{2}\dfrac{I_{1}I_{2}}{(I_{1}+I_{2})(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}}$ | London coefficient |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Van der Waals forces underpin numerous engineering phenomena:

- **DLVO Theory of Colloidal Stability:** Combined with electrostatic double-layer repulsion, van der Waals forces determine whether a colloidal dispersion is stable or flocculates. Critical in paints, pharmaceuticals, ceramics, and food emulsions.
- **Adhesion & Friction (Gecko Tape):** Geckos exploit van der Waals forces via millions of microscopic **setae** that maximize contact area with surfaces.
- **Drug Delivery & Nanomedicine:** Functionalized nanoparticles adhere to biological membranes through tuned van der Waals interactions.
- **Polymer Blends & Composites:** Miscibility and mechanical strength depend critically on the **Hildebrand solubility parameter**, which is rooted in dispersion forces.
- **Microelectronics / MEMS:** Stiction (static friction) in MEMS devices is often dominated by van der Waals forces when components are brought into close proximity.
- **Food Science:** Emulsion stability (mayonnaise, milk, butter) is governed by a balance of van der Waals attraction and steric/electrostatic repulsion.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Pairwise London Dispersion Energy (Quantum Mechanical)

**Objective:** Derive the famous **L = −C/r⁶** law for two identical atoms using second-order perturbation theory.

**Step 1 — Setup the unperturbed Hamiltonian**

Consider two identical atoms (1) and (2), each with a single valence electron, far apart. The unperturbed Hamiltonian is:

$$
H_{0} = H_{1} + H_{2}
$$

with ground-state wavefunctions $\psi_{n}^{(1)}$ and $\psi_{m}^{(2)}$, and eigenvalues $E_{n}^{(1)}, E_{m}^{(2)}$.

**Step 2 — Add the dipole–dipole perturbation**

The dipole–dipole interaction operator between the two instantaneous dipoles $\mu_{1}$ and $\mu_{2}$ separated by vector $\vec{r}$ is:

$$
V = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{3}}\left[\vec{\mu}_{1}\cdot\vec{\mu}_{2} - \frac{3(\vec{\mu}_{1}\cdot\vec{r})(\vec{\mu}_{2}\cdot\vec{r})}{r^{2}}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply second-order perturbation theory**

The energy shift of the ground state is:

$$
\Delta E = \sum_{n,m \neq 0}\frac{\vert\langle 0,0\vert V\vert n,m\rangle\vert^{2}}{E_{0}^{(1)}+E_{0}^{(2)}-E_{n}^{(1)}-E_{m}^{(2)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate the energy denominator**

Using the Unsöld approximation, replace all excited-state energies by an average excitation energy $E_{av} = I$ (≈ ionization potential):

$$
\Delta E \approx -\frac{\langle 0,0\vert V^{2}\vert 0,0\rangle}{I}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the expectation value**

For spherically symmetric atoms, the angular average gives:

$$
\langle 0,0\vert V^{2}\vert 0,0\rangle = \frac{2}{(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}r^{6}}\langle\mu^{2}\rangle^{2}
$$

where $\langle\mu^{2}\rangle = e^{2}\langle r_{e}^{2}\rangle$ relates to the polarizability.

**Step 6 — Introduce the static polarizability α**

For a ground-state atom in an electric field, $\mu = \alpha E$, and using $\alpha \approx 2\langle r_{e}^{2}\rangle / (4\pi\varepsilon_{0} I)$, we get:

$$
\langle\mu^{2}\rangle \approx \alpha I
$$

**Step 7 — Final London Dispersion Energy**

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\;U_{London}(r) = -\frac{3}{2}\frac{\alpha_{1}\alpha_{2}}{(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}}\cdot\frac{I_{1}I_{2}}{I_{1}+I_{2}}\cdot\frac{1}{r^{6}}\;}
$$

This is the **negative** sign because the system gains stability by lowering energy when the two transient dipoles are correlated.

**Why C/r⁶?** The first power of 1/r³ from the dipole–dipole coupling is **squared** in the second-order perturbation, giving 1/r⁶.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Van der Waals Force Between Two Semi-Infinite Plates (Hamaker Integration)

**Objective:** Obtain **U = −A/(12πD²)** by summing pairwise 1/r⁶ potentials over two half-spaces.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry**

Two identical semi-infinite plates of number density ρ, separated by vacuum gap D. Each atom in plate 1 at position $\vec{r}_{1}$ interacts with each atom in plate 2 at position $\vec{r}_{2}$.

**Step 2 — Write the total energy by double volume integration**

$$
U(D) = -\rho^{2}\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int \frac{C}{\vert\vec{r}_{12}\vert^{6}}\,dV_{1}\,dV_{2}
$$

For two flat slabs, we integrate over **slab thicknesses** and the **lateral coordinates** (x, y).

**Step 3 — Use cylindrical coordinates (r, θ, z)**

The lateral distance is $r = \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$, and the vertical separation is $z = D + z_{1} + z_{2}$ where $z_{1}, z_{2}$ are depths into each plate.

**Step 4 — Carry out the lateral integral**

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{2\pi r\,dr}{(r^{2}+z^{2})^{3}} = \frac{\pi}{2z^{4}}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over the depths of both plates**

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty}\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{dz_{1}\,dz_{2}}{(D+z_{1}+z_{2})^{4}} = \frac{1}{6D^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine all prefactors**

$$
U(D) = -\rho^{2}\,C \cdot \frac{\pi^{2}}{6D^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Define the Hamaker Constant**

By definition:

$$
\boxed{\;A \equiv \pi^{2} C \rho^{2}\;}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;U_{plates}(D) = -\frac{A}{12\pi D^{2}}\quad\text{(energy per unit area)}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **12π** arises from the precise numerical evaluation of the integrals in Steps 4 and 5. This is a board-favorite derivation in KTU examinations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Van der Waals Force Between Two Spheres (Derjaguin Approximation)

**Objective:** Derive **U = −(A R₁ R₂)/(6 D (R₁ + R₂))** using the **Derjaguin Approximation**.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry**

Two spheres of radii R₁ and R₂, with the smallest gap D between them.

**Step 2 — Apply the Derjaguin Approximation**

For two curved surfaces whose local radii of curvature are R₁ and R₂ at the closest point, the interaction energy is:

$$
U(D) = 2\pi R_{eff}\int_{D}^{\infty}U_{plates}(h)\,dh
$$

where the **effective radius** is:

$$
\frac{1}{R_{eff}} = \frac{1}{R_{1}}+\frac{1}{R_{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute U_plates(h) = −A/(12π h²)**

$$
U(D) = 2\pi R_{eff}\int_{D}^{\infty}\left(-\frac{A}{12\pi h^{2}}\right)dh
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral**

$$
\int_{D}^{\infty}\frac{dh}{h^{2}} = \frac{1}{D}
$$

Therefore:

$$
U(D) = -\frac{2\pi R_{eff}A}{12\pi}\cdot\frac{1}{D} = -\frac{A R_{eff}}{6D}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back R_eff**

$$
\boxed{\;U_{spheres}(D) = -\frac{A}{6D}\cdot\frac{R_{1}R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}\;}
$$

For **two equal spheres** of radius R: $R_{eff} = R/2$, so $U = -AR/(12D)$.

For **sphere–plate** (R₂ → ∞): $R_{eff} = R_{1}$, giving $U = -AR/(6D)$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Decay Laws

| Geometry | Unretarded Energy | Unretarded Force | Retarded Energy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atom–Atom | −C/r⁶ | −6C/r⁷ | −C'/r⁷ |
| Plate–Plate | −A/(12π D²) | −A/(6π D³) | −B/D³ |
| Sphere–Sphere (equal R) | −AR/(12D) | −AR/(12D²) | −BR/(20D²) |
| Sphere–Plate | −AR/(6D) | −AR/(6D²) | −BR/(10D²) |
| Cylinder–Cylinder (crossed) | −A√(R₁R₂)/(6D) | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** All geometries show that the force decays **faster** than the energy by one extra factor of D. This is because $F = -dU/dD$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Van der Waals forces. Enumerate the three types of Van der Waals interactions and state which one is universal.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Van der Waals forces are weak, distance-dependent intermolecular attractive forces arising from interactions between permanent or induced electrical dipoles in molecules.

The three types are:

| # | Type | Origin | Universality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Keesom (Orientation)** | Between two **permanent dipoles** | Only polar–polar |
| 2 | **Debye (Induction)** | Between a **permanent dipole** and an **induced dipole** | Polar–non-polar |
| 3 | **London (Dispersion)** | Between **two transient (induced) dipoles** | **Universal (all matter)** |

The **London dispersion force** is universal — it exists between every pair of atoms or molecules, regardless of polarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark), Three types enumerated (1 mark), Identification of universal one with reasoning (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is the Hamaker constant? Explain its significance in colloidal systems. State its typical order of magnitude.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Hamaker constant (A)** is a material property that characterizes the strength of van der Waals interactions between two macroscopic bodies immersed in a medium. It bundles all atomic-level dispersion properties into a single parameter with units of **energy (Joules)**.

For most condensed phases (e.g., hydrocarbons, oxides, metals in air or vacuum), the Hamaker constant lies in the range:

$$
A \approx 10^{-20}\;\text{to}\;10^{-19}\;\text{J}
$$

**Significance in colloidal systems:**
- Determines the **magnitude of attractive force** between colloidal particles.
- Direct input to **DLVO theory** for predicting colloidal stability.
- Sets the depth of the **primary minimum** in the potential energy curve.
- Used to predict **flocculation kinetics** and **adhesion** in paints, inks, food emulsions, and pharmaceutical suspensions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the **London dispersion potential** between two atoms, showing that the interaction energy decays as **1/r⁶**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two flat plates of the same material are separated by a vacuum gap of **D = 2 nm**. The Hamaker constant is **A = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**. Compute:
- (i) The interaction energy per unit area. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) The van der Waals force per unit area. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The force per unit area if the gap is increased to **D = 4 nm**. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of London Dispersion Potential (7 Marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. Each atom is treated as a harmonic oscillator with one characteristic frequency ν₀.
2. The interaction is treated as a **second-order perturbation**.
3. Spherically symmetric charge distribution; angular averaging applies.
4. The energy denominator is approximated by an average excitation energy (Unsöld approximation).

*Steps:*

**Step 1:** The unperturbed Hamiltonian for two non-interacting atoms:

$$
H_0 = H_A + H_B
$$

**Step 2:** The instantaneous dipole–dipole interaction energy (perturbation):

$$
V = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}\left[\vec{\mu}_A\cdot\vec{\mu}_B - \frac{3(\vec{\mu}_A\cdot\hat{r})(\vec{\mu}_B\cdot\hat{r})}{1}\right]
$$

For isotropic (random) orientations, the angular average gives:

$$
\langle V \rangle_{\text{angular}} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Apply **second-order perturbation theory**:

$$
\Delta E = -\sum_{n,m\neq 0}\frac{\vert\langle 0,0\vert V\vert n,m\rangle\vert^{2}}{E_{n} - E_{0} + E_{m} - E_{0}}
$$

**Step 4:** Use the **Unsöld closure** — replace all excited-state energies by a single mean excitation energy $h\nu_0$:

$$
\Delta E \approx -\frac{\langle 0,0\vert V^{2}\vert 0,0\rangle}{h\nu_0}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the squared dipole matrix element. For isotropic averaging:

$$
\langle 0,0\vert V^{2}\vert 0,0\rangle = \frac{2}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2 r^6}\,\mu_A^2\,\mu_B^2
$$

**Step 6:** Express dipole moments in terms of polarizabilities $\mu = \alpha E$ and the oscillator relation $\alpha = e^2/(4\pi^2 m \nu_0^2)$. After substituting:

$$
\Delta E = -\frac{3\alpha_A\alpha_B}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2}\cdot\frac{h\nu_0^A\cdot h\nu_0^B}{h\nu_0^A + h\nu_0^B}\cdot\frac{1}{r^6}
$$

**Step 7:** Final result:

$$
\boxed{\;U_{London}(r) = -\frac{C}{r^6} = -\frac{3}{2}\frac{\alpha_A\alpha_B}{(4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2}\cdot\frac{I_A I_B}{I_A + I_B}\cdot\frac{1}{r^6}\;}
$$

where $I_A = h\nu_0^A$ is the ionization potential.

**Conclusion:** The London dispersion energy between two atoms decays as **1/r⁶**, is **always attractive**, and is the dominant contributor to van der Waals forces for non-polar systems.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Given: A = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.

**(i) Energy per unit area at D = 2 nm:**

$$
U(D) = -\frac{A}{12\pi D^{2}}
$$

Convert D = 2 nm = 2 × 10⁻⁹ m:

$$
D^2 = (2\times 10^{-9})^2 = 4\times 10^{-18}\;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
U = -\frac{1.0\times 10^{-19}}{12\pi \times 4\times 10^{-18}} = -\frac{1.0\times 10^{-19}}{1.508\times 10^{-16}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;U \approx -6.63\times 10^{-4}\;\text{J/m}^2 = -0.663\;\text{mJ/m}^2\;}
$$

**(ii) Force per unit area at D = 2 nm:**

$$
F(D) = -\frac{dU}{dD} = -\frac{A}{6\pi D^{3}}
$$

$$
D^3 = (2\times 10^{-9})^3 = 8\times 10^{-27}\;\text{m}^3
$$

$$
F = -\frac{1.0\times 10^{-19}}{6\pi \times 8\times 10^{-27}} = -\frac{1.0\times 10^{-19}}{1.508\times 10^{-25}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;F \approx -6.63\times 10^{5}\;\text{N/m}^2 = 663\;\text{kPa (attractive)}\;}
$$

**(iii) Force per unit area at D = 4 nm:**

$$
F(D=4\text{nm}) = -\frac{A}{6\pi D^3} = -\frac{1.0\times 10^{-19}}{6\pi \times (4\times 10^{-9})^3}
$$

$$
D^3 = 64\times 10^{-27} = 6.4\times 10^{-26}\;\text{m}^3
$$

$$
F = -\frac{1.0\times 10^{-19}}{6\pi \times 6.4\times 10^{-26}} = -\frac{1.0\times 10^{-19}}{1.206\times 10^{-24}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;F \approx -82.9\;\text{kPa}\;}
$$

**Note:** Doubling the separation (2 nm → 4 nm) reduces the force by a factor of **8** (since F ∝ 1/D³), consistent with our calculation (663 → 82.9 kPa).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Derjaguin approximation** and use it to derive the van der Waals interaction energy between a **sphere of radius R** and a **flat plate** as a function of the closest separation D. State the final expression and explain its physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A colloidal particle of radius **R = 500 nm** approaches a planar substrate in water. The combined Hamaker constant is **A = 2.5 × 10⁻²⁰ J**. Compute:
- (i) The van der Waals interaction energy at **D = 1 nm**. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) The van der Waals interaction energy at **D = 5 nm**. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The corresponding interaction energies in units of **kT** at 298 K. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derjaguin Approximation Derivation (7 Marks):**

The **Derjaguin approximation** relates the interaction between two curved bodies to the interaction per unit area between two flat plates. It is valid when the **range of interaction is much smaller than the radii of curvature** of the bodies.

**Geometric Setup:**

A sphere of radius R rests above a flat plate at the closest approach distance D. The sphere's surface at a height h above the contact point lies at a lateral distance:

$$
r = \sqrt{2Rh}
$$

(from the Pythagorean theorem for small h: $(R-h)^2 + r^2 = R^2 \Rightarrow r^2 \approx 2Rh$).

**Derjaguin Formula:**

For two curved surfaces with effective radius $R_{eff}$, the total interaction energy is:

$$
U(D) = 2\pi R_{eff}\int_{D}^{\infty} U_{plates}(h)\,dh
$$

For sphere–plate geometry, $R_{eff} = R$ (since one surface is flat, $R_2 \to \infty$).

**Substituting the flat-plate van der Waals energy:**

$$
U_{plates}(h) = -\frac{A}{12\pi h^2}
$$

**Integration:**

$$
U(D) = 2\pi R\int_{D}^{\infty}\left(-\frac{A}{12\pi h^2}\right)dh = -\frac{AR}{6}\int_{D}^{\infty}\frac{dh}{h^2}
$$

$$
\int_{D}^{\infty}\frac{dh}{h^2} = \frac{1}{D}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;U_{sphere-plate}(D) = -\frac{AR}{6D}\;}
$$

**Physical Significance:**

1. **Decay as 1/D:** Sphere–plate interactions decay **more slowly** than plate–plate interactions (1/D vs 1/D²). Hence colloidal particles experience longer-ranged attraction to walls than to each other.
2. **Linear in R:** Larger particles experience stronger adhesion (which is why micron-sized particles stick readily while nanoparticles may remain suspended).
3. **Limiting case of sphere–sphere:** For two equal spheres, $R_{eff} = R/2$, giving $U = -AR/(12D)$.
4. **Applications:** Direct measurement technique in **AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy)** where a colloidal probe is retracted from a surface.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks):**

Given:
- R = 500 nm = 5 × 10⁻⁷ m
- A = 2.5 × 10⁻²⁰ J
- kT = 4.116 × 10⁻²¹ J (at 298 K)
- Formula: $U(D) = -AR/(6D)$

**(i) At D = 1 nm = 1 × 10⁻⁹ m:**

$$
U(1\text{ nm}) = -\frac{(2.5\times 10^{-20})(5\times 10^{-7})}{6 \times 1\times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
U = -\frac{1.25\times 10^{-26}}{6\times 10^{-9}} = -2.083\times 10^{-18}\;\text{J}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;U(1\text{ nm}) \approx -2.08\times 10^{-18}\;\text{J}\;}
$$

**(ii) At D = 5 nm = 5 × 10⁻⁹ m:**

$$
U(5\text{ nm}) = -\frac{(2.5\times 10^{-20})(5\times 10^{-7})}{6 \times 5\times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
U = -\frac{1.25\times 10^{-26}}{3\times 10^{-8}} = -4.167\times 10^{-19}\;\text{J}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;U(5\text{ nm}) \approx -4.17\times 10^{-19}\;\text{J}\;}
$$

**Observation:** Increasing D from 1 nm to 5 nm (5× increase) reduces |U| by a factor of 5, consistent with U ∝ 1/D.

**(iii) Energies in units of kT:**

At T = 298 K:

$$
kT = 1.381\times 10^{-23}\times 298 = 4.116\times 10^{-21}\;\text{J}
$$

At D = 1 nm:

$$
\frac{U}{kT} = \frac{-2.083\times 10^{-18}}{4.116\times 10^{-21}} \approx -506\;kT
$$

At D = 5 nm:

$$
\frac{U}{kT} = \frac{-4.167\times 10^{-19}}{4.116\times 10^{-21}} \approx -101\;kT
$$

$$
\boxed{\;U(1\text{ nm}) \approx -506\,kT,\quad U(5\text{ nm}) \approx -101\,kT\;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Even at 5 nm separation, the van der Waals attraction is ~100 kT — far exceeding thermal energy. This means the particle will be **irreversibly stuck** to the wall once it approaches within a few nanometers. In practical colloidal systems, this is counteracted by **electrostatic or steric repulsion** (DLVO theory).

---
