> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> **Common mistakes that cost marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** — van der Waals forces are attractive, so the energy is *negative*. Marks are deducted for missing signs.
> 2. **Confusing Energy and Force** — Examiners specifically ask "energy per unit area" or "force per unit area." These differ by one factor of D. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** — Always convert nm to m before computation. The Hamaker constant is in Joules, distances in **meters** in SI units.
> 4. **Mixing up geometry formulas** — Plate–plate is 1/D² in energy; sphere–plate is 1/D in energy; sphere–sphere is 1/D. Drawing a quick diagram before writing helps avoid this.
> 5. **Skipping the Hamaker constant definition** — In derivation questions, you must **define A** before using it. Many students use the formula without explaining what A represents.
> 6. **Wrong integration limits** — In the Derjaguin integration, the lower limit is D (the gap), not 0.
> 7. **Forgetting the retardation correction** — If the separation exceeds 10 nm, the simple Hamaker formula underestimates attraction. Mention retardation in long-range problems for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Vander Waals Forces**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Van der Waals forces = **Keesom + Debye + London** interactions.
- ☐ **London dispersion** is universal and dominant for non-polar systems.
- ☐ All three pairwise potentials decay as **1/r⁶** (unretarded, atom–atom).
- ☐ All van der Waals interactions are **attractive** (in their dominant mode).

**Macroscopic Body Laws (Unretarded):**
- ☐ **Plate–Plate:** Energy ∝ **A/(12π D²)**, Force ∝ **A/(6π D³)**.
- ☐ **Sphere–Plate:** Energy = **−AR/(6D)**, Force = **−AR/(6D²)**.
- ☐ **Sphere–Sphere (equal R):** Energy = **−AR/(12D)**, Force = **−AR/(12D²)**.

**Hamaker Constant:**
- ☐ Definition: **A = π²Cρ₁ρ₂** (microscopic).
- ☐ Typical magnitude: **A ≈ 10⁻²⁰ to 10⁻¹⁹ J** for condensed phases.
- ☐ Lifshitz approach: A calculated from dielectric constants and refractive indices.

**Retardation:**
- ☐ Retardation becomes important for **D > ~10 nm**.
- ☐ Decay law changes: plate–plate energy from **1/D²** to **1/D³**.
- ☐ Casimir–Polder regime for very large separations.

**Derivations to Master:**
- ☐ London 1/r⁶ via second-order perturbation theory.
- ☐ Plate–plate 1/D² by Hamaker pairwise summation.
- ☐ Sphere–plate via Derjaguin approximation.

**Numerical Benchmarks to Memorize:**
- ☐ kT at 298 K = **4.116 × 10⁻²¹ J**.
- ☐ Hamaker constant typical value = **10⁻²⁰ J** (≈ 2.4 kT).
- ☐ Sphere of R = 500 nm at D = 1 nm with A = 2.5 × 10⁻²⁰ J → |U| ≈ **500 kT**.

**DLVO Connections:**
- ☐ Van der Waals provides the **attractive** branch.
- ☐ Combined with **electrostatic double-layer repulsion**, sets colloidal stability.
- ☐ Energy barrier > 10 kT → stable; < 10 kT → rapid coagulation.
- ☐ Secondary minimum causes **weak, reversible flocculation**.

**Real-World Tie-Ins:**
- ☐ **Gecko adhesion** — millions of setae maximize van der Waals contact.
- ☐ **MEMS stiction** — van der Waals dominates when parts are sub-micron apart.
- ☐ **Food emulsions, paints, inks** — DLVO balance governs shelf-life.
- ☐ **Drug delivery** — nanoparticle adhesion to membranes tuned via surface chemistry.

**Common Exam Pitfalls:**
- ☐ Always carry the **negative sign** (attraction).
- ☐ Mind the **geometry** — plate–plate ≠ sphere–plate ≠ sphere–sphere.
- ☐ Convert **nm to m** before plugging numbers into SI formulas.
- ☐ State the **Hamaker constant value** explicitly when using it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimental Determination of Vanderwaals Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Experimental Determination of Vanderwaals Forces # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What are Van der Waals Forces? **Van der Waals (vdW) forces** are ubiquitous, long-range (effective over 0.2 nm to ~10 nm), non-covalent attractive forces that arise from transient and permanent dipolar interactions between atoms, molecules, and macroscopic bodies. In colloidal systems, they are the principal attractive force responsible for particle aggregation, thin-film stability, adhesion, and wetting phenomena. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Israelachvili, 2011):** > Van der Waals forces are *electrodynamic* forces between uncharged microscopic or macroscopic bodies caused by correlations in the fluctuating polarization of closely spaced bodies. They operate across vacuum, gases, and condensed media (liquids), and are fundamentally quantum-mechanical in origin. The three classical contributions are: 1. **Keesom (orientation) forces** – between permanent dipoles. 2. **Debye (induction) forces** – between a permanent dipole and an induced dipole. 3. **London (dispersion) forces** – between instantaneously induced dipoles (dominant term, ~80 % contribution). For macroscopic bodies, the combined effect is captured by the **Hamaker constant ($A$)**, typically in the range $10^{-20}$ to $10^{-19}$ J. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course treats the *microscopic* (molecular) formulation and the *macroscopic* (Lifshitz continuum) formulation of vdW forces as two complementary routes. Experimental determination bridges them by validating predicted $A$ values. --- ## 1.2 Why is Experimental Determination Necessary? - **Direct validation of theory:** Microscopic calculations of $A$ are difficult; Lifshitz theory requires optical/dielectric data over the entire UV–IR frequency range. - **Force–distance profiles** are needed to verify the $D^{-2}$ (sphere–flat) or $D^{-3}$ (parallel plates) laws predicted by pairwise summation. - **Surface roughness, contamination, and adsorbed layers** drastically alter the effective force – experiments reveal these "real-world" deviations. - **Polymer, surfactant, and biomolecular interactions** depend on vdW components, demanding precise measurement. > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy — "Two Magnets Through a Wall":** > Imagine two weakly magnetized paperclips on a table. You cannot see the magnetic field, but if you push them close enough, they snap together. Van der Waals forces are similar but arise from constantly flickering "mini-magnets" inside every atom. The challenge for experimentalists is like trying to measure that invisible magnetic pull using a super-sensitive spring scale while the paperclips sit on a vibrating table — that is exactly the engineering problem solved by the **Surface Force Apparatus (SFA)** and the **AFM colloidal probe**. ## 1.3 Classification of Experimental Techniques | Method | Type | Typical Range (nm) | Resolution | Medium | |---|---|---|---|---| | Surface Force Apparatus (SFA) | Direct force | 0.2 – 100 | 0.1 nm distance; 10 nN force | Gas, Liquid | | AFM Colloidal Probe | Direct force | 0.5 – 100 | 0.1 nm; 10 pN | Gas, Liquid | | Thin-Film Balance (Scheludko cell) | Disjoining pressure | 5 – 1000 | 0.5 nm | Aqueous | | Osmotic Stress / X-ray / SFA | Indirect | 1 – 100 | Sub-nm | Liquid | | Centrifugation / Sedimentation | Indirect | ~10 – 1000 | – | Liquid | | Optical / TIRM | Direct (trapping) | 5 – 500 | 1 nm | Liquid | > [!NOTE] > **Standard reference constants used throughout this module:** > - Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J·K$^{-1}$ > - Permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ C²·N$^{-1}$·m$^{-2}$ > - Typical Hamaker constant for hydrocarbons in vacuum $A \approx 10^{-19}$ J # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Theoretical Background Required for Interpreting Experiments Before examining the instruments, we must recall the three key theoretical relations that experiments seek to verify. ### 2.1.1 Pairwise Summation and the Hamaker Approximation For two macroscopic bodies (1) and (2) interacting across medium (3), the Hamaker constant is approximated by: $$ A_{132} = \left(\sqrt{A_{11}} - \sqrt{A_{33}}\right)\left(\sqrt{A_{22}} - \sqrt{A_{33}}\right) $$ This is the **Combining Relation** derived from the geometric-mean approximation (GMA). It is the practical workhorse for predicting vdW forces in colloid science. ### 2.1.2 Force Laws (Pairwise Summation) - **Sphere–sphere / sphere–flat of radius R:** $$ F(D) = -\dfrac{A_{132}\,R}{6\,D^{2}} $$ - **Flat–flat (parallel plates) of area A, separated by D:** $$ F(D) = -\dfrac{A_{132}\,A}{6\,\pi\,D^{3}} $$ - **Energy of interaction per unit area (flat plates):** $$ E(D) = -\dfrac{A_{132}}{12\,\pi\,D^{2}} $$ ### 2.1.3 Lifshitz Theory (Macroscopic Continuum Approach) The full **Lifshitz (1956)** theory expresses the Hamaker constant in terms of dielectric response functions. For two identical materials (1) interacting across medium (3): $$ A_{131} = \dfrac{3}{4}\,k_BT\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_3}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_3}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{3h}{4\pi}\int_{\nu_n}^{\infty}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_1(i\nu) - \varepsilon_3(i\nu)}{\varepsilon_1(i\nu) + \varepsilon_3(i\nu)}\right)^{2}d\nu $$ The first term is the zero-frequency (Keesom) contribution; the second is the dispersion (London) integral evaluated at imaginary frequencies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Lifshitz matters experimentally:** It shows that $A$ depends on the *entire* UV–IR absorption spectrum of the materials. Experimentalists can thus: > (a) Measure $A$ directly with a force apparatus, and > (b) Compare it with the value calculated from independently measured optical constants, providing a critical test of the theory. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Law | Mathematical Expression | Variables | Units | Validity / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | vdW force, sphere–flat | $F = -A_{132}R/(6D^{2})$ | $F$, $R$, $D$ | N, m, m | Pairwise-additivity, $D \ll R$ | | 2 | vdW force, flat–flat | $F = -A_{132}A_{\text{area}}/(6\pi D^{3})$ | $F$, $A_{\text{area}}$, $D$ | N, m², m | Per unit area: $f = -A_{132}/(6\pi D^{3})$ | | 3 | Interaction energy, flat–flat | $E = -A_{132}/(12\pi D^{2})$ | $E$, $D$ | J·m$^{-2}$ | Often called $V(D)$ | | 4 | **Derjaguin Approximation (DA)** | $F(D) = 2\pi R_{\text{eff}} E(D)$ | $F$, $R_{\text{eff}}$, $E$ | N, m, J·m$^{-2}$ | Converts flat–flat energy into sphere–flat force | | 5 | **Effective radius** for crossed cylinders | $R_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt{R_1 R_2}$ | – | m | SFA geometry | | 6 | Hamaker combining relation (GMA) | $A_{132} = (\sqrt{A_{11}} - \sqrt{A_{33}})(\sqrt{A_{22}} - \sqrt{A_{33}})$ | – | J | Approximate; needs $\varepsilon_1 \approx \varepsilon_2$ | | 7 | Disjoining pressure | $\Pi(D) = -A_{132}/(6\pi D^{3})$ | $\Pi$, $D$ | Pa, m | For symmetric films, no retardation | | 8 | Retardation correction | $F_{\text{ret}}(D) \approx F(D)/(1 + bD/\lambda)$ | $b \approx 5.32$ | – | Casimir–Polder regime $D > 10$ nm | | 9 | Lifshitz zero-freq. term | $A_{\nu=0} = \dfrac{3k_BT}{4}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_3}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_3}\right)^{2}$ | $\varepsilon$ | J | Keesom contribution | | 10 | Spring constant of AFM cantilever | $k = E w t^3/(4 L^3)$ | $E$, $w$, $t$, $L$ | N·m$^{-1}$ | Rectangular cantilever | | 11 | Cantilever deflection $\rightarrow$ Force | $F = k \cdot \Delta x$ | $k$, $\Delta x$ | N, m | Hooke's law | | 12 | Capillary pressure in SFA | $\Pi_{\text{cap}} = 2\gamma\cos\theta / R_{\text{meniscus}}$ | $\gamma$, $\theta$, $R$ | Pa, N·m$^{-1}$, –, m | Humidity control | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > - In **pharmaceutical formulation**, $A_{132}$ values predict the shelf-life of suspensions. > - In **mineral flotation**, vdW forces determine particle–bubble attachment. > - In **MEMS/NEMS design**, vdW-driven "stiction" causes device failure — knowing $A$ prevents catastrophic collapse. > - In **food colloids** (chocolate, milk, mayonnaise), vdW balance against steric repulsion controls texture. --- ## 2.3 Operational Logic: How an Experimental vdW Measurement Works Whether using SFA, AFM, or a thin-film balance, the universal experimental logic follows **four steps**: 1. **Prepare two atomically smooth (or well-characterized) surfaces** (e.g., freshly cleaved mica for SFA; silica microspheres for AFM; planar silica plates for the Scheludko cell). 2. **Bring the surfaces to a precisely known, sub-nanometre distance $D$.** This is achieved via mechanical actuators (SFA), piezoelectric scanners (AFM), or hydrostatic pressure (Scheludko). 3. **Measure the force (or pressure) at that $D$** using a calibrated spring (SFA), cantilever deflection (AFM), or film-thickness imaging (SFA/MBI). 4. **Fit the force–distance data to the theoretical law** $F(D) = -A R /(6 D^2)$ (or the retarded equivalent) to extract $A_{132}$. > [!NOTE] > The *separation* $D$ in vdW-force experiments is **not** the centre-to-centre distance, but the **shortest distance between the two bodies** — i.e., the surface-to-surface distance measured along the line of action of the force. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Derjaguin Approximation (DA) The DA is the single most important theoretical tool in vdW-force experiments because it converts the mathematically simpler parallel-plate energy into the experimentally measured sphere–flat force. **Starting Point:** Consider a sphere of radius $R$ at closest distance $D$ from a flat surface. Let $r$ be the lateral radial coordinate measured from the contact point. The local separation at radius $r$ is: $$ h(r) = D + \dfrac{r^{2}}{2R} $$ This follows from the Pythagoras expansion valid when $r \ll R$. **Step 1: Express the energy differential.** The interaction energy between two parallel annular rings at separation $h$ is, per unit area: $$ e(h) = \dfrac{-A_{132}}{12\pi h^{2}} $$ A small annular ring of width $dr$ at radius $r$ has area $2\pi r\,dr$. The contribution to the total energy from this ring is: $$ dE = e(h(r)) \cdot 2\pi r\,dr $$ **Step 2: Substitute $h(r)$ and set up the integral.** $$ E_{\text{tot}} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{-A_{132}}{12\pi \left(D + \dfrac{r^{2}}{2R}\right)^{2}}\,2\pi r\,dr $$ Simplify the constants: $$ E_{\text{tot}} = \dfrac{-A_{132}}{6} \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{r\,dr}{\left(D + \dfrac{r^{2}}{2R}\right)^{2}} $$ **Step 3: Apply the substitution $u = D + r^{2}/(2R)$.** Differentiating: $du = (r/R)\,dr \;\Rightarrow\; r\,dr = R\,du$. The limits change from $r = 0 \Rightarrow u = D$ to $r = \infty \Rightarrow u = \infty$. $$ E_{\text{tot}} = \dfrac{-A_{132}}{6} \int_{D}^{\infty} \dfrac{R\,du}{u^{2}} = \dfrac{-A_{132}R}{6} \left[-\dfrac{1}{u}\right]_{D}^{\infty} $$ **Step 4: Evaluate the boundary term.** $$ E_{\text{tot}} = \dfrac{-A_{132}R}{6} \left(0 - \left(-\dfrac{1}{D}\right)\right) = -\dfrac{A_{132}R}{6D} $$ **Step 5: Convert energy to force using $F = -dE/dD$.** $$ F(D) = -\dfrac{dE_{\text{tot}}}{dD} = -\dfrac{d}{dD}\left(-\dfrac{A_{132}R}{6D}\right) $$ $$ F(D) = -\dfrac{A_{132}R}{6}\left(\dfrac{-1}{D^{2}}\right) = -\dfrac{A_{132}R}{6D^{2}} $$ **Step 6: Generalise to two spheres (or crossed cylinders) of radii $R_1$ and $R_2$.** Replacing $R$ with the effective radius $R_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt{R_1 R_2}$ (crossed cylinders at right angles) or $R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ (two spheres): $$ \boxed{\;F(D) = -\dfrac{A_{132}\,R_{\text{eff}}}{6\,D^{2}} \;\;\;\;(D \ll R)\;} $$ This is the **Derjaguin Approximation** for vdW forces and is the equation directly fitted in SFA and AFM experiments. > [!IMPORTANT] > The DA requires $D \ll R$; for $D \gtrsim R$, the integration fails because the curvature can no longer be approximated by $r^{2}/(2R)$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hamaker Constant from Force–Distance Data (Graphical Method) In a typical SFA or AFM experiment, one obtains a curve of $F$ vs $D$. To extract $A_{132}$: **Step 1:** Plot $\log(\vert F \vert)$ against $\log(D)$. From $F = -A_{132}R/(6D^{2})$, a straight line of slope $-2$ is expected. **Step 2:** At any chosen $D$ value, the force is: $$ F = -\dfrac{A_{132}R}{6D^{2}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad A_{132} = \dfrac{6 \vert F \vert D^{2}}{R} $$ **Step 3:** Multiple $D$ values are averaged (or a linear-regression slope/intercept is used) to obtain $A_{132}$ with statistical confidence. **Step 4:** The retardation correction is applied at separations $D > 5$–$10$ nm: $$ A_{\text{eff}}(D) = A_{132}\left[\dfrac{1}{1 + bD/\lambda}\right] $$ with $b \approx 5.32$ and $\lambda$ the characteristic wavelength of material (often $\lambda \approx 100$ nm for mica). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Disjoining Pressure in a Symmetric Film (Thin-Film Balance) A planar liquid film of thickness $h$ sandwiched between two identical solid surfaces (e.g., a foam film). The disjoining pressure is defined as the excess pressure inside the film: $$ \Pi(h) = P_{\text{film}} - P_{\text{bulk liquid}} $$ For pure vdW interactions between two identical plates: **Energy per unit area** (parallel plates): $$ E(h) = -\dfrac{A_{131}}{12\pi h^{2}} $$ **Pressure is the negative derivative of energy with respect to gap:** $$ \Pi(h) = -\dfrac{dE}{dh} = -\dfrac{d}{dh}\left(-\dfrac{A_{131}}{12\pi h^{2}}\right) = -\dfrac{A_{131}}{6\pi h^{3}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\Pi(h) = -\dfrac{A_{131}}{6\pi h^{3}}\;} $$ The negative sign indicates that the film is under *tension* (pressure inside the film is lower than in the bulk), which is why thin films tend to thin spontaneously unless other repulsive forces (electrostatic, steric, structural) act. In a **Scheludko thin-film balance**, the pressure is applied externally via a porous frit connected to a manostat. The thickness $h$ is measured by micro-interferometry. The condition for mechanical equilibrium of the film is: $$ \Pi(h) = \Delta P_{\text{ext}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{frit}} $$ Hence, by measuring $h$ as a function of applied $\Delta P_{\text{ext}}$, one can extract $A_{131}$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Main Techniques | Feature | SFA (Israelachvili) | AFM Colloidal Probe | Thin-Film Balance | |---|---|---|---| | **Geometry** | Crossed cylinders | Sphere–flat | Parallel plates (film) | | **Distance resolution** | 0.1 nm (via FECO interferometry) | 0.1 nm (capacitive/piezo) | 0.5 nm (microinterferometry) | | **Force resolution** | 10 nN (cantilever springs) | 10 pN (soft cantilevers) | 0.1 Pa (manostat) | | **Typical $A_{132}$ precision** | ±10 % | ±15 % | ±20 % | | **Sample requirement** | Molecularly smooth (mica) | Microparticle (silica, PS) | Smooth plate + frit | | **Best use case** | High-precision fundamental studies | Routine nanoparticle interactions | Foam/emulsion films | | **Disadvantage** | Slow, expert-only | Tip convolution at very small $D$ | Limited to liquid films | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common question asks *why* the SFA remains the "gold standard". The answer lies in the FECO (Fringes of Equal Chromatic Order) interferometry that gives absolute distance with sub-nanometre precision **without** relying on piezo calibration, unlike AFM. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Surface Force Apparatus (SFA) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph source["LIGHT SOURCE (white lamp)"] lamp["Tungsten / Xenon Lamp"] end subgraph optics1["INPUT OPTICS"] lens1["Collimating Lens"] filter["Bandpass Filter"] end subgraph chamber["MEASUREMENT CHAMBER (sealed, temperature controlled)"] s1["Lower Surface
Mica on Cylindrical Lens"] s2["Upper Surface
Mica on Cylindrical Lens"] ddrive["Differential
Mechanical Drive
for D and X"] end subgraph spring["FORCE TRANSDUCER"] lever["Double-Cantilever
Spring
k approx 100 N/m"] dial["Dial Gauge
or Inductance Sensor"] end subgraph optics2["OUTPUT OPTICS"] spectro["Spectrograph
for FECO"] ccd["CCD / Camera"] end subgraph analysis["DATA ACQUISITION"] fit["Force vs D Fitting
DA Equation"] hamaker["Extract A132"] end lamp --> lens1 --> filter --> s1 s2 --> spectro --> ccd --> analysis s1 -.reflects.-> s2 ddrive --> s1 s2 --- lever lever --- dial ddrive --> spring spring --> fit fit --> hamaker ``` **Interpretation of the diagram:** The SFA couples *optical interferometry* (FECO) to *mechanical force transduction* (cantilever spring). White light enters, reflects from the back-silvered mica surfaces, and the resulting interference fringes are spectrally resolved. The fringe wavelength gives the absolute gap $D$; the cantilever deflection gives the force $F$. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — AFM Colloidal Probe ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph piezo["PIEZOELECTRIC SCANNER"] xy["XY Scanner"] z["Z Piezo
resolution approx 0.1 nm"] end subgraph cantilever["CANTILEVER ASSEMBLY"] lever["Rectangular
Cantilever
k approx 0.01 to 1 N/m"] sphere["Colloidal Particle
R approx 1 to 10 microns"] end subgraph sample["SAMPLE STAGE"] flat["Atomically Smooth Substrate
e.g. Mica or Silica Wafer"] end subgraph detection["DEFLECTION SENSOR"] laser["Laser Diode"] psd["Position Sensitive
Photodetector
PSD"] end subgraph control["FEEDBACK LOOP"] fb["PI Controller"] data["F vs D Curve
Storage"] end xy --> z z --- lever lever --- sphere sphere -. vdW .-> flat laser --> lever lever --> psd psd --> fb fb --> z z --> data data --> hamakerA["Extract A132 via
F = minus AR over 6 D squared"] ``` **Key block functions:** - *Piezo scanner*: moves the cantilever in three dimensions with sub-nm precision. - *Cantilever + sphere*: the force transducer (Hooke's law $F = k \Delta x$). - *PSD + laser*: detects the small bending of the cantilever. - *Feedback loop*: holds the cantilever at a constant deflection (force) while varying $D$, generating the $F(D)$ curve. --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Scheludko Thin-Film Balance ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph cell["SCHELUDKO CELL (closed, saturated vapour)"] film["Horizontal Liquid Film
Radius approx 0.5 mm"] meniscus["Film Meniscus"] end subgraph pressure["PRESSURE CONTROL"] manostat["Manostat / Syringe Pump"] deltaP["External Pressure
Delta P ext"] end subgraph opticsTF["THICKNESS MEASUREMENT"] micro["Micro-Interferometer
equal-thickness fringes"] thick["Extract Film Thickness h"] end subgraph balance["EQUILIBRIUM"] eq["Equilibrium Condition
Pi of h equals Delta P ext"] end subgraph dataTF["DATA ANALYSIS"] fitTF["Fit Pi of h to
Pi = minus A over 6 pi h cubed"] extractA["Extract A131"] end pressure --> cell manostat --> deltaP --> cell cell --> opticsTF micro --> thick --> eq deltaP --> eq eq --> fitTF fitTF --> extractA ``` **Operational logic:** The applied external pressure pushes the film thinner; at equilibrium, the applied pressure equals the disjoining pressure. By measuring $h$ as $\Delta P_{\text{ext}}$ is varied, the $A_{131}$ value is obtained. --- ## 4.4 Decision Flow — Selecting the Correct Experimental Method ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Need to Measure vdW Force?"] q1{"Sample Form?"} sfa["Use SFA
molecularly smooth surfaces"] afm["Use AFM Colloidal Probe
microparticles or rough surfaces"] film["Use Thin-Film Balance
free liquid films"] q2{"Required Resolution?"} high["High Resolution
SFA preferred"] med["Routine measurement
AFM sufficient"] q3{"Medium?"} liquid["Liquid Medium"] air["Air or Vacuum"] start --> q1 q1 -- "Two macroscopic surfaces" --> q2 q1 -- "Free thin film" --> film q2 -- "<0.2 nm" --> high q2 -- "0.5 nm acceptable" --> med q3 --> liquid liquid --> sfa liquid --> afm air --> sfa ``` > [!TIP] > **Mermaid compilation note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric and prefixed with descriptive text to avoid conflicts with reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > [!NOTE] > Answers are concise but board-exam-quality — sufficient depth to score full marks in 5–6 lines. ### Question 1. Define the Derjaguin Approximation. State its key geometric requirement. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Model Answer:** The Derjaguin Approximation (DA) relates the force $F(D)$ between two curved bodies to the interaction energy per unit area $E(D)$ between two flat surfaces: $$ F(D) = 2\pi R_{\text{eff}} \, E(D) $$ For two crossed cylinders, $R_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt{R_1 R_2}$; for sphere–flat, $R_{\text{eff}} = R$. Applied to vdW forces with $E(D) = -A_{132}/(12\pi D^2)$, the DA yields the standard sphere–flat force law $F(D) = -A_{132}R/(6D^2)$. **Key geometric requirement:** The separation $D$ must be much smaller than the local radii of curvature ($D \ll R$), so that the surfaces can be approximated as locally parallel over the interaction zone. --- ### Question 2. What is a Hamaker constant? Why is its experimental determination non-trivial? [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The **Hamaker constant $A$** (units: J) characterises the magnitude of vdW interactions between two macroscopic bodies. For identical bodies in vacuum, $A_{11} \sim 10^{-19}$ J; for dissimilar bodies across a medium, the combining relation $A_{132} = (\sqrt{A_{11}} - \sqrt{A_{33}})(\sqrt{A_{22}} - \sqrt{A_{33}})$ applies. Experimental determination is non-trivial because: 1. vdW forces are **weak** (pN–nN range) and require ultra-sensitive force transducers. 2. **Surface roughness, contamination, and adsorbed layers** reduce or eliminate vdW attraction. 3. **Retardation effects** at $D > 10$ nm weaken the force below pairwise-additivity predictions. 4. The measurement must be done in a vibration-isolated, thermally stable environment with **sub-nanometre distance control**. --- ## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern: Each module typically offers an internal choice. **Question A** and **Question B** below are independent alternatives; the student attempts one. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Choice 1]** **(a)** Describe with a labelled schematic the working principle of the **Surface Force Apparatus (SFA)** developed by Israelachvili. Discuss how absolute surface separation is determined in this instrument. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Using the Derjaguin Approximation, derive the expression for the vdW force between a sphere of radius $R$ and a flat surface, given that the per-unit-area interaction energy between two parallel plates separated by distance $D$ is $E(D) = -A/(12\pi D^{2})$. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution to Q-A(a) **Principle of SFA:** Two atomically smooth back-silvered mica surfaces (radius $R \approx 1$–$2$ cm) are mounted as crossed cylinders in a sealed, temperature-controlled chamber. The upper surface is rigidly attached to a double-cantilever spring of known stiffness $k$ (typically 50–500 N/m). The lower surface is moved via a three-stage mechanical drive: a coarse micrometer, a differential spring, and a fine piezo / differential transformer (resolution 0.1–1 nm). The force between the surfaces bends the spring; the deflection $\Delta x$ is measured by a sensitive dial gauge or inductance transducer, giving $F = k \Delta x$. **Absolute surface separation (FECO method):** White light is shone normal to the back of the two mica sheets. Multiple-beam interference produces sharp fringes called **Fringes of Equal Chromatic Order (FECO)**. Each fringe has a wavelength position governed by: $$ m \lambda = 2 n_{\text{mica}} d \cos\theta + \phi $$ where $d$ is the local mica thickness, $n_{\text{mica}}$ its refractive index, and $\phi$ a phase change at the silver. As the surfaces approach, the FECO wavelengths shift in a precisely calculable way. From this shift, the gap $D$ between the silvered interfaces is computed with an accuracy of $\pm 0.1$ nm — **without** any piezo calibration. **Advantages of SFA:** - Absolute distance measurement via FECO. - Direct optical visualisation of contact area. - Operates in controlled gas or liquid environments. **Limitations:** - Requires molecularly smooth, optically reflecting surfaces (mica is the standard). - Slow, expert operation; one full $F(D)$ curve takes hours. - Difficult for asymmetric or rough samples. #### Model Solution to Q-A(b) **Starting point:** Energy per unit area between two parallel plates: $$ E(D) = -\dfrac{A_{132}}{12\pi D^{2}} $$ **Step 1 — DA relation between curved-body force and flat-flat energy:** $$ F(D) = 2\pi R_{\text{eff}} \, E(D) $$ For a sphere of radius $R$ interacting with a flat, $R_{\text{eff}} = R$. **Step 2 — Substitution:** $$ F(D) = 2\pi R \left(-\dfrac{A_{132}}{12\pi D^{2}}\right) = -\dfrac{A_{132}R}{6D^{2}} $$ **Step 3 — Sign convention:** The negative sign indicates that the force is *attractive* (tending to pull the sphere and the flat together). **Final boxed result:** $$ \boxed{\;F(D) = -\dfrac{A_{132}R}{6D^{2}}\;} $$ **Derivation note:** The DA requires the local separation profile $h(r) = D + r^{2}/(2R)$ and the integration $\int_0^\infty (r\,dr)/h^{2} = R/D$ — see the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice 2]** **(a)** Explain the **AFM colloidal probe technique** for measuring vdW forces. Compare its advantages and limitations with the SFA. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** In a thin-film balance experiment on a free aqueous film, the equilibrium film thickness $h$ is measured as a function of the applied external pressure $\Delta P$. The data are: | $\Delta P$ (Pa) | 1.0 | 10 | 100 | 1000 | |---|---|---|---|---| | $h$ (nm) | 28.6 | 13.3 | 6.2 | 2.9 | Assuming pure vdW disjoining pressure $\Pi(h) = -A/(6\pi h^{3})$ and that $\Pi(h) = -\Delta P$ at equilibrium, determine the **Hamaker constant** $A$ for the system. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution to Q-B(a) **Principle of AFM colloidal probe:** A micron-sized sphere (silica, polystyrene, gold, etc.) of known radius $R$ is glued to the end of a soft AFM cantilever. The cantilever is approached towards a flat substrate using a piezoelectric scanner. As the sphere approaches, vdW (and other) forces bend the cantilever. A laser beam reflected from the back of the cantilever onto a position-sensitive photodetector (PSD) measures the bending $\Delta x$. The force is then $F = k \Delta x$ where $k$ is the cantilever spring constant (typically 0.01–1 N/m, calibrated by Sader method or thermal noise). **Experimental procedure:** 1. Calibrate cantilever $k$ (Sader–Hutter method). 2. Approach sphere to flat at constant speed; record PSD voltage vs $z$-piezo displacement. 3. Convert to $F(D)$ by subtracting cantilever deflection from the piezo displacement. 4. Fit to $F = -A R/(6 D^{2})$ in the vdW regime (usually $D < 10$ nm for non-retarded regime). **Comparison with SFA:** | Aspect | AFM | SFA | |---|---|---| | Sensitivity | 10 pN (better) | 10 nN | | Distance resolution | 0.1 nm (piezo-based) | 0.1 nm (FECO absolute) | | Surfaces | Spherical (1–10 µm) + flat | Cylindrical (1–2 cm) | | Sample versatility | Wide (rough, biological) | Restricted (molecularly smooth) | | Speed | Faster (minutes) | Slower (hours) | | Optical confirmation | None | Direct visualisation | **Advantages of AFM:** Higher force sensitivity, can probe realistic colloids (rough, biological surfaces), faster turnaround. **Limitations of AFM:** Piezo requires separate calibration, no absolute distance reference, tip-sphere geometry harder to model for highly asymmetric interactions, drift over long times. #### Model Solution to Q-B(b) **Step 1 — Equilibrium condition:** $$ \Pi(h) = -\Delta P $$ The disjoining pressure relation is: $$ \Pi(h) = -\dfrac{A}{6\pi h^{3}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \Delta P = \dfrac{A}{6\pi h^{3}} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for $A$:** $$ A = 6\pi h^{3} \Delta P $$ **Step 3 — Compute $A$ for each data point:** | $\Delta P$ (Pa) | $h$ (m) | $h^{3}$ (m³) | $A = 6\pi h^{3} \Delta P$ (J) | |---|---|---|---| | 1.0 | $2.86 \times 10^{-8}$ | $2.34 \times 10^{-23}$ | $6\pi \times 2.34 \times 10^{-23} \times 1 = 4.41 \times 10^{-22}$ | | 10 | $1.33 \times 10^{-8}$ | $2.35 \times 10^{-24}$ | $6\pi \times 2.35 \times 10^{-24} \times 10 = 4.43 \times 10^{-22}$ | | 100 | $6.20 \times 10^{-9}$ | $2.38 \times 10^{-25}$ | $6\pi \times 2.38 \times 10^{-25} \times 100 = 4.49 \times 10^{-22}$ | | 1000 | $2.90 \times 10^{-9}$ | $2.44 \times 10^{-26}$ | $6\pi \times 2.44 \times 10^{-26} \times 1000 = 4.60 \times 10^{-22}$ | **Step 4 — Average value:** $$ A_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{4.41 + 4.43 + 4.49 + 4.60}{4} \times 10^{-22}\;\text{J} \approx 4.48 \times 10^{-22}\;\text{J} $$ **Step 5 — Validity check:** The plot of $\log(\Delta P)$ vs $\log(h)$ should be linear with slope $-3$. From $h=28.6$ to $2.9$ nm, $h$ changes by factor $9.86$ and $\Delta P$ by $1000$. Slope $= \log(1000)/\log(9.86) = 3/0.994 \approx 3.02$, confirming the $-3$ power law and the consistency of the data. **Final result:** $$ \boxed{\;A \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-22}\;\text{J}\;} $$ This value is typical of a hydrocarbon (low $A$) or a low-polarity system in aqueous medium. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic — Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Confusing $A_{131}$ and $A_{132}$:** A frequently-seen error is using $A_{131}$ (identical bodies across medium 3) when the experimental system actually has dissimilar materials 1 and 2. **Loss: 2 marks.** > > 2. **Forgetting the retardation correction at large $D$:** For separations $D > 10$ nm in air/liquid, the simple $D^{-2}$ law underestimates the force-decay. Always state the validity range of the unretarded formula. > > 3. **Mixing up $R$ and $R_{\text{eff}}$:** For two crossed cylinders, the SFA reports $R = R_1 = R_2$ but the **effective** radius is $R_{\text{eff}} = R$, which equals the geometric radius. For two unequal spheres, $R_{\text{eff}} = R_1 R_2/(R_1 + R_2)$. A wrong substitution gives an $A$ value off by a factor of 2. **Loss: 3 marks.** > > 4. **Confusing sign conventions for $\Pi$:** The disjoining pressure in a free film is **negative** (tensile), so its magnitude equals the applied positive external pressure. Writing $\Pi = +\Delta P$ in a derivation loses 1 mark. > > 5. **Skipping the equilibrium condition in thin-film balance problems:** Always state explicitly: "At mechanical equilibrium, the applied external pressure equals the magnitude of the disjoining pressure" before substituting into the equation. > > 6. **Using $A_{11}$ in vacuum for liquid-medium systems:** This is a glaring conceptual error. Always use $A_{132}$, the effective Hamaker constant of materials 1 and 2 across medium 3. > > 7. **In numerical problems, mixing units:** $h$ must be in **metres** when calculating $A$ in Joules. A common mistake is to leave $h$ in nm, giving $A$ off by a factor of $10^{27}$. **Loss: 3 marks** (full marks for the numerical part). > > 8. **Failing to mention the geometric condition $D \ll R$ when invoking the Derjaguin Approximation:** This is an automatic 1-mark deduction in 7-mark derivations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (read before the exam):** - Van der Waals forces are the **sum of Keesom + Debye + London** contributions, dominated by dispersion. - The **Hamaker constant $A_{132}$** (combining relation) is the practical single parameter used in all colloidal force calculations. - **Force law (sphere–flat):** $F = -A_{132} R / (6 D^{2})$; **flat–flat:** $F/A_{\text{area}} = -A_{132}/(6\pi D^{3})$. - **Disjoining pressure:** $\Pi(D) = -A_{132}/(6\pi D^{3})$. - **Derjaguin Approximation** converts flat-flat energy to sphere-flat force: $F = 2\pi R E(D)$; valid when $D \ll R$. - **Lifshitz theory** predicts $A$ from dielectric spectra; experimentally validated by SFA/AFM/film balance. - **Retardation** reduces the force at $D > 10$ nm. - **SFA (Israelachvili):** Crossed cylinders, FECO absolute distance, $\pm 0.1$ nm resolution. - **AFM colloidal probe:** Sphere-on-cantilever, PSD detection, 10 pN sensitivity, FECO-less so relies on piezo calibration. - **Thin-film balance (Scheludko):** Parallel-plate free film, manostat + micro-interferometry; $\Pi(h) = \Delta P_{\text{ext}}$ at equilibrium. - **Typical $A$ values:** hydrocarbons $\sim 5 \times 10^{-20}$ J; mica in water $\sim 2 \times 10^{-21}$ J; metals $\sim 10^{-19}$ J. - **Combining relation (GMA):** $A_{132} = (\sqrt{A_{11}} - \sqrt{A_{33}})(\sqrt{A_{22}} - \sqrt{A_{33}})$. - **Standard procedure to extract $A$:** (i) measure $F$ vs $D$, (ii) verify slope $-2$ on log–log plot, (iii) compute $A = 6 F D^{2}/R$, (iv) compare with Lifshitz prediction. - **Common experimental artefacts:** surface roughness, contamination, capillary forces in ambient air, piezo drift (AFM), mechanical drift (SFA). - **Engineering applications:** stiction in MEMS, mineral flotation, emulsion stability, pharmaceutical shelf-life, biomolecular adhesion, nanoparticle drug delivery. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Board-Exam Mnemonic — "DARES":** > **D**erjaguin Approximation relates curved-flat to flat-flat forces. > **A**FM, **R**etardation, **E**quilibrium in thin-film balance, **S**urface Force Apparatus. > These five pillars constitute the experimental and theoretical backbone of vdW-force determination in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

topics.title: Estimation of Surface Tension from Hamaker Constant
DLVO Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DLVO Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DLVO Theory** is the classical quantitative framework developed independently by **Derjaguin & Landau (1941)** and **Verwey & Overbeek (1948)** that describes the **stability of colloidal dispersions** by quantitatively balancing two opposing inter-particle forces as a function of separation distance:

$$
V_{T}(H) \;=\; V_{A}(H) \;+\; V_{R}(H)
$$

Where:

- $V_{A}(H)$ = **Attractive potential energy** arising from **van der Waals (London dispersion) forces**
- $V_{R}(H)$ = **Repulsive potential energy** arising from **electrostatic double-layer overlap**
- $H$ = surface-to-surface separation distance between particles
- $V_{T}(H)$ = total interaction potential energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *"DLVO Theory states that the stability of a lyophobic colloidal system is governed by the sum of the van der Waals attractive energy and the electrostatic repulsive energy between particles; coagulation occurs when the kinetic energy of Brownian motion exceeds the maximum of the total potential energy barrier."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Two-Magnet-and-Tape Model"

Imagine two **steel balls** floating on water. Each ball carries a **weak, persistent magnet** (the attractive van der Waals force — always ON, short-ranged, but powerful when very close). At the same time, both balls are wrapped in a **slightly-charged invisible sponge** (the electrostatic double layer — repulsive, longer-ranged, but weak at close range).

| Condition | Analogy | Colloidal Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Sponge (repulsion) is thicker than the gap | Magnets never meet | **Stable colloid** |
| Magnets (attraction) overcome sponge | Balls snap together | **Coagulation** |
| Sponge is compressed by added salt | Squeezed sponges → magnets dominate | **Destabilisation** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two forces act **simultaneously and independently**. It is their **vector sum (algebraic sum, since both act along the line of centres)** that determines whether two colloidal particles will repel, attract, or remain in a metastable trapped state.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are essential for numerical problems under this module:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J K}^{-1}$** |
| $e$ | Elementary charge | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$** |
| $N_A$ | Avogadro number | **$6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$** |
| $\epsilon_0$ | Vacuum permittivity | **$8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F m}^{-1}$** |
| $A_H$ | Hamaker constant | **$10^{-21}$ to $10^{-19}$ J** |
| $\kappa^{-1}$ | Debye length (double-layer thickness) | **1 – 100 nm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the DLVO Interaction

DLVO theory treats colloidal stability as a **two-component force balance**:

### A. The Attractive Component — $V_{A}(H)$

- **Origin:** **London dispersion forces** between fluctuating induced dipoles in adjacent atoms/molecules.
- **Range:** Long-range (~ a few nm), but cumulative across **all atoms** in both particles → total force is strong.
- **Sign:** Always **negative** (attractive).
- **Magnitude parameter:** Governed by the **Hamaker constant $A_H$** (typically $10^{-20}$ to $10^{-19}$ J).

### B. The Repulsive Component — $V_{R}(H)$

- **Origin:** **Electrostatic repulsion** when the **diffuse Gouy-Chapman double layers** of two similarly charged particles overlap.
- **Range:** Exponentially decaying with characteristic length $\kappa^{-1}$ (the **Debye length**).
- **Sign:** Always **positive** (repulsive).
- **Magnitude parameter:** Governed by surface potential $\psi_0$ (or zeta potential $\zeta$) and ionic strength $I$.

## 2.2 The Total Interaction Energy Curve

Plotting $V_{T}(H)$ versus $H$ produces a **characteristic energy landscape** containing several textbook features:

1. **Primary Minimum ($V_{primary}$):** Very deep well at small $H$ (~ 0.5 nm). Particles here are **irreversibly coagulated** (primary coagulation).
2. **Primary Maximum / Energy Barrier ($V_{max}$):** The peak that **kinetically stabilises** the colloid. Particles must overcome it via Brownian (thermal) energy.
3. **Secondary Minimum ($V_{secondary}$):** Shallow well at larger $H$ (~ 5–20 nm). Trapped particles show **reversible flocculation** (e.g., sedimentation in paints).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Favourite Concept:** When $V_{max} \gg k_B T$ (~25.7 mJ at 298 K), the colloid is **kinetically stable**. When $V_{max} \to 0$, the system **coagulates rapidly** — this is the basis of the **Critical Coagulation Concentration (CCC)**.

## 2.3 The Schulze-Hardy Rule

A cornerstone empirical-cum-theoretical result of DLVO is the **Schulze-Hardy Rule**, which states that the **Critical Coagulation Concentration (CCC)** of an indifferent electrolyte is:

$$
\text{CCC} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{z^{6}}
$$

where $z$ is the **valency of the counter-ion**. Thus:
- Monovalent ($z=1$): CCC $\propto 1$
- Divalent ($z=2$): CCC $\propto 1/64$
- Trivalent ($z=3$): CCC $\propto 1/729$

A trivalent ion is therefore ~**64–100× more effective** at coagulating a colloid than a monovalent ion.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_T(H) = V_A(H) + V_R(H)$ | Fundamental DLVO superposition | $V$ in J; $H$ in m |
| 2 | $V_A(H) = -\dfrac{A_H R}{12 H}$ | Sphere–flat attraction | $R$ = particle radius |
| 3 | $V_A(H) = -\dfrac{A_H}{6}\left[\dfrac{2R^2}{r^2-4R^2}+\dfrac{2R^2}{r^2}+\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r^2-4R^2}{r^2}\right)\right]$ | Sphere–sphere attraction | $r$ = centre-to-centre distance |
| 4 | $V_A(H) = -\dfrac{A_H}{12\pi}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{H^2}+\dfrac{1}{(H+2R)^2}-\dfrac{2}{(H+R)^2}\right]$ | Sphere–sphere (simplified limit) | Approximation at $H \ll R$ |
| 5 | $\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon \epsilon_0 k_B T}{2 N_A e^2 \sum c_i z_i^2}}$ | Debye length | $c_i$ = ionic conc.; $z_i$ = valency |
| 6 | $V_R(H) = \dfrac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa^2}\exp(-\kappa H)$ | Repulsion between two spheres | $\gamma = \tanh(ze\psi_0/4k_BT)$ |
| 7 | $V_R(H) = \dfrac{32 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa}\exp(-\kappa H)$ | Repulsion at $H \ll \kappa^{-1}$ | Thin double-layer limit |
| 8 | $V_{max} \approx \dfrac{0.42 \, A_H R}{H_{max}^{1.2}}$ (approx.) | Height of energy barrier | — |
| 9 | $\text{CCC} \propto z^{-6}$ | Schulze–Hardy rule | Counter-ion valency |
| 10 | $W = \dfrac{K_{fast}}{K_{slow}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \kappa a}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{k_B T}\right)$ | Stability ratio | $K$ = rate constants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** DLVO theory underpins formulation of **pharmaceutical suspensions**, **paint stability**, **ceramic slurry processing**, **wastewater treatment via coagulation–flocculation**, and **inkjet ink design**.

## 2.5 Non-DLVO Forces (Brief)

Modern extensions recognise **additional non-DLVO forces** such as:
- **Solvation / hydration repulsion** (short-range, exponential)
- **Steric repulsion** (polymer-coated particles)
- **Hydrophobic attraction**
- **Depletion attraction**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests DLVO classical form; non-DLVO forces are mentioned only as supplementary awareness in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — van der Waals Attraction Between Two Flat Plates (Lifshitz Summation)

We begin with the unretarded London pair potential between two atoms:

$$
u(r) \;=\; -\frac{C}{r^{6}}
$$

where $C$ is the London dispersion coefficient and $r$ is the inter-atomic separation.

### Step 1 — Pairwise Summation (Hamaker Approach)

For two semi-infinite flat plates of **identical material** separated by a gap $H$, integrate the atom-pair potential over all atoms in both half-spaces.

Consider an atom in plate 1 at distance $x$ from the interface, interacting with all atoms in plate 2 located at distance $(H + x')$ where $x'$ is measured from the interface of plate 2.

The number density of atoms is $n$, so the integration is:

$$
V_A(H) \;=\; -n^2 \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C}{\left(H + x + x'\right)^{6}} \, dx' \, dx
$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Inner Integral

Let $u = H + x + x'$, so $du = dx'$. When $x' = 0$, $u = H + x$; when $x' \to \infty$, $u \to \infty$.

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{dx'}{(H+x+x')^{6}} \;=\; \left[-\frac{1}{5(H+x+x')^{5}}\right]_{0}^{\infty} \;=\; \frac{1}{5(H+x)^{5}}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Outer Integral

$$
V_A(H) \;=\; -\frac{C n^2}{5} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{(H+x)^{5}}
$$

Let $u = H+x$, $du = dx$. Limits: $u = H$ to $u = \infty$:

$$
\int_{H}^{\infty} \frac{du}{u^{5}} \;=\; \left[-\frac{1}{4 u^{4}}\right]_{H}^{\infty} \;=\; \frac{1}{4 H^{4}}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Plate–Plate Expression

Substituting back:

$$
V_A(H) \;=\; -\frac{C n^2}{20 H^{4}}
$$

By defining the **Hamaker constant** for identical materials across vacuum:

$$
A_H \;=\; \pi^{2} C n^{2}
$$

we obtain the classical DLVO result:

$$
\boxed{V_A(H) \;=\; -\frac{A_H}{12 \pi \, H^{2}}}
$$

This is the **attractive potential per unit area** between two flat plates. The $H^{-2}$ dependence explains why van der Waals forces in colloids are much stronger than in atomic systems — the integrated bulk effect amplifies the weak $r^{-6}$ atomic interaction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Sphere–Sphere Attraction via the Derjaguin Approximation

The **Derjaguin Approximation (DA)** relates the sphere–sphere interaction to the flat-plate interaction by summing infinitesimally thin annular rings.

For two identical spheres of radius $R$ with surface-to-surface separation $H$, and centre-to-centre distance $r = 2R + H$:

$$
V_{sphere}(H) \;=\; 2 \pi R_{eff} \, W_{plate}(H)
$$

where $R_{eff}$ is the effective radius. For identical spheres, $R_{eff} = R/2$, but using $r$ as centre-to-centre:

$$
V_{A,\,sphere}(H) \;=\; \pi R \int_{H}^{\infty} V_{A,\,plate}(h) \, dh
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Plate Potential

$$
V_{A,\,sphere}(H) \;=\; \pi R \int_{H}^{\infty} \left(-\frac{A_H}{12 \pi h^{2}}\right) dh \;=\; -\frac{A_H R}{12} \int_{H}^{\infty} h^{-2} \, dh
$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the Integral

$$
\int_{H}^{\infty} h^{-2} \, dh \;=\; \left[-\frac{1}{h}\right]_{H}^{\infty} \;=\; \frac{1}{H}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Sphere–Sphere Attractive Result

$$
\boxed{V_{A,\,sphere}(H) \;=\; -\frac{A_H R}{12 H}}
$$

This is the **most-used form** of the van der Waals attraction in DLVO calculations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Double-Layer Repulsion (Identical Spheres)

For two spheres of radius $R$ with constant surface potential $\psi_0$, in a symmetric $z:z$ electrolyte of bulk concentration $n_0$, the linearised Poisson–Boltzmann solution gives the interaction free energy (HHF expression):

$$
V_R(H) \;=\; \frac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa^2}\exp(-\kappa H)
$$

where $\gamma = \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{ze\psi_0}{4k_BT}\right)$.

### Step 8 — Identify the Two Limiting Cases

**Case (i) — Small particles, $\kappa R \ll 1$ (Hückel limit):**

$$
V_R(H) \;=\; \frac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa^2}\exp(-\kappa H) \;\;\text{(dominant for } R \to \text{small)}
$$

**Case (ii) — Large particles, $\kappa R \gg 1$ (Smoluchowski limit):**

Using the **Derjaguin approximation**, the sphere–sphere repulsion becomes:

$$
V_R(H) \;=\; \frac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa}\exp(-\kappa H)
$$

The factor $\kappa$ (instead of $\kappa^{2}$) arises because the effective interaction area grows with $R$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Total DLVO Energy and Identification of the Energy Barrier

### Step 9 — Sum the Two Components

$$
\boxed{V_T(H) \;=\; \underbrace{-\frac{A_H R}{12 H}}_{V_A} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa^2}\exp(-\kappa H)}_{V_R}}
$$

### Step 10 — Locate the Energy Barrier

At the maximum, $\dfrac{dV_T}{dH} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dV_T}{dH} \;=\; \frac{A_H R}{12 H^{2}} \;-\; \frac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma^2}{\kappa}\exp(-\kappa H) \;=\; 0
$$

Solving numerically (no closed-form analytic solution) gives $H_{max}$ and consequently $V_{max}$.

### Step 11 — Critical Coagulation Concentration (CCC) Condition

At the CCC, the energy barrier **vanishes** ($V_{max} \to 0$), meaning:

$$
\frac{dV_T}{dH} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \frac{d^{2}V_T}{dH^{2}} \le 0
$$

Applying this to the high-potential limit ($\psi_0 \gg 25$ mV so $\gamma \to 1$) yields:

$$
\text{CCC} \;=\; \frac{C \, \epsilon^{3}(k_B T)^{5}\gamma^{4}}{e^{2} z^{2} A_H^{2}}
$$

which reduces to:

$$
\text{CCC} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{z^{6}}
$$

recovering the **Schulze–Hardy rule** exactly.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Two spherical colloidal particles of radius $R = 100$ nm are dispersed in a $10^{-3}$ M NaCl solution at $T = 298$ K. The surface potential is $\psi_0 = 50$ mV, Hamaker constant $A_H = 10^{-20}$ J. Calculate (a) the Debye length and (b) the total interaction energy at $H = 2$ nm.

**Solution:**

**(a) Debye length:**

$$
\kappa^{-1} \;=\; \frac{0.304}{\sqrt{I}}\ \text{nm} \quad (I = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2)
$$

For NaCl: $I = 10^{-3}$ M, so:

$$
\kappa^{-1} \;=\; \frac{0.304}{\sqrt{10^{-3}}} \;\approx\; 9.6\ \text{nm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \kappa \;\approx\; 1.04 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**(b) Compute $\gamma$:**

$$
\gamma \;=\; \tanh\!\left(\frac{ze\psi_0}{4 k_B T}\right) \;=\; \tanh\!\left(\frac{1 \times 1.602\times 10^{-19} \times 0.050}{4 \times 1.381\times 10^{-23} \times 298}\right)
$$

$$
\gamma \;=\; \tanh(0.486) \;\approx\; 0.452
$$

**(c) Compute $V_A$:**

$$
V_A \;=\; -\frac{A_H R}{12 H} \;=\; -\frac{10^{-20} \times 10^{-7}}{12 \times 2\times 10^{-9}} \;\approx\; -4.17 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J}
$$

**(d) Compute $V_R$:**

$$
V_R \;=\; \frac{64 \pi (10^{-7})(10^{23.5})(1.381\times 10^{-23})(298)(0.452)^{2}}{(1.04\times 10^{8})^{2}}\exp(-1.04\times 10^{8}\times 2\times 10^{-9})
$$

$$
V_R \;\approx\; 1.92 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J}
$$

**(e) Total:**

$$
V_T(H=2\,\text{nm}) \;\approx\; -2.25 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J} \;\approx\; -5.5\ k_B T
$$

The negative value indicates **net attraction at 2 nm** — the barrier has been overcome. This matches the prediction that at $10^{-3}$ M, the system is near the CCC for monovalent ions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DLVO Interaction Energy Landscape — Functional Flow Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Particle Approach H Decreasing] --> B{V_A dominates?}
    B -- Yes at H smaller than 1 nm --> C[Primary Minimum V primary deep]
    C --> C1[Irreversible Coagulation]
    B -- No --> D{V_R dominates?}
    D -- Yes at H between 2 and 10 nm --> E[Energy Barrier V max]
    E --> E1[Stable Colloid V max greater than 15 kT]
    E --> E2[Metastable Colloid V max between 5 and 15 kT]
    D -- Balanced --> F[Secondary Minimum V secondary shallow]
    F --> F1[Reversible Flocculation]
    F1 --> F2[Sedimentation without true coagulation]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the DLVO Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Parameters: R, T, A_H, psi_0, I, z] --> P1[Module A: van der Waals V_A Computation]
    IN --> P2[Module B: Double-Layer Repulsion V_R Computation]
    P1 --> P3[Summation Engine V_T = V_A + V_R]
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4[Energy Curve Generator dV_T versus dH]
    P4 --> P5[Barrier Detector dV_T/dH = 0]
    P4 --> P6[Minimum Detector]
    P5 --> OUT1{Stability Verdict}
    P6 --> OUT2[CCC Estimation via Schulze-Hardy]
    OUT1 --> OUT3[Output: Stable / Coagulated / Flocculated]
    OUT2 --> OUT3
```

## 4.3 Process Decision Tree — Coagulation Kinetics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Two Colloidal Particles Approach] --> S2{Kinetic Energy kT greater than V_max?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Particles cross barrier]
    S3 --> S4[Trapped in Primary Minimum]
    S4 --> S5[Irreversible Coagulation Fast]
    S2 -- No --> S6[Particles reflected]
    S6 --> S7[Stable Colloid Maintained]
    S1 --> S8{Trapped in Secondary Minimum?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Reversible Flocculation Slow]
    S8 -- No --> S7
```

## 4.4 Effect of Electrolyte Concentration on Total Energy Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Low I: thick double layer] --> R1[V_R dominates at all H]
    R1 --> R2[Large V_max: Stable]
    Q2[Critical I: CCC] --> S1[V_max → 0]
    S1 --> S2[Coagulation begins]
    Q3[High I: compressed double layer] --> T1[V_A dominates]
    T1 --> T2[Rapid Coagulation]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
State and explain the **Schulze-Hardy rule** for the coagulation of lyophobic colloids. Mention its significance in water treatment.

**Model Answer:**

The Schulze-Hardy rule is an empirical rule, derivable from DLVO theory, which states that the **critical coagulation concentration (CCC)** of an indifferent electrolyte for a given colloid is **inversely proportional to the sixth power of the valency** of the counter-ion:

$$
\text{CCC} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{z^{6}}
$$

For example, for a negatively charged colloid (e.g., arsenious sulfide sol), the CCC values follow the order:
- Al$^{3+}$ (trivalent): $\approx 0.1$ mM
- Mg$^{2+}$ (divalent): $\approx 5$ mM  
- Na$^{+}$ (monovalent): $\approx 50$ mM

**Significance:** It guides the **selection of coagulants in water treatment** — trivalent ions like Al$^{3+}$ and Fe$^{3+}$ (from alum) are preferred due to their efficiency at very low doses, drastically reducing chemical cost and sludge volume.

*(3 marks — definition 1 mark, proportionality 1 mark, application 1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
Define the **Hamaker constant** and state its typical order of magnitude for colloidal systems interacting across water.

**Model Answer:**

The **Hamaker constant ($A_H$)** is a material property that quantifies the strength of van der Waals attractive interactions between two bodies of a given material separated by a vacuum (or another medium). It is defined as:

$$
A_H \;=\; \pi^{2} C \rho^{2}
$$

where $C$ is the London dispersion coefficient and $\rho$ is the number density of atoms.

For colloidal systems across water:
- Vacuum: $A_H \sim 10^{-19}$ J
- Across water: $A_H \sim 10^{-20}$ to $10^{-21}$ J

The reduction is because the **Lifshitz approach** gives an effective Hamaker constant:

$$
A_{131} \;\approx\; \left(\sqrt{A_{11}} - \sqrt{A_{33}}\right)^{2}
$$

where medium 3 is water. Since $A_{33}$ for water is comparable to $A_{11}$ for many solids, the effective value is much smaller.

*(3 marks — definition 1 mark, formula 1 mark, magnitude 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **ONE full question**; internal choice is given.

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **van der Waals attractive potential** between two identical spherical colloidal particles using the Derjaguin approximation, starting from the plate–plate interaction $V_A(H) = -\dfrac{A_H}{12\pi H^{2}}$.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The Derjaguin approximation relates sphere–sphere to plate–plate via:

$$
V_{sphere}(H) \;=\; \pi R_{eff} \int_{H}^{\infty} V_{plate}(h) \, dh
$$

For two identical spheres, $R_{eff} = R$.

**Step 2:** Substitute $V_{plate}(h) = -\dfrac{A_H}{12 \pi h^{2}}$:

$$
V_{A,sphere}(H) \;=\; -\frac{\pi R A_H}{12 \pi} \int_{H}^{\infty} h^{-2} \, dh
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$
\int_{H}^{\infty} h^{-2} \, dh \;=\; \frac{1}{H}
$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$
\boxed{V_{A,sphere}(H) \;=\; -\frac{A_H R}{12 H}}
$$

**Discussion:** The result is valid for $H \ll R$ (unretarded regime). The $H^{-1}$ dependence (weaker than the $H^{-2}$ for plates) arises from the curvature; for large separations ($H \gg R$), retardation effects reduce the force further.

---

**(b)** Calculate the **double-layer repulsion** between two identical spheres of radius $R = 50$ nm, surface potential $\psi_0 = 75$ mV, dispersed in $5 \times 10^{-4}$ M CaCl$_2$ at $T = 298$ K. Take $\epsilon_r = 78.5$ and $A_H = 10^{-20}$ J. Compute $V_T$ at $H = 3$ nm and comment on stability.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Debye length.** For $5\times 10^{-4}$ M CaCl$_2$:

$$
I \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\left[5\times 10^{-4}\times 4 + 10^{-3}\times 1\right] \;=\; 1.5\times 10^{-3}\ \text{M}
$$

$$
\kappa^{-1} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{78.5 \times 8.854\times 10^{-12} \times 1.381\times 10^{-23} \times 298}{2 \times 1500 \times (1.602\times 10^{-19})^{2}}}
$$

Computing:

$$
\kappa^{-1} \;\approx\; 7.8\ \text{nm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \kappa \;\approx\; 1.28 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\gamma$:**

$$
\gamma \;=\; \tanh\!\left(\frac{2 \times 1.602\times 10^{-19} \times 0.075}{4 \times 1.381\times 10^{-23} \times 298}\right) \;=\; \tanh(1.46) \;\approx\; 0.899
$$

**Step 3: Compute $V_A$ at $H = 3$ nm:**

$$
V_A \;=\; -\frac{10^{-20} \times 50 \times 10^{-9}}{12 \times 3 \times 10^{-9}} \;\approx\; -1.39 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $V_R$ at $H = 3$ nm (using $\kappa R = 6.4 \gg 1$, Smoluchowski limit):**

$$
V_R \;=\; \frac{64 \pi (50\times 10^{-9}) (1500 \times 6.022\times 10^{23}) (1.381\times 10^{-23})(298)(0.899)^{2}}{1.28 \times 10^{8}}\exp(-0.384)
$$

$$
V_R \;\approx\; 2.05 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J}
$$

**Step 5: Total:**

$$
V_T(H=3\,\text{nm}) \;\approx\; +0.66 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J} \;\approx\; +1.6\,k_BT
$$

**Comment:** Since $V_T > 0$, a small residual barrier exists, but it is **less than $5\,k_BT$** — the system is **marginally stable / near coagulation**. Adding more CaCl$_2$ will easily push it to the CCC (predicted by Schulze-Hardy: CCC for Ca$^{2+}$ ~64× smaller than for Na$^{+}$).

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **total interaction energy curve** in DLVO theory, labelling the **primary minimum, primary maximum (energy barrier), and secondary minimum**. Discuss the conditions under which **secondary minimum flocculation** is observed.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The total interaction potential $V_T(H) = V_A(H) + V_R(H)$ plotted against separation $H$ exhibits a characteristic multi-feature landscape:

**1. Primary Minimum (deep well at $H < 1$ nm):**
- $V_A \propto -1/H$ dominates over $V_R \propto \exp(-\kappa H)$.
- Particles reaching this well are **irreversibly coagulated**.
- Activation energy of escape is essentially infinite.

**2. Primary Maximum / Energy Barrier (peak at $H \sim 1$–5 nm):**
- $V_R$ slightly exceeds $V_A$ at this distance.
- Magnitude $V_{max}$ determines **kinetic stability**.
- Rule of thumb: $V_{max} > 15\,k_BT$ → stable for months/years; $V_{max} < 5\,k_BT$ → coagulates in seconds.

**3. Secondary Minimum (shallow well at $H \sim 5$–20 nm):**
- At larger separations, $V_A$ again slightly exceeds the exponentially decaying $V_R$.
- Depth $V_{secondary}$ is typically $1$–$10\,k_BT$.
- Particles trapped here form **reversible flocs** that can be redispersed by agitation or dilution.

**Conditions favouring secondary minimum flocculation:**
- **Large particle size** ($R > 1\ \mu$m) — the $V_A \propto R$ scaling makes the secondary well deep.
- **High Hamaker constant** materials.
- **Moderate electrolyte concentration** (not so high that $V_{max}$ vanishes).
- **Asymmetric particle shapes** (rods, plates) experience stronger secondary attraction.

**Engineering example:** Sedimentation in paints, thixotropy in gels.

---

**(b)** A colloidal dispersion of gold nanoparticles ($R = 10$ nm, $A_H = 2.5\times 10^{-19}$ J, $\psi_0 = -30$ mV) is destabilised by adding KNO$_3$. Estimate the **critical coagulation concentration (CCC)** using the Schulze-Hardy rule, given that the experimentally measured CCC for KNO$_3$ is 25 mM. What concentration of Al(NO$_3$)$_3$ would give the same coagulation effect?

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply Schulze-Hardy rule:

$$
\text{CCC} \;\propto\; z^{-6}
$$

For K$^{+}$ ($z=1$): CCC$_K$ = 25 mM.

**Step 2:** For Al$^{3+}$ ($z=3$):

$$
\frac{\text{CCC}_{Al}}{\text{CCC}_{K}} \;=\; \left(\frac{z_K}{z_{Al}}\right)^{6} \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{6} \;=\; \frac{1}{729}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$
\text{CCC}_{Al} \;=\; \frac{25\ \text{mM}}{729} \;\approx\; 0.034\ \text{mM} \;\approx\; 34\ \mu\text{M}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify using DLVO framework: At CCC, $V_{max} \to 0$, so:

$$
\text{CCC} \;\approx\; \frac{C \epsilon^{3}(k_B T)^{5}\gamma^{4}}{e^{2} z^{2} A_H^{2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{CCC} \propto z^{-2}\cdot z^{-4} = z^{-6}
$$

(Note: the $z^{-2}$ from Debye length and $z^{-4}$ from the surface potential term.)

**Answer:** A concentration of approximately **$34\ \mu$M** Al(NO$_3$)$_3$ is required — illustrating the **dramatic efficiency** of trivalent ions in destabilising colloids.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Valuation Pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** — $V_A$ is always **negative**, $V_R$ always **positive**. Many students write the magnitude without sign, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Debye length** $\kappa^{-1}$ with $\kappa$ — these are reciprocal. Units of $\kappa^{-1}$ are in **nm**, while $\kappa$ is in **m$^{-1}$**.
> 3. **Using the wrong formula** for sphere–sphere repulsion: use the **HHF (Hunter–Hölyer–Fowler)** form, not the simple linear superposition of Coulomb potentials.
> 4. **Ignoring the Derjaguin approximation limits** — the formula $V_A = -A_H R / 12H$ is valid only for $H \ll R$. For $H > 0.1 R$, **retardation corrections** must be added.
> 5. **Miscalculating CCC** — students often write CCC $\propto z^{-2}$ instead of $z^{-6}$. This costs full marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert units** — $A_H$ in J, $R$ in m, $H$ in m. Mixing nm and m gives errors of $\sim 10^{18}$.
> 7. **Missing the $64\pi$ factor** — the HHF expression has a numerical prefactor of $64\pi$, not $32\pi$ or $16\pi$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **DLVO = $V_A + V_R$** (algebraic sum along line of centres).
- ✅ **$V_A$** comes from London dispersion; **always attractive, $\propto -1/H$ for spheres.**
- ✅ **$V_R$** comes from double-layer overlap; **always repulsive, exponential decay with $\kappa^{-1}$.**
- ✅ **Hamaker constant** $A_H \sim 10^{-20}$ J across water; positive-definite material property.
- ✅ **Debye length** $\kappa^{-1} = \dfrac{0.304}{\sqrt{I}}$ nm at 25 °C in water.
- ✅ **Primary minimum** = irreversible coagulation; **secondary minimum** = reversible flocculation.
- ✅ **Energy barrier $V_{max} > 15\,k_BT$** ⇒ kinetically stable colloid.
- ✅ **Schulze-Hardy rule:** $\text{CCC} \propto z^{-6}$ — trivalent ions $\sim 64$–$729\times$ more efficient than monovalent.
- ✅ **Stability ratio** $W = \dfrac{1}{2\kappa a}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{k_BT}\right)$ — quantitative measure.
- ✅ **Derjaguin approximation** links flat-plate and sphere-sphere geometries.
- ✅ **HHF formula** for $V_R$ is the **standard exam-ready expression** for identical spheres.
- ✅ **Non-DLVO forces** (solvation, steric, hydrophobic) extend the theory beyond pure electrostatic + van der Waals.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** wastewater treatment (alum coagulation), pharmaceutical suspensions, ceramic processing, paint formulation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non DLVO Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non DLVO Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Non-DLVO Forces** are interparticle interaction forces in colloidal dispersions that operate beyond the classical framework of the **Derjaguin–Landau–Verwey–Overbeek (DLVO) theory**. While classical DLVO theory accounts for only two forces—**electrostatic double-layer repulsion** ($V_R$) and **van der Waals attraction** ($V_A$)—it consistently fails at very small interparticle separations (typically $d < 5\text{–}10$ nm) and in media containing polymers, surfactants, or structured solvents. Non-DLVO forces fill this critical gap.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Official Definition**
> *Non-DLVO forces are short-range and sometimes long-range interparticle forces arising from solvation (hydration), hydrophobic interactions, polymer-mediated effects (steric, bridging, depletion), magnetic effects, and structural correlations, which either augment or oppose the classical DLVO pair potential and ultimately determine the true stability, structure, and rheology of colloidal systems.*

The **total interaction energy** in a real colloidal system is therefore:

$$V_{total} = \underbrace{V_A + V_R}_{\text{DLVO}} + \underbrace{\sum V_{non-DLVO}}_{\text{Forces beyond DLVO}}$$

$$V_{non-DLVO} = V_{hydration} + V_{hydrophobic} + V_{steric} + V_{bridging} + V_{depletion} + V_{structural} + \dots$$

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy — The "Hidden Conductor" of an Orchestra**
> Imagine an orchestra (the colloid) where DLVO theory only accounts for the violins (attraction) and the trumpets (electrostatic repulsion). The complete musical harmony—the actual stability behavior of the colloid—also depends on the flutes, cellos, and percussion (hydration, hydrophobic, steric, depletion forces). Non-DLVO forces are the **hidden instruments** whose presence is often only noticed when the violins and trumpets fail to explain why the music is unstable or unexpectedly rigid.

## 1.2 Why DLVO Alone Fails — The Historical Motivation

In the **1960s–1980s**, Israelachvili, Ninham, Pashley, and Christenson used the **Surface Forces Apparatus (SFA)** and **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM)** to directly measure forces between mica sheets in liquids. They observed:

- An **extra short-range repulsive force** of exponential form in water → *hydration force*.
- A **long-range attractive force** between hydrophobic surfaces far exceeding van der Waals predictions → *hydrophobic force*.
- **Oscillatory forces** with period equal to molecular diameter in structured solvents → *structural/solvation force*.
- **Strong attractions** when polymer chains spanned between particles → *bridging force*.

These findings are **inconsistent with the universal $1/d^6$ van der Waals decay** and the **Yukawa-type** exponential decay of double-layer forces, leading to the formal extension of DLVO into the **Extended DLVO (EDLVO / XDLVO)** framework.

## 1.3 Classification of Non-DLVO Forces

| Sl. No. | Force Category | Physical Origin | Sign of Force | Typical Range (nm) |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Hydration / Solvation | Water structuring on hydrophilic surfaces | Repulsive (+) | 0.5 – 3 |
| 2 | Hydrophobic | Entropic gain from cavitation, water restructuring | Attractive (−) | 1 – 100 |
| 3 | Steric | Compression of adsorbed polymer layers | Repulsive (+) | 2 – 50 |
| 4 | Polymer Bridging | Polymer adsorbed on two particles simultaneously | Attractive (−) | 1 – 30 |
| 5 | Depletion | Osmotic pressure from non-adsorbing polymer/surfactant micelles | Attractive (−) | 1 – 50 |
| 6 | Structural / Oscillatory | Layering of solvent molecules | Repulsive (+) | 0.2 – 5 |
| 7 | Magnetic / Electric | Permanent dipoles in ferrofluids | Both | Long range |
| 8 | Thermal Undulation | Fluctuation of fluid/fluid interfaces | Repulsive (+) | 1 – 10 |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT863 Module 4)**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires a qualitative and quantitative treatment of **hydration forces, hydrophobic forces, steric and polymer-bridging forces, depletion forces, and structural forces**, including their range, decay law, temperature dependence, and role in colloidal stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydration Forces (Solvation Forces)

### Physical Origin
When two **hydrophilic** surfaces (e.g., silica, mica, oxide particles in water) approach each other, the strongly adsorbed and **H-bonded water layers** must be progressively desorbed. This requires extra work, leading to a **short-range exponentially decaying repulsion**.

### Mathematical Form
Hydration repulsion is universally described as:

$$F_{hyd}(d) = F_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{d}{\lambda_{hyd}}\right)$$

$$V_{hyd}(d) = F_0 \lambda_{hyd} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{d}{\lambda_{hyd}}\right)$$

where:
- $F_0$ is the pre-exponential force constant ($\sim 0.01\text{–}10$ N/m).
- $\lambda_{hyd}$ is the **decay length** ($\sim 0.2\text{–}1.0$ nm for water).
- $d$ is the surface-to-surface separation.

### Key Physics
- **Temperature:** Force increases with $T$ (more ordered water at higher $T$).
- **Ion specificity:** Follows **Hofmeister series**; $\text{Li}^+ > \text{Na}^+ > \text{K}^+ > \text{Cs}^+$.
- **Surface chemistry:** Stronger for highly hydroxylated, freshly cleaved surfaces.
- **Engineering role:** Prevents aggregation of **silica, alumina, titanium dioxide, and iron oxide** dispersions in water-based paints, ceramics, and pharmaceutical suspensions.

> [!NOTE] **Why hydration force is NOT a double-layer force?**
> Hydration force persists even at **very high ionic strengths (1 M NaCl)** where double-layer forces are completely screened. It also has a much **shorter decay length** ($\sim 0.3$ nm) than the Debye length ($\sim 1$ nm at 0.1 M). Hence it is treated as a distinct non-DLVO contribution.

## 2.2 Hydrophobic Forces

### Physical Origin
Two **hydrophobic** surfaces in water induce the surrounding water to form **clathrate-like (iceberg) cages** of high entropy cost. When the surfaces come together, these cages overlap and the trapped water molecules are released, **gaining entropy** and yielding a long-range **attractive force**.

### Mathematical Form — Double Exponential Decay

$$F_{hp}(d) = C_1 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{d}{D_1}\right) + C_2 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{d}{D_2}\right)$$

where:
- $C_1$ and $C_2$ are pre-exponential constants ($\text{mN/m}$).
- $D_1 \sim 1\text{–}2$ nm (short-range part).
- $D_2 \sim 5\text{–}100$ nm (long-range "true" hydrophobic part).

### Key Physics
- **Temperature:** Decreases with increasing $T$ (opposite trend to hydration).
- **pH:** Stronger at pH near isoelectric point; weaker when surfaces are charged.
- **Surface chemistry:** Enhanced by surface roughness and **nanobubbles** (still debated).
- **Engineering role:** Drives **self-assembly of hydrophobic drug molecules**, **flocculation in water-treatment**, **protein folding**, and **bioseparation**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Van der Waals vs. Hydrophobic — A Critical Distinction**
> Van der Waals force decays as $1/d^3$ (per energy) or $1/d^4$ (per force between flat plates). The **long-range hydrophobic component** with $D_2 \sim 10\text{–}100$ nm decays far more slowly than van der Waals, which is the principal experimental anomaly that DLVO cannot explain.

## 2.3 Steric Forces (Polymeric Stabilization)

### Physical Origin
When surfaces are grafted or adsorbed with **polymer chains**, the chains extend into the solvent creating a "brush" or "mushroom" configuration. On close approach, the chains **lose configurational entropy** and may also experience **excluded-volume (osmotic) repulsion** if in good solvent.

### Mathematical Forms

**(a) At low surface coverage (mushroom regime, Alexander–de Gennes):**

$$F_{steric}(d) \approx \frac{k_B T}{s^3} \left[\left(\frac{2L}{d}\right)^{9/4} - \left(\frac{d}{2L}\right)^{3/4}\right] \quad \text{for } d < 2L$$

**(b) At high surface coverage (dense brush regime):**

$$F_{brush}(d) \approx \frac{k_B T}{s^3} \left[\left(\frac{2L}{d}\right)^{8/3} - 1\right] \quad \text{for } d < 2L$$

where:
- $L$ is the brush thickness / chain end-to-end distance.
- $s$ is the **mean grafting spacing** between polymer chains.
- $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant $= 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

### Key Physics
- **Good solvent (Flory $\chi < 0.5$):** Repulsive (steric stabilization).
- **Bad solvent ($\chi > 0.5$):** Attractive (polymer-induced flocculation).
- **Theta solvent ($\chi = 0.5$):** No net steric interaction.
- **Engineering role:** Basis of **non-aqueous paint formulations, sterically stabilized drug nanoparticles (Stealth liposomes, PEGylated NPs), and Pickering emulsions**.

> [!NOTE] **Why steric forces are temperature- and solvent-dependent?**
> The Flory–Huggins parameter $\chi$ varies with $T$ and solvent quality. A polymer that sterically stabilizes in ethanol may flocculate in cyclohexane. This is **engineered in pharmaceutical formulations** to trigger drug release at body temperature.

## 2.4 Polymer Bridging Forces

### Physical Origin
A **single high-molecular-weight polymer chain** adsorbs on **two (or more) particles** simultaneously, effectively "stitching" them together. The force is attractive and strongly dependent on the polymer molecular weight and surface coverage.

### Mathematical Form
At low surface coverage $\theta$ (fraction of surface covered):

$$F_{bridge}(d) \approx -2 \pi R \, \Pi_{polymer} \left[1 - \frac{d}{2L_g}\right] \quad \text{for } d < 2L_g$$

where $L_g$ is the radius of gyration of the adsorbed polymer and $\Pi_{polymer}$ is the osmotic pressure of adsorbed segments.

### Key Physics
- **High MW polymers** → longer bridges → stronger attraction.
- **Low surface coverage** → bridging dominates; **high coverage** → steric repulsion dominates.
- **pH and ionic strength** control adsorption conformation.
- **Engineering role:** Used in **flocculation (water treatment, mining, paper making)** and in **bridging flocculation design**.

## 2.5 Depletion Forces

### Physical Origin
When a colloidal dispersion contains **non-adsorbing polymer or surfactant micelles** in the continuous phase, two particles approaching each other exclude the free polymer/micelle from the gap between them. This creates an **osmotic pressure imbalance** that pushes the particles together.

### Asakura–Oosawa (AO) Model

$$\Pi_{depletion} = -\rho_p k_B T \left(1 + \frac{3}{2}\frac{R_g}{R_p} + \dots \right) \quad \text{(polymer as hard sphere of } R_g\text{)}$$

For two **flat plates** at separation $d < 2 R_g$:

$$F_{dep}(d) = -\Pi_{polymer} \cdot V_{overlap}(d) \quad \text{where } V_{overlap} = $ excluded volume$$

For two **spheres** of radius $R$ (Asakura–Oosawa):

$$F_{dep}(d) = -2 \pi R \, \Pi_{polymer} \left(\frac{2 R_g - d}{2}\right)^2 \left(3 R_g + \frac{3R}{2} - \frac{d}{2}\right)$$

$$\approx -2 \pi R \, R_g \, \Pi_{polymer} \left(2 R_g - d\right) \quad \text{for } d \ll 2R_g$$

### Key Physics
- **Range:** Set by $2 R_g$ of the depletant.
- **Depth:** Proportional to $\phi_p$ (volume fraction) and $c_p$ (concentration).
- **Reversible:** Disappears on dilution.
- **Engineering role:** Drives **depletion flocculation in latex paints, dairy products (casein micelles), and food colloids**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Bridging vs. Depletion — The Crucial Difference**
> - **Bridging** → polymer **adsorbs** on the particle surface.
> - **Depletion** → polymer/micelle is **non-adsorbing**; the effect is purely osmotic and entropic.

## 2.6 Structural / Oscillatory Forces

### Physical Origin
In confined molecular solvents (especially near smooth surfaces), solvent molecules form **discrete layers** (like onions). When two surfaces approach, layers must be squeezed out one by one, producing a **sinusoidally modulated force** superimposed on the DLVO potential.

### Mathematical Form

$$F_{struct}(d) = F_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{d}{\lambda_s}\right) \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{d_0} + \phi\right)$$

where:
- $d_0 \approx$ molecular diameter ($\sim 0.25\text{–}0.5$ nm for water).
- $\lambda_s$ is the damping length ($\sim 0.5\text{–}1$ nm).
- $\phi$ is a phase shift.

### Engineering Role
- Determines **lubrication in MEMS/NEMS devices**.
- Governs **boundary friction in nanotribology**.
- Controls **nanoparticle ordering in thin films** (self-assembly).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Force | Energy $V(d)$ or Force $F(d)$ | Decay Type | Sign | Decay Length / Range |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Van der Waals (DLVO) | $V_A = -A_{Ham} R / (12 d)$ | Power law $\sim 1/d$ | Attractive | Long |
| 2 | Double Layer (DLVO) | $V_R \propto \exp(-\kappa d)$ | Exponential | Repulsive | $\kappa^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Hydration | $V_{hyd} = F_0 \lambda_h \exp(-d/\lambda_h)$ | Exponential | Repulsive (+) | $\lambda_h \sim 0.3$ nm |
| 4 | Hydrophobic | $F = C_1 \exp(-d/D_1) + C_2 \exp(-d/D_2)$ | Double exponential | Attractive (−) | $D_2$ up to 100 nm |
| 5 | Steric (brush) | $F \sim (k_B T / s^3)(2L/d)^{8/3}$ | Power law | Repulsive (+) | $\sim 2L$ |
| 6 | Bridging | $F \sim \Pi_{pol}(1 - d/2L_g)$ | Linear cutoff | Attractive (−) | $2 L_g$ |
| 7 | Depletion (AO) | $F = -2\pi R \Pi (2R_g - d)^2 / 4$ | Polynomial | Attractive (−) | $2 R_g$ |
| 8 | Structural | $F = F_0 \exp(-d/\lambda_s)\cos(2\pi d/d_0)$ | Damped oscillation | Repulsive (+) | $d_0 \sim$ molecular |

> [!NOTE] **S.I. Units Reminder**
> Energies in Joules (J), forces in Newtons (N), distances in meters (m), decay lengths typically in nanometers ($1\,\text{nm} = 10^{-9}$ m), Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, temperature in Kelvin (K).
