# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Asakura–Oosawa (AO) Depletion Force for Two Plates

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Problem

Consider two **large flat plates** of area $A$ immersed in a solution containing **non-adsorbing polymer coils** modeled as **hard spheres of radius $R_g$** (radius of gyration) at number density $\rho_p$.

- Outside the gap (bulk region): the polymer coils are free to occupy the full volume.
- Inside the gap of width $d$: the polymer center cannot come closer than $R_g$ to either surface. So the polymer-free region has width $(d - 2R_g)$ on each side. Polymer is **excluded** if $d < 2R_g$.

### 3.1.2 Osmotic Pressure in the Bulk

The bulk osmotic pressure of an ideal polymer solution is:

$$\Pi_{bulk} = \rho_p k_B T$$

### 3.1.3 Osmotic Pressure in the Gap

When $d < 2 R_g$, the polymer is **completely excluded** from the gap. The polymer concentration in the gap is **zero**, hence its osmotic pressure contribution there is:

$$\Pi_{gap} = 0$$

### 3.1.4 Net Force Calculation

The two plates are pushed together because the **outside bulk** has a higher osmotic pressure than the **inside gap**. The net attractive force per unit area is the pressure difference:

$$F_{dep}/A = \Pi_{bulk} - \Pi_{gap} = \rho_p k_B T - 0$$

$$\boxed{F_{dep}/A = -\rho_p k_B T \quad \text{for } d < 2R_g}$$

The negative sign indicates the force is **attractive** (pulling plates together).

### 3.1.5 Force at Finite Surface Separation

For $d > 2R_g$, the polymer can enter the gap fully, and the depletion force vanishes:

$$F_{dep}/A = 0 \quad \text{for } d \geq 2R_g$$

### 3.1.6 Conversion to Pair Potential (Energy per unit area)

Integrating the force from $d$ to $\infty$ gives the interaction energy per unit area:

$$V_{dep}(d)/A = -\int_{d}^{\infty} F(d')/A \, dd'$$

For $d < 2R_g$:

$$V_{dep}(d)/A = -\rho_p k_B T (2 R_g - d)$$

$$\boxed{V_{dep}(d)/A = -\rho_p k_B T (2 R_g - d) \quad \text{for } d < 2R_g}$$

This shows depletion energy is **linear in $d$** and vanishes at $d = 2 R_g$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Hydration Force Decay Length from Linearization of Surface Energy

Consider the **free energy density** $g(d)$ of ordered water at separation $d$. A simple model: the ordering energy per unit volume is $u_0 e^{-z/\lambda_h}$ where $z$ is distance from surface.

The **total ordering energy per unit area** between two surfaces is:

$$G(d) = 2 \int_0^{\infty} u_0 e^{-z/\lambda_h} dz + 2 \int_d^{\infty} u_0 e^{-(z-d)/\lambda_h} dz$$

Wait — this double-counts. The correct form is the **overlap integral** (similar to Hamaker):

$$G(d) = 2 \int_0^{\infty} u_0 e^{-z/\lambda_h} dz - 2 \int_0^{d} u_0 e^{-z/\lambda_h} dz$$

Wait — the standard approach uses the **disjoining pressure**:

$$\Pi_{hyd}(d) = -\frac{\partial G}{\partial d}$$

For two surfaces with an exponentially decaying energy density $u(z) = u_0 e^{-z/\lambda_h}$ emanating from each surface, the **overlap energy** is:

$$G(d) = \int_d^{\infty} u_0 e^{-z/\lambda_h} \cdot u_0 e^{-(z-d)/\lambda_h} dz = u_0^2 \lambda_h e^{-d/\lambda_h}$$

Then the disjoining pressure is:

$$\Pi_{hyd}(d) = -\frac{\partial G}{\partial d} = \frac{u_0^2}{\lambda_h} e^{-d/\lambda_h} \cdot \lambda_h = u_0^2 e^{-d/\lambda_h}$$

The **force per unit area** is:

$$\boxed{\Pi_{hyd}(d) = F_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{d}{\lambda_h}\right) \quad \text{where } F_0 = u_0^2}$$

### Physical Interpretation
- The hydration pressure decays as the **exponential of distance divided by decay length**.
- Decay length $\lambda_h$ reflects the **range of water ordering**, typically $0.2\text{–}1.0$ nm in water.
- The pre-factor $F_0$ is determined by the **strength of water-surface H-bonding** and is maximum on freshly cleaved, fully hydroxylated surfaces.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Forces at 1 nm Separation

Assume a colloidal particle of radius $R = 100$ nm in water with typical parameters:

| Force | Magnitude @ $d = 1$ nm | Sign | Dominant Region |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Van der Waals (DLVO) | $\sim 10\,k_B T$ | Attractive | All distances |
| Double Layer (DLVO) at 0.01 M | $\sim 1\,k_B T$ | Repulsive | $\kappa^{-1} \sim 3$ nm |
| Hydration | $\sim 5\,k_B T$ | Repulsive | $< 2$ nm |
| Hydrophobic ($D_2$ part) | $\sim 3\,k_B T$ | Attractive | $< 30$ nm |
| Steric (brush $L=10$ nm) | $\sim 50\,k_B T$ | Repulsive | $< 20$ nm |
| Depletion ($\phi = 0.1$) | $\sim 2\,k_B T$ | Attractive | $< 2R_g \sim 10$ nm |

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight**
> At $d = 1$ nm, **hydration, steric, and hydrophobic forces can exceed DLVO by 1–2 orders of magnitude**. This is why classical DLVO underpredicts stability in real systems and why non-DLVO corrections are essential in the **Extended DLVO (XDLVO)** treatment.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Depletion Force

**Problem:** Two flat plates are immersed in a solution of non-adsorbing polymer with $R_g = 5$ nm and bulk number density $\rho_p = 10^{22}$ m$^{-3}$. Calculate the depletion force per unit area at $d = 4$ nm. Take $T = 298$ K.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Check the condition $d < 2 R_g$:
$$d = 4 \text{ nm} < 2 R_g = 10 \text{ nm} \quad \checkmark$$

Step 2: Apply the AO formula for the force per unit area:

$$\frac{F_{dep}}{A} = -\rho_p k_B T$$

Step 3: Substitute values:
- $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T = 298$ K
- $\rho_p = 10^{22}$ m$^{-3}$

$$\frac{F_{dep}}{A} = -10^{22} \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 298$$

Step 4: Multiply stepwise:

$$10^{22} \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} = 1.381 \times 10^{-1} = 0.1381$$

$$0.1381 \times 298 = 41.15$$

Step 5: Result:

$$\frac{F_{dep}}{A} \approx -41.2 \text{ Pa} \quad (\text{attractive, sign convention negative})$$

This corresponds to a **force of about 41 N on every square meter of plate area** — non-trivial for colloidal stability calculations.

> [!WARNING] **Common Sign Convention Pitfall**
> The negative sign in the AO formula is **often dropped** in textbooks. Always state explicitly: **negative force = attractive; positive force = repulsive**. Examiners expect you to mention this convention explicitly.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECHT863]`
**Q: Define non-DLVO forces. List any four examples with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Non-DLVO forces are interparticle forces in colloidal systems that exist **outside the classical DLVO theory**, which considers only van der Waals attraction and electrostatic double-layer repulsion. (1 mark)

Four examples:

1. **Hydration force** – short-range repulsion due to structured water on hydrophilic surfaces. (0.5 mark)
2. **Hydrophobic force** – long-range attraction between hydrophobic surfaces due to entropy gain when structured water is released. (0.5 mark)
3. **Steric force** – repulsion arising from compression of adsorbed or grafted polymer chains. (0.5 mark)
4. **Depletion force** – attraction caused by non-adsorbing polymer creating an osmotic pressure imbalance. (0.5 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECHT863]`
**Q: Distinguish between bridging flocculation and depletion flocculation in colloidal systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bridging Flocculation | Depletion Flocculation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Polymer behavior | Polymer **adsorbs** on particles | Polymer is **non-adsorbing** |
| Mechanism | One chain binds to **two** particles | Polymer excluded from gap creates osmotic pressure imbalance |
| Effect of coverage | Dominates at **low** surface coverage | Independent of adsorption; depends on free polymer concentration |
| Reversibility | Strong (often irreversible) | Reversible on dilution |

(3 marks: 1 for each distinction row + 1 mark for clarity of explanation)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECHT863]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the origin, mathematical form, range, and temperature dependence of **hydration forces** in colloidal dispersions. Mention their role in colloidal stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Asakura–Oosawa model** of depletion forces. Derive the expression for the depletion force per unit area between two flat plates separated by distance $d$ in a solution of non-adsorbing polymer modeled as hard spheres of radius $R_g$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a): Hydration Forces (7 marks)

**Origin (2 marks):**
- When two hydrophilic surfaces (e.g., silica, mica, oxide) approach in water, strongly H-bonded and **ordered water layers** are present near each surface.
- These layers resist desorption → short-range **repulsive force**.
- Beyond the range of ordered water ($\sim 0.5\text{–}3$ nm), the force vanishes.

**Mathematical form (2 marks):**

$$F_{hyd}(d) = F_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{d}{\lambda_{hyd}}\right)$$

where $F_0$ is the pre-exponential factor ($0.01\text{–}10$ N/m) and $\lambda_{hyd} \sim 0.2\text{–}1.0$ nm.

**Range and temperature dependence (1.5 marks):**
- Range: typically $0.5\text{–}3$ nm.
- Decay length $\lambda_{hyd}$ **increases** with temperature.
- Force magnitude $F_0$ **increases** with temperature.
- Force shows **ion-specific effects** following Hofmeister series: $\text{Li}^+ > \text{Na}^+ > \text{K}^+$.

**Role in colloidal stability (1.5 marks):**
- Prevents aggregation of **silica, alumina, titania, iron oxide** dispersions in aqueous media.
- Explains why some colloids are stable even at **high ionic strength** where double-layer is screened.
- Important in **pharmaceutical suspensions, ceramic processing, paints, and coatings**.

---

#### Solution A(b): Asakura–Oosawa Depletion Force Derivation (7 marks)

**Model description (1 mark):**
- Two large flat plates (area $A$) immersed in a polymer solution.
- Polymer modeled as **non-adsorbing hard spheres** of radius $R_g$ at number density $\rho_p$.
- Polymer **cannot enter** the gap if surface-to-surface distance $d < 2 R_g$.

**Step 1: Osmotic pressure in bulk (1.5 marks):**

$$\Pi_{bulk} = \rho_p k_B T$$

**Step 2: Osmotic pressure in gap (1.5 marks):**

For $d < 2R_g$, polymer is fully excluded, so $\rho_{p,\,gap} = 0$:

$$\Pi_{gap} = 0$$

**Step 3: Net force per unit area (2 marks):**

$$\frac{F_{dep}}{A} = \Pi_{bulk} - \Pi_{gap} = \rho_p k_B T - 0 = \rho_p k_B T$$

The negative sign convention is used to indicate attraction:

$$\frac{F_{dep}}{A} = -\rho_p k_B T \quad \text{for } d < 2R_g$$

**Step 4: Pair potential (1 mark):**

$$V_{dep}(d)/A = -\rho_p k_B T (2 R_g - d) \quad \text{for } d < 2 R_g$$

**Key observations (bonus clarity marks):**
- Force is **attractive** (pulls plates together).
- **Range** is $2 R_g$ (set by polymer size).
- **Depth** scales with $\rho_p$ (concentration of free polymer).
- **Independent of $d$** in the simple AO model — the force is constant inside the gap.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECHT863]`

**Q: (a)** What is the **hydrophobic force**? Discuss its physical origin, mathematical form (double exponential), and how it differs from the van der Waals force. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **steric stabilization** in colloidal dispersions. Discuss the role of **Flory–Huggins interaction parameter $\chi$**, polymer brush thickness $L$, and grafting density $s$ in determining whether the interaction is repulsive (stabilizing) or attractive (flocculating). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a): Hydrophobic Force (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The hydrophobic force is a long-range **attractive force** acting between hydrophobic surfaces (or particles) immersed in water. It is much stronger and longer-ranged than the van der Waals force.

**Physical origin (2 marks):**
- Water near hydrophobic surfaces forms **clathrate-like (iceberg) structures** of high entropy cost.
- When two hydrophobic surfaces approach, these ordered water regions **overlap** and the structured water is released into the bulk.
- The release **gains entropy** ($\Delta S > 0$) and decreases free energy ($-\!T \Delta S < 0$), producing an attractive force.

**Mathematical form (2 marks):**
The force is described empirically by a **double exponential** decay:

$$F_{hp}(d) = C_1 \exp\!\left(-\frac{d}{D_1}\right) + C_2 \exp\!\left(-\frac{d}{D_2}\right)$$

- $D_1 \sim 1\text{–}2$ nm (short-range part).
- $D_2 \sim 5\text{–}100$ nm (long-range "true" hydrophobic).
- $C_1, C_2$: pre-exponential constants (mN/m scale).

**Comparison with van der Waals (2 marks):**

| Property | Van der Waals | Hydrophobic |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Decay law | $1/d^3$ (energy) — power law | $\exp(-d/D)$ — exponential |
| Typical range | $\sim 5$ nm effective | Up to 100 nm |
| Temperature | Independent (at short range) | **Decreases** with $T$ |
| Origin | Dispersion forces | Entropic release of structured water |
| Sign | Always attractive | Attractive |

---

#### Solution B(b): Steric Stabilization (7 marks)

**Concept (1.5 marks):**
Steric stabilization is achieved by **adsorbing or grafting polymers** on colloidal particles. When two such particles approach, the polymer layers overlap, and depending on solvent quality and coverage, the interaction is **repulsive (stabilizing)** or **attractive (flocculating)**.

**Flory–Huggins parameter $\chi$ (2 marks):**
- $\chi < 0.5$ (good solvent) → osmotic repulsion between segments dominates → **steric stabilization**.
- $\chi > 0.5$ (bad solvent) → segment-segment attraction → **flocculation**.
- $\chi = 0.5$ (theta solvent) → no net interaction beyond hard core.

**Polymer brush thickness $L$ (1.5 marks):**
- Determines the **range** of steric force.
- $L \propto N \sigma^{1/3}$ (in good solvent, dense brush).
- For $d < 2L$, the layers overlap and produce interaction; for $d > 2L$, no force.

**Grafting density $s$ (1.5 marks):**
- $s$ = average distance between grafted chain anchor points.
- High grafting density (small $s$) → **brush regime** → strong repulsion.
- Low grafting density (large $s$) → **mushroom regime** → weak interaction.
- $s < R_g$ → brush; $s > R_g$ → mushroom.

**Mathematical form (0.5 mark):**
For dense brushes:

$$F_{brush}(d) \sim \frac{k_B T}{s^3}\left[\left(\frac{2L}{d}\right)^{8/3} - 1\right] \quad \text{for } d < 2L$$

**Engineering examples (bonus):**
- **PEGylated drug nanoparticles** (Stealth liposomes) for blood circulation.
- **Sterically stabilized pigments** in non-aqueous paints.
- **Polymer-stabilized Pickering emulsions**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing hydration force with double-layer force** (LOSES 2–3 marks): Both are repulsive, but hydration has a **shorter decay length (0.3 nm)** and persists at **high ionic strength** (1 M). Always mention these distinguishing features.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up bridging and depletion** (LOSES 2–3 marks): Bridging requires **adsorbing** polymer; depletion requires **non-adsorbing** polymer/micelles. Use the correct word — "adsorbing" vs. "non-adsorbing".
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the sign convention in depletion force** (LOSES 1 mark): The Asakura–Oosawa force is **attractive** (negative). If you state it as positive, half the conceptual credit is lost.
> 
> 4. **Writing $\chi > 0.5$ for good solvent** (LOSES 1 mark): The correct inequality is $\chi < 0.5$ for **good solvent**; $\chi > 0.5$ is for **bad solvent**. This is reversed in many student answers.
> 
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical problems** (LOSES 1 mark): Force in N, energy in J, distances in m. A 41 Pa result without units is incomplete.
> 
> 6. **Omitting temperature dependence** (LOSES 1 mark): Hydration increases with $T$, hydrophobic decreases with $T$, depletion is proportional to $k_B T$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Non-DLVO Forces**

- [x] **Definition:** Non-DLVO = interparticle forces **beyond** van der Waals and double-layer interactions.
- [x] **Motivation:** DLVO fails at $d < 5\text{–}10$ nm and in polymer/surfactant systems — extended DLVO (XDLVO) is required.
- [x] **Hydration Force:** Exponential repulsion, $\lambda_h \sim 0.3$ nm, increases with $T$, persists at high ionic strength, dominant on **hydrophilic** surfaces.
- [x] **Hydrophobic Force:** Double-exponential attraction, $D_2$ up to 100 nm, **decreases** with $T$, dominant on **hydrophobic** surfaces, entropic in origin.
- [x] **Steric Force:** Repulsion in **good solvent** ($\chi < 0.5$), governed by brush thickness $L$ and grafting spacing $s$. Strongest in dense brush regime ($F \propto s^{-3}$).
- [x] **Bridging Force:** Attraction from **adsorbing** high-MW polymer bridging two particles; dominant at **low surface coverage**.
- [x] **Depletion Force (AO model):** $F/A = -\rho_p k_B T$ for $d < 2 R_g$, attractive, range set by polymer size, reversible.
- [x] **Structural Force:** Damped oscillation $F = F_0 e^{-d/\lambda_s}\cos(2\pi d/d_0)$, relevant to lubrication and nanoconfinement.
- [x] **Key constants to memorize:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; Debye length $\kappa^{-1} = 0.304/\sqrt{I}$ nm (water, 25 °C).
- [x] **Sign convention:** Negative force = attractive; positive = repulsive.
- [x] **Engineering contexts:** Paints, pharmaceuticals, ceramics, food colloids, water treatment, lubrication, bioseparation.
- [x] **XDLVO total potential:** $V_{total} = V_A + V_R + V_{hyd} + V_{hp} + V_{steric} + V_{bridge} + V_{dep} + \dots$

> [!NOTE] **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip**
> For 14-mark questions, always write the **origin (2 marks) + mathematical form with all symbols defined (3 marks) + decay length and range (2 marks) + comparison with DLVO (2 marks) + engineering example (1 mark) + neat labeled diagram (1 mark) + summary statement (1 mark) = 12–14 marks.** Memorize the formula and at least **one engineering application** for each force.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorption at Solid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adsorption at Solid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Adsorption at a solid surface** is the spontaneous accumulation (concentration) of molecules, atoms, or ions of a gas, liquid, or dissolved solute (the **adsorbate**) onto the external and/or internal surface of a porous or particulate solid (the **adsorbent**), driven by unbalanced surface free energy of the solid. The phenomenon is strictly a **surface phenomenon** (not bulk), and the residual forces at the surface are responsible for retaining the adsorbate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Terminology Box (Must Memorize):**
> - **Adsorbent** → The solid *surface* that does the grabbing.
> - **Adsorbate** → The species that gets *grabbed* onto the surface.
> - **Sorbent** → Generic term for either phase; **Sorption** = Adsorption + Absorption.
> - **Desorption** → The reverse process (releasing the adsorbate).
> - **ΔG (adsorption) < 0**, **ΔH < 0** → spontaneous, exothermic (for physisorption and most chemisorption).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a freshly cut slice of white bread dropped onto a dusty table — within seconds, dust particles *cling* to the bread surface. The bread (solid = adsorbent) does not absorb the dust inside; the dust (gas/vapor = adsorbate) only sticks to the **outer interface**. In the same way, activated charcoal (a high-surface-area solid) "traps" pollutant molecules in a gas mask on its exterior, never letting them penetrate into the bulk carbon. That surface-only trapping = **adsorption**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Contrast:**
> - *Adsorption* = surface-only (2-D phenomenon)
> - *Absorption* = bulk penetration (3-D phenomenon, e.g., sponge soaking water)
> - *Sorption* = collective word for both
> - *Desorption* = reverse of adsorption (heat/vacuum releases adsorbate)

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard surface excess (Γ)**: mol·m⁻² — moles adsorbed per unit area.
- **BET surface area (S_BET)**: m²·g⁻¹ — typically 500–3000 m²/g for activated carbon.
- **Cross-sectional area of N₂ molecule**: **0.162 nm²** (most commonly used probe gas in BET).
- **Avogadro's number**: **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**.
- **Molar volume of gas at STP**: **22.414 L·mol⁻¹**.

### 1.4 Two Principal Modes of Adsorption at Solids

1. **Physisorption (Physical Adsorption)**: Driven by weak **van der Waals (London dispersion) forces**, no electron transfer, low enthalpy (20–40 kJ·mol⁻¹), reversible, multi-layered, non-specific.

2. **Chemisorption (Chemical Adsorption)**: Driven by formation of strong **covalent or ionic bonds** between adsorbate and surface atoms, high enthalpy (40–400 kJ·mol⁻¹), often irreversible, mono-layered, highly specific (requires activation energy).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural "Why & How" — The Two Modes Explained

**Physisorption — Why & How**
- **Why it happens:** Every solid has unsaturated coordination at the surface (broken bonds), creating a *surface free energy* penalty. Van der Waals attraction between adsorbate molecules and the surface lowers this energy.
- **How it behaves:** Forms multi-layers (each layer attracts the next), low ΔH, no activation energy, **fully reversible** by mild heating or pressure reduction. The adsorbate retains its chemical identity.

**Chemisorption — Why & How**
- **Why it happens:** Adsorbate electrons interact with localized surface orbitals → true chemical bond formation (covalent/ionic). Often requires an *activation energy* to initiate.
- **How it behaves:** Strictly **monolayer** (only direct surface contact), high ΔH, may be irreversible (forms new compound), highly **specific** (only certain pairs adsorb). Used in heterogeneous catalysis (e.g., H₂ on Ni, O₂ on Pt).

### 2.2 Comparison Table — Physisorption vs. Chemisorption

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | van der Waals forces | Covalent / ionic bonds |
| Enthalpy ΔH | 20 – 40 kJ·mol⁻¹ | 40 – 400 kJ·mol⁻¹ |
| Layers | Multilayer possible | Strictly monolayer |
| Reversibility | Reversible, easy | Often irreversible |
| Specificity | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| Activation energy | None (spontaneous) | Often required |
| Temperature range | Low (≈ T_b.p. of adsorbate) | High (well above T_b.p.) |
| Effect of pressure | Marked effect | Less pronounced |
| Effect on surface | No electron transfer | Electron transfer / bond formation |
| Examples | N₂ on Fe, O₂ on charcoal | H₂ on Ni, O₂ on Pt, CO on metals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Trap:** Many students write "physisorption is monolayer" — this is **wrong**. Physisorption is **multilayer**; chemisorption is the **monolayer** mode.

### 2.3 Adsorption Isotherms — The "Pressure–Coverage" Relationship

An **adsorption isotherm** is a plot of the *amount adsorbed* (Γ or V) versus the *equilibrium pressure* (P) or concentration (C) at a **fixed temperature**. The shape of the isotherm encodes information about the surface, the porosity, and the strength of interaction.

The five most important isotherms for KTU are: **Langmuir, Freundlich, BET, Temkin, and the IUPAC classification.**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Isotherm | Non-Linear Form | Linear Form | Plot Gives | Key Constant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Langmuir** | $\theta = \dfrac{K P}{1 + K P}$ | $\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{1}{K V_m} + \dfrac{P}{V_m}$ | $V_m$ (slope), $K$ (intercept) | $K = k_a / k_d$ (equilibrium constant) |
| **Langmuir (Volumetric)** | $V = \dfrac{V_m K P}{1 + K P}$ | $\dfrac{1}{V} = \dfrac{1}{K V_m}\cdot\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{V_m}$ | $V_m$ (intercept), $K$ (slope) | $V_m$ = monolayer volume |
| **Freundlich** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = k P^{1/n}$ | $\log\dfrac{x}{m} = \log k + \dfrac{1}{n}\log P$ | $k$ (intercept), $1/n$ (slope) | $1/n$ between 0 and 1 |
| **BET (Multilayer)** | $V = \dfrac{V_m c x}{(1-x)(1-x+cx)}$, where $x = P / P_0$ | $\dfrac{x}{(1-x)V} = \dfrac{1}{V_m c} + \dfrac{c-1}{V_m c}\,x$ | $V_m$, $c$ | $c \approx \exp[(E_1 - E_L)/RT]$ |
| **Temkin** | $V = B \ln(A P)$ | — | $B$, $A$ | Accounts for adsorbate–adsorbate interaction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols Used Above (decode for KTU answers):**
> - $\theta$ = fractional surface coverage (dimensionless, 0–1)
> - $V$ = volume of gas adsorbed at equilibrium pressure $P$
> - $V_m$ = volume corresponding to a complete monolayer
> - $K$ = Langmuir equilibrium constant (Pa⁻¹)
> - $P_0$ = saturation vapor pressure of the adsorbate at experimental $T$
> - $x = P / P_0$ = relative pressure (dimensionless)
> - $c$ = BET constant (related to heat of adsorption of first layer)
> - $k$, $n$ = Freundlich empirical constants ($n > 1$ indicates favourable adsorption)
> - $x/m$ = mass of adsorbate per unit mass of adsorbent

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Activated carbon in water purification:** BET surface area ≈ 1000 m²·g⁻¹ traps organic pollutants by physisorption.
- **Catalytic converters in automobiles:** Chemisorption of CO, NOₓ onto Pt-Pd-Rh surfaces.
- **Gas masks / respirators:** Activated charcoal removes toxic vapours (physisorption in micropores).
- **Drying agents (silica gel):** Physisorbs water vapour in packaging and laboratory desiccators.
- **Heterogeneous catalysts:** Chemisorption of H₂ on Ni for hydrogenation of vegetable oils.
- **BET analysis:** Standard method in pharma, ceramics, and nanomaterials for **specific surface area** characterization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm (Monolayer, Homogeneous Surface)

**Assumptions of the Langmuir Model (1918):**
1. The solid surface exposes a *fixed number* of equivalent adsorption sites.
2. Each site can hold *only one* adsorbate molecule (strictly monolayer).
3. There is *no interaction* between adsorbate molecules on neighbouring sites.
4. The surface is *energetically homogeneous* (all sites have equal heat of adsorption).
5. At equilibrium, the *rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption*.

**Define variables:**
- $\theta$ = fraction of surface sites *covered* by adsorbate at equilibrium.
- $(1 - \theta)$ = fraction of surface sites *vacant*.
- $P$ = equilibrium gas pressure of adsorbate above the surface.
- $k_a$ = rate constant of adsorption (depends on sticking probability).
- $k_d$ = rate constant of desorption.

---

**Step 1 — Rate of Adsorption (forward process):**

Adsorption can only occur on a *vacant* site, and the rate is directly proportional to both the gas pressure $P$ and the fraction of vacant sites $(1 - \theta)$.

$$r_{\text{ads}} = k_a \, P \, (1 - \theta)$$

*Conversion logic:* Molecules striking the surface from gas phase $\propto P$; only bare sites can capture them $\propto (1-\theta)$.

---

**Step 2 — Rate of Desorption (reverse process):**

Desorption can only occur from an *occupied* site. Hence it is directly proportional to the fraction of covered sites $\theta$.

$$r_{\text{des}} = k_d \, \theta$$

*Conversion logic:* Molecules already on the surface have a thermal escape probability per unit time, and only filled sites can release them.

---

**Step 3 — Apply the Equilibrium Condition:**

At equilibrium, the net rate is zero, so adsorption rate exactly equals desorption rate:

$$k_a \, P \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta$$

*Conversion logic:* Thermodynamic equilibrium demands forward rate = reverse rate (dynamic balance).

---

**Step 4 — Expand the Left-Hand Side:**

$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \, \theta$$

*Conversion logic:* Distributive expansion of $k_a P (1-\theta)$.

---

**Step 5 — Group the $\theta$ Terms:**

$$k_a P = k_d \, \theta + k_a P \, \theta = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$

*Conversion logic:* Move all $\theta$-containing terms to the right-hand side.

---

**Step 6 — Isolate $\theta$:**

$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P}$$

*Conversion logic:* Divide both sides by $(k_d + k_a P)$.

---

**Step 7 — Introduce the Equilibrium Constant $K$:**

Define $K = k_a / k_d$. Then divide numerator and denominator by $k_d$:

$$\theta = \frac{\dfrac{k_a}{k_d}\, P}{1 + \dfrac{k_a}{k_d}\, P} = \frac{K P}{1 + K P}$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the canonical **Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm**.

---

**Step 8 — Convert to Adsorbed Volume Form:**

If $V$ is the volume adsorbed at pressure $P$, and $V_m$ is the volume for a *complete* monolayer ($\theta = 1$), then $\theta = V / V_m$. Substituting:

$$V = \frac{V_m \, K P}{1 + K P}$$

*Conversion logic:* Coverage fraction is also the ratio of actual uptake to monolayer capacity.

---

**Step 9 — Derive the Linear Form (essential for KTU plot-based questions):**

Take the reciprocal:

$$\frac{1}{V} = \frac{1 + K P}{V_m K P} = \frac{1}{V_m K P} + \frac{1}{V_m}$$

*Conversion logic:* Splitting the fraction and simplifying.

Multiply through by $P$ to obtain the most commonly plotted form:

$$\frac{P}{V} = \frac{1}{V_m K} + \frac{1}{V_m} \, P$$

*Conversion logic:* A plot of $P/V$ (y-axis) versus $P$ (x-axis) is a straight line with **slope = 1/V_m** and **intercept = 1/(V_m K)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit Check (KTU favourite):**
> - At **very low P** ($K P \ll 1$): $\theta \approx K P$ → linear Henry's law regime.
> - At **very high P** ($K P \gg 1$): $\theta \to 1$ → monolayer saturation plateau (classic Type I isotherm).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the BET Equation (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, 1938) — Multilayer Extension

**BET Assumptions (extension of Langmuir):**
1. Adsorbate molecules form *infinite multilayers* on the surface.
2. The *first* layer has heat of adsorption $E_1$ (specific interaction).
3. The *second and subsequent* layers have heat of adsorption equal to the **heat of liquefaction** $E_L$ (essentially condensation).
4. No horizontal interaction between adsorbed molecules.
5. Langmuir kinetics applies to *each* layer independently.

**Define variables:**
- $V$ = total volume of gas adsorbed at relative pressure $x = P/P_0$.
- $V_m$ = volume of gas in a *complete monolayer*.
- $c = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right]$ = BET constant (typically 1 to 1000).
- $P_0$ = saturation vapor pressure of the adsorbate at temperature $T$.
- $\theta_i$ = fraction of surface covered by exactly $i$ layers.

---

**Step 1 — Sum the Layer Coverages:**

The total volume adsorbed is the sum over all layers, weighted by the number of molecules in each layer:

$$V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \, \theta_i$$

*Conversion logic:* A molecule in the $i$-th layer contributes $i$ "monolayer-equivalents" to the total.

---

**Step 2 — Express $\theta_i$ Recursively (Langmuir per layer):**

Let $S_0, S_1, S_2, \ldots$ be the fraction of surface bearing $0, 1, 2, \ldots$ layers. With $\alpha = (K_1 P)$ for the first layer and $\beta = (K_L P)$ for higher layers (where $K_L$ corresponds to liquefaction):

$$S_i = S_{i-1} \cdot \beta = S_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \beta^{i-1}$$

*Conversion logic:* Sequential Langmuir equilibrium: probability of $i$ layers = probability of $(i-1)$ layers times the sticking probability for one more layer.

---

**Step 3 — Normalize the Coverage Fractions:**

The total surface must equal unity:

$$1 = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} S_i = S_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \beta^{i-1} \right]$$

The geometric sum simplifies to $1/(1-\beta)$ for $\beta < 1$, giving $S_0 = (1-\beta)/(1-\beta + \alpha\beta)$.

---

**Step 4 — Sum the Volume Series:**

$$V = V_m \, S_0 \cdot \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \, \beta^{i-1}$$

Using $\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \beta^{i-1} = 1/(1-\beta)^2$ and $\alpha = c \beta$ (where $c = K_1 / K_L$), the algebra reduces (after careful cancellation) to:

$$V = \frac{V_m \, c \, x}{(1 - x)\,(1 - x + c x)}$$

where $x = P/P_0$ is the **relative pressure**.

*Conversion logic:* This is the canonical **BET Adsorption Isotherm** — the foundation of every modern surface-area measurement.

---

**Step 5 — Derive the Linear (Working) Form:**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 - x)$ and rearrange:

$$\frac{x}{(1 - x)\, V} = \frac{1}{V_m c} + \frac{c - 1}{V_m c}\, x$$

*Conversion logic:* A plot of $y = \dfrac{x}{(1-x)V}$ vs. $x = P/P_0$ is a straight line:
- **Slope** $s = \dfrac{c - 1}{V_m c}$
- **Intercept** $i = \dfrac{1}{V_m c}$

From which:

$$V_m = \frac{1}{s + i} \qquad \text{and} \qquad c = \frac{s}{i} + 1$$

*Conversion logic:* This linearization is the heart of the BET experimental method, valid typically in the range $0.05 \le P/P_0 \le 0.35$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Linkage:** From $V_m$ (monolayer volume), the specific surface area $S_{BET}$ follows from the cross-sectional area $a_m$ of the probe molecule (N₂: $a_m = 0.162\ \text{nm}^2$):
>
> $$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \, N_A \, a_m}{22414\, m} \quad \left[ \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{g}^{-1} \right]$$
>
> where $m$ is the sample mass (g) and 22414 mL·mol⁻¹ is the molar volume at STP.

### 3.3 The Freundlich Isotherm (Empirical — Heterogeneous Surfaces)

Freundlich proposed (1909) an empirical power-law relation that often fits **heterogeneous surfaces** (energetically non-uniform) and **low-to-moderate** concentrations:

$$\frac{x}{m} = k \, P^{1/n}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\log\dfrac{x}{m} = \log k + \dfrac{1}{n} \log P$$

A plot of $\log(x/m)$ vs. $\log P$ is linear; **slope = 1/n** and **intercept = log k**.
- $n > 1$ indicates *favourable* adsorption.
- $0 < 1/n < 1$ indicates surface heterogeneity.
- Freundlich **fails** at high pressure (predicts infinite uptake, which is unphysical).

### 3.4 IUPAC Classification of Adsorption Isotherms (5 Types)

| Type | Shape | Pore Nature | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Concave to P/P₀ axis, plateau | **Micropores** (< 2 nm) | Langmuir-type; chemisorption; monolayer | Activated carbon + N₂ |
| **II** | Sigmoidal, no saturation | **Non-porous / Macroporous** | Strong adsorbate–adsorbent; BET-type | N₂ on silica |
| **III** | Convex to P/P₀ axis | Non-porous | Weak adsorbate–adsorbent; multilayer from start | I₂ on silica |
| **IV** | Plateau + hysteresis loop | **Mesoporous** (2–50 nm) | Capillary condensation | MCM-41, MOFs |
| **V** | Weak, with hysteresis at high P/P₀ | Mesoporous | Weak interaction + capillary condensation | Water on activated carbon |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Adsorption at Solids

```mermaid
graph TD
    ADSORP[Adsorption at Solid] --> PHYS[Physisorption]
    ADSORP --> CHEM[Chemisorption]

    PHYS --> P1[van der Waals forces]
    PHYS --> P2[Enthalpy 20 to 40 kJ per mol]
    PHYS --> P3[Multilayer possible]
    PHYS --> P4[Fully reversible]
    PHYS --> P5[Non-specific]

    CHEM --> C1[Covalent or ionic bonds]
    CHEM --> C2[Enthalpy 40 to 400 kJ per mol]
    CHEM --> C3[Strictly monolayer]
    CHEM --> C4[Often irreversible]
    CHEM --> C5[Highly specific]

    PHYS --> EX1[Examples: N2 on Fe at 77K]
    CHEM --> EX2[Examples: H2 on Ni catalyst]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Langmuir Isotherm Equilibrium Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GAS[Gas Phase Pressure P] -->|Strikes surface| SITES[Vacant Sites fraction 1 minus theta]
    SITES -->|Adsorption rate ka times P times 1 minus theta| OCCUPIED[Occupied Sites fraction theta]
    OCCUPIED -->|Desorption rate kd times theta| GAS
    OCCUPIED -->|At equilibrium dynamic balance| EQ[Equilibrium Coverage theta equals KP over 1 plus KP]
    EQ --> VOL[Volume Form V equals Vm times KP over 1 plus KP]
    VOL -->|Linearize| LIN[Plot P over V versus P straight line]
    LIN -->|Slope 1 over Vm| VM[Monolayer Volume Vm]
    LIN -->|Intercept 1 over Vm K| KC[Equilibrium Constant K]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — BET Multilayer Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0[Empty Surface 0 layers] -->|Layer 1 heat E1| L1[First Layer Molecules]
    L1 -->|Layer 2 heat EL liquefaction| L2[Second Layer Molecules]
    L2 --> L3[Third Layer]
    L3 --> L4[Higher Layers]
    L4 --> L5[Multilayer Stack up to infinity]

    L1 --> EQB[Langmuir Equilibrium at each layer]
    L2 --> EQB
    L3 --> EQB

    EQB --> BETEQ[BET Equation V equals Vm times c times x over 1 minus x times 1 minus x plus c x]
    BETEQ --> LINB[Linear Form x over 1 minus x V equals 1 over Vm c plus c minus 1 over Vm c times x]
    LINB --> SLP[Slope equals c minus 1 over Vm c]
    LINB --> INT[Intercept equals 1 over Vm c]
```

### 4.4 IUPAC Isotherm Classification Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    ISO[IUPAC Adsorption Isotherms] --> T1[Type I Microporous Langmuir]
    ISO --> T2[Type II Non-porous BET]
    ISO --> T3[Type III Non-porous weak]
    ISO --> T4[Type IV Mesoporous hysteresis]
    ISO --> T5[Type V Mesoporous weak hysteresis]

    T1 --> T1D[Sharp knee at low P over P0 followed by plateau]
    T2 --> T2D[Point B marks monolayer completion then multilayer]
    T3 --> T3D[Convex shape low coverage slow uptake]
    T4 --> T4D[Plateau plus hysteresis loop from capillary condensation]
    T5 --> T5D[Weak initial uptake plus hysteresis at high P over P0]
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture — Surface Area Measurement by BET

```mermaid
graph TD
    SAMPLE[Porous Solid Sample] --> DEGAS[Degassing Step Remove Pre-adsorbed Species]
    DEGAS --> N2ADS[Adsorb N2 gas at 77 K]
    N2ADS --> MEAS[Measure Volume Adsorbed V at Various P]
    MEAS --> PLOT[Plot x over 1 minus x V versus x equal P over P0]
    PLOT --> SLOPE[Extract Slope and Intercept]
    SLOPE --> VM[Compute Vm from 1 over slope plus intercept]
    VM --> C[Compute c from slope over intercept plus 1]
    VM --> AREA[Specific Surface Area SBET equals Vm NA am over 22414 m]
    AREA --> REPORT[Final Result in m2 per g]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between physisorption and chemisorption. Mention any three differences.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Nature of forces | Weak van der Waals (London dispersion) forces between adsorbate and surface | Strong covalent or ionic chemical bonds formed with surface atoms |
| 2. Enthalpy of adsorption | Low, typically 20 – 40 kJ·mol⁻¹ | High, typically 40 – 400 kJ·mol⁻¹ |
| 3. Layers formed | Multi-layered (one layer does not block others) | Strictly monolayer (only one molecule per site) |
| 4. Reversibility | Fully reversible by lowering P or mild heating | Often irreversible; desorption may be difficult |
| 5. Specificity | Non-specific — any gas adsorbs on any surface | Highly specific — depends on chemical affinity |
| 6. Activation energy | Nil (spontaneous) | Often a finite activation energy is required |

*(Any three points with brief explanation = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an adsorption isotherm. Write the linear form of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
An **adsorption isotherm** is a graphical or mathematical relationship that expresses the *amount of gas (or solute) adsorbed per unit mass (or area) of the solid adsorbent* as a function of the *equilibrium pressure (or concentration)* of the adsorbate **at a constant temperature**.

**Linear form of Langmuir isotherm (1.5 marks):**

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{P}{V} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{V_m K} \;+\; \dfrac{1}{V_m}\, P\;}$$

A plot of $P/V$ versus $P$ yields a straight line. **Slope = $1/V_m$** and **intercept = $1/(V_m K)$**, from which the monolayer capacity $V_m$ and the Langmuir equilibrium constant $K$ are evaluated.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option Set 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** starting from kinetic considerations. State all assumptions clearly. Discuss the **limiting cases** at low and high pressures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **BET adsorption isotherm** for multilayer adsorption. State its assumptions, derive the final equation, and discuss how the monolayer capacity $V_m$ is obtained experimentally. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Langmuir Isotherm (7 marks):**

**Assumptions of Langmuir model (2 marks):**
1. The solid surface contains a fixed number of energetically *equivalent* adsorption sites.
2. Each site accommodates *only one* adsorbate molecule → strict monolayer coverage.
3. There is *no lateral interaction* between adsorbed molecules on adjacent sites.
4. The surface is *homogeneous* (uniform heat of adsorption across all sites).
5. At dynamic equilibrium, the **rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption**.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Let $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered, $(1-\theta)$ = fraction bare, $P$ = equilibrium pressure, $k_a$ and $k_d$ = rate constants.

Rate of adsorption: $r_{\text{ads}} = k_a P (1 - \theta)$ — proportional to gas pressure and to the bare fraction.

Rate of desorption: $r_{\text{des}} = k_d \theta$ — proportional to the covered fraction.

At dynamic equilibrium:
$$k_a P (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

Expanding: $k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta \;\Rightarrow\; k_a P = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$.

Therefore:
$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P} = \frac{K P}{1 + K P} \quad \text{where } K = \frac{k_a}{k_d}$$

**Limiting cases (1 mark):**
- **Low pressure** ($K P \ll 1$): $\theta \approx K P$ — Henry's law region, coverage is linear in pressure.
- **High pressure** ($K P \gg 1$): $\theta \to 1$ — surface saturates with a complete monolayer (Type I isotherm plateau).

---

**(b) BET Isotherm for Multilayer Adsorption (7 marks):**

**Concept (1.5 marks):** Brunauer, Emmett, and Teller (1938) extended the Langmuir model to **multilayer adsorption**. The first layer has heat of adsorption $E_1$ (specific surface interaction); the second and subsequent layers have heat of adsorption equal to the **heat of liquefaction $E_L$** (i.e., the molecules above the first layer behave essentially like a liquid film).

**Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
1. Infinite number of layers can form.
2. Langmuir kinetics apply independently to each layer.
3. No horizontal interaction between adjacent adsorbate molecules.
4. The heat of adsorption for the $2^{\text{nd}}$ layer onwards is constant and equal to $E_L$.

**Final BET equation (2 marks):**

$$V = \frac{V_m \, c \, x}{(1 - x)\,(1 - x + c x)} \quad \text{where } x = \frac{P}{P_0},\ c = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right]$$

**Experimental determination of $V_m$ (2 marks):**
The linearized form is

$$\frac{x}{(1 - x)V} = \frac{1}{V_m c} + \frac{c - 1}{V_m c}\, x$$

A plot of $y = x/[(1-x)V]$ vs. $x = P/P_0$ is a straight line in the range $0.05 \le P/P_0 \le 0.35$.
- **Slope** $s = (c-1)/(V_m c)$
- **Intercept** $i = 1/(V_m c)$

Therefore:
$$V_m = \frac{1}{s + i} \qquad c = \frac{s}{i} + 1$$

From $V_m$, the **BET specific surface area** is calculated as:
$$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \, N_A \, a_m}{22414\, m} \quad [\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{g}^{-1}]$$

where $a_m$ is the cross-sectional area of the probe molecule (N₂: $a_m = 0.162\ \text{nm}^2$) and $m$ is the sample mass.

---

### **Question B — Option Set 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** What is the **Freundlich adsorption isotherm**? Derive its linear form and explain the significance of the constants $k$ and $1/n$. State the limitations of the Freundlich isotherm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **IUPAC classification of adsorption isotherms** (Types I to V) with suitable diagrams and examples. How does the shape of the isotherm relate to the porosity of the solid? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm (7 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** Freundlich (1909) proposed an **empirical** relation between the amount of gas adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent and the equilibrium gas pressure:

$$\frac{x}{m} = k \, P^{1/n}$$

where $x$ = mass of gas adsorbed, $m$ = mass of adsorbent, $k$ and $n$ are empirical constants that depend on the nature of the adsorbent and adsorbate, and on temperature.

**Linear form derivation (2 marks):**
Taking logarithms (base 10) of both sides:

$$\log\dfrac{x}{m} = \log k + \dfrac{1}{n}\log P$$

A plot of $\log(x/m)$ versus $\log P$ gives a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** = $1/n$
- **Intercept** = $\log k$

**Significance of constants (2 marks):**
- **$1/n$** (slope): measures *surface heterogeneity* and *adsorption intensity*. For favourable adsorption, $0 < 1/n < 1$.
- **$k$** (intercept): indicates the *relative adsorption capacity* of the adsorbent. A larger $k$ means higher capacity.
- $n > 1$ → favourable adsorption; $n = 1$ → linear (Henry's law); $n < 1$ → unfavourable.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Purely empirical — no theoretical foundation.
2. Fails at *high pressure* (predicts infinite uptake, which is physically impossible).
3. Fails at *very low pressure* (does not reduce to Henry's law).
4. Valid only in a limited range of pressure and temperature.
5. The constants $k$ and $n$ are temperature-dependent and must be re-evaluated for each system.

---

**(b) IUPAC Classification of Adsorption Isotherms (7 marks):**

The **International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)** has classified physical adsorption isotherms into **six types (I–VI)**. The five most relevant to KTU Module 4 are summarized below (3 marks):

| Type | Shape | Pore Nature | Example System |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** (Langmuir) | Sharp uptake at low P/P₀, then plateau | **Microporous** (< 2 nm) | Activated carbon + N₂ at 77 K; O₂ on charcoal at 193 K |
| **II** (BET/Sigmoidal) | Strong adsorbate–adsorbent interaction, point B marks monolayer completion, then multilayer | **Non-porous or macroporous** | N₂ on silica, Fe powder |
| **III** (Weak) | Convex to P/P₀ axis, no point B | Non-porous; weak interaction | I₂ vapor on silica gel, N₂ on polyethylene |
| **IV** (Hysteresis) | Monolayer–multilayer followed by a hysteresis loop from capillary condensation | **Mesoporous** (2–50 nm) | MCM-41, SBA-15, porous glass, MOFs |
| **V** (Weak + Hysteresis) | Weak initial uptake, hysteresis at high P/P₀ | Mesoporous; weak interaction | Water on activated carbon |

**Correlation between isotherm shape and porosity (4 marks):**

1. **Type I — Microporous solids:** The initial steep uptake corresponds to filling of *narrow micropores*. Once all micropores are filled, no further adsorption occurs → plateau. The Langmuir equation fits this shape because the small pore volume limits adsorption to essentially a monolayer.

2. **Type II — Non-porous or macroporous solids with strong interaction:** Point B in the curve marks the onset of multilayer formation; beyond it, successive layers build up indefinitely. The BET equation applies directly.

3. **Type III — Non-porous with weak interaction:** The convex shape arises because the adsorbate–adsorbate interaction is stronger than adsorbate–adsorbent, so molecules prefer to cluster rather than spread on the surface.

4. **Type IV — Mesoporous solids:** The hysteresis loop (adsorption branch ≠ desorption branch) is the signature of **capillary condensation** within mesopores. The lower closure point of the loop identifies the critical pore size.

5. **Type V — Mesoporous with weak interaction:** Similar hysteresis to Type IV, but the initial uptake is weak (Type III-like) due to poor adsorbate–adsorbent affinity.

> **Engineering significance:** The shape of the isotherm tells a *materials engineer* whether the solid is microporous (Type I), mesoporous (Type IV), or non-porous (Types II/III) — critical for selecting adsorbents in catalysis, separation, and gas storage applications.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in this Topic (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Reversing the layers in the table:** Many students wrongly state "physisorption is monolayer" — it is the **chemisorption** that is monolayer. Physisorption is *multilayer*. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Missing assumptions:** In Langmuir derivation, examiners expect **all 5 assumptions** to be stated. Skipping the *homogeneity* and *no lateral interaction* assumptions = **(-1 mark)**.
> 3. **Forgetting the limit cases:** Always end the Langmuir derivation by discussing $\theta \to KP$ (low P) and $\theta \to 1$ (high P). Missing this = **(-1 mark)**.
> 4. **Mixing symbols:** $V_m$ is the **monolayer volume**, $V$ is the *actual* volume adsorbed. Swapping them in the equation = full marks lost for the sub-part.
> 5. **Wrong linear form for Langmuir:** Two common forms exist:
>    - $P/V = 1/(K V_m) + (1/V_m) P$ (most used — slope = $1/V_m$)
>    - $1/V = 1/V_m + [1/(K V_m)] (1/P)$ (alternative — slope = $1/(K V_m)$)
>    
>    Students often write the *Freundlich* linear form ($\log x/m$ vs. $\log P$) where Langmuir is required. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
> 6. **BET range:** The BET linear form is valid only in the range $0.05 \le P/P_0 \le 0.35$. Outside this range, the plot deviates from linearity.
> 7. **No units in final answer:** $V_m$ must be in mL or cm³·g⁻¹, $K$ in Pa⁻¹, $S_{BET}$ in m²·g⁻¹. Missing units = **(-0.5 to -1 mark)**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Flash-Card for "Adsorption at Solid":**

- ☐ **Adsorption** = surface accumulation; **Absorption** = bulk penetration; **Sorption** = both.
- ☐ **Physisorption** = van der Waals, 20–40 kJ·mol⁻¹, **multilayer**, **reversible**, no activation energy.
- ☐ **Chemisorption** = covalent/ionic bonds, 40–400 kJ·mol⁻¹, **monolayer**, **often irreversible**, may need activation energy.
- ☐ **Langmuir assumptions (5):** fixed sites, monolayer, no interaction, homogeneous surface, equilibrium (rate ads = rate des).
- ☐ **Langmuir equation:** $\theta = KP/(1+KP)$ — Type I shape.
- ☐ **Langmuir linear form (for plot):** $P/V = 1/(K V_m) + (1/V_m) P$ — slope gives $V_m$, intercept gives $K$.
- ☐ **Freundlich equation:** $x/m = k P^{1/n}$ — empirical, fits heterogeneous surfaces, fails at high $P$.
- ☐ **Freundlich linear form:** $\log(x/m) = \log k + (1/n) \log P$ — slope = $1/n$, intercept = $\log k$.
- ☐ **BET constant:** $c = \exp[(E_1 - E_L)/RT]$; $c$ large ⇒ strong first-layer interaction.
- ☐ **BET equation:** $V = V_m c x / [(1-x)(1-x+cx)]$ where $x = P/P_0$.
- ☐ **BET linear form:** $x/[(1-x)V] = 1/(V_m c) + [(c-1)/(V_m c)] x$.
- ☐ **$V_m$ from BET:** $V_m = 1/(s+i)$ where $s$ = slope, $i$ = intercept.
- ☐ **$S_{BET}$** = $V_m N_A a_m / (22414 m)$ — uses N₂ ($a_m = 0.162$ nm²) at 77 K.
- ☐ **Valid BET range:** $0.05 \le P/P_0 \le 0.35$.
- ☐ **IUPAC Type I** = Langmuir = **microporous** (e.g., activated carbon + N₂).
- ☐ **IUPAC Type II** = BET shape = **non-porous** with strong interaction.
- ☐ **IUPAC Type III** = convex = **non-porous with weak interaction** (e.g., I₂ on silica).
- ☐ **IUPAC Type IV** = hysteresis loop = **mesoporous** (2–50 nm), capillary condensation.
- ☐ **IUPAC Type V** = weak + hysteresis = **mesoporous with weak interaction** (e.g., H₂O on activated carbon).
- ☐ **Type IV & V hysteresis** = signature of **capillary condensation** in mesopores.
- ☐ **High $K$ in Langmuir** = strong adsorbate–adsorbent binding; **low $K$** = weak.
- ☐ **Spontaneous adsorption:** $\Delta G < 0$, $\Delta H < 0$, $|T\Delta S| > |\Delta H|$ (entropy-driven at high T).
- ☐ **Engineering uses:** activated carbon (water/air purification), silica gel (desiccant), zeolites (ion exchange), Pt-Pd-Rh (catalytic converter), Ni catalyst (hydrogenation).
- ☐ **KTU's most-asked:** Langmuir derivation (7 marks), physisorption vs chemisorption table, BET monolayer calculation, IUPAC types with porosity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluid Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fluid Interface?

A **fluid interface** is the two-dimensional boundary region that separates two immiscible fluid phases (liquid–vapor, liquid–liquid, or liquid–solid where the solid behaves as a quasi-fluid for molecular rearrangements). Although the interface is only a few molecular diameters thick (≈ 0.3–1.0 nm), it possesses thermodynamic properties distinct from the bulk phases — most notably an excess free energy per unit area known as the **interfacial (or surface) tension**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Examiner Wording)**
> A *fluid interface* is the region of finite thickness across which the intensive properties of a fluid (density, composition, energy) change continuously from the value of one bulk phase to that of another. The thermodynamic state function that quantifies this inhomogeneity is the **interfacial tension** $\gamma$, defined as the reversible work required to create one unit area of new interface at constant $T$, $P$, and composition.

Mathematically:

$$\gamma \;\equiv\; \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial A}\right)_{T,P,n_i}$$

where $G$ is the Gibbs free energy and $A$ is the interfacial area.

## 1.2 Surface Tension vs. Interfacial Tension — Distinction

| Term | Interface Involved | Typical Magnitude (mN/m at 25 °C) |
|------|--------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Surface tension** | Liquid–vapor (L–V) | 72.0 (water), 22.3 (benzene), 485 (mercury) |
| **Interfacial tension** | Liquid–liquid (L–L) | 35.0 (water/benzene), ~0 (water/ethanol) |
| **Interfacial tension** | Liquid–solid (L–S) | Not directly measurable — inferred via Young's equation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stretched Membrane"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** Imagine a taut, invisible rubber sheet stretched across the surface of a liquid. If you try to stretch or cut it, you feel a resistance. This is precisely what surface tension is — a **contractile force per unit length** that the surface exerts to minimise its area. Raindrops become spherical, insects walk on water, and mercury beads up on glass, *all* because the interface is trying to shrink itself to the lowest possible area (a sphere).

- **Force interpretation:** $\gamma$ = force per unit length acting tangentially along the surface (units: **N/m**).
- **Energy interpretation:** $\gamma$ = excess Helmholtz/Gibbs free energy per unit area (units: **J/m²**, numerically identical to N/m).

## 1.4 The Three Principal Fluid Interfaces

1. **Liquid–Vapor (L–V):** governed by **surface tension** $\gamma_{LV}$.
2. **Liquid–Liquid (L–L):** governed by **interfacial tension** $\gamma_{LL}$ (e.g., oil–water).
3. **Liquid–Solid (L–S):** governed by **interfacial tension** $\gamma_{LS}$, accessible only via Young's equation.

These three tensions meet at the **three-phase contact line** (solid–liquid–vapor junction) and their mechanical balance gives rise to the **contact angle** $\theta$ — the central geometric quantity of fluid-interface science.

## 1.5 Why Fluid Interfaces Matter in Engineering

- **Detergency & cleaning:** Reducing $\gamma_{LV}$ drives oil detachment from fabrics.
- **Enhanced oil recovery:** Lowering $\gamma_{LL}$ mobilises trapped crude.
- **Coating & printing:** Wetting behaviour governs film uniformity.
- **Biomedical implants:** Surface tension dictates protein adsorption and cell adhesion.
- **Microfluidics & lab-on-chip:** Capillary forces drive passive fluid transport.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Origin of Interfacial Tension

Molecules in the **bulk** of a liquid experience symmetric attractive forces from all directions, resulting in a zero net force. Molecules at the **surface**, however, experience an unbalanced, inward-pulling force because the vapour side offers far fewer attractive neighbours. To bring a molecule from bulk to surface, work must be done against this net inward pull — this is the physical origin of $\gamma$.

The **excess surface Helmholtz free energy per unit area** is:

$$dF \;=\; -S\,dT \;-\; P\,dV \;+\; \gamma\,dA \;+\; \sum_i \mu_i\,dn_i$$

The **excess surface internal energy** is:

$$dU^S \;=\; T\,dS^S \;+\; \gamma\,dA \;+\; \sum_i \mu_i\,dn_i^S$$

## 2.2 Mechanical Equilibrium — The Young–Laplace Pressure Jump

For any curved fluid interface separating two phases at mechanical equilibrium, there exists a **pressure difference** across the interface. This is the **Young–Laplace equation**, the single most-tested formula in KTU Module 4.

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P \;=\; \gamma \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)\;}$$

- $R_1$, $R_2$ = principal radii of curvature at a point on the interface.
- $\Delta P = P_{inside} - P_{outside}$.
- For a **spherical** interface of radius $R$: $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{R}$.
- For a **soap bubble** (two surfaces, gas–liquid–gas): $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\gamma}{R}$.
- For a **cylindrical** jet: $\Delta P = \dfrac{\gamma}{R}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This pressure jump is what drives capillary rise, controls the droplet size in emulsions, and underlies the operation of the **tensiometer** used to measure $\gamma$ via the maximum bubble pressure method.

## 2.3 Wetting — Young's Equation at the Three-Phase Line

At the **three-phase contact line** (where solid, liquid, and vapour meet), horizontal force balance of the three interfacial tensions yields **Young's equation** (1805):

$$\boxed{\;\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta\;}$$

The angle $\theta$ is the **contact angle** measured through the liquid.

- $\theta < 90°$ → liquid **wets** the solid (hydrophilic for water).
- $\theta > 90°$ → liquid **does not wet** (hydrophobic).
- $\theta = 0°$ → **complete wetting** (spreading).
- $\theta = 180°$ → theoretical **non-wetting limit**.

## 2.4 Work of Adhesion, Work of Cohesion, and Spreading

### Work of Adhesion ($W_A$)
Reversible work to separate a unit area of liquid from solid in vacuum:

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$$

Combining with Young's equation:

$$\boxed{\;W_A \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)\;} \quad\text{— Young–Dupré Equation}$$

### Work of Cohesion ($W_C$)
Reversible work to separate a unit column of pure liquid into two parts:

$$W_C \;=\; 2\gamma_{LV}$$

### Spreading Coefficient ($S$)
The Harkins spreading coefficient predicts whether one liquid will spontaneously spread over another:

$$\boxed{\;S \;=\; W_A \;-\; W_C \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL} \;-\; \gamma_{LV}\;}$$

- $S > 0$ → complete spreading (e.g., kerosene on water).
- $S < 0$ → partial spreading, finite contact angle.

## 2.5 Capillary Rise

When a capillary tube of radius $r$ is dipped into a wetting liquid, the liquid rises to a height $h$ given by balancing capillary pressure with hydrostatic pressure:

$$\boxed{\;h \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{\rho\, g\, r}\;}$$

For **complete wetting** ($\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$):

$$h \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{LV}}{\rho\, g\, r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the principle behind the **capillary-rise tensiometer** — the most accurate classical method for measuring $\gamma_{LV}$.

## 2.6 Kelvin Equation — Curvature Effect on Vapour Pressure

For a liquid droplet of radius $r$ in equilibrium with its vapour, the chemical potential equality between the curved liquid surface and the planar bulk leads to:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right) \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{LV}\, V_m}{r\,R\,T}\;}$$

- $P^\circ$ = saturation vapour pressure over a flat surface.
- $V_m$ = molar volume of liquid.
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**).
- $P > P^\circ$ for a convex droplet → smaller droplets evaporate more easily.

## 2.7 Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm

At a fluid interface, the surface concentration differs from the bulk. The **Gibbs surface excess** $\Gamma_i$ (mol/m²) for component $i$ is related to the surface-tension variation by:

$$\boxed{\;d\gamma \;=\; -\sum_i \Gamma_i\, d\mu_i\;}$$

For a binary system (solvent + surfactant) at constant temperature:

$$\Gamma_2 \;=\; -\frac{1}{RT}\!\left(\frac{\partial \gamma}{\partial \ln c}\right)_T$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\Gamma_2 > 0$ (positive adsorption) whenever $\gamma$ decreases with increasing surfactant concentration — the basis of surfactant action at interfaces.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | KTU Typical Application |
|---|----------|------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\gamma = (\partial G/\partial A)_{T,P,n_i}$ | Definition of $\gamma$ | Short answer / 3-mark |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \gamma(1/R_1 + 1/R_2)$ | Young–Laplace | 14-mark derivation |
| 3 | $\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$ | Spherical droplet | Numerical problems |
| 4 | $\Delta P = 4\gamma/R$ | Soap bubble | Numerical problems |
| 5 | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Young's equation | Wetting problems |
| 6 | $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$ | Young–Dupré | Adhesion problems |
| 7 | $W_C = 2\gamma_{LV}$ | Cohesion | Comparison problems |
| 8 | $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ | Spreading coefficient | Spreading/non-spreading |
| 9 | $h = 2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta/(\rho g r)$ | Jurin's law | Capillary-rise numerical |
| 10 | $\ln(P/P^\circ) = 2\gamma_{LV}V_m/(rRT)$ | Kelvin equation | Nucleation problems |
| 11 | $\Gamma = -(1/RT)(\partial\gamma/\partial \ln c)$ | Gibbs adsorption | Surfactant problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems, **always quote $\gamma$ in SI units (N/m)** before substituting into the capillary-rise or Kelvin equation. Forgetting this unit conversion is the **#1 reason students lose 1–2 marks** on 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Young–Laplace Equation (The Crown Jewel of Fluid Interfaces)

**Starting premise:** Consider a small element of a curved spherical fluid interface of radius $R$, bounded by a circular arc. Two principal radii of curvature exist for a general surface, $R_1$ and $R_2$, and we will derive the pressure jump for a **general curved surface**, then specialise.

### Step 1 — Define the Surface Element

Consider an infinitesimal rectangular patch of the interface with side lengths $a$ and $b$. The patch has area:

$$A \;=\; a \cdot b$$

### Step 2 — Express the Two Principal Radii of Curvature

Let the patch have radii of curvature $R_1$ and $R_2$ along the two perpendicular directions. After a small displacement $\delta z$ of the patch outward (normal to surface) due to the pressure jump, the new area becomes:

$$A' \;=\; (a + \delta z / R_1)\,(b + \delta z / R_2)$$

Expanding to first order in $\delta z$ (neglecting $(\delta z)^2$ terms since $\delta z$ is infinitesimal):

$$A' \;\approx\; a\,b \;+\; a\,\frac{\delta z}{R_2} \;+\; b\,\frac{\delta z}{R_1}$$

Therefore the **change in area** is:

$$\delta A \;=\; A' - A \;=\; a\,b\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)\delta z \;=\; A\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)\delta z$$

### Step 3 — Apply Thermodynamic Work Equivalence

The work done by the pressure difference $\Delta P$ pushing the patch outward by $\delta z$ is:

$$W_{\text{pressure}} \;=\; \Delta P \cdot A \cdot \delta z$$

The work done **against surface tension** to create the new area $\delta A$ is:

$$W_{\text{tension}} \;=\; \gamma \cdot \delta A \;=\; \gamma \cdot A \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)\delta z$$

### Step 4 — Equate the Two Works (Mechanical Equilibrium)

At equilibrium the pressure work equals the surface work:

$$\Delta P \cdot A \cdot \delta z \;=\; \gamma \cdot A \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)\delta z$$

Cancel $A \cdot \delta z$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P \;=\; \gamma \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)\;}$$

This is the **Young–Laplace equation** in its general form.

### Step 5 — Specialisations

- **Sphere** ($R_1 = R_2 = R$):
$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$
- **Soap bubble** (two interfaces, both with the same $\gamma$):
$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$
- **Cylindrical jet** ($R_1 = R$, $R_2 = \infty$):
$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{\gamma}{R}$$
- **Plane interface** ($R_1 = R_2 = \infty$):
$$\Delta P \;=\; 0 \quad \text{(no pressure jump across flat interface)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Young's Equation (Wetting Equilibrium)

**Starting premise:** At the three-phase contact line where solid (S), liquid (L), and vapour (V) meet, three interfacial tensions act horizontally.

### Step 1 — Identify the Three Interfacial Tensions

- $\gamma_{SV}$: solid–vapour tension (pulls the contact line along the solid surface, away from the liquid).
- $\gamma_{SL}$: solid–liquid tension (pulls along the solid surface, toward the liquid).
- $\gamma_{LV}$: liquid–vapour tension (pulls tangent to the liquid surface at the contact line).

### Step 2 — Resolve $\gamma_{LV}$ into Horizontal and Vertical Components

The vector $\gamma_{LV}$ acts along the liquid surface tangent, making angle $\theta$ with the solid surface (contact angle, measured through the liquid).

- Horizontal component: $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ (pulling away from solid, toward vapour side).
- Vertical component: $\gamma_{LV}\sin\theta$ (pulling upward along the surface — balanced by elasticity of solid; not needed for horizontal equilibrium).

### Step 3 — Horizontal Force Balance

Equilibrium of horizontal forces per unit length of contact line:

$$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

Rearranging for $\cos\theta$:

$$\boxed{\;\cos\theta \;=\; \frac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}}\;}$$

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation

- If $\gamma_{SV} > \gamma_{SL}$ → $\cos\theta > 0$ → $\theta < 90°$ → wetting.
- If $\gamma_{SV} < \gamma_{SL}$ → $\cos\theta < 0$ → $\theta > 90°$ → non-wetting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention:** All $\gamma$'s are strictly positive. $\cos\theta$ can range from $-1$ to $+1$, fixing $\theta \in [0°, 180°]$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise)

**Starting premise:** A glass capillary tube of inner radius $r$ is dipped into a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$ that wets the glass with contact angle $\theta$.

### Step 1 — Capillary Pressure Pulls Liquid Up

By Young–Laplace, across a meniscus of radius $R$, the pressure just below the meniscus is lower than atmospheric by:

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{LV}}{R}$$

For a hemispherical meniscus in a tube of radius $r$ (wetting case):

$$R \;=\; \frac{r}{\cos\theta}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$$

### Step 2 — Hydrostatic Pressure of Liquid Column

The liquid rises to height $h$ such that the hydrostatic pressure balances the capillary pressure:

$$\rho\, g\, h \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $h$

$$\boxed{\;h \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{\rho\, g\, r}\;}$$

For **complete wetting** ($\theta = 0$):

$$h_{\max} \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{LV}}{\rho\, g\, r}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** Water at 25 °C ($\gamma_{LV} = 0.0720$ N/m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) rises in a glass capillary of radius $r = 0.50$ mm with contact angle $\theta = 20°$. Calculate the height of rise. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Solution:**

Given: $\gamma_{LV} = 0.0720$ N/m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $r = 0.50 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\theta = 20°$.

$$\cos 20° \;=\; 0.9397$$

$$h \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.0720 \times 0.9397}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.50 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Numerator:

$$2 \times 0.0720 \times 0.9397 \;=\; 0.13532 \; \text{N/m}$$

Denominator:

$$1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.50 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 4.905 \; \text{N/m}^2$$

$$h \;=\; \frac{0.13532}{4.905} \;=\; 0.02759 \; \text{m} \;\approx\; 2.76 \; \text{cm}$$

> **Answer:** $h \approx 2.76$ cm.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Young–Dupré Equation (Work of Adhesion)

### Step 1 — Definition

The **work of adhesion** is the reversible work required to separate a unit area of liquid–solid interface into unit areas of liquid–vapour and solid–vapour interfaces:

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Young's Equation

From Young's equation: $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$.

Therefore:

$$W_A \;=\; (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}) \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;+\; \gamma_{LV}$$

$$\boxed{\;W_A \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)\;}$$

### Step 3 — Range of Values

- $\theta = 0°$ → $W_A = 2\gamma_{LV}$ → **maximum adhesion**.
- $\theta = 90°$ → $W_A = \gamma_{LV}$.
- $\theta = 180°$ → $W_A = 0$ → **zero adhesion** (no contact).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Spreading Coefficient

### Step 1 — Definition

The **Harkins spreading coefficient** $S$ is defined as:

$$S \;=\; W_A \;-\; W_C$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

Using $W_A = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}$ and $W_C = 2\gamma_{LV}$:

$$S \;=\; (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}) \;-\; 2\gamma_{LV}$$

$$\boxed{\;S \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL} \;-\; \gamma_{LV}\;}$$

### Step 3 — Physical Interpretation

- $S > 0$ → **complete spreading** (one liquid spreads over the other indefinitely).
- $S < 0$ → **partial spreading** (lens formation, finite contact angle).
- $S = 0$ → **critical condition** (spreading threshold).

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Kelvin Equation

### Step 1 — Chemical Potential Equality

A liquid droplet of radius $r$ is in equilibrium with its vapour at pressure $P$. The chemical potential of the liquid at the curved surface must equal the chemical potential of the vapour:

$$\mu_L^{\text{curved}}(T, P_L) \;=\; \mu_V(T, P)$$

For the same liquid in bulk with a flat surface:

$$\mu_L^{\text{flat}}(T, P_L^\circ) \;=\; \mu_V(T, P^\circ)$$

### Step 2 — Express Liquid Chemical Potential Difference

The pressure inside the curved droplet is higher than outside by Young–Laplace:

$$P_L - P_L^\circ \;=\; \Delta P \;=\; \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

The change in liquid chemical potential with pressure (at constant $T$):

$$\mu_L^{\text{curved}} - \mu_L^{\text{flat}} \;=\; V_m\,(P_L - P_L^\circ) \;=\; \frac{2\gamma V_m}{r}$$

### Step 3 — Express Vapour Chemical Potential Difference

Assuming ideal gas behaviour for the vapour:

$$\mu_V(T,P) - \mu_V(T,P^\circ) \;=\; RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Equate and Solve

$$\frac{2\gamma V_m}{r} \;=\; RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right) \;=\; \frac{2\gamma V_m}{r R T}\;}$$

This is the **Kelvin equation** — foundation of nucleation theory and capillary condensation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign of curvature:** For a **convex droplet** (liquid in vapour), $r > 0$, $P > P^\circ$ → smaller droplets have higher vapour pressure → evaporate. For a **concave meniscus** (liquid in a capillary), $r$ is taken negative → $P < P^\circ$ → capillary condensation.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Self-Test (Before Exam)

| Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Key Variable | Engineering Example |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| Capillary rise | $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta/(\rho g r)$ | $\theta$, $r$ | Wick in a candle |
| Droplet pressure | $\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$ | $R$ | Emulsion stability |
| Bubble (soap) | $\Delta P = 4\gamma/R$ | $\gamma$ | Froth flotation |
| Capillary condensation | Kelvin equation | $r$ | MOF gas storage |
| Wetting / spreading | Young, $S$ | $\theta$ | Painting, coating |
| Detergency | $W_A$, $\gamma_{SL}$ reduction | Surfactant | Soap action |

    nodeIn["Inside phase pressure P_in"]

## 4.4 Process Flow — Capillary Rise Measurement (Engineering Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT1["Insert clean glass capillary tube of known radius r into wetting liquid"]
    nodeT2["Wait until liquid level stabilises inside tube"]
    nodeT3["Measure vertical rise h from bulk liquid surface to bottom of meniscus"]
    nodeT4["Measure contact angle theta via goniometer if not assumed zero"]
    nodeT5["Apply Jurins Law: gamma = h rho g r divided by 2 cos theta"]
    nodeT6["Record surface tension gamma in SI units N per m"]

    nodeT1 --> nodeT2 --> nodeT3 --> nodeT4 --> nodeT5 --> nodeT6
```

## 4.5 Functional Flow — Derivation Logic Tree for the Young–Laplace Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStep1["Define infinitesimal surface element with sides a and b"]
    nodeStep2["Displace element outward by delta z along normal"]
    nodeStep3["Compute new area A prime equals a plus delta z over R1 times b plus delta z over R2"]
    nodeStep4["Expand to first order: delta A equals A times 1 over R1 plus 1 over R2 times delta z"]
    nodeStep5["Write pressure work Wp equals DELTA P times A times delta z"]
    nodeStep6["Write tension work Wt equals gamma times delta A"]
    nodeStep7["Equate Wp and Wt for mechanical equilibrium"]
    nodeStep8["Cancel A times delta z to obtain Young Laplace DELTA P = gamma times 1 over R1 plus 1 over R2"]

    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams follow a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** style — chosen because the physical phenomena involve invisible molecular forces and curved surfaces that are best represented as logical flow rather than pictorial drawings.
