# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 model]** Define interfacial tension and surface tension. State their units and explain why interfacial tension between two immiscible liquids is always less than the surface tension of the higher-tension liquid.

**Model Answer:**

**Surface tension** $\gamma_{LV}$ is the reversible work required to create one unit area of new liquid–vapour interface at constant $T$, $P$, and composition. **Interfacial tension** $\gamma_{LL}$ is the analogous quantity for a liquid–liquid interface between two immiscible liquids. Both have units of **N/m** (force per unit length) or equivalently **J/m²** (energy per unit area).

For two immiscible liquids A and B with surface tensions $\gamma_A$ and $\gamma_B$ (against their own vapours), the interfacial tension is empirically given by **Antonow's rule**:

$$\gamma_{AB} \;=\; \vert \gamma_A - \gamma_B \vert$$

Because $\gamma_{AB}$ is the *difference* between two surface tensions, it is always less than or equal to the larger of the two. Physically, when the two liquids are brought into contact, mutual saturation occurs, and the molecules at the interface experience attractive forces from *both* sides — partially cancelling the inward pull that creates surface tension in the first place.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 model]** What is the contact angle? Write down Young's equation and explain the physical significance of each term.

**Model Answer:**

The **contact angle** $\theta$ is the angle formed at the three-phase contact line (where solid, liquid, and vapour phases meet), measured through the liquid between the solid surface and the tangent to the liquid–vapour interface at the contact point.

**Young's equation (1805):**

$$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

Significance of each term:
- $\gamma_{SV}$ = solid–vapour interfacial tension; represents the solid's affinity for the vapour phase.
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid–liquid interfacial tension; represents the solid's affinity for the liquid phase.
- $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ = horizontal component of the liquid–vapour tension pulling along the solid surface.

The equation expresses **horizontal force balance per unit length** of the contact line. It allows calculation of the contact angle from the three interfacial tensions, or alternatively, inference of $\gamma_{SL}$ (which is otherwise unmeasurable) once $\theta$, $\gamma_{LV}$, and $\gamma_{SV}$ are known.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** — Young–Laplace Equation: Derivation and Application

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 model | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the Young–Laplace equation for the pressure difference across a curved fluid interface. Explain its significance in fluid-interface science. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A soap bubble of radius $R = 1.50$ cm is formed from an aqueous solution of surface tension $\gamma = 0.040$ N/m at 25 °C. Calculate (i) the pressure difference between the inside and outside of the bubble, and (ii) the excess pressure inside a droplet of the same liquid of the same radius. Comment on why the two answers differ. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — Derivation of Young–Laplace Equation

**Statement of the result:** The Young–Laplace equation relates the pressure difference across a curved fluid interface to its mean curvature:

$$\Delta P \;=\; \gamma \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup.** Consider a small rectangular element of the curved interface with side lengths $a$ and $b$ measured along the principal directions of curvature. Let $R_1$ and $R_2$ be the principal radii of curvature at the centre of the element.

**Step 2 — Displacement Normal to the Surface.** Allow the element to displace outward by a small distance $\delta z$ along the surface normal (this displacement is opposed by surface tension and driven by the pressure jump $\Delta P$).

**Step 3 — New Area Calculation.** After displacement, the new side lengths become $(a + \delta z/R_1)$ and $(b + \delta z/R_2)$. Hence the new area is:

$$A' \;=\; \left(a + \frac{\delta z}{R_1}\right)\left(b + \frac{\delta z}{R_2}\right) \;=\; a\,b + a\,\frac{\delta z}{R_2} + b\,\frac{\delta z}{R_1} + \frac{(\delta z)^2}{R_1 R_2}$$

Neglecting the second-order $(\delta z)^2$ term:

$$A' \;\approx\; a\,b + a\,\frac{\delta z}{R_2} + b\,\frac{\delta z}{R_1} \;=\; A\left(1 + \frac{\delta z}{a}\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)\right)$$

The change in area is therefore:

$$\delta A \;=\; A\,\delta z\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Work Done by the Pressure Difference.** The pressure jump $\Delta P = P_{\text{inside}} - P_{\text{outside}}$ does work as it pushes the element outward by $\delta z$:

$$W_{\text{pressure}} \;=\; (\Delta P)(A)(\delta z)$$

**Step 5 — Work Done Against Surface Tension.** Creating the additional area $\delta A$ requires work against surface tension:

$$W_{\text{tension}} \;=\; \gamma\,(\delta A) \;=\; \gamma\, A\, \delta z\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Mechanical Equilibrium.** At equilibrium, the two works are equal:

$$(\Delta P)(A)(\delta z) \;=\; \gamma\, A\, \delta z\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$

Cancelling the common factor $A\,\delta z$:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P \;=\; \gamma\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)\;}$$

**Significance:**
- It is the **fundamental equation governing capillary phenomena**.
- It explains **droplet/bubble formation**, **capillary rise**, **capillary condensation**, and **nucleation**.
- It is the foundation of **Laplace pressure tensiometry**.
- It directly couples a **macroscopic measurable** ($\Delta P$) with a **microscopic thermodynamic quantity** ($\gamma$).

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Application

**Given:**
- $R = 1.50$ cm $= 1.50 \times 10^{-2}$ m.
- $\gamma = 0.040$ N/m.
- For soap bubble: **two surfaces** (inner and outer films).
- For droplet: **one surface**.

**(i) Pressure difference across the soap bubble:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{bubble}} \;=\; \frac{4\gamma}{R} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 0.040}{1.50 \times 10^{-2}}$$

Numerator:

$$4 \times 0.040 \;=\; 0.160 \; \text{N/m}$$

Denominator:

$$1.50 \times 10^{-2} \;=\; 0.0150 \; \text{m}$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{bubble}} \;=\; \frac{0.160}{0.0150} \;=\; 10.67 \; \text{Pa}$$

**(ii) Pressure difference across the droplet (single surface):**

$$\Delta P_{\text{droplet}} \;=\; \frac{2\gamma}{R} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.040}{1.50 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{droplet}} \;=\; \frac{0.080}{0.0150} \;=\; 5.33 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Comment:** The soap-bubble pressure is **exactly double** the droplet pressure because the bubble has **two** liquid–gas interfaces (an inner surface and an outer surface), both contributing to the surface-tension force. A droplet, by contrast, has only **one** such interface. Hence the formula for a bubble carries a factor of 4, while that for a droplet carries a factor of 2.

---

### **Question B** — Wetting, Work of Adhesion, and Spreading Coefficient

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 model | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define contact angle and derive Young's equation. Using Young's equation, derive the Young–Dupré equation for the work of adhesion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid with surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 0.060$ N/m forms a contact angle of $\theta = 35°$ on a flat solid substrate. The solid–vapour interfacial tension is $\gamma_{SV} = 0.045$ N/m. Calculate (i) the work of adhesion $W_A$, (ii) the work of cohesion $W_C$, (iii) the solid–liquid interfacial tension $\gamma_{SL}$, and (iv) the spreading coefficient $S$. Comment on whether the liquid will spread on the solid. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — Derivation of Young's Equation and Young–Dupré

**Contact angle definition:** Already given in Q2 above.

**Derivation of Young's Equation:**

At the three-phase contact line (where solid, liquid, and vapour meet), three interfacial tensions act per unit length:

- $\gamma_{SV}$ — solid–vapour tension; acts along the solid surface, directed outward from the liquid.
- $\gamma_{SL}$ — solid–liquid tension; acts along the solid surface, directed inward toward the liquid.
- $\gamma_{LV}$ — liquid–vapour tension; acts along the liquid surface tangent, making angle $\theta$ with the solid surface.

For mechanical equilibrium in the **horizontal direction** (parallel to the solid surface):

$$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

This is **Young's equation**.

**Derivation of Young–Dupré equation:**

The **work of adhesion** $W_A$ is the reversible work per unit area required to separate a liquid–solid interface into a liquid–vapour and a solid–vapour interface:

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$$

Substituting Young's equation $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$:

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;+\; \gamma_{LV}$$

$$\boxed{\;W_A \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)\;}$$

This is the **Young–Dupré equation**.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Application

**Given:**
- $\gamma_{LV} = 0.060$ N/m.
- $\theta = 35°$ → $\cos 35° = 0.8192$.
- $\gamma_{SV} = 0.045$ N/m.

**(i) Work of adhesion:**

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) \;=\; 0.060 \times (1 + 0.8192) \;=\; 0.060 \times 1.8192$$

$$\boxed{\;W_A \;=\; 0.1092 \;\text{J/m}^2\;}$$

**(ii) Work of cohesion:**

$$W_C \;=\; 2\gamma_{LV} \;=\; 2 \times 0.060 \;=\; 0.120 \;\text{J/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\;W_C \;=\; 0.120 \;\text{J/m}^2\;}$$

**(iii) Solid–liquid interfacial tension:**

From Young's equation:

$$\gamma_{SL} \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;-\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;=\; 0.045 \;-\; 0.060 \times 0.8192$$

$$0.060 \times 0.8192 \;=\; 0.04915$$

$$\gamma_{SL} \;=\; 0.045 - 0.04915 \;=\; -0.00415 \;\text{N/m}$$

Hmm — this gives a slightly **negative** value, which is physically inadmissible in the strict sense but indicates the system is very close to the **spreading threshold** ($S \approx 0$). In practice, this means the actual contact angle is essentially zero, and the assumption $\theta = 35°$ may not perfectly reflect equilibrium. We proceed formally:

$$\boxed{\;\gamma_{SL} \;\approx\; -0.0042 \;\text{N/m}\; (mathematically)\;}$$

**More realistic interpretation:** Since $\gamma_{SL} < 0$ is unphysical, the system must have $\theta \to 0$ (complete spreading), and the $35°$ value represents a metastable state on a rough or heterogeneous surface.

**(iv) Spreading coefficient:**

$$S \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL} \;-\; \gamma_{LV}$$

Using the computed $\gamma_{SL} = -0.00415$:

$$S \;=\; 0.045 \;-\; (-0.00415) \;-\; 0.060 \;=\; 0.045 + 0.00415 - 0.060$$

$$S \;=\; -0.01085 \;\text{J/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\;S \;\approx\; -0.0109 \;\text{J/m}^2 < 0\;}$$

**Comment:** Since $S < 0$, the liquid will **not** spontaneously spread over the solid; instead, it forms a finite contact angle (consistent with $\theta = 35°$). The negative $\gamma_{SL}$ in the formal calculation signals that the liquid is **marginally close** to the spreading threshold, and minor surface modification could push the system to complete wetting.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in fluid-interface problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 vs 4 in Young–Laplace** — soap bubbles use $4\gamma/R$, droplets use $2\gamma/R$. Wrong factor = **2 marks lost**.
> 2. **Sign of $\cos\theta$** — students often write $\cos\theta$ instead of accounting for $\theta > 90°$ correctly (negative cosine). Always check the quadrant.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — plugging $r$ in cm instead of m into Jurin's law. Result is off by a factor of 100. KTU examiner will deduct **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Gibbs equation sign error** — the equation has a MINUS sign: $d\gamma = -\sum \Gamma_i d\mu_i$. Confusing the sign loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps** in Young–Laplace — students must show the explicit cancellation of $(\delta z)^2$ terms. Skipping this step loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **Failing to state the condition of validity** of Jurin's law ($r \ll$ capillary length $\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\gamma/\rho g}$, typically $< 1$ mm) — losing **1 mark** for completeness.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Surface tension $\gamma$** = work per unit area to create interface; units **N/m** or **J/m²**.
- [ ] **Young–Laplace:** $\Delta P = \gamma(1/R_1 + 1/R_2)$ → sphere $2\gamma/R$ → bubble $4\gamma/R$.
- [ ] **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$.
- [ ] **Contact angle:** $\theta < 90°$ wetting, $\theta > 90°$ non-wetting.
- [ ] **Young–Dupré:** $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$.
- [ ] **Work of cohesion:** $W_C = 2\gamma_{LV}$.
- [ ] **Spreading coefficient:** $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$; $S > 0$ ⇒ complete spreading.
- [ ] **Jurin's law (capillary rise):** $h = 2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta/(\rho g r)$.
- [ ] **Kelvin equation:** $\ln(P/P^\circ) = 2\gamma V_m/(rRT)$.
- [ ] **Gibbs adsorption:** $\Gamma = -(1/RT)(\partial \gamma / \partial \ln c)$.
- [ ] **Curvature always raises vapour pressure of a convex surface** (Kelvin).
- [ ] **Smaller droplets have higher internal pressure** (Laplace) → drives Ostwald ripening.
- [ ] **Two surfaces for bubbles, one for droplets** — common exam pitfall.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** $\Delta P = P_{\text{concave side}} - P_{\text{convex side}} > 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Henry, Freundlich and Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Henry, Freundlich and Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Adsorption Isotherm?

An **adsorption isotherm** is a fundamental thermodynamic relationship that describes how the quantity of an adsorbate (molecules/ions accumulated at an interface) binds to a unit mass of an adsorbent (the bulk solid or liquid phase) as a function of equilibrium pressure (for gases) or equilibrium concentration (for solutes), measured at a **constant temperature**.

Mathematically, it expresses the functional dependence:

$$q_e = f(P \text{ or } C_e) \quad \text{at} \quad T = \text{constant}$$

where $q_e$ is the equilibrium loading (typically expressed as $x/m$, mass adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The three classical isotherms — **Henry, Freundlich, and Langmuir** — form the analytical backbone of all adsorption engineering calculations in KTU's Colloid and Interfacial Science paper. Mastery of their derivations, linear plots, and physical assumptions is **non-negotiable** for ESE (End Semester Examination).

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Magnet on a Wall"

Imagine a **magnet pinned to a refrigerator door**:

1. **The refrigerator door** = the surface of the adsorbent (solid).
2. **The paperclips** sticking to it = adsorbate molecules.
3. **How crowded the door gets with paperclips** = the loading $(x/m)$.
4. **How hard the wind blows** = the equilibrium pressure $P$ (or concentration $C_e$).
5. **As the wind picks up, more clips stick, until every magnetized spot is occupied.** This is exactly what an isotherm plots — *loading vs. "wind"* at one fixed temperature.

| Magnet Analogy | Adsorption Concept |
|---|---|
| Magnet with fixed slots | Langmuir (monolayer, finite sites) |
| Magnet with mixed-strength spots | Freundlich (heterogeneous, energetic distribution) |
| Very few clips, all stick freely | Henry (linear, low-coverage limit) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Snapshot:** At very *low* pressure, every collision of a gas molecule with the surface finds a vacant, energetically attractive site → adsorption is purely proportional to pressure (**Henry region**). As pressure rises, sites begin to saturate and a plateau forms (**Langmuir region**). On a *patchy, uneven* surface, no plateau ever truly forms and a *power-law* (Freundlich) trend emerges instead.

---

### 1.3 Why Three Different Isotherms?

Each isotherm is a **mathematical model built on different physical assumptions** about the adsorbent surface and adsorbate-adsorbate interaction. No single model is universally correct; the engineer must select the appropriate one based on the system under study.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants & Notation (adopted KTU-wide):**
> - $x$ = mass (or moles) of adsorbate adsorbed at equilibrium.
> - $m$ = mass of the adsorbent.
> - $P$ = equilibrium pressure of the adsorbate gas (Pa, atm, or bar).
> - $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration of the solute (mol/L or mg/L).
> - $q_e = x/m$ = equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg/g, mol/g, or cm³ STP/g).
> - $T$ = absolute temperature (**K**).
> - **STP** = 273.15 K and 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa (**Standard Temperature and Pressure**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Henry's Adsorption Isotherm

#### 2.1.1 Assumptions
1. Valid only in the **low-pressure / dilute limit** ($P \to 0$ or $C_e \to 0$).
2. Surface coverage $\theta \ll 1$ — virtually every adsorbate molecule finds an *isolated, energetically equivalent* site.
3. Adsorbate-adsorbate lateral interactions are **negligible**.
4. The surface behaves as an **ideal "solution" of adsorbed molecules** in the adsorbent.

#### 2.1.2 The Relationship (Mathematical Statement)
At sufficiently low pressure, adsorption is **directly proportional** to pressure:

$$\frac{x}{m} = K_H \cdot P \qquad \text{(gas)} \qquad \text{or} \qquad \frac{x}{m} = K_H' \cdot C_e \qquad \text{(solution)}$$

- $K_H$ is the **Henry's constant** (units of mol·kg⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ for gases; L·kg⁻¹ for solutions).
- A larger $K_H$ implies **stronger affinity** of the surface for the adsorbate.

#### 2.1.3 Physical Origin
It can be derived rigorously by taking the **low-coverage Taylor-series limit** of the Langmuir equation ($bP \ll 1$):

$$q = \frac{q_m \, bP}{1 + bP} \;\xrightarrow{bP\,\ll\,1}\; q_m \, bP = K_H \, P$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Henry's law is the **"common ancestor"** of all three isotherms. It is the *boundary condition* that Langmuir and Freundlich must both obey at extreme dilution.

---

### 2.2 Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm

#### 2.2.1 Origin & Assumptions
Empirically proposed by **Herbert Freundlich** in 1909 from experimental data on activated carbon, before any rigorous kinetic theory existed. Its assumptions:

1. The surface is **heterogeneous** — site energies follow an exponential distribution.
2. **Multilayer adsorption** is permitted (no fixed monolayer capacity).
3. Valid over a **moderate range** of pressures/concentrations.
4. Fails at very low (where Henry is exact) and very high pressures (no saturation plateau).

#### 2.2.2 Mathematical Form
The non-linear Freundlich expression:

$$\boxed{\frac{x}{m} = k_F \cdot P^{\,1/n}}$$

For solutes: $x/m = k_F \cdot C_e^{\,1/n}$

where:
- $k_F$ = Freundlich capacity constant (units depend on exponent).
- $1/n$ = heterogeneity / intensity parameter (**dimensionless**, with $0 < 1/n < 1$ for favorable adsorption).
- If $1/n = 1$: reduces to **Henry's law** ($k_F$ becomes $K_H$).
- If $1/n = 0$: $x/m \to k_F$ (saturation — never truly attained).

#### 2.2.3 Linearization (Critical for KTU Graph Problems)
Taking the logarithm of both sides:

$$\log\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \log(k_F) + \frac{1}{n} \log(P)$$

A plot of $\log(x/m)$ vs. $\log(P)$ gives a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** = $1/n$
- **Intercept** = $\log(k_F)$

---

### 2.3 Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

#### 2.3.1 Origin & Assumptions
Proposed by **Irving Langmuir** in 1916 and awarded the **Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1932)** for his contributions to surface chemistry. Assumptions:

1. The surface contains a **fixed number of identical, energetically equivalent adsorption sites**.
2. Each site can hold **only one** adsorbate molecule — **strict monolayer** adsorption.
3. No adsorbate-adsorbate lateral interaction (energy of adsorption is independent of coverage).
4. Adsorption is localized; once a site is filled, it cannot host another molecule.
5. The process is reversible, reaching dynamic equilibrium between adsorption and desorption.

#### 2.3.2 Mathematical Form (Non-linear)

$$\boxed{\frac{x}{m} = \frac{k_L \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}}$$

where:
- $x/m$ = amount adsorbed per gram at equilibrium.
- $k_L$ (or $q_m$) = **Langmuir monolayer capacity** (max possible loading when all sites are filled).
- $b$ (or $K_L$) = **Langmuir adsorption constant** = $k_a/k_d$ (ratio of rate constants of adsorption to desorption).
- Larger $b$ → stronger binding affinity.

#### 2.3.3 Coverage (θ) Form

$$\theta = \frac{x/m}{k_L} = \frac{bP}{1 + bP}$$

#### 2.3.4 Linearized Forms (Multiple Variants — KTU-Favorite Question Type)

| Linearized Form | y-axis | x-axis | Slope | Intercept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | $P/(x/m)$ | $P$ | $1/k_L$ | $1/(k_L \, b)$ |
| **Type II** | $1/(x/m)$ | $1/P$ | $1/(k_L \, b)$ | $1/k_L$ |
| **Type III** | $(x/m)/P$ | $x/m$ | $b$ | $k_L \, b$ |

The most commonly tested form (Type I) is:

$$\frac{P}{x/m} = \frac{1}{k_L \, b} + \frac{P}{k_L}$$

#### 2.3.5 Dimensionless Equilibrium Parameter ($R_L$) — Separator for "Irreversibility"

Langmuir introduced a separation factor:

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + b \, P_0}$$

where $P_0$ is the initial (highest) adsorbate pressure.

| $R_L$ Value | Nature of Isotherm |
|---|---|
| $R_L > 1$ | Unfavorable |
| $R_L = 1$ | Linear (Henry) |
| $0 < R_L < 1$ | **Favorable** |
| $R_L = 0$ | Irreversible |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD TABLE — Memorize this for KTU ESE. Examiners frequently give 7-mark questions asking to "write the isotherm, derive it, and identify the constants."**

| Isotherm | Equation | Linear Form | Plot | Slope | Intercept | Applicable Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Henry** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = K_H P$ | $x/m$ vs $P$ | Straight line through origin | $K_H$ | $0$ | $P \to 0$, $\theta \ll 1$ |
| **Freundlich** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = k_F P^{1/n}$ | $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log P$ | Straight line | $1/n$ | $\log k_F$ | Moderate $P$; heterogeneous surface |
| **Langmuir** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = \dfrac{k_L \, b P}{1 + b P}$ | $P/(x/m)$ vs $P$ | Straight line | $1/k_L$ | $1/(k_L b)$ | Homogeneous surface, monolayer |

| Constant | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $K_H$ | Slope of adsorption at zero coverage | mol·kg⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ or L·kg⁻¹ |
| $k_F$ | Relative adsorption capacity | (mol·g⁻¹)(Pa⁻¹)^(1/n) |
| $1/n$ | Surface heterogeneity / intensity | dimensionless (0 < 1/n < 1) |
| $k_L$ or $q_m$ | Monolayer saturation capacity | mol·g⁻¹ or mg·g⁻¹ |
| $b$ or $K_L$ | Adsorption affinity constant | Pa⁻¹ or L·mol⁻¹ |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Isotherm | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Drinking water purification (removal of dyes, heavy metals like Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺) | **Langmuir** or **Freundlich** | Activated carbon surfaces; controlled single-pass columns. |
| Gas mask / respirator cartridges (toxic vapors at trace levels) | **Henry** + **Langmuir** | Trace concentrations require accurate low-coverage modeling. |
| Heterogeneous catalysis (zeolites, supported metals) | **Freundlich** | Active site energy distributions. |
| Compressed gas storage in porous carbons (CH₄, H₂ tanks) | **Langmuir** | Saturation capacity is the design-limiting parameter. |
| Wastewater treatment (phenol, methylene blue on biosorbents) | **Freundlich** | Biomass surfaces are highly heterogeneous. |
| Air separation by pressure-swing adsorption (PSA) | **Langmuir** | Predictable monolayer saturation essential for cycle design. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A single material (e.g., activated carbon) is *rarely* described by one isotherm across the full pressure range. Real systems transition **Henry → Freundlich → Langmuir plateau** as pressure rises. Designers use composite isotherms (e.g., the **BET isotherm** in Module 4's extension) when multilayer coverage becomes significant.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Langmuir Isotherm (Kinetic Approach)

The Langmuir isotherm is **the single most derivation-heavy topic** in KTU Module 4. You must be able to derive it from first principles.

#### Step 1 — Define the Surface Coverage

Let $N$ = total number of adsorption sites per unit mass of adsorbent.  
Let $N^*$ = number of sites **occupied** at equilibrium.  
Define fractional coverage:

$$\theta = \frac{N^*}{N}$$

Therefore, fraction **vacant** = $1 - \theta$.

#### Step 2 — Rate of Adsorption

Adsorption requires **two events to occur simultaneously** at a single site:
1. A gas-phase molecule must **collide** with the surface.
2. The collision site must be **vacant**.

Rate of molecular collision with the surface (from kinetic theory of gases) is proportional to pressure $P$.  
So the **adsorption rate** is:

$$r_{\text{ads}} = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

where $k_a$ is the adsorption rate constant.

#### Step 3 — Rate of Desorption

Desorption can occur only from an **occupied** site, and its rate is independent of pressure:

$$r_{\text{des}} = k_d \cdot \theta$$

where $k_d$ is the desorption rate constant.

#### Step 4 — Dynamic Equilibrium

At equilibrium, $r_{\text{ads}} = r_{\text{des}}$:

$$k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for $\theta$

Expanding:

$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta$$

Grouping $\theta$ terms on one side:

$$k_a P = k_d \theta + k_a P \theta = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P}$$

#### Step 6 — Introduce the Adsorption Equilibrium Constant $b$

Define $b = k_a / k_d$ (with units of Pa⁻¹). Substituting:

$$\theta = \frac{b P}{1 + b P}$$

#### Step 7 — Convert from $\theta$ to Loading $(x/m)$

By definition of $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{x/m}{x/m_{\max}} = \frac{x/m}{k_L}$$

where $k_L$ = maximum monolayer capacity (loading at $\theta = 1$). Substituting:

$$\frac{x/m}{k_L} = \frac{b P}{1 + b P}$$

Solving explicitly for $x/m$:

$$\boxed{\frac{x}{m} = \frac{k_L \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}}$$

#### Step 8 — Derive the Linearized (Type I) Form

Starting from:

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{k_L \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}$$

Take the reciprocal:

$$\frac{m}{x} = \frac{1 + b P}{k_L \, b \, P} = \frac{1}{k_L \, b \, P} + \frac{b P}{k_L \, b \, P}$$

Simplify the second term ($b P$ cancels):

$$\frac{m}{x} = \frac{1}{k_L \, b \, P} + \frac{1}{k_L}$$

Multiplying both sides by $P$:

$$\boxed{\frac{P}{x/m} = \frac{1}{k_L \, b} + \frac{P}{k_L}}$$

This is the famous **Langmuir linearized plot**:
- Plot $P/(x/m)$ on the y-axis.
- Plot $P$ on the x-axis.
- **Slope** = $1/k_L$ → gives monolayer capacity.
- **Intercept** = $1/(k_L \, b)$ → gives the affinity constant $b$.

#### Step 9 — Verify Limiting Behavior

| Limit | Substitution | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $bP \ll 1$ (low pressure) | $1 + bP \approx 1$ | $x/m \approx k_L \, b \, P = K_H P$ → **Henry region** |
| $bP \gg 1$ (high pressure) | $1 + bP \approx bP$ | $x/m \approx k_L$ → **Saturation plateau** |
| $bP = 1$ | $1 + bP = 2$ | $x/m = k_L/2$ → half-monolayer coverage |

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Henry's Isotherm as a Limiting Case of Langmuir

Starting from the Langmuir equation:

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{k_L \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}$$

When $bP \ll 1$ (low-coverage limit), the denominator $1 + bP \approx 1$:

$$\frac{x}{m} \approx k_L \, b \, P = K_H \, P$$

where $K_H = k_L \, b$. This is the **Henry isotherm**.

**Physical interpretation:** At extremely low pressures, sites are so sparse that the probability of a molecule landing on an already-occupied site is negligible. Each collision is essentially with a *fresh, identical site*, giving a linear (Henry) response.

---

### 3.3 Freundlich Isotherm — Analytical Framework

The Freundlich isotherm is **empirical** — it cannot be derived from kinetic theory without invoking a *specific surface-energy distribution*. However, it can be motivated as follows.

#### Step 1 — Assume an Exponential Energy Distribution

Let $N(E)$ = number of sites with adsorption energy $\geq E$:

$$N(E) = N_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

#### Step 2 — At Equilibrium, Adsorption Prefers Lower-Energy Sites First

Using the Boltzmann distribution, fractional coverage of sites with energy $E$:

$$\theta(E) = \frac{b \, P}{1 + b \, P}$$

with $b = b_0 \exp(E/RT)$ (an Arrhenius-type relation).

#### Step 3 — Integrate Over All Energies

The total amount adsorbed is proportional to the integral of $\theta(E)$ weighted by the site density $N(E)$. Performing this integration (a non-trivial exercise in variational calculus) yields:

$$\frac{x}{m} = k_F \cdot P^{1/n}$$

with $1/n$ related to the breadth of the energy distribution.

#### Step 4 — Linearize for Graphical Determination

Taking the base-10 logarithm:

$$\log\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \log(k_F) + \frac{1}{n} \log(P)$$

This is the form students plot in the KTU lab/practical paper.

#### Step 5 — Limiting Case: $1/n = 1$

$$\frac{x}{m} = k_F \cdot P^{1} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{m} = k_F \, P$$

This is the **Henry isotherm** again. So Freundlich reduces to Henry at low coverage.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Comparison-Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Spot:** Direct 3-mark questions asking to *"Compare Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms"* appear almost every year.

| Feature | Henry | Freundlich | Langmuir |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Limiting (linear) law | Empirical | Theoretical (kinetic) |
| **Surface assumption** | Homogeneous, very low $\theta$ | Heterogeneous, energy distribution | Homogeneous, identical sites |
| **Multilayer adsorption** | No (irrelevant at low $\theta$) | Yes (implicit) | No (strict monolayer) |
| **Saturation plateau** | No | No (approached only as $1/n \to 0$) | Yes ($x/m \to k_L$) |
| **Number of parameters** | 1 ($K_H$) | 2 ($k_F$, $1/n$) | 2 ($k_L$, $b$) |
| **Linear form** | $x/m$ vs $P$ | $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log P$ | $P/(x/m)$ vs $P$ |
| **Best-fit application** | Trace gas capture | Dye/heavy-metal removal on rough biosorbents | Gas storage, clean catalyst surfaces |
| **Validity range** | $bP \ll 1$ | Moderate $P$ | All $P$ (within monolayer assumption) |
| **Derivable?** | Yes (limit of Langmuir) | No (empirical fit) | Yes (from kinetic equilibrium) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Numerical Type)

> **Problem:** A 2.0 g sample of activated charcoal adsorbs 50 mg of acetic acid at an equilibrium concentration of 0.06 mol/L. Using the Langmuir equation (assume monolayer capacity $k_L = 200$ mg/g and constant $b = 12.5$ L/mol), verify the data and determine $R_L$ for an initial concentration of 0.10 mol/L.

**Step 1 — Compute experimental loading:**

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{50 \text{ mg}}{2.0 \text{ g}} = 25 \text{ mg/g}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Langmuir prediction:**

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{k_L \, b \, C_e}{1 + b \, C_e} = \frac{200 \times 12.5 \times 0.06}{1 + 12.5 \times 0.06}$$

Compute denominator: $1 + 0.75 = 1.75$

Compute numerator: $200 \times 12.5 \times 0.06 = 150$

Therefore:

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{150}{1.75} = 85.71 \text{ mg/g}$$

**Step 3 — Verify (note discrepancy, comment on assumption violation):**

The model predicts 85.71 mg/g vs. observed 25 mg/g → indicates that $C_e = 0.06$ mol/L is *not* the equilibrium concentration at saturation; the assumption of $k_L = 200$ mg/g may overestimate.

**Step 4 — Compute $R_L$:**

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + b \, C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + 12.5 \times 0.10} = \frac{1}{2.25} = 0.444$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

$R_L = 0.444 \in (0, 1)$ → **Favorable adsorption** on the activated carbon surface.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Relationship Between the Three Isotherms

The following block-level architecture illustrates how the three isotherms interrelate across the pressure domain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Adsorption Isotherms"] --> B["Henry Region"]
    A --> C["Freundlich Region"]
    A --> D["Langmuir Region"]
    A --> E["BET Region (Multilayer Extension)"]

    B --> B1["x divided by m equals K sub H times P"]
    B --> B2["Valid when bP is much less than 1"]
    B --> B3["Linear through origin"]

    C --> C1["x divided by m equals k sub F times P to the power 1 over n"]
    C --> C2["Slope one over n"]
    C --> C3["Intercept log k sub F"]

    D --> D1["x divided by m equals k sub L b P divided by 1 plus b P"]
    D --> D2["Monolayer plateau at k sub L"]
    D --> D3["Slope one over k sub L"]

    D --> D5["Linear form P over x divided by m equals 1 over k sub L b plus P over k sub L"]

    E --> E1["Used when P over P0 is between 0.05 and 0.35"]
    E --> E2["Multi-layer coverage"]

    B --> F["All three reduce to linear Henry at very low P"]
    D --> F
    C --> F
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Selecting an Isotherm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: Choose Adsorption Isotherm"] --> Q1{"Is the surface energetically uniform?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes, homogeneous" --> Q2{"Is coverage in monolayer regime?"}
    Q1 -- "No, heterogeneous or unknown" --> Q3["Use Freundlich Empirical"]
    Q2 -- "Yes, monolayer" --> Q4["Use Langmuir Theoretical"]
    Q2 -- "No, multilayer" --> Q5["Use BET"]
    Q3 --> Q6["Plot log x divided by m versus log P"]
    Q4 --> Q7["Plot P over x divided by m versus P"]
    Q5 --> Q8["Plot P over P sub 0 over x divided by m one minus P over P sub 0"]
    Q6 --> Q9["Obtain k sub F and one over n"]
    Q7 --> Q10["Obtain k sub L and b"]
    Q8 --> Q11["Obtain monolayer capacity and C constant"]
    Q9 --> R["Apply for engineering design"]
    Q10 --> R
    Q11 --> R
```

### 4.3 Langmuir Adsorption–Desorption Equilibrium Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GA["Gas Phase Molecules Pressure P"] --> C["Collision with Surface"]
    C --> S1["Vacant Site Fraction one minus theta"]
    S1 --> A["Adsorption Event Rate k sub a"]
    A --> M["Molecule Bound to Site"]

    M --> D["Desorption Event Rate k sub d"]
    D --> GP["Molecule Returns to Gas Phase"]

    M -.Equilibrium.-> EQ["k sub a P times one minus theta equals k sub d theta"]
    EQ --> SOL["Solution theta equals bP divided by one plus bP"]

    SOL --> OUT["Final Form x divided by m equals k sub L b P divided by one plus b P"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** State Henry's adsorption isotherm. Under what conditions is it valid?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Henry's adsorption isotherm states that **at very low pressures (or concentrations) and constant temperature, the amount of gas adsorbed by a given mass of adsorbent is directly proportional to the equilibrium pressure**:

$$\frac{x}{m} = K_H \cdot P$$

where $K_H$ is the Henry constant. **Validity conditions:** (i) very low surface coverage $\theta \ll 1$, (ii) homogeneous surface with energetically uniform sites, (iii) negligible adsorbate–adsorbate interactions. It is the limiting form of both Langmuir ($bP \ll 1$) and Freundlich ($1/n = 1$) isotherms.

*(Marks distribution: 1 — equation, 1 — explanation of $K_H$, 1 — validity conditions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** Differentiate between Freundlich and Langmuir adsorption isotherms.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Freundlich | Langmuir |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Empirical (curve-fitting) | Theoretical (kinetic) |
| Surface | Heterogeneous, energetically non-uniform | Homogeneous, uniform sites |
| Multilayer | Implicit, no saturation plateau | Strict monolayer, saturates at $k_L$ |
| Equation | $x/m = k_F \, P^{1/n}$ | $x/m = (k_L \, b \, P)/(1 + b P)$ |
| Linear form | $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log P$ | $P/(x/m)$ vs $P$ |

*(Marks distribution: 1 — basis difference, 1 — equation difference, 1 — linear-form / saturation difference.)*
