### 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

#### Model Solution to Question A (Part a — 7 Marks)

**(1) Statement of Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The surface has a **fixed number of identical adsorption sites**.
2. Each site can accommodate **only one molecule** (monolayer).
3. Adsorbate-adsorbate interactions are **negligible**.
4. Adsorption is **reversible**, reaching dynamic equilibrium.
5. The adsorption energy is **constant** — independent of $\theta$.

**(2) Kinetic Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Define coverage: $\theta = N^* / N$; vacant fraction = $1 - \theta$.

Step 2 — Adsorption rate: $r_{\text{ads}} = k_a P (1 - \theta)$ (collision frequency $\propto P$, requires vacant site).

Step 3 — Desorption rate: $r_{\text{des}} = k_d \theta$ (only from occupied sites).

Step 4 — Equilibrium: $k_a P (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$.

Step 5 — Solve:

$$k_a P = k_d \theta + k_a P \theta = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P}$$

Step 6 — Define $b = k_a / k_d$:

$$\theta = \frac{b P}{1 + b P}$$

Step 7 — Multiply by monolayer capacity $k_L$:

$$\frac{x}{m} = k_L \theta = \frac{k_L \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}$$

*(Marks distribution: 1 — defining θ; 1 — adsorption rate; 1 — desorption rate; 1 — equilibrium condition; 1 — algebraic solution; 1 — introduction of b. Total 6 marks for derivation + 1 mark for assumptions = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (Part b — 7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $P/(x/m)$ for each data point (2 marks):**

| $P$ (kPa) | $x/m$ (cm³/g) | $P/(x/m)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 32 | 0.1563 |
| 10 | 56 | 0.1786 |
| 20 | 84 | 0.2381 |
| 40 | 116 | 0.3448 |
| 80 | 156 | 0.5128 |

**Step 2 — Test linearity via $P/(x/m)$ vs $P$ (2 marks):**

Compute slope between first and last points:

$$\text{slope} \approx \frac{0.5128 - 0.1563}{80 - 5} = \frac{0.3565}{75} = 4.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/cm}^3$$

Intermediate slopes between consecutive points should match for true linearity. The data show consistent trends — **Langmuir fit is reasonable**.

**Step 3 — Determine $k_L$ from slope (1 mark):**

$$\text{slope} = \frac{1}{k_L} \Rightarrow k_L = \frac{1}{4.75 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 210 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 4 — Determine $b$ from intercept (2 marks):**

Using $P/(x/m) = 1/(k_L b) + P/k_L$:

$$0.1563 = \frac{1}{210 \, b} + \frac{5}{210}$$

$$0.1563 - 0.0238 = \frac{1}{210 \, b} = 0.1325$$

$$b = \frac{1}{210 \times 0.1325} = \frac{1}{27.82} \approx 0.0359 \text{ kPa}^{-1}$$

**Conclusion (additional credit):** The data fits the Langmuir isotherm reasonably well, indicating **monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous carbon surface**. Monolayer capacity $\approx 210$ cm³ STP/g of carbon.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (Part a — 7 Marks)

**(1) Derivation of Linear Form (3 marks):**

Starting from the empirical Freundlich equation:

$$\frac{x}{m} = k_F \, C_e^{1/n}$$

Taking the logarithm of both sides:

$$\log\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \log(k_F) + \frac{1}{n} \log(C_e)$$

This is a **straight-line equation** in $\log(C_e)$.

**(2) Physical Significance (2 marks):**

- $k_F$: **Freundlich capacity constant** — measures the *relative adsorption capacity* of the adsorbent. Larger $k_F$ → higher capacity.
- $1/n$: **Heterogeneity / intensity factor** — measures *surface heterogeneity* and *adsorption intensity*. The closer $1/n$ is to zero, the more heterogeneous the surface. Values $0 < 1/n < 1$ indicate favorable adsorption.

**(3) Limiting Behavior (2 marks):**

- **Low $C_e$:** as $C_e \to 0$, the exponent $1/n$ loses significance and the curve approaches linear behavior → reduces to **Henry's law**.
- **High $C_e$:** the Freundlich equation predicts continuously increasing $x/m$ with no plateau. Physically, this is unrealistic for monolayer adsorption — Freundlich fails at saturation. For multilayer behavior, BET is preferred.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (Part b — 7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $\log(C_e)$ and $\log(x/m)$ (2 marks):**

| $C_e$ (mol/L) | $x/m$ (mg/g) | $\log C_e$ | $\log(x/m)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.05 | 12.5 | $-1.301$ | $1.097$ |
| 0.10 | 20.0 | $-1.000$ | $1.301$ |
| 0.20 | 30.0 | $-0.699$ | $1.477$ |
| 0.40 | 45.0 | $-0.398$ | $1.653$ |
| 0.80 | 60.0 | $-0.097$ | $1.778$ |

**Step 2 — Test linearity (2 marks):**

Compute slope between extreme points:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{1.778 - 1.097}{-0.097 - (-1.301)} = \frac{0.681}{1.204} = 0.566$$

Intermediate slopes:
- (0.05→0.10): $(1.301-1.097)/(−1.000+1.301) = 0.204/0.301 = 0.678$
- (0.10→0.20): $(1.477−1.301)/(−0.699+1.000) = 0.176/0.301 = 0.585$
- (0.20→0.40): $(1.653−1.477)/(−0.398+0.699) = 0.176/0.301 = 0.585$
- (0.40→0.80): $(1.778−1.653)/(−0.097+0.398) = 0.125/0.301 = 0.415$

Approximate linearity → **Freundlich fit is valid**, with mean slope $\approx 1/n \approx 0.55$.

**Step 3 — Determine $1/n$ (1 mark):**

$$\frac{1}{n} \approx 0.55 \Rightarrow n \approx 1.82$$

**Step 4 — Determine $k_F$ (1 mark):**

Using the midpoint point $(C_e = 0.20, x/m = 30)$:

$$\log k_F = \log(30) - 0.55 \times \log(0.20) = 1.477 - 0.55 \times (-0.699) = 1.477 + 0.384 = 1.861$$

$$k_F = 10^{1.861} \approx 72.6$$

**Step 5 — Final Equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\frac{x}{m} = 72.6 \, C_e^{0.55}}$$

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Top 5 Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting to linearize before plotting.** Examiners explicitly want the linear form $P/(x/m)$ vs $P$ for Langmuir. Plotting raw $x/m$ vs $P$ and claiming "it saturates" without computing constants = **0 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up which constant is slope vs. intercept.** For Langmuir linear plot $P/(x/m)$ vs $P$: slope = $1/k_L$, intercept = $1/(k_L b)$. Reversing them yields wrong $b$ — **lose 2 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting assumptions.** A derivation without clearly listed assumptions = **lose 2 of 7 marks**. KTU strictly enforces this per the CO (Course Outcome) mapping.
> 4. **Not specifying the units of constants.** $k_F$ units depend on $1/n$; $k_L$ in mg/g; $b$ in Pa⁻¹ or L/mol. Always declare units. Missing units = **−1 mark per occurrence**.
> 5. **Confusing $1/n$ with $n$.** $1/n$ is the slope; $n$ alone is rarely used directly in the plot. Many students write "$n = 0.55$" when they should say "$1/n = 0.55$" → **lose 1 mark + misinterpretation risk**.
> 6. **Skipping dimensional analysis of limiting behavior.** Examiners love asking "show that at low pressure Langmuir reduces to Henry" — if you don't write the substitution step, **lose 2 marks**.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Henry, Freundlich & Langmuir Isotherms**

**Core Equations (memorize verbatim):**
- Henry: $x/m = K_H P$
- Freundlich: $x/m = k_F P^{1/n}$ → linear: $\log(x/m) = \log k_F + (1/n)\log P$
- Langmuir: $x/m = (k_L b P)/(1 + b P)$ → linear: $P/(x/m) = 1/(k_L b) + P/k_L$

**Three Lines Every Examiner Wants:**
1. Each isotherm assumes a **specific surface model**: Henry (ideal dilute), Langmuir (homogeneous + monolayer), Freundlich (heterogeneous, no plateau).
2. The Henry isotherm is the **common low-coverage limit** of both Langmuir (when $bP \ll 1$) and Freundlich (when $1/n \to 1$).
3. Langmuir is **theoretically derivable** from kinetic arguments; Freundlich is **empirical**; Henry is **thermodynamically motivated**.

**Five Constants to Recognize Instantly:**
- $K_H$ → Henry constant (low-coverage slope)
- $k_F$ → Freundlich capacity
- $1/n$ → Freundlich intensity / heterogeneity (0 < 1/n < 1)
- $k_L$ (or $q_m$) → Langmuir monolayer capacity
- $b$ (or $K_L$) → Langmuir adsorption affinity ($k_a/k_d$)

**Plot Signatures (identify from shape alone):**
- **Linear through origin** → Henry
- **Power-law concave-downward, no plateau** → Freundlich
- **Concave-downward with clear plateau at high P** → Langmuir

**Engineering Decision Heuristics:**
- Trace gases / respirators → **Henry**
- Dye/heavy-metal removal on biomass → **Freundlich**
- Gas storage / clean catalysts / PSA columns → **Langmuir**

**Numbers to Memorize:**
- $bP \ll 1$ → linear (Henry)
- $bP \gg 1$ → plateau ($x/m \to k_L$)
- $R_L = 1/(1 + bC_0)$ → favorable if $0 < R_L < 1$
- $1/n = 1$ → reduces to Henry

**Exam-Day Checklist:**
1. ☐ Identify the isotherm class from the data trend.
2. ☐ Apply the correct linearization (Langmuir: $P/(x/m)$; Freundlich: $\log$).
3. ☐ Compute slope and intercept → extract constants with **units**.
4. ☐ Verify by checking limiting behavior.
5. ☐ State all assumptions explicitly when deriving.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BET Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** BET Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**BET Theory** (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller Theory, 1938) is a generalized multilayer physical adsorption model that extends the **Langmuir monolayer theory** to describe the adsorption of gas molecules on solid surfaces where more than one molecular layer can form. It is the foundational model for determining the **specific surface area** of porous and finely divided solids (catalysts, adsorbents, pigments, clays, and pharmaceutical powders) from gas adsorption isotherms, typically using **nitrogen at 77 K**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** BET theory is the gold-standard method for surface area determination in chemical engineering practice. Mastery of the linear BET equation, monolayer capacity ($V_m$), and the BET constant ($C$) is **mandatory** for KTU ESE valuation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Multi-Story Parking Lot"

Imagine a **flat, dusty parking lot** (the solid surface) on a rainy day:

- **Raindrops (gas molecules)** start falling and fill the **ground floor first** (first adsorbed layer).
- Once the ground floor is **partially wet**, a second layer of drops can stack on top of those already on the ground (second layer).
- The process continues: layer 2, layer 3, layer 4… like **parking cars on multiple stories** of an open-deck garage.
- **Langmuir theory** only describes the *ground floor*. **BET theory** describes *all stories* — and the cars on upper decks behave differently (their adhesion energy equals the heat of liquefaction, $E_L$, not the much stronger surface binding energy $E_1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multilayer matters:** Real solids are not perfectly flat at the atomic level. Adsorption begins in **micropores and high-energy sites**, then propagates outward, building a *statistical* stack of layers. The **BET equation** mathematically describes this stack and, from a single isotherm, tells you the *area of the ground floor* — i.e., the true surface area of the solid.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standard Metrics)

- **Cross-sectional area of N₂ molecule:** $A_m = \mathbf{16.2\ \text{Å}^2 = 16.2 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{m}^2}$
- **Molar volume at STP (0 °C, 1 atm):** $V_{\text{molar}} = \mathbf{22{,}414\ \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}}$
- **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = \mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}}$
- **Valid BET range:** $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$ (linear region)
- **Typical BET constant $C$ range:** $\mathbf{2 \leq C \leq 200}$ for physical adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Fundamental Assumptions of BET Theory

1. **Multilayer Adsorption:** Gas molecules form an *infinite* number of adsorbed layers on the surface (in practice, condensation is complete near $P_0$).
2. **Layer-by-Layer Langmuir Behavior:** Each adsorbed layer is treated as an *independent* Langmuir-type equilibrium (no lateral interaction between adjacent molecules in the same layer).
3. **First-Layer Heat of Adsorption ($E_1$):** Molecules in the **first** layer experience a *unique* heat of adsorption with the solid, generally much higher than for higher layers.
4. **Higher-Layer Heat = Heat of Liquefaction ($E_L$):** All layers above the first have a heat of adsorption equal to the **heat of liquefaction (condensation)** of the adsorbate gas.
5. **No Transverse Interactions:** The forces of adsorption are *purely vertical* (perpendicular to the surface); lateral interactions between adsorbed molecules in the same layer are neglected.
6. **Dynamic Equilibrium:** Each layer is in dynamic equilibrium — the rate of condensation onto a layer equals the rate of evaporation from it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The crucial conceptual leap in BET vs. Langmuir is that **only the first layer** has a special adsorption energy. From the *second layer upward*, the molecules behave as if they are condensing into a *bulk liquid-like film*. This is what creates the characteristic *S-shaped* Type II isotherm.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Directive:** In the formula table below, **DO NOT** use the `|` symbol inside table cells (e.g., writing $\vert V \vert$ breaks markdown parsing). Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

| # | Equation (LaTeX form) | Symbol Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | **Linear BET equation** — $P$: equilibrium pressure; $V$: adsorbed gas volume (STP); $V_m$: monolayer capacity; $C$: BET constant | $\text{cm}^{-3}\text{·g}$ |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{V_m \, C \, x}{(1-x)\bigl[1 + (C-1)x\bigr]}$ | **Non-linear BET form**; $x = P/P_0$ (relative pressure) | $\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ |
| 3 | $C = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right]$ | **BET constant** — $E_1$: heat of adsorption (1st layer); $E_L$: heat of liquefaction; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $S_{\text{BET}} = \dfrac{V_m \, N_A \, A_m}{V_{\text{molar}}}$ | **Specific surface area** — $A_m$: cross-sectional area of adsorbate; $N_A$: Avogadro number | $\text{m}^2/\text{g}$ |
| 5 | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C}$ ; $\text{Intercept} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C}$ | From a **BET plot** of $P/[V(P_0-P)]$ vs. $P/P_0$ | — |
| 6 | $V_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{Slope} + \text{Intercept}}$ | Direct monolayer volume from linear regression | $\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ |
| 7 | $C = \dfrac{\text{Slope}}{\text{Intercept}} + 1$ | Direct BET constant from linear regression | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$ | **Validity range** of linear BET | — |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of BET |
|---|---|
| **Heterogeneous Catalysis** | Surface area of catalyst supports (e.g., $\gamma$-Al₂O₃, zeolites, activated carbon) dictates active site density and turnover frequency. |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Drug dissolution rate, tablet compaction, and powder flow depend on specific surface area. |
| **Ceramics & Pigments** | Sintering kinetics and tinting strength are governed by particle surface area. |
| **Adsorption Engineering** | Designing fixed-bed adsorbers (PSA, TSA) for CO₂ capture, VOCs, and gas purification. |
| **Battery & Fuel Cell R&D** | Electrode surface area in supercapacitors and PEMFCs is characterized by BET. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the BET Equation

### 3.1.1 Defining Surface Coverages

Let $S$ be the total surface area of the adsorbent. Define $S_i$ as the area covered by $i$ layers of adsorbed gas. Then:

$$S = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} S_i$$

The **fractional coverage** of the $i$-th layer is $\theta_i = S_i / S$, and clearly:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \theta_i = 1$$

### 3.1.2 Equilibrium of the First Layer

Molecules strike the bare surface (layer 0) at a rate proportional to pressure $P$, with condensation constant $a_1$. They evaporate at rate $b_1 \exp(-E_1/RT)$, where $E_1$ is the heat of adsorption for the first layer.

At **dynamic equilibrium**:

$$a_1 P \, \theta_0 = b_1 \theta_1 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_1}{RT}\right)$$

Solving for $\theta_1$:

$$\theta_1 = \left(\dfrac{a_1}{b_1}\right) P \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_1}{RT}\right) \theta_0 = y \, \theta_0$$

where $y = \dfrac{a_1 P}{b_1} \exp(E_1/RT)$.

### 3.1.3 Equilibrium of the $i$-th Layer ($i \geq 2$)

For the second and higher layers, the heat of adsorption equals the **heat of liquefaction** $E_L$, and the condensation/evaporation constants are equal ($a_i = b_i = a$). The equilibrium for layer $i$:

$$a P \, \theta_{i-1} = b \, \theta_i \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_L}{RT}\right) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta_i = x \, \theta_{i-1}$$

where $x = \dfrac{a P}{b} \exp(E_L/RT)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step logic:** The ratio $y/x$ collapses into the famous **BET constant $C$**:
> $$C = \dfrac{y}{x} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right]$$
> A large $C$ means $E_1 \gg E_L$ (strong surface–adsorbate interaction, Type II isotherm).

### 3.1.4 Summing the Coverage Fractions

Therefore:

$$\theta_1 = C x \theta_0, \quad \theta_2 = x \theta_1 = C x^2 \theta_0, \quad \theta_i = C x^i \theta_0 \;\; (i \geq 1)$$

Substitute into the closure condition $\sum \theta_i = 1$:

$$\theta_0 \left[1 + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} C x^i \right] = \theta_0 \left[1 + C x \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} x^i \right] = 1$$

For $|x| < 1$ (valid below saturation):

$$\theta_0 \left[1 + \dfrac{C x}{1-x}\right] = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta_0 = \dfrac{1-x}{1 + (C-1)x}$$

### 3.1.5 Total Volume Adsorbed

The total volume of gas adsorbed $V$ equals the monolayer capacity $V_m$ times the *average* number of layers per surface site:

$$V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \, \theta_i = V_m \, C x \theta_0 \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \, x^{i-1}$$

Using the identity $\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i x^{i-1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-x)^2}$:

$$V = V_m \, C x \theta_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1-x)^2} = \dfrac{V_m \, C x}{(1-x)\bigl[1 + (C-1)x\bigr]}$$

### 3.1.6 Linear Form (The Working Equation)

Substitute $\theta_0 = (1-x)/[1+(C-1)x]$ back and rearrange. With $x = P/P_0$:

$$\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The linear form is what KTU examiners use.** Plotting $P/[V(P_0-P)]$ (y-axis) vs. $P/P_0$ (x-axis) in the range $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$ gives a **straight line** with:
> - **Slope** $= (C-1)/(V_m C)$
> - **Intercept** $= 1/(V_m C)$
>
> From these, you can solve for $V_m$ and $C$ — and hence the surface area.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem Statement
The following nitrogen adsorption data (at 77 K) were obtained on a finely divided solid catalyst. The saturation pressure $P_0 = 1$ atm.

| $P/P_0$ | $V$ (cm³/g at STP) |
|---|---|
| 0.05 | 30.0 |
| 0.10 | 35.0 |
| 0.15 | 40.0 |
| 0.20 | 47.0 |

**Calculate the monolayer capacity $V_m$, the BET constant $C$, and the specific surface area of the catalyst.** (Take $A_m = 16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ m² for N₂.)

### Step 1 — Compute the BET Function $P/[V(P_0-P)]$

For each row, compute $y = \dfrac{P/P_0}{V(1 - P/P_0)}$.

**Row 1:** $P/P_0 = 0.05$, $V = 30.0$

$$y_1 = \dfrac{0.05}{30.0 \times (1 - 0.05)} = \dfrac{0.05}{30.0 \times 0.95} = \dfrac{0.05}{28.5} = 1.754 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Row 2:** $P/P_0 = 0.10$, $V = 35.0$

$$y_2 = \dfrac{0.10}{35.0 \times 0.90} = \dfrac{0.10}{31.5} = 3.175 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Row 3:** $P/P_0 = 0.15$, $V = 40.0$

$$y_3 = \dfrac{0.15}{40.0 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{0.15}{34.0} = 4.412 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Row 4:** $P/P_0 = 0.20$, $V = 47.0$

$$y_4 = \dfrac{0.20}{47.0 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{0.20}{37.6} = 5.319 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

### Step 2 — Linear Regression (Slope and Intercept)

Let $x_i = P/P_0$ and $y_i = P/[V(P_0-P)]$. With $n = 4$:

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{0.05 + 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.20}{4} = 0.125$$

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{1.754 + 3.175 + 4.412 + 5.319}{4} \times 10^{-3} = 3.665 \times 10^{-3}$$

Slope:

$$m = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

Computing each term:

| $i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}\ (\times 10^{-3})$ | Product $(\times 10^{-3})$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-0.075$ | $-1.911$ | $+0.1433$ | $0.005625$ |
| 2 | $-0.025$ | $-0.490$ | $+0.01225$ | $0.000625$ |
| 3 | $+0.025$ | $+0.747$ | $+0.01868$ | $0.000625$ |
| 4 | $+0.075$ | $+1.654$ | $+0.12405$ | $0.005625$ |
| **Σ** | — | — | **0.2983** | **0.012500** |

$$m = \dfrac{0.2983 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01250} = 0.02386\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

Intercept:

$$b = \bar{y} - m \bar{x} = 3.665 \times 10^{-3} - 0.02386 \times 0.125 = 3.665 \times 10^{-3} - 2.983 \times 10^{-3} = 0.682 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

### Step 3 — Extract $V_m$ and $C$

Recall: $b = 1/(V_m C)$ and $m = (C-1)/(V_m C)$.

$$V_m C = \dfrac{1}{b} = \dfrac{1}{0.682 \times 10^{-3}} = 1466.3\ \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$$

$$C - 1 = m \cdot V_m C = 0.02386 \times 1466.3 = 34.99 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C \approx 36.0$$

$$V_m = \dfrac{V_m C}{C} = \dfrac{1466.3}{36.0} = 40.7\ \text{cm}^3/\text{g (STP)}$$

### Step 4 — Specific Surface Area

$$S_{\text{BET}} = \dfrac{V_m \, N_A \, A_m}{V_{\text{molar}}} = \dfrac{40.7 \times (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \times (16.2 \times 10^{-20})}{22{,}414}$$

Numerator:

$$40.7 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 245.1 \times 10^{23}$$

$$245.1 \times 10^{23} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20} = 3970.6 \times 10^{3} = 3.971 \times 10^{6}$$

Therefore:

$$S_{\text{BET}} = \dfrac{3.971 \times 10^{6}}{22{,}414} = 177.2\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A specific surface area of $\sim 177\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$ classifies this material as a **mesoporous catalyst** (e.g., silica-alumina, MCM-41, or a similar high-area support), well within the range typical for industrial hydrocracking or hydrotreating catalysts ($100$–$400\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: BET vs. Langmuir

| Feature | Langmuir (1918) | BET (1938) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of layers | One (monolayer) | Infinite (multilayer) |
| Heat of adsorption | Uniform $E$ for all sites | $E_1$ (1st layer), $E_L$ (rest) |
| Lateral interaction | Neglected | Neglected |
| Isotherm shape | Type I (Langmuir) | Type II (BET sigmoid) |
| Typical $C$ value | N/A | 2–200 |
| Primary use | Chemisorption studies, active site count | **Specific surface area** measurement |
| Validity range | All $P/P_0$ for Type I | $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$ |
| $V$ at $P \to P_0$ | Saturates at $V_m$ | Diverges (bulk condensation) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multilayer Adsorption Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    gasM[Gas Phase Molecules] --> impinge[Impinge on Solid Surface]
    impinge --> site1[First Layer Adsorption]
    site1 --> heat1[Heat Released E1]
    site1 --> eq1{Dynamic Equilibrium}
    eq1 -->|Condensation Wins| layer2[Second Layer Forms]
    eq1 -->|Evaporation Wins| desorb1[Molecule Returns to Gas]
    layer2 --> heatL[Heat Released E sub L equal to Heat of Liquefaction]
    layer2 --> eq2{Layer 2 Equilibrium}
    eq2 --> layer3[Third Layer Forms]
    eq2 --> desorb2[Molecule Returns to Lower Layer]
    layer3 --> upperN[Higher Layers n-1 n ...]
    upperN --> bulk[Bulk Liquid Like Condensate at P near P0]
    site1 --> bareVac[Bare Sites S0]
    bareVac --> measureP[Measure V at Various P over P0]
    measureP --> plotBET[Plot P over V P0 minus P vs P over P0]
    plotBET --> linFit[Linear Regression Slope m Intercept b]
    linFit --> getVm[Extract Vm and C]
    getVm --> surfArea[Compute S sub BET in m2 per g]
```

## 4.2 BET Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SAMPLE[Stage A Sample Preparation]
        dirA1[Weigh Degassed Sample] --> dirA2[Outgas at 300 degree C]
        dirA2 --> dirA3[Transfer to Analysis Port]
    end
    subgraph DOSING[Stage B Gas Dosing]
        dirB1[Admit N2 Pulses] --> dirB2[Record Equilibrium P]
        dirB2 --> dirB3[Record Adsorbed V]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS[Stage C Data Analysis]
        dirC1[Compute P over V P0 minus P] --> dirC2[BET Plot in valid range]
        dirC2 --> dirC3[Slope m and Intercept b]
        dirC3 --> dirC4[Compute Vm and C]
    end
    subgraph REPORT[Stage D Reporting]
        dirD1[Specific Surface Area m2 per g] --> dirD2[Pore Volume and Pore Size Distribution]
    end
    SAMPLE --> DOSING
    DOSING --> ANALYSIS
    ANALYSIS --> REPORT
```

## 4.3 Six BET Assumptions — Logic Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[BET Theory Core Assumptions] --> a1[Multilayer Adsorption Infinite Stack]
    root --> a2[Independent Langmuir Layers]
    root --> a3[First Layer Heat equals E1 unique]
    root --> a4[Higher Layers Heat equals E sub L]
    root --> a5[No Lateral Interactions]
    root --> a6[Dynamic Equilibrium Each Layer]
    a3 --> cmath[Result: BET Constant C equals exp of E1 minus E sub L over RT]
    a4 --> cmath
    cmath --> output[Governs Adsorption Isotherm Shape Type II]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
>
> **Q1. Define the BET constant $C$. What does a high value of $C$ indicate about the nature of adsorption?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The BET constant $C$ is defined as:
> $$C = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right]$$
> where $E_1$ is the heat of adsorption in the **first layer** and $E_L$ is the **heat of liquefaction** of the adsorbate. A **high value of $C$** (e.g., $C > 100$) indicates that $E_1 \gg E_L$, meaning the adsorbate interacts **very strongly** with the bare surface — characteristic of **strong physical adsorption** (Type II isotherm) or borderline chemisorption. Conversely, a low $C$ (close to 2) indicates weak, almost liquefaction-like interaction throughout.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for interpretation of high C.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
>
> **Q2. Why is the linear form of the BET equation only valid in the relative pressure range $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The BET linearization rests on two assumptions that break down outside this range:
> 1. **Below $P/P_0 = 0.05$:** Surface heterogeneity dominates; the assumption of uniform $E_1$ for the first layer fails, and the data fall off the straight line.
> 2. **Above $P/P_0 = 0.35$:** Capillary condensation in mesopores and the onset of bulk-like multilayer stacking invalidate the BET model's assumption of *independent* layers. The isotherm curves upward, deviating from linearity.
>
> Hence, the linear range $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$ is the **only region** where BET theory quantitatively describes the system.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for low-P reason, 1 for high-P reason, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 (Model)]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** State the **assumptions of BET theory** and derive the **linear form** of the BET adsorption isotherm equation. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Using the following N₂ adsorption data at 77 K, calculate the **monolayer capacity $V_m$**, the **BET constant $C$**, and the **specific surface area** of the sample. Given: $A_m = 16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ m², $V_{\text{molar}} = 22{,}414$ cm³/mol (STP). (7 marks)
>
> | $P/P_0$ | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.20 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | $V$ (cm³/g, STP) | 28.0 | 33.0 | 38.0 | 46.0 |

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

State the **six assumptions** (covered in Section 2.1 above): 1 mark for each significant assumption (≈3 marks), and 4 marks for the **derivation** as in Section 3.1, leading to:

$$\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$$

*(Full derivation as in Section 3.1 should be reproduced on the answer script. The final linear form must be boxed/highlighted for full credit.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute $y_i = \dfrac{P/P_0}{V(1 - P/P_0)}$.

- $P/P_0 = 0.05$: $y_1 = 0.05 / (28.0 \times 0.95) = 0.05 / 26.6 = 1.880 \times 10^{-3}$
- $P/P_0 = 0.10$: $y_2 = 0.10 / (33.0 \times 0.90) = 0.10 / 29.7 = 3.367 \times 10^{-3}$
- $P/P_0 = 0.15$: $y_3 = 0.15 / (38.0 \times 0.85) = 0.15 / 32.3 = 4.644 \times 10^{-3}$
- $P/P_0 = 0.20$: $y_4 = 0.20 / (46.0 \times 0.80) = 0.20 / 36.8 = 5.435 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 2:** Linear regression: $\bar{x} = 0.125$, $\bar{y} = 3.832 \times 10^{-3}$.

$$m = \dfrac{0.05 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0125 + \cdots}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

Performing the regression (or using the two-endpoint approximation for brevity, marked accordingly):

$$m \approx 0.0238\ \text{g/cm}^3, \quad b \approx 0.71 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3:** $V_m C = 1/b = 1/(0.71 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 1408$ cm³/g; $C = m/b + 1 = 0.0238/(0.71 \times 10^{-3}) + 1 = 33.5 + 1 = 34.5$.

$$V_m = 1408 / 34.5 \approx 40.8\ \text{cm}^3/\text{g (STP)}$$

**Step 4:** Surface area:

$$S_{\text{BET}} = \dfrac{40.8 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20}}{22{,}414} \approx 178\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

*(2 marks for $V_m$, 2 marks for $C$, 3 marks for surface area calculation with units.)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
>
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **physical picture of multilayer adsorption** as conceived by BET. How does it differ from the **Langmuir model**? Discuss the significance of the BET constant $C$ in characterizing adsorption strength. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A catalyst has a BET specific surface area of $250\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$ and a measured monolayer nitrogen capacity of $V_m = 57.4$ cm³/g at STP. Calculate the **BET constant $C$** and the **heat of adsorption in the first layer** ($E_1$) at 77 K. Given: Heat of liquefaction of N₂ at 77 K is $E_L = 5.56$ kJ/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 77$ K. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Physical picture (3 marks):** Draw a cross-section of the solid surface showing the bare surface $S_0$, monolayer $S_1$, second layer $S_2$, etc., as in Section 4.1 above. Explain that the first layer binds with $E_1$, while all subsequent layers bind with $E_L$ (heat of liquefaction, the same as bulk condensation).

**Langmuir vs. BET (2 marks):** Langmuir considers only *one* layer with a *single* heat of adsorption $E$. BET extends this to *infinitely many* layers with two distinct energies: $E_1$ for the first and $E_L$ for the rest.

**Significance of $C$ (2 marks):** $C = \exp[(E_1 - E_L)/RT]$ quantifies the *excess* heat of adsorption of the first layer over bulk condensation. A high $C$ ($\gg 100$) signals strong surface affinity; a $C \to 1$ indicates nearly liquefaction-dominated behavior.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Use the surface area equation in reverse to find $V_m C$ from $S_{\text{BET}}$. Alternative: Use the relationship between $S_{\text{BET}}$ and $V_m$ via cross-sectional area. Actually, given that both $S_{\text{BET}}$ and $V_m$ are stated independently, the most direct method is to use:

$$V_m C = \dfrac{S_{\text{BET}} \cdot V_{\text{molar}}}{N_A \cdot A_m} \cdot C$$

which simplifies once we recognize that the *given* $V_m$ is sufficient. A cleaner approach is to use the BET slope-intercept form. We have:

$$S_{\text{BET}} = \dfrac{V_m \, N_A \, A_m}{V_{\text{molar}}} = 250\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

Check consistency:

$$V_m = \dfrac{250 \times 22{,}414}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20}} = \dfrac{5{,}603{,}500}{97.56} \approx 57.4\ \text{cm}^3/\text{g}\ \checkmark$$

**Step 2 (3 marks):** For a Type II isotherm with strong adsorption, the BET plot intercept is small. Using the standard relationship for $C$ from the catalyst's reported "Point B" position and assuming the $P/P_0$ at Point B is approximately 0.10 (typical for high-$C$ systems), one can approximate $C$ from:

$$C \approx \dfrac{1 - x_B}{x_B} \cdot \dfrac{V_B}{V_m - V_B}$$

where $V_B$ is the adsorbed volume at Point B. For a typical $C \approx 50$ (a common value for catalysts like this), $E_1$ is computed as follows.

**Alternatively — direct calculation from a single $C$ assumption:** If we assume (consistent with catalyst literature) $C = 50$:

**Step 3 (2 marks):**

$$E_1 = E_L + RT \ln C = 5560 + (8.314)(77) \ln(50)$$

$$E_1 = 5560 + 640.2 \times 3.912 = 5560 + 2504 = 8064\ \text{J/mol} = 8.06\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The problem as posed is solvable only when a second piece of information (e.g., the intercept $b$ of the BET plot, or the Point B) is given. With only $S_{\text{BET}}$ and $V_m$, $C$ is *not* uniquely determined from these data alone — $C$ is fundamentally an isotherm-shape parameter. In a typical KTU ESE question, the data table (as in Question A) would be provided, from which $C$ is regressed. The model solution above shows the calculation method once $C$ is established.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on BET Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $V$ to STP:** $V$ in BET must be in cm³/g at **STP (0 °C, 1 atm)**, not at the experimental temperature. The molar volume $V_{\text{molar}} = 22{,}414$ cm³/mol is *only* valid at STP. Converting via the ideal gas law ($V_{\text{STP}} = V_T \times 273.15/T$) is essential.
> 2. **Using the wrong $A_m$:** $A_m$ depends on the adsorbate gas. For **N₂ at 77 K**, it is $16.2\ \text{Å}^2$. For **Kr at 77 K** (low-area samples), it is $20.5\ \text{Å}^2$. For **Ar at 87 K**, it is $13.8\ \text{Å}^2$. Using the wrong value is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Inverting the slope-intercept logic:** Students often confuse the slope with $1/V_m$ and the intercept with $C$. Remember: **Slope** $= (C-1)/(V_m C)$, **Intercept** $= 1/(V_m C)$. So $V_m C = 1/(\text{intercept})$ and $C = 1 + \text{slope}/(\text{intercept})$.
> 4. **Plotting outside the valid range:** Using $P/P_0 > 0.35$ data points in the linear regression will yield a *non-linear* deviation and a wrong $V_m$.
> 5. **Units of surface area:** $S_{\text{BET}}$ is conventionally reported in $\text{m}^2/\text{g}$. A common error is reporting it in cm²/g or m²/kg.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **BET = multilayer extension of Langmuir**; only the *first* layer has unique heat $E_1$; *all higher* layers have heat $E_L$.
- **Linear BET equation:** $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0-P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$
- **BET plot range:** $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$ — outside this, theory breaks down.
- **BET constant:** $C = \exp[(E_1 - E_L)/RT]$; high $C$ ⇒ strong surface interaction.
- **Surface area formula:** $S_{\text{BET}} = V_m N_A A_m / V_{\text{molar}}$ with $V_{\text{molar}} = 22{,}414$ cm³/mol.
- **N₂ cross-sectional area:** $A_m = 16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ m² ($= 16.2\ \text{Å}^2$).
- **From slope $m$ and intercept $b$ of the BET plot:** $V_m C = 1/b$ and $C = 1 + m/b$, hence $V_m = 1/(m + b)$.
- **Type II isotherm = BET regime** (non-porous or macroporous solids); Type IV = mesoporous with capillary condensation (BET still applies for surface area).
- **Limitation of BET:** Assumes *uniform* surface energy and *no pore filling*; fails in micropores (use $t$-plot, Dubinin–Radushkevich, or NLDFT instead).
- **Standard conditions:** All $V$ values used in BET calculation must be at **STP** ($0$ °C, $1$ atm).
- **Engineering importance:** BET is the **industry standard** for catalyst support characterization, drug formulation, and adsorbent screening — mastering it is essential for placements in petrochemical, pharma, and process industries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Experimental Techniques for Measurement of Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT863 - COLLOID AND INTERFACIAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Direct Experimental Techniques for Measurement of Adsorption # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Direct Experimental Techniques for Measurement of Adsorption** are a class of methods that quantify the amount of an adsorbate retained at an interface by monitoring a primary physical property (mass, volume, heat, optical constant, or radioactivity) directly proportional to the surface excess concentration, **Γ** (mol/m²), without recourse to secondary model-based interpretation. The governing measurable quantity is the **surface excess concentration**: $$\Gamma = \frac{n^{\sigma}}{A}$$ where: - $n^{\sigma}$ = Gibbs surface excess amount of adsorbate (mol) - $A$ = interfacial area (m²) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 mandates a working knowledge of at least four direct techniques — **gravimetric (QCM)**, **volumetric (manometric)**, **calorimetric**, and **spectroscopic/ellipsometric**. Each is classified by whether it yields *integral* (total uptake) or *differential* (rate/process) information. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners Imagine a dry kitchen sponge sitting on a precision weighing balance. As you slowly drip water onto it: - **Gravimetric method** = Watching the balance needle rise. The mass increase *is* the adsorption amount. - **Volumetric method** = Tracking how much water disappeared from a measuring cup before it touched the sponge. The missing volume *equals* what was adsorbed. - **Calorimetric method** = Placing a thermometer against the sponge. The warmth tells you *how vigorously* it drank the water. - **Optical method (Ellipsometry)** = Shining a torch at the wet sponge surface and measuring how the reflected light changed — even a single molecular layer alters the reflection. The sponge (adsorbent) and water (adsorbate) interact at the *interface*, and every direct technique probes a *different physical fingerprint* of that interaction. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Reference:** The IUPAC-recommended unit for surface excess is **μmol/m²** for dissolved solutes and **cm³(STP)/g** for gas adsorption on porous solids. ## 1.3 Classification of Direct Techniques | Class | Property Measured | Typical Sensitivity | Information Type | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Gravimetric | Mass change | ng/cm² | Integral | | Volumetric (Manometric) | Pressure/volume | 0.1 cm³ STP | Integral | | Calorimetric | Heat flow | μJ | Differential | | Optical (Ellipsometry/SPR) | Polarization/refractive index | 0.1 Å layer thickness | Both | | Radiotracer | Radioactive decay counts | sub-monolayer | Integral | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Technique 1 — Gravimetric Method (Quartz Crystal Microbalance, QCM) ### Operating Principle A thin AT-cut quartz crystal is excited at its fundamental shear-mode resonance frequency $f_0$ (typically **5–10 MHz**). Adsorption of mass onto the crystal surface decreases the resonance frequency. The relationship is **linear and quantitative**. ### Sauerbrey Equation (1959) — The Master Equation For thin, rigid, uniformly distributed films: $$\Delta f = -\frac{2 f_0^{2}}{A \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}} \cdot \Delta m$$ For a 5 MHz crystal in air ($\rho_q = 2648$ kg/m³, $\mu_q = 2.947 \times 10^{10}$ N/m²), this simplifies to the **sensitivity constant**: $$C_f = \frac{\Delta m}{-\Delta f} \approx 17.7 \ \text{ng} \cdot \text{Hz}^{-1} \cdot \text{cm}^{-2}$$ ### Why It Works - The added mass behaves as an extension of the crystal's inertia. - **Frequency shift is directly proportional to mass** — no calibration with adsorbate needed. - Operates under vacuum, in gas, or in liquid flow cells. ## 2.2 Technique 2 — Volumetric (Manometric) Method ### Operating Principle A known dose of gas is expanded into a sample chamber containing the adsorbent. The pressure drop between dosing volume and sample volume, after equilibration, is used to compute moles adsorbed via the **Ideal Gas Law** (or virial equation for high pressure). ### Calculation Logic 1. Introduce $n_{dose}$ moles into a calibrated dose volume $V_d$ at measured $P_d, T$. 2. Open valve to sample volume $V_s$ containing adsorbent at $T$. 3. Wait for equilibrium → final pressure $P_{eq}$. 4. Apply material balance: $$n_{ads} = n_{dose} - n_{gas,eq} = \frac{P_d V_d}{RT} - \frac{P_{eq}(V_d + V_s - V_{ads})}{RT}$$ The term $V_{ads}$ (penetration volume of solid) is small and corrected by helium blank runs. ## 2.3 Technique 3 — Calorimetric Method ### Operating Principle Adsorption is inherently exothermic. A sensitive calorimeter measures the heat evolved, $Q_{ads}$ (J/mol), giving the **differential heat of adsorption** $q_{diff}$: $$q_{diff} = \left(\frac{\partial Q_{ads}}{\partial n_{ads}}\right)_{T,A}$$ Types used in KTU-scope study: - **Isothermal heat-conduction calorimeter** (heat flow measured by Peltier sensors). - **Tian-Calvet microcalorimeter** (twin-cell differential design, sensitivity ~μW). - **Adsorption calorimetry coupled with manometry** (yields simultaneous $n$ vs. $q$). ## 2.4 Technique 4 — Ellipsometry (Optical Method) ### Operating Principle Polarized light reflects off a surface; the state of polarization changes by two angles — **Ψ** (amplitude ratio) and **Δ** (phase difference). The Fresnel equations relate ($\Psi$, $\Delta$) to film thickness $d$ and refractive index $n_f$. For a single transparent film: $$\rho = \frac{r_p}{r_s} = \tan(\Psi) e^{i\Delta}$$ Solving the inverse problem with a multi-layer model yields $d$ (and hence $\Gamma$ via $\Gamma = d \cdot \rho_{bulk} / M$). ## 2.5 Technique 5 — Radiotracer Method ### Operating Principle A radiolabeled adsorbate (e.g., ¹⁴C-labeled surfactant, ³H-labeled polymer) is adsorbed onto a substrate. The decay count rate $R$ (counts/s) is linearly proportional to surface concentration: $$\Gamma = \frac{R}{\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot N_A \cdot \lambda_{spec}}$$ where $\varepsilon$ = counting efficiency, $\lambda_{spec}$ = specific activity (Bq/mol). ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Use | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | $\Gamma = n^{\sigma}/A$ | Surface excess | mol/m² | Universal definition | | $\Delta f = -C_f \Delta m$ | Sauerbrey (simplified) | Hz, ng | QCM | | $n_{ads} = \dfrac{P_d V_d - P_{eq} V_{free}}{RT}$ | Material balance | mol | Manometry | | $q_{diff} = \partial Q/\partial n_{ads}$ | Differential heat | J/mol | Calorimetry | | $\rho = \tan\Psi \cdot e^{i\Delta}$ | Ellipsometric ratio | dimensionless | Ellipsometry | | $\theta = P/(P+P_0)$ for Langmuir | Surface coverage | fraction | Adsorption isotherms | | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0-P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C}\cdot\dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | BET equation | — | Multi-layer adsorption | | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT}\dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$ | Gibbs isotherm | mol/m² | Liquid interface | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No pipes in any table cell!** The vertical bar `$\vert x \vert$` symbol was avoided in this sheet; instead, parentheses or explicit context ("fraction", "dimensionless") are used to preserve markdown integrity. ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **QCM** → biosensors (DNA hybridization, antigen detection), corrosion monitoring in oil pipelines. - **Volumetric** → BET surface area of catalysts in petrochemical refining (e.g., FCC zeolites). - **Calorimetric** → screening catalyst acidity in ammonia synthesis (Haber-Bosch). - **Ellipsometry** → thin-film quality control in semiconductor fabrication (SiO₂ on Si wafer). - **Radiotracer** → surfactant adsorption at oil-water interfaces in enhanced oil recovery (EOR). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: BET Equation from Kinetic Model (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, 1938) ### Step 1 — Define Surface States Let $\theta_i$ = fraction of surface covered by $i$ adsorbed layers ($i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty$). At equilibrium, the total adsorbed volume per gram is: $$V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \theta_i$$ ### Step 2 — Apply Kinetic Equilibrium Rate of condensation on layer $i-1$ = Rate of evaporation from layer $i$. Define $s$ = sticking probability ratio (upper layers vs. first layer). The recurrence relation: $$a_1 \theta_0 P = b_1 \theta_1 e^{-E_1/RT}$$ $$a_i \theta_{i-1} P = b_i \theta_i e^{-E_L/RT} \quad \text{for } i \geq 2$$ Here $E_L$ = heat of liquefaction, $E_1$ = heat of first-layer adsorption. ### Step 3 — Introduce Sticking Coefficient Ratio Define $x = P/P_0$ (relative pressure). For $i \geq 2$ layers: $$\theta_i = C x^i \theta_0, \quad \text{where } C = \frac{a_1 b_2}{a_2 b_1} e^{(E_1 - E_L)/RT}$$ ### Step 4 — Sum the Geometric Series $$\theta_0 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} C x^i} = \dfrac{1 - x}{1 - x + Cx}$$ Therefore: $$\theta_0 = \frac{1 - x}{1 + (C-1)x}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Total Volume Adsorbed $$V = V_m \cdot \frac{Cx}{(1-x)(1 + (C-1)x)} \cdot \frac{1}{1-x}$$ After algebraic simplification, divide both sides by $(1-x)$: $$V = V_m \cdot \frac{Cx}{(1-x)(1 + (C-1)x)}$$ ### Step 6 — Rearrange into the Linear BET Form Multiply numerator and denominator by 1/(P/P₀): $$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$ This linear form is the **plot used to extract $V_m$ and $C$** in any volumetric experiment. ## 3.2 Derivation: Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm (for Liquid Interfaces) ### Starting Point — Gibbs Adsorption Equation $$d\gamma = -\sum_i \Gamma_i d\mu_i$$ For a binary solute (1 = solvent, 2 = solute) at constant T: $$d\gamma = -\Gamma_1 d\mu_1 - \Gamma_2 d\mu_2$$ ### Convention: Gibbs Dividing Surface Choose the dividing surface such that $\Gamma_1 = 0$ (solvent excess vanishes). Then: $$d\gamma = -\Gamma_2 d\mu_2$$ Since $d\mu_2 = RT \, d\ln a_2$ (ideal-dilute approximation: $a_2 \approx C$): $$\Gamma_2 = -\frac{1}{RT} \cdot \frac{d\gamma}{d\ln C} = -\frac{C}{RT}\left(\frac{d\gamma}{dC}\right)$$ ### Engineering Interpretation Surface tension *decreases* with surfactant concentration $\Rightarrow$ $\Gamma_2 > 0$ → **positive adsorption**. This is the bridge connecting a measurable bulk property ($\gamma$) to the interfacial excess. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — QCM Gravimetric Analysis **Given:** A 6 MHz AT-cut QCM crystal (sensitivity constant $C_f = 12.3$ ng·Hz⁻¹·cm⁻², electrode area $A = 0.196$ cm²) registers a frequency shift $\Delta f = -85$ Hz during protein adsorption. **Find:** Mass adsorbed ($\Delta m$) and surface excess ($\Gamma$). ### Step 1 — Apply Sauerbrey Equation $$\Delta m = -C_f \cdot \Delta f \cdot A$$ Substituting: $$\Delta m = -(12.3)(-85)(0.196)$$ $$\Delta m = +204.95 \ \text{ng} \approx 205 \ \text{ng}$$ ### Step 2 — Convert to Moles (Molecular Mass M = 66 000 g/mol for BSA) $$n_{ads} = \frac{205 \times 10^{-9}}{66\,000} = 3.106 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{mol}$$ ### Step 3 — Surface Excess $$\Gamma = \frac{n_{ads}}{A} = \frac{3.106 \times 10^{-12}}{0.196 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.585 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{mol/m}^2$$ Converting: $$\Gamma \approx 158.5 \ \mu\text{mol/m}^2 \approx 1.58 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{mmol/m}^2$$ > [!NOTE] > This value lies in the typical physiological range for bovine serum albumin (BSA) monolayer coverage, confirming the result is physically reasonable. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Volumetric BET Analysis **Given:** N₂ adsorption at 77 K on an unknown catalyst yields the following data (after blank correction): | $P/P_0$ | Volume adsorbed $V$ (cm³ STP/g) | |:---:|:---:| | 0.05 | 85 | | 0.10 | 110 | | 0.20 | 140 | | 0.30 | 160 | **Find:** $V_m$ (monolayer capacity) and $C$ (BET constant). ### Step 1 — Build the BET Plot Variables Compute $X = P/P_0$ and $Y = X/[V(1-X)]$: $$X_1 = 0.05 \Rightarrow Y_1 = \frac{0.05}{85(0.95)} = 6.19 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$X_2 = 0.10 \Rightarrow Y_2 = \frac{0.10}{110(0.90)} = 1.01 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$X_3 = 0.20 \Rightarrow Y_3 = \frac{0.20}{140(0.80)} = 1.79 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$X_4 = 0.30 \Rightarrow Y_4 = \frac{0.30}{160(0.70)} = 2.68 \times 10^{-3}$$ ### Step 2 — Linear Regression Linear fit $Y = mX + b$: $$m \approx 8.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{(per cm}^3 \text{ STP/g)}$$ $$b \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{(per cm}^3 \text{ STP/g)}$$ ### Step 3 — Extract $V_m$ and $C$ From the BET linear equation: $$V_m = \frac{1}{m + b} = \frac{1}{8.5 \times 10^{-3} + 2.5 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 114.3 \ \text{cm}^3 \text{ STP/g}$$ $$C = \frac{m}{b} + 1 = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} + 1 \approx 35$$ ### Step 4 — Surface Area (Using Cross-Sectional Area of N₂ = 0.162 nm²) $$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot \sigma_{N_2}}{V_{molar}}$$ where $V_{molar}$ at STP = 22 414 cm³/mol: $$S_{BET} = \frac{114.3 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162 \times 10^{-18}}{22\,414}$$ $$S_{BET} \approx 497 \ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$$ This is characteristic of a **mesoporous catalyst** (MCM-41 type). # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Explain briefly the principle of the Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) for adsorption measurement.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) QCM relies on the **piezoelectric effect** of an AT-cut quartz crystal oscillating at a characteristic shear-mode resonance frequency $f_0$. - (1 mark) When mass adsorbs on the crystal surface, the resonance frequency decreases; the shift $\Delta f$ is directly proportional to the adsorbed mass via the **Sauerbrey equation**: $\Delta f = -C_f \Delta m$. - (1 mark) The technique provides a **direct, in-situ, real-time** measurement of mass loading with nanogram sensitivity, applicable in both gas and liquid phases. ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is the role of a blank (helium) run in a volumetric adsorption measurement?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) Helium is non-adsorbing at room temperature, so any measured volume change in a helium run represents only the **dead volume / free space** $V_{free}$ of the sample cell. - (1 mark) This $V_{free}$ is subtracted from subsequent adsorbate runs to obtain the *true* volume of gas adsorbed on the solid, excluding gas present in the gas phase. - (1 mark) Without this correction, the calculated $n_{ads}$ would be erroneously high, leading to an overestimation of the BET surface area. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **(a)** Describe, with a neat labelled diagram, the **principle and working of a manometric (volumetric) apparatus** for gas adsorption measurement. List the key components and their functions. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** The following data was obtained for N₂ adsorption at 77 K on a porous solid. Using the BET equation, calculate the **monolayer volume $V_m$**, the **BET constant $C$**, and the **specific surface area** (take the cross-sectional area of N₂ as $0.162$ nm²). **[7 Marks]** | $P/P_0$ | $V$ (cm³ STP/g) | |:---:|:---:| | 0.06 | 92 | | 0.12 | 118 | | 0.20 | 142 | | 0.30 | 162 | #### Model Solution to A(a): **Principle (2 marks):** A known quantity of gas is admitted from a calibrated dose volume into a sample cell containing the adsorbent. The pressure drop observed after equilibration is converted to moles adsorbed via the ideal gas law: $$n_{ads} = \frac{1}{RT}\left(P_d V_d - P_{eq} V_{free}\right)$$ **Key Components (3 marks):** - **Gas dosing manifold** with precision pressure regulator. - **Calibrated dose volume $V_d$** with isolation valve (V1). - **Sample cell** of known volume $V_s$ containing the adsorbent, maintained at 77 K (liquid N₂ bath). - **High-accuracy pressure transducer** (0.01% of full scale). - **Vacuum system** (turbo + rotary) achieving $10^{-6}$ mbar. - **Temperature control** — sample in dewar of liquid N₂; dose volume in thermostatted bath. **Procedure Outline (2 marks):** Degassing → He blank run → stepwise N₂ dosing → equilibrium recorded → repeat for 6–10 relative pressures → apply BET plot. #### Model Solution to A(b): **Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the BET variables $X = P/P_0$ and $Y = X/[V(1-X)]$: $$Y_1 = \frac{0.06}{92 \times 0.94} = 6.94 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$Y_2 = \frac{0.12}{118 \times 0.88} = 1.155 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$Y_3 = \frac{0.20}{142 \times 0.80} = 1.761 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$Y_4 = \frac{0.30}{162 \times 0.70} = 2.646 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Linear regression on $Y$ vs $X$ gives slope $m \approx 8.5 \times 10^{-3}$ and intercept $b \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Extract $V_m$ and $C$: $$V_m = \frac{1}{m + b} = \frac{1}{8.7 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 115 \ \text{cm}^3 \text{ STP/g}$$ $$C = \frac{m}{b} + 1 = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} + 1 \approx 43.5$$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Specific surface area: $$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m N_A \sigma_{N_2}}{V_{molar}} = \frac{115 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162 \times 10^{-18}}{22\,414} \approx 500 \ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$$ --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]` **(a)** Derive the **BET equation** for multilayer adsorption starting from the kinetic model. Clearly state the assumptions. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the **principle and instrumentation of Ellipsometry** for measuring adsorption at the solid-liquid interface. Mention any two advantages and two limitations. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution to B(a): **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Multilayer adsorption occurs; the heat of adsorption for the first layer is $E_1$, and for all subsequent layers equals the heat of liquefaction $E_L$. 2. At equilibrium, the rate of condensation on an empty site equals the rate of evaporation from an occupied site in that layer. 3. No lateral interaction between adsorbed molecules. **Derivation (5 marks):** As developed in Section 3.1 above, after defining $\theta_i$ and applying the kinetic balance: $$\theta_0 = \frac{1-x}{1 + (C-1)x}$$ $$V = V_m \cdot \frac{Cx}{(1-x)(1+(C-1)x)}$$ Rearranging into the linear form: $$\frac{P}{V(P_0-P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C-1}{V_m C}\cdot\frac{P}{P_0}$$ The plot of $P/[V(P_0-P)]$ vs $P/P_0$ yields slope $(C-1)/(V_m C)$ and intercept $1/(V_m C)$, allowing extraction of $V_m$ and $C$. #### Model Solution to B(b): **Principle (2 marks):** Polarized monochromatic light reflects off the substrate; the polarization state changes described by $\Psi$ (amplitude ratio) and $\Delta$ (phase shift). A thin adsorbed film (thickness $d$, refractive index $n_f$) modifies these angles; fitting Fresnel equations yields $d$ from which $\Gamma = d \rho / M$. **Instrumentation (3 marks):** Light source (laser, He-Ne 632.8 nm) → polarizer (sets input polarization) → compensator (quarter-wave plate for nulling) → sample stage (with liquid cell or liquid flow cell) → analyzer → photodetector. **Advantages (1 mark):** - Sub-angstrom thickness sensitivity. - Non-destructive, works through transparent media (ideal for solid-liquid interfaces). **Limitations (1 mark):** - Requires optically reflective substrates. - Model-dependent — multiple fitting parameters in complex multi-layer systems. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **In BET numericals**, students often forget to *correct for the dead volume* before plotting — losing 2 marks immediately. > 2. **In derivations**, examiners expect the explicit definition of $C$ in terms of $E_1 - E_L$. Omitting this loses the conceptual mark. > 3. **Ellipsometry** answers that confuse $\Psi$ (amplitude) with $\Delta$ (phase) are penalized. Memorize: **Ψ = tan⁻¹(|rₚ/rₛ|)** and **Δ = phase(rₚ) − phase(rₛ)**. > 4. **QCM numericals**: do not forget the *electrode area* $A$ in the Sauerbrey equation; writing $\Delta m = -C_f \Delta f$ without $A$ yields a 1-mark deduction. > 5. **Units**: surface excess $\Gamma$ must be in **mol/m²**, not mol/g — a common slip worth 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Five direct techniques** are in the KTU scope: **Gravimetric (QCM)**, **Volumetric (Manometric)**, **Calorimetric**, **Optical (Ellipsometry/SPR)**, and **Radiotracer**. - **Sauerbrey equation** is the heart of QCM: $\Delta f = -C_f \Delta m \cdot A$, sensitivity **17.7 ng·Hz⁻¹·cm⁻²** for 5 MHz crystal. - **Manometric method** requires a **helium blank run** to subtract dead volume. - **BET equation** is valid in the range $0.05 < P/P_0 < 0.35$; outside this, capillary condensation or monolayer-only Langmuir behavior applies. - **Calorimetry** yields **differential heats of adsorption** $q_{diff}$ — a powerful fingerprint of surface heterogeneity. - **Ellipsometry** measures two angles $\Psi$ and $\Delta$, from which film thickness is recovered via Fresnel modeling. - **Radiotracer** is uniquely powerful for **sub-monolayer** adsorption where QCM sensitivity is inadequate. - **Gibbs adsorption isotherm**: $\Gamma = -(C/RT)(d\gamma/dC)$ — bridges bulk surface tension to surface excess. - **Units checklist for KTU exam**: $\Gamma$ → **mol/m²**; $V$ → **cm³ STP/g**; $q$ → **kJ/mol**; $d$ → **Å or nm**; $\Delta f$ → **Hz**. - **Diagram must always be drawn** in manometric/apparatus questions — bare-text answers lose at least 2 marks. - The **surface area of N₂** used in BET calculation is **0.162 nm²** — memorize this constant. - IUPAC classifies pores by width: **micropore < 2 nm**, **mesopore 2–50 nm**, **macropore > 50 nm** — relevant when interpreting isotherm shape.
